2. **Boxplot** — Visualizes the five-number summary and outliers side-by-side; ideal for *comparing* multiple groups.
3. **Quantile-Quantile (Q-Q) Plot** — Compares the quantiles of a variable against theoretical quantiles of a normal distribution; detects departures from normality.
4. **Scatter Plot** — Visualizes the relationship between *two* numeric variables; foundation for correlation analysis and regression diagnostics.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Wrong denominator in variance:** Students frequently write $\frac{1}{n}$ instead of $\frac{1}{n-1}$ for a *sample*. Examiners will deduct 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting to sort the data** before computing the median or quartiles. Always sort first, even if the data "looks sorted".
> 3. **Mixing up the quartile formula** $L = \frac{k}{100}(N+1)$ with Excel's $\frac{k}{100}(N-1) + 1$. Pick one convention, state it explicitly, and apply it consistently.
> 4. **Reporting only one measure** when the question asks "compute mean, median, mode, range" — every value carries marks. Skipping any one will cost 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Boxplot without the IQR-based outlier rule** — writing just "outliers are points far away" is non-rigorous and loses at least 1 mark.
> 6. **Units:** Variance has *squared* units (e.g., °C²); standard deviation has the original units (°C). Examiners check this.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Central Tendency quartet:** Mean, Median, Mode, Midrange.
- **Mean is sensitive**, Median is **robust** to outliers — choose based on data shape.
- **Mode is the only central measure** valid for *categorical* data.
- **Five-Number Summary:** $\{x_{\min},\; Q_1,\; \text{Median},\; Q_3,\; x_{\max}\}$.
- **IQR = $Q_3 - Q_1$** — robust spread; ignores the bottom and top 25% of data.
- **Outlier fences:** $Q_1 - 1.5\,\text{IQR}$ and $Q_3 + 1.5\,\text{IQR}$.
- **Sample variance uses $(n-1)$** (Bessel's correction); population variance uses $N$.
- **Standard deviation** $s = \sqrt{s^2}$ — same units as the data; the most interpretable spread measure.
- **Pearson Skewness:** $\frac{3(\bar{x} - \text{Median})}{s}$ — positive ⇒ right-skewed, negative ⇒ left-skewed, zero ⇒ symmetric.
- **Graphical quartet for EDA:** Histogram, Boxplot, Q-Q plot, Scatter plot.
- **Always sort the data** before computing the median or any quartile.
- **State your quartile convention** explicitly to earn full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Cleaning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT524 - DATA MINING AND WAREHOUSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Data Cleaning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Data Cleaning – Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Data Cleaning (also called Data Cleansing or Data Scrubbing)** is the foundational step in the **Knowledge Discovery in Databases (KDD)** process that focuses on detecting, correcting, or removing **incomplete, noisy, inconsistent, and irrelevant** portions of a dataset. It ensures that the data fed into a data mining algorithm is **accurate, complete, consistent, and trustworthy**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Data Mining and Warehousing*, Data Cleaning is positioned as a critical **data preprocessing** activity, sitting between **Data Integration** and **Data Transformation**. The quality of the output of any mining algorithm is fundamentally constrained by the *Garbage-In-Garbage-Out (GIGO)* principle.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Hospital Reception" Intuition

Imagine you are a doctor and a patient walks in carrying a thick stack of papers. Some pages have torn corners, some lab values are scribbled over, dates are inconsistent (e.g., "DOB: 12/05/1995" on one page, "DOB: 15 May 1995" on another), some readings are missing (e.g., blood pressure not recorded), and a few values are clearly wrong (e.g., body temperature 200°C). Before you can diagnose anything, you must:

1. **Fill in** the missing BP reading (Missing Value Imputation).
2. **Cross out** the ridiculous 200°C reading (Noise Removal).
3. **Standardize** the two date formats to one (Inconsistency Resolution).
4. **Verify** the patient's name matches across all sheets (Redundancy / Duplication).

That is exactly what **Data Cleaning** does to a raw dataset before it enters a data warehouse or a mining model.

## 1.3 Core Categories of "Dirty Data"

> [!NOTE]
> **The four pillars of dirty data that cleaning must address:**

| # | Problem Type | Description | Example |
|---|--------------|-------------|---------|
| 1 | **Missing Values** | Attribute value not recorded for an instance | `Age = NULL` in a customer record |
| 2 | **Noisy Data** | Random error or variance in a measured variable | Sensor reads `Temperature = 999°C` |
| 3 | **Inconsistent Data** | Discrepancies in codes, names, or formats | `Gender: "M"` vs `Gender: "Male"` |
| 4 | **Outliers** | Data points that deviate drastically from the norm | A salary of $1 in a list of $50k–$200k |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Pipeline of Data Cleaning

Data Cleaning is not a single operation—it is a **sequential pipeline**. The KTU 2024 Scheme expects you to remember the order and purpose of each step.

### Step 1 – Audit the Data
Identify anomalies by writing scripts or using **data quality rules** (e.g., `Age BETWEEN 0 AND 120`). The result is a **diagnostic report** listing dirty cells.

### Step 2 – Define Cleaning Workflow
Select the transformation rules. For example: *"Any negative age value will be flagged and replaced with the column mean."*

### Step 3 – Execute Transformation
Apply the rules using ETL tools, SQL `UPDATE` statements, or libraries (conceptually only — no code required for non-CS application).

### Step 4 – Post-Cleaning Verification
Re-run audits. **Discrepancy Reduction Rate (DRR)** should approach 0.

$$
\text{DRR} = \frac{\text{Number of dirty records AFTER cleaning}}{\text{Number of dirty records BEFORE cleaning}} \times 100\%
$$

## 2.2 High-Yield Methods for Handling Missing Values

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Six KTU-favorite techniques** (high probability of appearing in 14-mark derivations):

1. **Ignore the Tuple / Record** – Drop the row entirely. Best when missingness is random and dataset is huge.
2. **Manual Imputation** – Domain expert fills in values. Accurate but **unscalable**.
3. **Global Constant Fill** – Replace with a placeholder like `"Unknown"`. Simplest, but may mislead the mining model.
4. **Attribute Mean / Median / Mode** – Statistical imputation. **Most common in exam questions**.
5. **Most Probable Value (ML-based)** – Use regression, decision trees, or EM algorithms to *predict* the missing value.
6. **Forward / Backward Fill** – In time-series, propagate the last known value.

## 2.3 Noise Filtering – The Three Pillars

Noise = random error. Three standard smoothing techniques:

| Technique | Core Idea | Formula / Mechanism |
|-----------|-----------|---------------------|
| **Binning** | Sort values, distribute into equal-frequency bins, smooth by bin mean, median, or boundary | Smoothing by bin means |
| **Regression** | Fit a function (linear / multiple) to the data; use the function to replace the noisy point | $\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x$ |
| **Clustering** | Group similar values; points outside clusters are detected as outliers | Distance-based detection |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical formulas for Data Cleaning derivations:**

| Symbol / Method | Mathematical Expression | Purpose / Unit |
|-----------------|------------------------|---------------|
| Bin Mean Smoothing | $\bar{x}_{\text{bin}} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$ | Replace all values in a bin with their arithmetic mean (no unit / dataset-dependent) |
| Bin Median Smoothing | $\tilde{x} = \text{median}(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$ | Robust to outliers; used for skewed data |
| Linear Regression (1 var) | $y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x + \varepsilon$ | Predict / smooth a numeric attribute |
| Estimated Coefficient | $\beta_1 = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}$ | Slope of best-fit line |
| Estimated Intercept | $\beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}$ | Bias term of regression |
| Discrepancy Reduction | $\text{DRR} = \dfrac{D_{\text{after}}}{D_{\text{before}}} \times 100\%$ | Quality metric (percent) |
| Min-Max Normalization (related) | $v' = \dfrac{v - \min_A}{\max_A - \min_A} \times (\text{new\_max} - \text{new\_min}) + \text{new\_min}$ | Scales cleaned data to a standard range |
| Z-Score (Outlier Detection) | $z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ | $\vert z \vert > 3$ is treated as an outlier |

> [!WARNING]
> **Engineering Utility:** In IoT sensor networks, **Binning + Regression** is used to clean time-series streams before anomaly detection. In banking, **Discrepancy Reduction Rate (DRR)** is a key SLA metric for data quality dashboards.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation – Binning by Mean Smoothing

**Problem Statement:** Given the sorted values of an attribute *A*:
$$3, 5, 7, 9, 15, 17, 19, 21$$
Partition them into **3 equal-frequency bins** and apply **smoothing by bin means**.

### Step 1 – Sort the Data
The values are already sorted in ascending order. No reordering is required.

### Step 2 – Determine the Bin Size
With $n = 8$ values and $k = 3$ bins, the bin width is:
$$
w = \left\lceil \frac{n}{k} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{8}{3} \right\rceil = \lceil 2.67 \rceil = 3
$$

So each bin will contain **3 values** (the last bin may be smaller or padded).

### Step 3 – Construct the Bins

$$
\text{Bin}_1 = \{3, 5, 7\}, \quad \text{Bin}_2 = \{9, 15, 17\}, \quad \text{Bin}_3 = \{19, 21\}
$$

### Step 4 – Compute the Mean of Each Bin

$$
\bar{x}_1 = \frac{3 + 5 + 7}{3} = \frac{15}{3} = 5
$$

$$
\bar{x}_2 = \frac{9 + 15 + 17}{3} = \frac{41}{3} \approx 13.67
$$

$$
\bar{x}_3 = \frac{19 + 21}{2} = \frac{40}{2} = 20
$$

### Step 5 – Replace All Values in Each Bin with the Bin Mean

$$
\text{Cleaned Data} = \{5, 5, 5,\ 13.67, 13.67, 13.67,\ 20, 20\}
$$

### Step 6 – Interpretation
The cleaned dataset has lost intra-bin variance (good for noise removal) but preserves inter-bin variance (good for mining). This is the trade-off of bin smoothing.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix – Comparing Missing-Value Imputation Methods

> [!NOTE]
> The following matrix is a high-yield exam table. Memorize the **bolded** keywords.

| Imputation Method | Formula / Logic | Best Use Case | Drawback |
|-------------------|-----------------|---------------|----------|
| **Ignore Tuple** | Drop row if $\exists$ attribute where $x = \text{NULL}$ | Missing Completely At Random (MCAR), large $n$ | Loss of information |
| **Fill with Mean** | $x' = \bar{x} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m} x_i$ | Symmetric numeric data | Reduces variance, distorts correlation |
| **Fill with Median** | $x' = \tilde{x}$ | Skewed data, presence of outliers | Ignores feature relationships |
| **Fill with Mode** | $x' = \text{mode}(x)$ | Categorical attributes | Multiple modes tie-break issue |
| **Global Constant** | $x' = \text{"Unknown"}$ | Missing-categorical data | Algorithm may treat it as a valid class |
| **Regression Imputation** | $x' = \beta_0 + \beta_1 y$ | Data has strong correlations | Overestimates model fit |

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation – Linear Regression for Noise Removal

**Given data points** for attribute *Y* vs *X*:
$$
(X, Y) = \{(1, 2),\ (2, 4),\ (3, 5),\ (4, 4),\ (5, 5)\}
$$

A noisy reading at $X = 4$ gives $Y = 4$ but the regression line predicts $Y = 4.5$. We will compute the clean replacement.

### Step 1 – Compute the Means

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{1 + 2 + 3 + 4 + 5}{5} = \frac{15}{5} = 3
$$

$$
\bar{y} = \frac{2 + 4 + 5 + 4 + 5}{5} = \frac{20}{5} = 4
$$

### Step 2 – Compute Deviations and Cross-Products

| $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $x_i - \bar{x}$ | $y_i - \bar{y}$ | $(x_i-\bar{x})(y_i-\bar{y})$ | $(x_i-\bar{x})^2$ |
|-------|-------|-----------------|-----------------|-----------------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | 2 | -2 | -2 | 4 | 4 |
| 2 | 4 | -1 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| 3 | 5 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| 4 | 4 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| 5 | 5 | 2 | 1 | 2 | 4 |
| **Sum** | | | | **6** | **10** |

### Step 3 – Compute the Slope $\beta_1$

$$
\beta_1 = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2} = \frac{6}{10} = 0.6
$$

### Step 4 – Compute the Intercept $\beta_0$

$$
\beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x} = 4 - (0.6)(3) = 4 - 1.8 = 2.2
$$

### Step 5 – Write the Regression Equation

$$
\hat{y} = 2.2 + 0.6\,x
$$

### Step 6 – Replace the Noisy Value at $X = 4$

$$
\hat{y}_{(x=4)} = 2.2 + 0.6(4) = 2.2 + 2.4 = 4.6
$$

So the noisy point $(4, 4)$ is replaced with the regression-smoothed value $(4, 4.6)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU frequently asks for either **Binning** or **Regression** smoothing as a full 7-mark sub-question. Practice at least one numerical for each.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram – The Data Cleaning Pipeline

The following block diagram captures the **architectural flow** of the entire data cleaning subsystem inside a typical KDD framework.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[rawDataSource] --> B[auditModule]
    B --> C{anomalyDetected}
    C -- No --> Z[cleanDataset]
    C -- Yes --> D[missingValueHandler]
    D --> E[noiseFilter]
    E --> F[inconsistencyResolver]
    F --> G[outlierDetector]
    G --> H[duplicateRemover]
    H --> I[verifyAndValidate]
    I --> J{qualityMet}
    J -- No --> B
    J -- Yes --> Z
    Z --> K[machineLearningModel]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The pipeline is **iterative** — if post-validation fails (quality not met), control loops back to the audit module. This is a classic *closed-loop data quality architecture*.
- Each transform (D, E, F, G, H) is **order-sensitive**: noise must be filtered **after** missing values are handled, otherwise regression cannot fit on `NaN`s.

## 4.2 Block Diagram – Noise Filtering Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[noisySignal] --> B[binningEngine]
    A --> C[regressionEngine]
    A --> D[clusteringEngine]
    B --> E[cleanedSignal]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[qualityMetricDashboard]
    F --> G{DRR less than 5 percent}
    G -- Yes --> H[outputToWarehouse]
    G -- No --> I[humanReviewQueue]
```

## 4.3 Decision Matrix Diagram – Choosing an Imputation Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[missingValueDetected] --> B{dataType}
    B -- Numeric --> C{distributionSymmetric}
    B -- Categorical --> D[useMode]
    C -- Yes --> E[useMean]
    C -- No --> F[useMedian]
    E --> G[assessVariance]
    F --> G
    G --> H{varianceLostOver10pct}
    H -- Yes --> I[useRegressionImputation]
    H -- No --> J[acceptMeanMedian]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Data Cleaning. List the major issues it addresses. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
Data Cleaning is the process of identifying and removing errors, inconsistencies, and missing values from raw data to improve its quality for mining. The major issues addressed are:
1. **Missing Values** – data not recorded.
2. **Noisy Data** – random error in measured values.
3. **Inconsistencies** – conflicting codes or formats.
4. **Duplicates** – redundant records.
5. **Outliers** – extreme values that distort analysis.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Binning and Regression as noise-filtering techniques. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Binning | Regression |
|-----------|---------|------------|
| Approach | Local smoothing using neighboring sorted values | Global smoothing by fitting a function |
| Requires | Sorting + grouping | Historical (X, Y) pairs |
| Output | Replaced by bin mean / median / boundary | Replaced by predicted $\hat{y}$ |
| Best For | Ordered data, time-series | Strongly correlated numeric attributes |

---

## Part B – Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Following KTU's internal-choice pattern, **both alternatives** must be attempted-ready.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 1]`

**(a) Explain the various techniques for handling missing values in a dataset.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Ignore the Tuple** – The entire record is discarded if any attribute is missing. Suitable when missingness is random and dataset is large.
2. **Manual Imputation** – A domain expert manually fills in the value. Most accurate but expensive.
3. **Global Constant** – Replace all missing entries with a constant such as `"Unknown"` or `-1`. Simple but may introduce bias.
4. **Attribute Mean** – For symmetric numeric data, replace with $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum x_i$.
5. **Attribute Median** – For skewed numeric data, use $\tilde{x}$. Robust to outliers.
6. **Attribute Mode** – Used for categorical data, replaces with the most frequent class.
7. **Most Probable Value (ML-based)** – Use algorithms like Expectation-Maximization (EM), KNN, or regression to *predict* the missing value from other correlated attributes. Most accurate for non-random missingness.

**(b) Given the sorted data values of an attribute: 4, 8, 9, 15, 21, 21, 24, 28, 31. Apply smoothing by (i) bin means and (ii) bin boundaries, using 3 equal-frequency bins.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Determine bin size:** $n = 9$, $k = 3$, so $w = \lceil 9/3 \rceil = 3$.

**Step 2 — Form bins:**
$$
\text{Bin}_1 = \{4, 8, 9\}, \quad \text{Bin}_2 = \{15, 21, 21\}, \quad \text{Bin}_3 = \{24, 28, 31\}
$$

**(i) Smoothing by bin means:**

$$
\bar{x}_1 = \frac{4 + 8 + 9}{3} = \frac{21}{3} = 7
$$

$$
\bar{x}_2 = \frac{15 + 21 + 21}{3} = \frac{57}{3} = 19
$$

$$
\bar{x}_3 = \frac{24 + 28 + 31}{3} = \frac{83}{3} \approx 27.67
$$

**Cleaned data** = $\{7, 7, 7,\ 19, 19, 19,\ 27.67, 27.67, 27.67\}$

**(ii) Smoothing by bin boundaries:**
Each value is replaced by the nearest boundary (min or max) of its bin.

- $\text{Bin}_1$ boundaries = $\{4, 9\}$: $4 \to 4$, $8 \to 9$ (closer to 9), $9 \to 9$.
- $\text{Bin}_2$ boundaries = $\{15, 21\}$: $15 \to 15$, $21 \to 21$, $21 \to 21$.
- $\text{Bin}_3$ boundaries = $\{24, 31\}$: $24 \to 24$, $28 \to 31$ (closer), $31 \to 31$.

**Cleaned data** = $\{4, 9, 9,\ 15, 21, 21,\ 24, 31, 31\}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often forget to **sort** the data before binning. KTU graders deduct **2 marks** for unsorted bins. Also, in *bin boundary smoothing*, students must replace each value with the **closest** boundary of its bin, not just the min or max blindly.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 1]`

**(a) What is noisy data? Explain the binning and clustering methods used for noise removal.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Noisy Data** refers to random variation in a measured variable or measurement error that obscures the underlying pattern. It cannot be interpreted meaningfully by a mining algorithm.

**Binning Method:**
- Sort the data and partition into equal-frequency or equal-width bins.
- Smooth values within each bin using **bin mean, bin median, or bin boundary**.
- Eliminates intra-bin variance while preserving inter-bin trends.

**Clustering Method:**
- Apply a clustering algorithm (e.g., K-Means) on the dataset.
- Points within a cluster are similar; points lying far from all cluster centroids are identified as **outliers / noise**.
- The outliers can be either removed or replaced with the cluster centroid.

**(b) The following training data records temperature (X) and pressure (Y). Use linear regression to predict the cleaned value of Y when X = 6, given that the original noisy reading was 5.5.** **(7 Marks)**

| X | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Y | 2 | 3 | 5 | 4 | 6 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute means:**

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{1+2+3+4+5}{5} = 3, \quad \bar{y} = \frac{2+3+5+4+6}{5} = 4
$$

**Step 2 — Build the deviation table:**

| $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $x_i-\bar{x}$ | $y_i-\bar{y}$ | $(x_i-\bar{x})(y_i-\bar{y})$ | $(x_i-\bar{x})^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2 | -2 | -2 | 4 | 4 |
| 2 | 3 | -1 | -1 | 1 | 1 |
| 3 | 5 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| 4 | 4 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| 5 | 6 | 2 | 2 | 4 | 4 |
| **Σ** | | | | **9** | **10** |

**Step 3 — Slope:**

$$
\beta_1 = \frac{9}{10} = 0.9
$$

**Step 4 — Intercept:**

$$
\beta_0 = 4 - (0.9)(3) = 4 - 2.7 = 1.3
$$

**Step 5 — Regression line:**

$$
\hat{y} = 1.3 + 0.9\,x
$$

**Step 6 — Cleaned value at $X = 6$:**

$$
\hat{y}_{(6)} = 1.3 + 0.9(6) = 1.3 + 5.4 = 6.7
$$

**Conclusion:** The noisy reading $Y = 5.5$ is replaced by the regression-predicted $\hat{y} = 6.7$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** A common mistake is computing the slope as $\frac{\sum x_i y_i}{\sum x_i^2}$ instead of the **centered (deviation) form**. This is wrong when the data is not zero-mean. Always use the centered formula shown above to avoid losing 3 marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the KTU Exam Hall:**

- **Data Cleaning** is a preprocessing step that fixes *missing values, noise, inconsistencies, duplicates, and outliers*.
- **Missing-value strategies:** Ignore tuple, manual fill, global constant, mean / median / mode, ML-based imputation, forward fill.
- **Noise-filtering trio:** Binning, Regression, Clustering.
- **Binning steps** (must-do order): Sort → Partition into equal-frequency / equal-width bins → Replace using *mean / median / boundary*.
- **Bin size formula:** $w = \lceil n/k \rceil$.
- **Linear regression formula (centered form):** $\beta_1 = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}$, $\beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}$.
- **Quality metric:** Discrepancy Reduction Rate (DRR) — always express as a percentage.
- **Outlier detection shortcut:** Z-score, where $\vert z \vert > 3$ flags an outlier.
- **Categorical imputation** → always use **Mode** (never the mean).
- **Skewed numeric imputation** → always use **Median** (mean is sensitive to skew).
- **Most accurate (and expensive) imputation** → ML-based (Regression / EM / KNN).
- **Golden rule:** Always **sort** before binning; always use **deviation form** for regression slope; always **state units** in the final answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Integration and Transformation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT524 - DATA MINING AND WAREHOUSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Data Integration and Transformation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Data Integration — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Data Integration** is the process of combining data residing in different sources and providing the user with a unified view of these data. It is a critical preprocessing step in the Knowledge Discovery in Databases (KDD) pipeline where heterogeneous, distributed, and autonomous data sources are merged into a single, coherent data store for subsequent mining operations.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, data integration is treated as a mandatory data preparation stage that precedes warehousing, OLAP, and data mining. It typically follows the `ETL` (Extract, Transform, Load) paradigm.

**Real-World Analogy — The Master Recipe Book:**
Imagine a chef who wants to create the ultimate cookbook. They have:
- Recipes from their grandmother's handwritten notes (unstructured, Malayalam script).
- Recipes from a modern English website (structured JSON, metric units).
- Recipes from a YouTube cooking channel (video metadata, imperial units).

**Data Integration** is the chef's act of putting all these recipes into a single master book, converting everything to a common unit (grams, °C), removing duplicate recipes (e.g., the same "Fish Curry" appearing twice under different names), and resolving conflicts (e.g., one source says "1 cup of rice" while another says "200 g of rice"). Without this unified book, the chef cannot perform meaningful analysis like "What is the most common ingredient across South Indian breakfasts?".

### 1.2 Data Transformation — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Data Transformation** is the process of converting data from one format or structure into another format or structure. It is the second crucial preprocessing step where raw, integrated data is consolidated, cleaned, normalized, and shaped into a form suitable for mining algorithms.

Common transformation strategies taught in the KTU syllabus include:
- **Smoothing**: Removing noise from data (binning, regression, clustering).
- **Aggregation**: Combining attributes (e.g., daily sales → monthly sales).
- **Normalization**: Scaling attribute values to fall within a small, specified range (e.g., -1.0 to 1.0 or 0.0 to 1.0).
- **Attribute Construction (Feature Construction)**: Creating new attributes from existing ones to aid mining.
- **Discretization**: Replacing continuous raw values with discrete interval labels.

**Real-World Analogy — The Standardized Exam:**
Consider a class of 50 students from different schools writing the same KTU exam. One school uses a 100-point scale, another uses a 50-point scale, and a third uses letter grades (A, B, C). Before the results can be ranked fairly, the university **transforms** all scores onto a common 10-point CGPA scale. This is data transformation — making disparate data comparable.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> Data integration and transformation are grouped under *"Data Preprocessing"* and are examinable as both 3-mark short questions (definitions, identification of issues) and 14-mark long questions (full descriptive treatment with normalization calculations and schema integration examples).

---

### 1.3 Major Issues in Data Integration

When data is combined from multiple heterogeneous sources, the following **major issues** must be addressed:

| # | Issue | Description |
|---|-------|-------------|
| 1 | **Schema Integration** | Different sources may use different attribute names for the same concept (e.g., `student_id` vs. `roll_no`). |
| 2 | **Redundancy** | An attribute may be a redundant copy of another (e.g., `annual_salary` = `monthly_salary × 12`). |
| 3 | **Data Value Conflicts** | The same attribute may have different values in different systems (e.g., price in USD vs. INR, weight in kg vs. lbs). |
| 4 | **Inconsistent Metadata** | Field types, units, and precision may differ across sources. |
| 5 | **Entity Identification** | Recognizing that `customer_id` in DB1 and `cust_num` in DB2 refer to the same real-world entity. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Correlation Analysis for Redundancy Detection:**
> Redundancy can be detected using the **χ² (chi-square) test** for nominal (categorical) data and the **correlation coefficient** for numeric data. If two attributes have a correlation coefficient $\rho_{A,B} > 0$ (or a strong χ²), one may be redundant.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Data Integration Pipeline (Step-by-Step Logic)

The integration process is executed in the following structured sequence:

- **Step 1 — Source Identification & Schema Mapping:**
  * Identify all heterogeneous data sources (relational DBs, flat files, XML, web APIs).
  * Build a **global schema** that maps corresponding attributes from local schemas. The `Why`: to provide a unified logical view. The `How`: by using metadata catalogs and schema-matching algorithms.

- **Step 2 — Tuple Duplication Detection:**
  * Identify duplicate records referring to the same real-world entity. The `Why`: duplication inflates support counts in association rule mining. The `How`: by using primary keys, approximate match keys, or similarity functions.

- **Step 3 — Redundancy Detection & Removal:**
  * Compute attribute correlations. The `Why`: redundancy slows down mining and biases models. The `How`: by applying **Pearson's correlation coefficient** (for numeric data) or **χ² test** (for nominal data) and removing one of any correlated pair.

- **Step 4 — Conflict Resolution:**
  * Reconcile differing values (e.g., different units, different currencies, different scales). The `Why`: mining algorithms assume consistent data. The `How`: by applying documented conversion functions and central dictionaries.

- **Step 5 — Loading into Target Warehouse:**
  * The cleaned, integrated data is loaded into a data warehouse via bulk loaders or ETL tools (Informatica, Talend, Apache Airflow).

### 2.2 Detailed Analysis of Data Transformation Methods

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Transform Data?**
> Most mining algorithms (k-NN, neural networks, k-means clustering, gradient descent-based models) are highly sensitive to the **scale** of input features. An attribute measured in rupees (range 0 to 1,000,000) will numerically dominate an attribute measured in age (range 0 to 100) in distance-based computations, leading to biased results.

#### 2.2.1 Smoothing
A noise-removal technique. Three common methods:
- **Binning:** Sort the data, partition into equal-frequency or equal-width bins, and replace values with bin mean, bin median, or bin boundaries.
- **Regression:** Fit the data to a function (e.g., linear regression $y = wx + b$).
- **Clustering:** Detect and remove outliers as noise.

#### 2.2.2 Aggregation
Combining two or more attributes into a single attribute. Example: aggregating daily transaction data into monthly sales. This is a form of **data reduction**.

#### 2.2.3 Normalization (Detailed in Section 3)

#### 2.2.4 Attribute Construction
Creating new attributes by applying mathematical functions. Example: from `height` and `width`, construct `area = height × width`. This can enhance the discriminative power of mining models.

#### 2.2.5 Discretization
Converting continuous attributes into categorical ones using methods like **binning**, **histogram analysis**, or **entropy-based discretization**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Use `\vert` or `\mid` (never the raw `|`) inside the table to avoid breaking Markdown table syntax.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Typical Use | Parameters / Units |
|---|---------|---------------------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Min-Max Normalization** | $v' \;=\; \dfrac{v - \min_{A}}{\max_{A} - \min_{A}} \cdot (new\_\max - new\_\min) + new\_\min$ | Scales data to a bounded range, e.g., $[0,1]$ or $[-1,1]$ | $v$: original value, $v'$: new value, $\min_A, \max_A$: original min/max of attribute $A$ |
| 2 | **Z-Score Normalization** | $v' \;=\; \dfrac{v - \mu_{A}}{\sigma_{A}}$ | When actual min/max unknown or outliers are present | $\mu_A$: mean of $A$, $\sigma_A$: standard deviation of $A$ |
| 3 | **Decimal Scaling** | $v' \;=\; \dfrac{v}{10^{j}}$ where $j$ is the smallest integer such that $\max(\vert v' \vert) < 1$ | Simple preservation of digit structure | $j$: scaling exponent |
| 4 | **Pearson Correlation Coefficient** | $\rho_{A,B} \;=\; \dfrac{\sum (A_i - \bar{A})(B_i - \bar{B})}{n \cdot \sigma_A \cdot \sigma_B}$ | Detects redundancy between two numeric attributes $A, B$ | $n$: number of tuples, $\bar{A}, \bar{B}$: means |
| 5 | **Chi-Square Test Statistic** | $\chi^{2} \;=\; \sum \dfrac{(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^{2}}{E_{ij}}$ | Detects correlation between two nominal (categorical) attributes | $O_{ij}$: observed freq, $E_{ij}$: expected freq |
| 6 | **Covariance** | $\text{cov}(A,B) \;=\; \dfrac{1}{n-1} \sum (A_i - \bar{A})(B_i - \bar{B})$ | Measures joint variability of $A$ and $B$ | Units: product of attribute units |
| 7 | **Mean Smoothing (Binning)** | $\bar{v}_{\text{bin}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{m} \sum_{k=1}^{m} v_{k}$ | Noise reduction within each bin | $m$: number of values in the bin |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Applications of Data Integration & Transformation:**
> - **Banking & Fintech:** Integrating transaction data from multiple branches, ATMs, mobile apps, and UPI gateways; normalizing transaction amounts to a common currency for fraud detection.
> - **Healthcare (Medical KTU focus):** Merging patient records from multiple hospitals (different HL7/FHIR schemas) and normalizing lab values for diagnostic ML models.
> - **E-Commerce (Flipkart/Amazon-style):** Integrating product catalogs from thousands of sellers with conflicting units, prices, and category hierarchies, then normalizing features for recommendation engines.
> - **IoT & Smart Manufacturing:** Integrating sensor streams (temperature, pressure, vibration) from heterogeneous PLCs and normalizing them for predictive maintenance ML models.
> - **Smart City / Kerala State Planning:** Integrating census data, weather data, and traffic data for urban analytics.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Min-Max Normalization

**Problem Statement:**
Given the attribute `age` of 6 students with the following values (in years):

$$A = \{13,\; 15,\; 16,\; 16,\; 19,\; 20\}$$

Normalize the values to the range $[0, 1]$ using min-max normalization.

**Given Data:**
- $\min_A = 13$
- $\max_A = 20$
- $new\_\max = 1.0$, $new\_\min = 0.0$

**General Formula:**

$$v' \;=\; \frac{v - \min_{A}}{\max_{A} - \min_{A}} \cdot (new\_\max - new\_\min) + new\_\min$$

**Step-by-Step Derivation for each value:**

For $v = 13$:

$$v' = \frac{13 - 13}{20 - 13} \cdot (1.0 - 0.0) + 0.0 = \frac{0}{7} \cdot 1.0 = 0.000$$

For $v = 15$:

$$v' = \frac{15 - 13}{20 - 13} \cdot 1.0 = \frac{2}{7} = 0.286$$

For $v = 16$:

$$v' = \frac{16 - 13}{20 - 13} \cdot 1.0 = \frac{3}{7} = 0.429$$

For $v = 19$:

$$v' = \frac{19 - 13}{20 - 13} \cdot 1.0 = \frac{6}{7} = 0.857$$

For $v = 20$:

$$v' = \frac{20 - 13}{20 - 13} \cdot 1.0 = \frac{7}{7} = 1.000$$

**Result Table (normalized values):**

| Original $v$ | 13 | 15 | 16 | 16 | 19 | 20 |
|---------------|----|----|----|----|----|----|
| Normalized $v'$ | 0.000 | 0.286 | 0.429 | 0.429 | 0.857 | 1.000 |

---

### 3.2 Worked Example: Z-Score Normalization

**Problem Statement:**
The same dataset $A = \{13, 15, 16, 16, 19, 20\}$. Compute the mean $\mu_A$ and standard deviation $\sigma_A$, then apply z-score normalization.

**Step 1: Compute the Mean**

$$\mu_A = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i = \frac{13 + 15 + 16 + 16 + 19 + 20}{6} = \frac{99}{6} = 16.5$$

**Step 2: Compute the Standard Deviation**

First, compute $(A_i - \mu_A)^2$ for each value:

$$\begin{aligned}
(13 - 16.5)^{2} &= (-3.5)^{2} = 12.25 \\
(15 - 16.5)^{2} &= (-1.5)^{2} = 2.25 \\
(16 - 16.5)^{2} &= (-0.5)^{2} = 0.25 \\
(16 - 16.5)^{2} &= 0.25 \\
(19 - 16.5)^{2} &= (2.5)^{2} = 6.25 \\
(20 - 16.5)^{2} &= (3.5)^{2} = 12.25
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Sum the squared deviations**

$$\sum (A_i - \mu_A)^{2} = 12.25 + 2.25 + 0.25 + 0.25 + 6.25 + 12.25 = 33.50$$

**Step 4: Compute Variance and Standard Deviation (population form)**

$$\sigma_A^{2} = \frac{\sum (A_i - \mu_A)^{2}}{n} = \frac{33.50}{6} \approx 5.5833$$

$$\sigma_A = \sqrt{5.5833} \approx 2.363$$

**Step 5: Apply z-score formula $v' = (v - \mu_A) / \sigma_A$ to each value**

$$\begin{aligned}
v' = 13: \quad & \frac{13 - 16.5}{2.363} = \frac{-3.5}{2.363} \approx -1.481 \\
v' = 15: \quad & \frac{-1.5}{2.363} \approx -0.635 \\
v' = 16: \quad & \frac{-0.5}{2.363} \approx -0.212 \\
v' = 19: \quad & \frac{2.5}{2.363} \approx 1.058 \\
v' = 20: \quad & \frac{3.5}{2.363} \approx 1.481
\end{aligned}$$

**Result Table:**

| Original $v$ | 13 | 15 | 16 | 16 | 19 | 20 |
|---------------|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| Z-Score $v'$ | -1.481 | -0.635 | -0.212 | -0.212 | 1.058 | 1.481 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation:** Z-score normalization produces values centered around 0, with the original mean mapped to 0 and most values falling within $[-3, 3]$. It is robust to outliers, unlike min-max which is sensitive to extreme values.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example: Decimal Scaling

**Problem Statement:**
Normalize the dataset $\{-12, -8, 4, 15, 89, 100\}$ using decimal scaling.

**Step 1: Find the absolute maximum**

$$\max(\vert v \vert) = 100$$

**Step 2: Determine $j$**

We need the smallest integer $j$ such that:

$$\frac{100}{10^{j}} < 1 \implies j = 3$$

**Step 3: Apply the formula $v' = v / 10^{j}$**

$$\begin{aligned}
-12 \to -12/10^{3} = -0.012 \\
-8 \to -0.008 \\
4 \to 0.004 \\
15 \to 0.015 \\
89 \to 0.089 \\
100 \to 0.100
\end{aligned}$$

**Result Table:**

| Original $v$ | -12 | -8 | 4 | 15 | 89 | 100 |
|---------------|-------|-----|-----|-----|-----|-------|
| Decimal Scaled $v'$ | -0.012 | -0.008 | 0.004 | 0.015 | 0.089 | 0.100 |

---

### 3.4 Worked Example: Binning (Smoothing by Bin Means)

**Problem Statement:**
Sort the data $\{4, 8, 9, 15, 21, 21, 24, 25, 26, 28, 29, 34\}$ into 3 equal-frequency bins and smooth each bin using the **bin mean**.

**Step 1: Equal-Frequency Binning (4 values per bin)**

| Bin 1 | Bin 2 | Bin 3 |
|-------|-------|-------|
| 4, 8, 9, 15 | 21, 21, 24, 25 | 26, 28, 29, 34 |

**Step 2: Compute Bin Means**

$$\text{Mean}_1 = \frac{4 + 8 + 9 + 15}{4} = \frac{36}{4} = 9$$

$$\text{Mean}_2 = \frac{21 + 21 + 24 + 25}{4} = \frac{91}{4} = 22.75$$

$$\text{Mean}_3 = \frac{26 + 28 + 29 + 34}{4} = \frac{117}{4} = 29.25$$

**Step 3: Replace each value with its bin mean (Smoothing)**

| Bin 1 (Smoothed) | Bin 2 (Smoothed) | Bin 3 (Smoothed) |
|------------------|------------------|------------------|
| 9, 9, 9, 9 | 22.75, 22.75, 22.75, 22.75 | 29.25, 29.25, 29.25, 29.25 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative Smoothing Methods:** (i) Replace by **bin median**, (ii) Replace by **bin boundaries** (e.g., the min and max of the bin). Binning acts as a simple noise filter and is widely used in KTU-level preprocessing problems.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Normalization Techniques

| Method | Range Produced | Sensitive to Outliers? | Preserves Distribution Shape? | Computational Cost | Best Used When |
|--------|----------------|------------------------|-------------------------------|-------------------|----------------|
| **Min-Max** | Fixed, bounded (e.g., $[0,1]$) | **Yes** (very high) | Yes | Low (single pass) | Min/max known, no outliers |
| **Z-Score** | Unbounded (typically $[-3, 3]$) | **Low** (relatively robust) | Yes | Low | Outliers present, need mean=0 |
| **Decimal Scaling** | $[-1, 1]$ | **Yes** (moderate) | Yes | Very Low | Quick scaling, simple cases |
| **Log Transformation** | Compressed, unbounded | No (very robust) | No (changes shape) | Low | Highly skewed distributions |
| **Binning + Aggregation** | Discrete / categorical | Medium | No (data is replaced) | Medium | Noise removal |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Data Integration & Transformation Pipeline (Block Architecture)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the end-to-end flow of data through the integration and transformation stages, as typically taught in the KTU Data Mining module.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    src1[Source 1: Relational DB]
    src2[Source 2: Flat Files CSV]
    src3[Source 3: Web API JSON]
    src4[Source 4: Legacy XML]

    extr[Extract Layer: Pull Raw Data]
    schm[Schema Mapping and Global Schema Construction]
    dup[Duplicate and Redundancy Detection]
    conf[Conflict Resolution Unit and Unit Conversion]
    integ[Integrated Unified Data Store]

    smooth[Smoothing: Binning, Regression, Clustering]
    aggr[Aggregation: Daily to Monthly Roll-up]
    norm[Normalization: Min-Max, Z-Score, Decimal Scaling]
    attr[Attribute Construction: Feature Engineering]
    disc[Discretization: Continuous to Categorical]

    clean[Cleaned Transformed Dataset]
    mine[Mining Algorithms: Classification, Clustering, Association]
    know[Knowledge: Patterns, Models, Insights]

    src1 --> extr
    src2 --> extr
    src3 --> extr
    src4 --> extr

    extr --> schm
    schm --> dup
    dup --> conf
    conf --> integ

    integ --> smooth
    smooth --> aggr
    aggr --> norm
    norm --> attr
    attr --> disc

    disc --> clean
    clean --> mine
    mine --> know

    classDef srcStyle fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000000
    classDef procStyle fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000000
    classDef transStyle fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000000
    classDef outStyle fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000000

    class src1,src2,src3,src4 srcStyle
    class extr,schm,dup,conf,integ procStyle
    class smooth,aggr,norm,attr,disc transStyle
    class clean,mine,know outStyle
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** Blue nodes are heterogeneous data sources. Green nodes are the integration pipeline. Yellow nodes are the transformation operations applied sequentially. Red nodes represent the final output that feeds downstream mining tasks.

### 4.2 Mermaid State Diagram — Normalization Decision Flow

This diagram assists the student in **selecting the right normalization method** for a given scenario, a common viva and exam question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is the attribute range known?}
    Q2{Are outliers present?}
    Q3{Need mean zero and unit variance?}
    A1[Use Min-Max Normalization]
    A2[Use Z-Score Normalization]
    A3[Use Decimal Scaling]
    A4[Use Robust Scaler or Log Transform]

    Q1 -- Yes, no outliers --> A1
    Q1 -- No, or outliers exist --> Q2
    Q2 -- No outliers --> A3
    Q2 -- Yes, outliers --> Q3
    Q3 -- Yes, need mean 0 --> A2
    Q3 -- No, need compression --> A4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Many students lose marks by blindly applying min-max to data with extreme outliers. The decision diagram above is an *examinable* KTU-level skill — examiners often test whether the student can **justify** the choice of normalization method, not merely apply a formula.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define data integration. List any four major issues in data integration.**

**Model Answer:**

Data integration is the process of combining data from multiple heterogeneous sources into a single, unified, and consistent data store so that downstream mining and analysis can be performed on a coherent view of the data. It is a critical preprocessing step in the KDD pipeline.

**Four major issues in data integration:**

1. **Schema Integration** — Different sources may use different attribute names or structures for the same real-world concept (e.g., `roll_no` vs. `student_id`).
2. **Redundancy** — An attribute may duplicate information already captured by another attribute (e.g., `annual_income` is derivable from `monthly_income`).
3. **Data Value Conflicts** — The same attribute may have values in different units, scales, or representations across sources (e.g., weight in kg vs. lbs).
4. **Duplicate Detection** — Identifying the same real-world entity represented under different keys or formats across sources.

*(4 marks = 2 for definition + 2 for the list with one-line explanation each.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] Differentiate between min-max normalization and z-score normalization. State one situation where each is preferred.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Min-Max Normalization | Z-Score Normalization |
|--------|----------------------|------------------------|
| **Formula** | $v' = (v - \min_A)/(\max_A - \min_A)$ | $v' = (v - \mu_A)/\sigma_A$ |
| **Output Range** | Bounded, e.g., $[0,1]$ | Unbounded, typically $[-3,3]$ |
| **Requires** | Min and Max | Mean and Standard Deviation |
| **Outlier Sensitivity** | High | Low |
| **Preferred When** | Known min/max, no outliers, bounded range needed (e.g., neural network inputs) | Outliers present, distribution approximately Gaussian, mean-centering required |

*(3 marks = 1 for distinction + 1 each for the two situations.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the major steps involved in data integration with a neat diagram. Discuss in detail the methods used for redundancy detection.**

**Model Answer:**

**Data Integration Major Steps:**

1. **Source Identification:** Identify all heterogeneous sources (databases, files, APIs).
2. **Schema Mapping:** Map local schemas to a unified global schema.
3. **Data Extraction & Loading:** Pull data from sources into a staging area.
4. **Conflict Resolution:** Reconcile value-level conflicts (units, scales, currencies).
5. **Redundancy Detection & Removal:** Identify and drop redundant attributes.
6. **Duplicate Detection:** Merge records referring to the same entity.
7. **Load into Target Warehouse:** Final integrated data is loaded.

**Redundancy Detection Methods:**

- **For Numeric Attributes — Pearson Correlation Coefficient:**

$$\rho_{A,B} = \frac{\sum (A_i - \bar{A})(B_i - \bar{B})}{n \cdot \sigma_A \cdot \sigma_B}$$

If $\vert \rho_{A,B} \vert > 0$ (especially $\geq 0.5$), strong correlation suggests redundancy.

- **For Categorical Attributes — Chi-Square Test:**

$$\chi^{2} = \sum \frac{(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^{2}}{E_{ij}}$$

A high $\chi^{2}$ value (relative to the critical value at the chosen degrees of freedom and significance level) indicates a significant dependency, signaling redundancy.

- **Covariance:**

$$\text{cov}(A,B) = \frac{1}{n-1} \sum (A_i - \bar{A})(B_i - \bar{B})$$

Positive covariance implies $A$ and $B$ increase together; negative implies inverse relationship.

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source DBs] --> B[Schema Mapping]
    B --> C[Conflict Resolution]
    C --> D[Redundancy Detection]
    D --> E[Integrated Data]
    D -.-> F[Chi-Square and Correlation Tests]
    F -.-> D
```

*(7 marks = 3 for steps + 3 for methods + 1 for diagram.)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks] For the dataset of attribute A = {200, 300, 400, 600, 800, 1000, 1200, 1500}:**
**(i) Perform min-max normalization to the range [0, 1].**
**(ii) Perform z-score normalization. Show all steps clearly.**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $A = \{200, 300, 400, 600, 800, 1000, 1200, 1500\}$, $n = 8$.

**Step 1: Compute Min and Max**

$$\min_A = 200, \quad \max_A = 1500$$

**Step 2: Apply Min-Max Normalization** $v' = (v - 200) / (1500 - 200) = (v - 200) / 1300$

| $v$ | Calculation | $v'$ |
|-----|-------------|------|
| 200 | $(200-200)/1300$ | 0.000 |
| 300 | $100/1300$ | 0.077 |
| 400 | $200/1300$ | 0.154 |
| 600 | $400/1300$ | 0.308 |
| 800 | $600/1300$ | 0.462 |
| 1000 | $800/1300$ | 0.615 |
| 1200 | $1000/1300$ | 0.769 |
| 1500 | $1300/1300$ | 1.000 |

**Step 3: Compute Mean for Z-Score**

$$\mu_A = \frac{200+300+400+600+800+1000+1200+1500}{8} = \frac{6000}{8} = 750$$

**Step 4: Compute Standard Deviation**

$(A_i - 750)^2$ values: $302500, 202500, 122500, 22500, 2500, 62500, 202500, 562500$

$$\sum (A_i - 750)^2 = 1440000$$

$$\sigma_A^2 = 1440000 / 8 = 180000 \implies \sigma_A = \sqrt{180000} \approx 424.26$$

**Step 5: Apply Z-Score $v' = (v - 750) / 424.26$**

| $v$ | $v - 750$ | $v' = (v-750)/424.26$ |
|-----|-----------|----------------------|
| 200 | -550 | -1.296 |
| 300 | -450 | -1.061 |
| 400 | -350 | -0.825 |
| 600 | -150 | -0.354 |
| 800 | 50 | 0.118 |
| 1000 | 250 | 0.589 |
| 1200 | 450 | 1.061 |
| 1500 | 750 | 1.768 |

*(7 marks = 3 for min-max + 4 for z-score with all intermediate steps.)*

---

> **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2023] — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks] Discuss in detail various data transformation strategies with suitable examples. Explain why normalization is essential before applying distance-based mining algorithms.**

**Model Answer:**

**Data Transformation Strategies:**

1. **Smoothing:** Removes noise. Example: binning daily temperature readings $\{20, 22, 21, 35, 24\}$ — the value 35 is noise; binning replaces it with the bin mean 22.
2. **Aggregation:** Combines attributes. Example: daily sales aggregated to monthly or quarterly totals.
3. **Normalization:** Scales values. Example: scaling income (range 10,000 to 1,000,000) to $[0,1]$ so that it does not dominate age in k-NN distance calculations.
4. **Attribute Construction:** New attributes from existing ones. Example: deriving `BMI = weight / height^2` from `weight` and `height`.
5. **Discretization:** Continuous to categorical. Example: age $\{5, 17, 25, 60\}$ mapped to {Child, Adult, Senior}.

**Why Normalization is Essential for Distance-Based Algorithms:**

- Distance-based algorithms (k-NN, k-means, SVM with RBF kernel) compute Euclidean distance:

$$d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{m} (x_j - y_j)^{2}}$$

- If feature $X_1$ ranges from 0 to 1,000,000 and feature $X_2$ ranges from 0 to 1, the squared term for $X_1$ will completely dominate the sum, making $X_2$ effectively invisible to the algorithm.
- Normalization equalizes the influence of all features on the distance metric, leading to unbiased and meaningful clusters or classifications.

*(7 marks = 5 for strategies + 2 for justification.)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks] The following data is sorted: {4, 8, 9, 15, 21, 21, 24, 25, 26, 28, 29, 34}. Perform:**
**(i) Equal-frequency binning into 3 bins and smooth using bin means.**
**(ii) Equal-width binning into 3 bins and smooth using bin boundaries.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Equal-Frequency Binning (4 values per bin):**

| Bin | Values | Bin Mean | Smoothed |
|-----|--------|----------|----------|
| 1 | 4, 8, 9, 15 | 9 | 9, 9, 9, 9 |
| 2 | 21, 21, 24, 25 | 22.75 | 22.75 × 4 |
| 3 | 26, 28, 29, 34 | 29.25 | 29.25 × 4 |

**(ii) Equal-Width Binning:**
- Range = 34 - 4 = 30, Bin width = 30/3 = 10
- Bin 1: [4, 14), Bin 2: [14, 24), Bin 3: [24, 34]

Applying bin boundaries (min and max of each bin):

| Bin | Values | Boundaries | Smoothed |
|-----|--------|-----------|----------|
| 1 | 4, 8, 9 | 4, 9 | 4, 9, 9, 9 |
| 2 | 15, 21, 21 | 15, 21 | 15, 21, 21, 21 |
| 3 | 24, 25, 26, 28, 29, 34 | 24, 34 | 24, 34, 34, 34, 34, 34 |

*(7 marks = 3 for equal-frequency + 4 for equal-width with full working.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Data Integration & Transformation Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the denominator in min-max normalization.** Many students write $(v - \min_A)/(\max_A - \min_A)$ correctly but then forget to multiply by $(new\_\max - new\_\min)$ when the new range is not $[0, 1]$. Lose **1 mark**.
> 2. **Confusing population and sample standard deviation.** For z-score, KTU typically expects the population form $\sigma = \sqrt{\sum(x_i - \mu)^2 / n}$. Using $n-1$ (sample form) is acceptable but must be stated. Mismatch without explanation = **0.5–1 mark** deduction.
> 3. **Not specifying which smoothing method is used.** For binning questions, students often write "smoothed by mean" but the KTU key requires the *bin mean* explicitly. Lose up to **1 mark**.
> 4. **Schema integration vs. data integration.** Students often confuse these. Schema integration is a *sub-step* of data integration. Misnaming loses conceptual marks.
> 5. **Skipping unit conversion examples in conflict resolution.** When asked about "data value conflicts," examiners expect at least one concrete example (USD vs. INR, kg vs. lbs). Vague answers lose **1–2 marks**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Data Integration and Transformation**

- **Data Integration** = combining heterogeneous sources into a unified view. **Key issues:** schema integration, redundancy, value conflicts, duplicates.
- **Data Transformation** = converting integrated data into a mining-friendly form. **Methods:** Smoothing, Aggregation, Normalization, Attribute Construction, Discretization.
- **Min-Max Normalization** formula: $v' = (v - \min)/(\max - \min) \cdot (\text{new range})$. Output bounded, sensitive to outliers.
- **Z-Score Normalization** formula: $v' = (v - \mu)/\sigma$. Output unbounded, robust to outliers, mean becomes 0.
- **Decimal Scaling** formula: $v' = v / 10^j$ where $j$ chosen so $\max(\vert v' \vert) < 1$.
- **Redundancy Detection:** Use **Pearson correlation** (numeric) and **Chi-square test** (categorical).
- **Binning methods:** Equal-frequency (quantiles) vs. equal-width (fixed interval). Smoothing by **mean**, **median**, or **boundaries**.
- **Normalization is MANDATORY** before distance-based algorithms (k-NN, k-means, SVM) and gradient descent-based models.
- **Always show all steps** in normalization problems — partial marking requires intermediate values.
- **Common KTU exam traps:** (i) Forgetting to scale to the specified range, (ii) using $n-1$ vs. $n$ in std dev, (iii) confusing schema vs. data integration.
- **Real-world use cases:** Banking (multi-branch transaction merging), Healthcare (multi-hospital patient records), E-commerce (multi-seller product catalogs), IoT (multi-sensor fusion).
- **Exam scoring tip:** When asked to "compare," use a **table format** — KTU examiners reward structured, tabular answers with **1–2 extra marks** over plain text.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Reduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT524 - DATA MINING AND WAREHOUSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Data Reduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Data Reduction** is the process of obtaining a reduced representation of a dataset that is significantly smaller in volume (number of records, attributes, or both) yet produces the same (or nearly the same) analytical results. It is a critical **preprocessing step** in the Knowledge Discovery in Databases (KDD) pipeline, designed to overcome the curse of high dimensionality, reduce storage I/O, accelerate mining algorithms, and improve model generalization.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**  
> *Data Reduction refers to strategies for dimensionality reduction (e.g., PCA, wavelet transforms), numerosity reduction (e.g., regression, histograms, clustering, sampling), and data compression to produce a compressed representation of the original data that preserves the original integrity for analysis.*

The three orthogonal pillars of data reduction accepted by the KTU syllabus are:

1. **Dimensionality Reduction** – Reducing the *number of attributes* (features/columns) in the dataset.
2. **Numerosity Reduction** – Reducing the *number of records* (tuples/rows) by parametric or non-parametric models.
3. **Data Compression** – Applying *encoding/transformation* (lossy or lossless) to compact the raw bit-stream.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy 🎯

Imagine you are a photographer given a 500-page encyclopedia to summarize into a 10-page pocket guide before an exam.

- **Dimensionality Reduction** = Throwing out *unrelated chapters* (e.g., astronomy chapters for a biology exam). You keep only the "principal" topics.
- **Numerosity Reduction** = Summarizing *repetitive paragraphs* into a single rule (e.g., "All mammals breathe oxygen" replaces 5,000 species descriptions). This is like a **histogram** or **regression model**.
- **Data Compression** = Converting the English text into Morse code or shorthand. The information is identical, but the *physical storage* is smaller.

The result? A "pocket guide" that lets you answer **90–95% of the questions** with **only 2% of the original bulk**.

## 1.3 Why Data Reduction is Mandatory in Modern Data Mining

| Challenge | Consequence Without Reduction | Standard Metric |
|---|---|---|
| High Dimensionality | "Curse of Dimensionality" — distance metrics lose meaning | Time Complexity: $\mathcal{O}(N \cdot d)$ |
| Massive Volume | Mining algorithms become infeasible on RAM | Storage: $N \times d \times 8$ bytes |
| Noise Redundancy | Models overfit to irrelevant features | Accuracy degradation |
| I/O Bottleneck | Repeated disk reads slow ETL pipelines | Disk I/O throughput (MB/s) |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Standard Reduction Threshold:** In KTU/KDD literature, a reduction is considered **acceptable** if the data retains at least **~95% variance/information** while shrinking the volume by an order of magnitude (10× or more).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Data Reduction Strategies

The KTU 2024 syllabus mandates mastery of the following six primary techniques. We classify them into the three pillars:

### 🧱 Pillar I — Dimensionality Reduction
1. **Wavelet Transforms**
2. **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)**
3. **Attribute Subset Selection (Feature Selection)**

### 🧱 Pillar II — Numerosity Reduction
1. **Regression (Linear & Log-Linear)**
2. **Histograms**
3. **Clustering**
4. **Sampling**

### 🧱 Pillar III — Data Compression
1. **Lossless Compression** (e.g., Huffman, Run-Length Encoding)
2. **Lossy Compression** (e.g., JPEG, Vector Quantization)

---

## 2.2 Pillar I: Dimensionality Reduction — Deep Theory

### 🔹 2.2.1 Principal Component Analysis (PCA)

PCA is a **linear orthogonal transformation** that maps data into a new coordinate system such that the greatest variance lies along the first axis (the **first principal component**), the second greatest variance along the second axis, and so on.

**Operational Logic — Stepwise:**

1. **Standardize the d-dimensional data matrix** $X \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times d}$ so that each feature has zero mean and unit variance. This prevents features with large scales from dominating the analysis.
2. **Compute the Covariance Matrix** $\Sigma$ to capture how each pair of features co-varies.
3. **Solve the Eigenvalue Problem** $\Sigma v_i = \lambda_i v_i$ to find the *principal axes*.
4. **Sort Eigenvalues** in descending order: $\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2 \geq \dots \geq \lambda_d$.
5. **Select the top-k eigenvectors** (where $k \ll d$) corresponding to the largest eigenvalues.
6. **Project the data** onto this k-dimensional subspace to get $Y = X W_k$, where $W_k = [v_1, v_2, \dots, v_k]$.

**Why does it work?** The eigenvectors of the covariance matrix are the directions of maximum variance. By keeping only the top-k, we discard directions that carry little information (low variance = mostly noise).

### 🔹 2.2.2 Wavelet Transforms

A wavelet transform decomposes a signal $f(t)$ into a set of basis functions called **wavelets** $\psi_{a,b}(t)$, which are scaled and translated copies of a "mother wavelet."

$$C(\text{scale}, \text{position}) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(t) \, \psi_{a,b}(t) \, dt$$

**Discretized form** (Dyadic grid, $a = 2^{-j}, b = k \cdot 2^{-j}$):

$$W_\psi (j, k) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2^j}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(t) \, \psi\left(\frac{t - k 2^j}{2^j}\right) dt$$

For data reduction, we keep only the **largest k wavelet coefficients** (typically $k \ll N$) and set the rest to zero. This is **lossy compression** but well-suited for sparse, multi-resolution signals.

### 🔹 2.2.3 Attribute Subset Selection

- **Branch-and-Bound Search:** Exhaustive but optimal.
- **Forward Selection:** Greedy — start with empty set, add best feature.
- **Backward Elimination:** Greedy — start with full set, remove worst feature.
- **Decision Tree Induction:** Features appearing in the tree are retained.

---

## 2.3 Pillar II: Numerosity Reduction — Deep Theory

### 🔹 2.3.1 Linear Regression

Models data as a straight line: $Y = \alpha + \beta X + \epsilon$.

**Least-Squares Estimate:**

$$\hat{\beta} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}$$

$$\hat{\alpha} = \bar{y} - \hat{\beta}\,\bar{x}$$

> **Engineering Utility:** In manufacturing, regression models are used to predict *machine wear* ($Y$) from *operating temperature* ($X$) using only 2 stored numbers ($\alpha, \beta$) instead of 1 million sensor logs.

### 🔹 2.3.2 Histograms

A histogram partitions attribute $A$ into $k$ non-overlapping buckets and stores the **mean (or mode)** of each bucket instead of raw data.

**Optimal bucket width (Sturges' Rule):**

$$k = \lceil \log_2(N) + 1 \rceil$$

where $N$ is the number of records.

### 🔹 2.3.3 Clustering

Group $N$ records into $k$ clusters; store only the $k$ cluster centroids. Common algorithms: **k-Means**, **BIRCH**, **CLARANS**.

### 🔹 2.3.4 Sampling

| Sampling Type | Formula | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| Simple Random (without replacement) | $P(\text{record selected}) = \frac{s}{N}$ | Baseline |
| Simple Random (with replacement) | $P = \frac{s}{N}$ per trial | Bootstrapping |
| Stratified | Preserves class distribution | Imbalanced data |
| Cluster | Pick entire clusters | Geographically distributed data |

---

## 2.4 Pillar III: Data Compression

- **Lossless:** Original data is reconstructed **exactly** (Huffman, LZW, RLE).
- **Lossy:** Approximate reconstruction (JPEG for images, Vector Quantization).

---

## 2.5 📊 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation / Rule | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | PCA Covariance | $\Sigma = \frac{1}{N-1} X^{T} X$ | Covariance matrix of centered data |
| 2 | Eigen Decomposition | $\Sigma v_i = \lambda_i v_i$ | Find principal directions |
| 3 | Variance Retained | $\text{Cumulative Ratio} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{d} \lambda_i} \geq 0.95$ | Stopping criterion for k |
| 4 | Linear Regression Slope | $\hat{\beta} = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}$ | Two-point data fitting |
| 5 | Regression Intercept | $\hat{\alpha} = \bar{y} - \hat{\beta} \bar{x}$ | Line position offset |
| 6 | Sturges' Histogram Bins | $k = \lceil \log_2 N + 1 \rceil$ | Optimal bucket count |
| 7 | Sampling Fraction | $f = \frac{s}{N}$ | Sample size selection |
| 8 | Wavelet Coefficients | $W_\psi(j,k) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2^j}} \int f(t) \psi\!\left(\frac{t - k2^j}{2^j}\right) dt$ | Multi-resolution transform |
| 9 | Compression Ratio | $CR = \frac{\text{Original Size}}{\text{Compressed Size}}$ | Efficiency measure |
| 10 | Mean Squared Error (Lossy) | $MSE = \frac{1}{N}\sum (x_i - \hat{x}_i)^2$ | Compression quality |

> [!NOTE]  
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always quote the **variance-retention threshold of 0.95** when answering PCA questions — it is the industry-standard cutoff for component selection.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: PCA on a 3D → 2D Reduction (14-Mark Classic)

**Problem (KTU-Style):**  
Given the data matrix $X$ (centered, 5 records × 3 features):

$$
X = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 2 & 1 \\ -2 & -1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Reduce the dataset to **2 principal components** using PCA.

---

### **Step 1: Verify the data is mean-centered**

For each column, compute the mean:
- $\bar{x}_1 = \frac{2+0-1+1-2}{5} = 0$ ✓
- $\bar{x}_2 = \frac{0+1+0+2-1}{5} = 0.4$  → Subtract 0.4 to center.
- $\bar{x}_3 = \frac{1-1+0+1-1}{5} = 0$ ✓

After centering column 2 (let new $X$ be the centered data):

$$
X_{\text{centered}} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -0.4 & 1 \\ 0 & 0.6 & -1 \\ -1 & -0.4 & 0 \\ 1 & 1.6 & 1 \\ -2 & -1.4 & -1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

---

### **Step 2: Compute the Covariance Matrix**

Using $\Sigma = \frac{1}{N-1} X^{T} X$, where $N = 5$.

First compute $X^{T} X$:

$$
X^{T} X = \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 4.4 & 5 \\ 4.4 & 5.2 & -3.2 \\ 5 & -3.2 & 4 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Now divide by $N - 1 = 4$:

$$
\Sigma = \frac{1}{4} \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 4.4 & 5 \\ 4.4 & 5.2 & -3.2 \\ 5 & -3.2 & 4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.50 & 1.10 & 1.25 \\ 1.10 & 1.30 & -0.80 \\ 1.25 & -0.80 & 1.00 \end{bmatrix}
$$

---

### **Step 3: Solve the Eigenvalue Equation**

We solve $\det(\Sigma - \lambda I) = 0$.

$$
\det \begin{bmatrix} 2.50-\lambda & 1.10 & 1.25 \\ 1.10 & 1.30-\lambda & -0.80 \\ 1.25 & -0.80 & 1.00-\lambda \end{bmatrix} = 0
$$

Expanding (using the characteristic polynomial):

$$
\lambda^{3} - 4.80\,\lambda^{2} + 5.95\,\lambda - 1.10 = 0
$$

Solving numerically (Newton's method or numerical solver):

$$
\lambda_1 \approx 3.42, \quad \lambda_2 \approx 1.21, \quad \lambda_3 \approx 0.17
$$

**Check:** Sum of eigenvalues $= 3.42 + 1.21 + 0.17 = 4.80 = \text{trace}(\Sigma)$ ✓

---

### **Step 4: Variance Retained Test**

$$
\text{Total Variance} = 3.42 + 1.21 + 0.17 = 4.80
$$

$$
\text{Variance Retained (k=2)} = \frac{3.42 + 1.21}{4.80} = \frac{4.63}{4.80} \approx 0.9646 \; (96.46\%)
$$

Since $0.9646 \geq 0.95$, **k = 2 is acceptable**. ✓

---

### **Step 5: Find the Top-2 Eigenvectors**

For $\lambda_1 = 3.42$, solve $(\Sigma - 3.42 I) v_1 = 0$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} -0.92 & 1.10 & 1.25 \\ 1.10 & -2.12 & -0.80 \\ 1.25 & -0.80 & -2.42 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} a \\ b \\ c \end{bmatrix} = 0
$$

Solving: $v_1 \approx (0.74, \; 0.13, \; 0.65)^{T}$. Normalize: $\|v_1\| = 1$ ✓.

For $\lambda_2 = 1.21$, similarly: $v_2 \approx (0.16, \; -0.91, \; 0.38)^{T}$.

---

### **Step 6: Construct Projection Matrix $W_k$**

$$
W_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.74 & 0.16 \\ 0.13 & -0.91 \\ 0.65 & 0.38 \end{bmatrix}
$$

---

### **Step 7: Project the Data**

$$
Y = X_{\text{centered}} \cdot W_2
$$

For the first record $(2, -0.4, 1)$:

$$
Y_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ -0.4 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}^{T} \begin{bmatrix} 0.74 & 0.16 \\ 0.13 & -0.91 \\ 0.65 & 0.38 \end{bmatrix} = (1.62, \; 0.84)
$$

The 3D point $(2, -0.4, 1)$ is now reduced to the 2D point $(1.62, 0.84)$ — a **33% dimension reduction** with **96.46% information preserved**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Linear Regression for Numerosity Reduction

**Problem:** Fit $Y = \alpha + \beta X$ to the data: $(1, 3), (2, 5), (3, 7), (4, 9), (5, 11)$.

### **Step 1: Compute Means**

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{1+2+3+4+5}{5} = 3, \quad \bar{y} = \frac{3+5+7+9+11}{5} = 7
$$

### **Step 2: Compute the Slope $\hat{\beta}$**

$$
\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y}) = (-2)(-4) + (-1)(-2) + (0)(0) + (1)(2) + (2)(4) = 8+2+0+2+8 = 20
$$

$$
\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 = 4 + 1 + 0 + 1 + 4 = 10
$$

$$
\hat{\beta} = \frac{20}{10} = 2
$$

### **Step 3: Compute the Intercept $\hat{\alpha}$**

$$
\hat{\alpha} = \bar{y} - \hat{\beta}\,\bar{x} = 7 - 2(3) = 1
$$

### **Step 4: Final Regression Equation**

$$
\boxed{Y = 1 + 2X}
$$

**Compression achieved:** 5 records → 2 parameters ($\alpha, \beta$). A **60% reduction** in storage.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Interpretation:** The regression model $Y = 1 + 2X$ is the "compressed representation" of the original data. To reconstruct, plug in any $X$ to get an estimated $Y$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 📙 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Taxonomy of Data Reduction (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DATA REDUCTION] --> B[Dimensionality Reduction]
    A --> C[Numerosity Reduction]
    A --> D[Data Compression]

    B --> B1[Wavelet Transforms]
    B --> B2[PCA]
    B --> B3[Attribute Subset Selection]

    B1 --> B1a[Discrete Wavelet Transform]
    B1 --> B1b[Haar Wavelet]

    B2 --> B2a[Compute Covariance Matrix]
    B2 --> B2b[Eigen Decomposition]
    B2 --> B2c[Sort Eigenvalues]
    B2 --> B2d[Project to k Dimensions]

    B3 --> B3a[Forward Selection]
    B3 --> B3b[Backward Elimination]
    B3 --> B3c[Decision Tree Induction]

    C --> C1[Parametric Regression]
    C --> C2[Non-Parametric]
    C --> C3[Sampling]

    C1 --> C1a[Linear Regression]
    C1 --> C1b[Log-Linear Regression]

    C2 --> C2a[Histograms]
    C2 --> C2b[Clustering]

    C3 --> C3a[Simple Random]
    C3 --> C3b[Stratified]
    C3 --> C3c[Cluster Sampling]

    D --> D1[Lossless Compression]
    D --> D2[Lossy Compression]

    D1 --> D1a[Huffman Coding]
    D1 --> D1b[Run Length Encoding]

    D2 --> D2a[JPEG Image Coding]
    D2 --> D2b[Vector Quantization]
```

## 4.2 PCA Operational Pipeline (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Raw[Raw Data Matrix X] --> Std[Step 1: Standardize / Mean Center]
    Std --> Cov[Step 2: Compute Covariance Matrix]
    Cov --> Eig[Step 3: Solve Eigenvalue Problem]
    Eig --> Sort[Step 4: Sort Eigenvalues Desc]
    Sort --> Sel{Step 5: k satisfies 95 percent variance}
    Sel -- Yes --> Proj[Step 6: Project to k Dimensions]
    Sel -- No --> Inc[Increase k]
    Inc --> Sel
    Proj --> Out[Reduced Dataset Y of size N x k]
```

## 4.3 Sampling Strategy Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is class balance critical?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Strat[Stratified Sampling]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Are records geographically grouped?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Clu[Cluster Sampling]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is bootstrap variance estimation needed?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> SRWr[Simple Random With Replacement]
    Q3 -- No --> SRWor[Simple Random Without Replacement]
```
