2024"] B["Product Axis
Phones"] C["Location Axis
Kerala"] D["Measure Cell
Revenue = 50L"] A --- D B --- D C --- D end subgraph Rollup["ROLLUP OPERATION"] A1["Aggregate to Year 2024"] D1["Total Annual Revenue"] A1 --- D1 end subgraph Drilldown["DRILLDOWN OPERATION"] A2["Split Year into Quarters"] D2["Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4"] A2 --- D2 end subgraph Slice["SLICE OPERATION"] A3["Fix Time = Q3 2024"] D3["2D Plane"] A3 --- D3 end subgraph Dice["DICE OPERATION"] A4["Fix Product=Phones, Location=Kerala"] D4["Sub-cube"] A4 --- D4 end subgraph Pivot["PIVOT OPERATION"] A5["Reorient axes"] D5["Same data, new view"] A5 --- D5 end ``` > [!TIP] > **Mermaid Compilation Safeguard Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (`factSales`, `dimProduct`, `dimTime`, etc.) with no reserved keywords. All labels use raw uppercase alphanumeric text only. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define the Multidimensional Data Model. List any four OLAP operations.** **Model Answer:** The **Multidimensional Data Model** is a logical data structure used in data warehousing that organizes data into **facts** (numeric measures) and **dimensions** (descriptive perspectives), enabling OLAP queries on n-dimensional data cubes. The four OLAP operations are: 1. **Roll-up** — Aggregates data by climbing the dimension hierarchy. 2. **Drill-down** — Navigates deeper into the hierarchy for more detail. 3. **Slice** — Selects a single value of one dimension to reduce dimensionality. 4. **Dice** — Selects a sub-cube by specifying ranges on multiple dimensions. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Distinguish between Star Schema and Snowflake Schema (any 3 points).** **Model Answer:** | # | Star Schema | Snowflake Schema | |---|---|---| | 1 | Dimension tables are **denormalized** (flat, single-level). | Dimension tables are **normalized** (multi-level with sub-tables). | | 2 | **Faster query execution** due to fewer JOINs. | **Slower query execution** due to more JOINs. | | 3 | **High data redundancy** in dimension tables. | **Low data redundancy**, no duplication. | | 4 | Easier to design and maintain. | Complex to design due to hierarchical splitting. | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU ESE Rule (2024 Scheme):** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and **must contain an internal choice**. We provide two full alternatives below. --- ### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — **(14 Marks)** **(a)** Explain the architecture of a Data Warehouse with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of the Multidimensional Data Model in this architecture. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** With a suitable example, describe the **Star Schema** and **Snowflake Schema**. Compare them on at least 5 parameters. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Q.A (a): A Data Warehouse is a **subject-oriented, integrated, time-variant, and non-volatile** collection of data used for managerial decision support. Its architecture has four tiers: 1. **Data Source Tier** — Operational systems (OLTP DBs, flat files, web logs). 2. **Data Staging Tier** — ETL processes (Extract, Transform, Load). 3. **Data Storage Tier** — The warehouse repository, organized using a **Multidimensional Data Model**. 4. **Presentation Tier** — OLAP servers and front-end tools (BI dashboards). **Role of Multidimensional Data Model:** - It transforms normalized OLTP data into a **subject-oriented, aggregated, query-optimized** structure. - It enables **drill-down/roll-up** analytical queries that are impossible in OLTP. - It supports the **OLAP engine** by pre-aggregating data into data cubes. #### Model Solution to Q.A (b): **Star Schema Example — Retail Sales:** - **Fact Table:** `SALES(sales_id, product_key, store_key, date_key, units_sold, revenue)` - **Dimensions:** - `PRODUCT(product_key, product_name, category, brand)` - `STORE(store_key, store_name, city, state)` - `TIME(date_key, day, month, quarter, year)` The schema visually resembles a **star**, with the fact table at the center and dimension tables radiating outward like points of a star. **Snowflake Schema Example:** The same data is normalized: - `PRODUCT(product_key, product_name, category_id)` → `CATEGORY(category_id, category_name)` → `DEPARTMENT(department_id, department_name)` - `STORE(store_key, store_name, city_id)` → `CITY(city_id, state_id)` → `STATE(state_id, country_id)` The schema branches out like a **snowflake**, with sub-dimension tables connected to main dimension tables. **Comparison (5 parameters):** | Parameter | Star | Snowflake | |---|---|---| | Normalization | Denormalized | Normalized | | Query Speed | Faster (fewer joins) | Slower (more joins) | | Storage | Higher (redundancy) | Lower | | Maintenance | Easier | Harder | | Complexity | Simple | Complex | --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — **(14 Marks)** **(a)** Explain the concept of an **OLAP cube**. Discuss the following OLAP operations with examples: **Roll-up, Drill-down, Slice, Dice, and Pivot**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** What is a **Fact Constellation Schema**? Explain with a suitable example diagram. Why is it also called a "Galaxy Schema"? **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Q.B (a): An **OLAP Cube** is a multi-dimensional array of data, where each dimension represents a business perspective (e.g., Time, Product, Geography), and each cell holds a numeric measure (e.g., Revenue, Profit). **OLAP Operations Explained:** 1. **Roll-up (Drill-up):** Aggregates data by moving up the hierarchy. *Example:* Summing daily sales into monthly, then quarterly, then yearly totals. 2. **Drill-down:** The reverse of roll-up; navigates down the hierarchy to reveal more detail. *Example:* Breaking yearly sales into quarterly, then monthly, then daily. 3. **Slice:** Reduces a cube by fixing one dimension. *Example:* Selecting only Q3 of 2024 → produces a 2-D plane (Product × Location). 4. **Dice:** Reduces a cube by specifying ranges on two or more dimensions. *Example:* Selecting (Samsung OR Apple) AND (Kerala OR Tamil Nadu) AND (Q3 OR Q4) → a smaller sub-cube. 5. **Pivot (Rotate):** Reorients the cube axes to provide an alternative visual perspective. *Example:* Swapping the row and column axes in a pivot table — no data change. #### Model Solution to Q.B (b): A **Fact Constellation Schema** (also called a **Galaxy Schema**) contains **multiple fact tables** that share one or more common **dimension tables**. It is used to model complex enterprise scenarios where multiple business processes share common dimensions. **Example:** A retail enterprise may have: - **Fact Table 1:** `SALES(product_key, store_key, date_key, units_sold, revenue)` - **Fact Table 2:** `SHIPPING(store_key, date_key, carrier_key, shipping_cost, delivery_days)` - **Fact Table 3:** `INVENTORY(product_key, store_key, date_key, stock_level, reorder_point)` **Shared Dimensions:** `PRODUCT`, `STORE`, `TIME` are shared across all three fact tables. **Why "Galaxy Schema"?** The diagram resembles a **galaxy** in space — multiple "stars" (fact tables) orbiting around shared "planets" (dimension tables). The word *constellation* emphasizes the structured but interconnected nature of these multiple fact tables. --- ## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing Fact and Dimension:** Students often list *product_name* as a measure. Always remember: **measures are numeric and additive**; dimensions are descriptive. > > 2. **Forgetting Schema Diagrams:** A textual description *without a diagram* loses **2–3 marks** in Part B. Always include a labeled Mermaid/ER diagram. > > 3. **OLAP Operation Mix-up:** Slice operates on **one** dimension; Dice operates on **two or more**. Mixing these up is a **frequent mark-loser**. > > 4. **Snowflake vs Star — Missing Comparison Points:** Examiners expect **at least 4–5 comparison points**. Writing only 2 will cap your score at 60% of the section. > > 5. **No Measure Type Classification:** Always classify measures as **additive / semi-additive / non-additive** when describing a fact table — this is a favorite KTU question. > > 6. **Cardinality Mismatch:** In a Star Schema, the relationship is **1 : N** (one dimension row → many fact rows). Reversing this leads to a wrong diagram and mark deduction. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ⚡ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Multidimensional Data Model** - ✅ **MDM** organizes data into **Facts + Dimensions** around numeric **Measures**. - ✅ A **Fact Table** holds FKs to dimensions + numeric measures; **Dimension Tables** hold descriptive attributes with hierarchies. - ✅ **Measure types:** Additive, Semi-additive, Non-additive — always classify them. - ✅ **Three schemas:** Star ⭐ (denormalized), Snowflake ❄ (normalized), Fact Constellation 🌌 (multiple facts, shared dims). - ✅ **Star Schema:** Faster queries, more redundancy, simpler design. - ✅ **Snowflake Schema:** Slower queries, no redundancy, complex normalized hierarchy. - ✅ **Fact Constellation:** Multiple fact tables sharing dimensions; used in enterprise DWs. - ✅ **Five OLAP Operations:** Roll-up, Drill-down, Slice, Dice, Pivot — *memorize examples for each*. - ✅ **Roll-up** decreases granularity (climbs hierarchy); **Drill-down** increases granularity. - ✅ **Slice** = 1 dimension fixed; **Dice** = ≥ 2 dimensions fixed (creates a sub-cube). - ✅ **Pivot** reorients axes — data is unchanged. - ✅ **Total cuboids in n-D cube = $2^{n}$** (proved by binary-choice combinatorial argument). - ✅ **Base cuboid** = atomic detail; **Apex cuboid** = grand total (single cell). - ✅ **Total cells** in n-D cube = $\prod_{i=1}^{n} \vert D_i \vert$. - ✅ **KTU 2024 Scheme Trend:** Expect at least one Part B question comparing schemas and one Part A question defining OLAP operations.

topics.title: Data Warehouse Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT524 - DATA MINING AND WAREHOUSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Data Warehouse Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Data Warehouse Architecture** refers to the **structured blueprint** that defines the complete set of components, layers, databases, processes, and data flows that together constitute a Data Warehouse (DW) system. It specifies **how data is sourced, extracted, transformed, loaded, stored, and accessed** for analytical and decision-support purposes.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEITT524 – Module 2)**, a Data Warehouse Architecture is generally classified into three canonical models based on the number of **tiers** (separation layers) between the user and the central data store:

1. **Single-Tier Architecture** (Conceptual / Theoretical)
2. **Two-Tier Architecture** (Legacy / Direct Client-Server)
3. **Three-Tier Architecture** (Industry Standard / Production)

> [!NOTE]
> **W.H. Inmon's Definition (widely cited in KTU references):**
> *"A Data Warehouse is a subject-oriented, integrated, time-variant, and non-volatile collection of data in support of management's decision-making process."* — Architecture refers to the **structural realization** of this definition.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library Warehouse" Model

Imagine you run a **massive central library** that needs to store millions of books arriving every day from hundreds of small branch libraries across the country.

- **Branch Libraries** = Operational/Source Databases (OLTP systems at banks, supermarkets, ATMs)
- **Central Library Building** = Data Warehouse
- **Loading Dock & Sorting Section** = ETL (Extraction, Transformation, Loading) layer
- **Cataloging Department** = Metadata Repository
- **Reading Halls (Reference, Periodicals, Journals, Archives)** = Data Marts
- **Librarian's Desk** = OLAP Server (answers complex queries)
- **Visitors (Researchers, Analysts)** = End Users / Decision Makers

Just like a library has **separate floors, departments, and standardized systems** to manage this flow efficiently, a Data Warehouse has **distinct architectural layers** to handle ingestion, storage, and retrieval.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** When asked "Define Data Warehouse Architecture," always mention it is a **systematic framework** that supports **OLAP, decision support systems (DSS), and data mining** activities.

---

### 1.3 Core Components Recap (At a Glance)

| **Component** | **Function** |
|---|---|
| **Source Systems** | Operational OLTP databases, flat files, ERP/CRM, web logs |
| **ETL Tools** | Extract → Transform → Load data into warehouse |
| **Staging Area** | Temporary buffer for raw data before transformation |
| **Data Warehouse Database** | Central integrated, historical data store |
| **Data Marts** | Subject-specific subsets (e.g., Sales DM, Finance DM) |
| **OLAP Server** | Multidimensional analysis engine (ROLAP/MOLAP/HOLAP) |
| **Front-End Tools** | Query, reporting, data mining, dashboards |
| **Metadata Repository** | "Data about data" — schema, mappings, lineage |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Canonical Architectures — Deep Dive

#### 🔹 2.1.1 Single-Tier Architecture

- **Concept:** Theoretically, the user interface, OLAP server, and data warehouse database all reside on a **single platform** with no physical separation.
- **Status:** **Not practically used.** It is treated as a *conceptual reference model* in textbooks because storing massive historical data alongside the application logic creates performance bottlenecks.
- **Use Case:** Mentioned in KTU exams mainly for definition/comparison purposes.

#### 🔹 2.1.2 Two-Tier Architecture

The two-tier (client-server) architecture physically separates the system into:

1. **Client Tier** — Contains the **front-end tools** (query, reporting, analysis) used by end-users.
2. **Server Tier** — Contains the **Data Warehouse database** along with the **OLAP server** bundled together.

**Characteristics:**
- Direct communication between client and server (no middleware).
- Suitable for **small-scale departments** with limited concurrent users.
- **Disadvantages:**
  - Scalability issues when users increase.
  - Network congestion due to large query results transmitted to clients.
  - No intermediate processing layer for query optimization.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Two-tier architecture is **rarely used in modern enterprises** but is asked in exams for contrast purposes.

#### 🔹 2.1.3 Three-Tier Architecture (Industry Standard ✅)

This is the **de facto production architecture** and the most exam-relevant model.

**Layer Breakdown:**

**Tier 1 — Bottom Tier (Data Storage Layer)**
- Contains the **Data Warehouse Database Server**.
- Stores **integrated, historical, subject-oriented** data.
- Often implemented using **RDBMS** (Oracle, Teradata, Snowflake) or **columnar storage** (Parquet, Redshift).
- Uses **star schema, snowflake schema, or fact constellation schema**.

**Tier 2 — Middle Tier (OLAP Server Layer)**
- Acts as the **brain** of the architecture.
- Provides **multidimensional view** of data using OLAP cubes.
- Three sub-types:
  - **ROLAP** (Relational OLAP) — Uses relational DBMS with star schema.
  - **MOLAP** (Multidimensional OLAP) — Uses sparse multidimensional arrays.
  - **HOLAP** (Hybrid OLAP) — Combines ROLAP + MOLAP strengths.

**Tier 3 — Top Tier (Client/Presentation Layer)**
- Contains **front-end tools**: query, reporting, analysis, data mining, dashboards.
- Users interact via tools like Tableau, Power BI, SQL clients, or custom BI portals.

**Advantages:**
- ✅ **Scalability** — Each tier can be independently scaled.
- ✅ **Performance** — Middle tier handles query processing, reducing client load.
- ✅ **Flexibility** — Supports multiple front-end tools.
- ✅ **Security** — Layered access control.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Comparison Matrix

> [!TIP]
> **CRITICAL:** The table below is a **high-yield revision sheet** — memorize it for direct 3-mark and 14-mark questions.

| **Parameter** | **Single-Tier** | **Two-Tier** | **Three-Tier** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Number of Layers** | 1 (Conceptual) | 2 (Client + Server) | 3 (Client + OLAP + DB) |
| **Separation of Concerns** | None | Partial | Full |
| **Scalability** | Very Poor | Moderate | Excellent |
| **Performance** | Poor | Moderate | High |
| **Practical Use** | Theoretical Only | Legacy / Small setups | **Enterprise Standard** |
| **OLAP Server Location** | Same as DB | Bundled with server | Independent middle tier |
| **Query Optimization** | None | At server only | Dedicated middle layer |
| **Concurrency Support** | Very Low | Limited | High |
| **Cost** | Low | Moderate | High (but justified) |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | Low (definition) | Medium (comparison) | **Very High (detailed)** |

---

### 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Banking:** Three-tier DW powers fraud detection, credit risk analysis, customer segmentation.
- **Retail:** Inventory forecasting, recommendation engines, demand sensing.
- **Healthcare:** Patient outcome analytics, epidemic tracking, drug efficacy studies.
- **Telecom:** Churn prediction, network optimization, CDR (Call Detail Record) mining.
- **E-Commerce:** Real-time personalization, dynamic pricing, supply chain DW.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modern Variants (Beyond KTU Syllabus but Useful):**
> - **Cloud-Native Architecture** (e.g., Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift Spectrum).
> - **Lambda Architecture** — Batch + Speed layer.
> - **Data Lakehouse** — Combines DW + Data Lake flexibility.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Since "Data Warehouse Architecture" is a **conceptual design topic** (not a numerical physics/math problem), this section presents an **exhaustive logical decomposition** of the architectural flow — analogous to a mathematical derivation of the system's behavior.

### 3.1 Analytical Decomposition of the Three-Tier Data Flow

Let us formally define the data flow as a sequential transformation pipeline. We model the system as:

$$D_{final} = f_{OLAP} \circ f_{ETL} \circ f_{Source}$$

Where:
- $f_{Source}$ = Data acquisition function from heterogeneous OLTP sources.
- $f_{ETL}$ = Extract-Transform-Load function.
- $f_{OLAP}$ = Multidimensional query/aggregation function.
- $\circ$ denotes **functional composition** (data flows left → right).

---

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Architecture Flow Analysis

**Step 1 — Source Identification**
Identify all operational databases, flat files, logs, and external feeds.

$$\{S_1, S_2, S_3, \ldots, S_n\} \in \text{SourceSystems}$$

Example: $S_1$ = ATM Transaction DB, $S_2$ = Internet Banking Logs, $S_3$ = CRM Database.

---

**Step 2 — Extraction (E)**
Pull raw data from sources without modifying semantics.

$$E: S_i \rightarrow R_i \quad \text{(Raw extracted records)}$$

Data is dumped into the **Staging Area** — a temporary buffer.

---

**Step 3 — Transformation (T)**
Apply cleansing, standardization, deduplication, and integration rules:

$$T: R_i \rightarrow C_i \quad \text{(Cleansed/Conformed data)}$$

Operations include:
- Null handling
- Format standardization (e.g., date formats → ISO 8601)
- Unit conversions
- Key integration across sources

---

**Step 4 — Loading (L)**
Insert cleansed data into the central warehouse:

$$L: C_i \rightarrow DW \quad \text{(Data Warehouse)}$$

This step may use **Full Load** or **Incremental (Delta) Load** strategies.

---

**Step 5 — Multidimensional Modeling (OLAP Server)**
The DW data is logically organized into:

$$\text{Cube} = (\text{Dimensions}) \times (\text{Measures})$$

Where dimensions are categorical axes (Time, Product, Region) and measures are numerical facts (Sales, Profit).

---

**Step 6 — Query/Analysis (Client Layer)**
End-user issues an OLAP query:

$$Q: \text{Cube} \rightarrow \text{Aggregated Result}$$

Tools return pivoted, sliced, diced, drilled-down views.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Capacity Model

Let:
- $N$ = number of users
- $Q_s$ = average query size (MB)
- $T_p$ = processing time per query
- $B$ = network bandwidth (Mbps)

Then the **concurrent throughput** $\Theta$ of a three-tier architecture approximates:

$$\Theta = \frac{N \cdot Q_s}{T_p + \frac{Q_s}{B}}$$

**Interpretation:**
- Two-tier systems lack the middle OLAP processing tier, so $T_p$ increases dramatically as $N$ grows.
- Three-tier's middle tier **pre-aggregates** results, effectively reducing $T_p$ by a factor of $\approx 5\text{–}20\times$ in real workloads.
- This justifies why three-tier architecture dominates **enterprise DW deployments**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "Why three-tier is preferred?", write: *"The middle OLAP tier decouples storage from presentation, enabling scalable, optimized, multidimensional query processing."*

---

### 3.4 Architecture Decision Matrix (Quantitative)

| **Criterion** | **Weight** | **Single-Tier Score** | **Two-Tier Score** | **Three-Tier Score** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Scalability | 0.30 | 1 | 5 | 9 |
| Performance | 0.25 | 2 | 6 | 9 |
| Maintainability | 0.20 | 3 | 6 | 9 |
| Cost-Effectiveness | 0.15 | 8 | 6 | 5 |
| Security | 0.10 | 4 | 5 | 9 |
| **Weighted Total** | **1.00** | **2.95** | **5.75** | **8.30** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Three-tier architecture scores **8.30/10**, confirming its superiority for production DW systems.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Three-Tier Data Warehouse Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph src["SOURCE TIER - OPERATIONAL SYSTEMS"]
        s1[OLTP Database 1 - Banking]
        s2[OLTP Database 2 - CRM]

    end

    subgraph mid["MIDDLE TIER - OLAP AND ETL LAYER"]

        stage[Staging Area]

        oltp[OLAP Server - ROLAP MOLAP HOLAP]
    end

    subgraph storage["BOTTOM TIER - DATA STORAGE LAYER"]

    end

    subgraph client["TOP TIER - CLIENT PRESENTATION LAYER"]

        u2[BI Dashboard - Power BI Tableau]

    end

    s1 --> etl
    s2 --> etl
    s3 --> etl

```

---

### 4.2 End-to-End Data Flow Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR

```

---

### 4.3 Architecture Comparison Schematic

```mermaid
    end
```

### 4.4 ETL Pipeline Architecture (Detailed)

```mermaid
    subgraph load["LOADING PHASE"]
        l1[Full Load]
        l2[Incremental Load]
        l3[Refresh - Scheduled]

    e1 --> t1
    e2 --> t2

```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

---

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define Data Warehouse Architecture. List its main components.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
A Data Warehouse Architecture is a structured framework that describes the complete set of components, processes, data stores, and their interconnections used to build, maintain, and use a Data Warehouse for analytical decision-making.

**Main Components (2 Marks):**
1. **Source Systems** — Operational OLTP databases and external data sources.
2. **ETL Layer** — Extraction, Transformation, Loading subsystem.
3. **Data Warehouse Database** — Central integrated, historical data repository.
4. **OLAP Server** — Multidimensional analysis engine (ROLAP/MOLAP/HOLAP).
5. **Data Marts** — Subject-specific departmental subsets.
6. **Metadata Repository** — Stores schema, lineage, and transformation rules.
7. **Front-End Tools** — Query, reporting, dashboards, and mining interfaces.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] Differentiate between Two-Tier and Three-Tier Data Warehouse Architectures.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Feature** | **Two-Tier** | **Three-Tier** |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of Layers** | 2 (Client + Server) | 3 (Client + OLAP + DB) |
| **OLAP Server** | Bundled with DB server | Independent middle tier |
| **Scalability** | Limited | High |
| **Query Processing** | At DB level only | Dedicated OLAP layer |
| **Industry Adoption** | Legacy systems only | Modern enterprise standard |

**Conclusion (1 Mark):** Three-tier is preferred due to better scalability, performance, and separation of concerns.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Explain the Three-Tier Data Warehouse Architecture in detail with a neat diagram. List its advantages over two-tier architecture.**

**OR**

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024] With a neat block diagram, describe the major components of a Data Warehouse Architecture. Explain the role of the ETL process and OLAP server in it.**

---

### 🔹 SOLUTION FOR QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**Part (a) — Explain Three-Tier Architecture (7 Marks)**

The **Three-Tier Data Warehouse Architecture** is the industry-standard model that physically separates the system into three logical layers:

**Tier 1 — Bottom Tier (Data Storage Layer):**
- Houses the **central Data Warehouse database server**.
- Contains **integrated, time-variant, subject-oriented, non-volatile** data.
- Implemented using RDBMS (e.g., Oracle Exadata, Teradata) or columnar databases.
- Data is modeled using **star, snowflake, or fact constellation schemas**.

**Tier 2 — Middle Tier (OLAP Server):**
- Provides a **multidimensional view** of warehouse data.
- Three implementations:
  - **ROLAP** — Relational OLAP using SQL on star schemas.
  - **MOLAP** — Multidimensional OLAP using sparse arrays.
  - **HOLAP** — Hybrid combining both.
- Performs query optimization, aggregation, and cube navigation.

**Tier 3 — Top Tier (Client/Presentation Layer):**
- Contains **front-end tools** for end-user interaction.
- Tools include reporting (Crystal Reports), BI dashboards (Tableau, Power BI), data mining (Weka, RapidMiner), and ad-hoc query engines.

**Diagram (2 Marks):**
*(Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 — Three-Tier Architecture.)*

**Part (b) — Advantages over Two-Tier Architecture (7 Marks)**

1. **Scalability** — Each tier can be scaled independently (e.g., add more OLAP servers without touching storage).
2. **Performance** — The middle OLAP tier handles heavy aggregation, reducing response time by 5–20×.
3. **Reduced Network Load** — Pre-aggregated results transmitted instead of raw fact rows.
4. **Better Concurrency** — Supports hundreds of simultaneous users via the middle tier's caching.
5. **Security** — Layered access controls; clients never directly access the DB.
6. **Flexibility** — Multiple front-end tools can share the same OLAP backend.
7. **Maintainability** — ETL, storage, and presentation can be updated independently.

---

### 🔹 SOLUTION FOR QUESTION B (14 Marks)

**Part (a) — Major Components with Block Diagram (7 Marks)**

A complete Data Warehouse Architecture consists of the following components (see diagram in Section 4.1):

1. **Source Systems** — OLTP databases, logs, external feeds.
2. **Staging Area** — Temporary buffer for raw data.
3. **ETL Engine** — Performs extraction, transformation, and loading.
4. **Data Warehouse Database** — Central integrated repository.
5. **Data Marts** — Departmental subsets (Sales, Finance, HR).
6. **OLAP Server** — Multidimensional analysis layer.
7. **Metadata Repository** — Stores mappings, schemas, lineage.
8. **Front-End Tools** — BI, reporting, mining, dashboards.

**Part (b) — Role of ETL Process and OLAP Server (7 Marks)**

**Role of ETL (3.5 Marks):**
- **Extraction:** Pulls data from heterogeneous sources (relational, flat files, APIs).
- **Transformation:** Cleanses, integrates, deduplicates, and conforms data.
- **Loading:** Inserts data into the warehouse using full or incremental strategies.
- **Importance:** Ensures data quality, consistency, and integration across sources — the foundation of the warehouse's analytical value.

**Role of OLAP Server (3.5 Marks):**
- Provides **multidimensional view** via OLAP cubes.
- Enables operations: **Roll-up, Drill-down, Slice, Dice, Pivot**.
- Supports complex analytical queries efficiently.
- **Three flavors:**
  - **ROLAP** — Scalable, uses relational engine.
  - **MOLAP** — Fast pre-computed cubes.
  - **HOLAP** — Hybrid of both.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. ❌ **Confusing OLAP Server with Data Warehouse itself** — They are *separate* layers in the three-tier model.
> 2. ❌ **Forgetting to mention Metadata Repository** — Examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for this.
> 3. ❌ **Drawing Two-Tier diagram for a Three-Tier question** — Always label all three tiers explicitly.
> 4. ❌ **Writing "Data Warehouse = Database"** — It is a *system*, not just a database; mention ETL, OLAP, metadata, and data marts.
> 5. ❌ **Skipping the "Why three-tier is better" reasoning** — Always justify with scalability and performance arguments.
> 6. ❌ **Mixing up ROLAP/MOLAP/HOLAP definitions** — Memorize: **R**elational, **M**ultidimensional, **H**ybrid.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Save for Last-Minute Review):**

- ✅ **Definition:** Architecture = structured blueprint of DW components and data flows.
- ✅ **Three Architectures:** Single-tier (conceptual) | Two-tier (legacy) | Three-tier (standard).
- ✅ **Three-Tier Layers:**
  - **Bottom** → Data Warehouse DB (storage)
  - **Middle** → OLAP Server (processing)
  - **Top** → Front-end tools (presentation)
- ✅ **OLAP Flavors:** **R**OLAP (Relational), **M**OLAP (Multidimensional), **H**OLAP (Hybrid).
- ✅ **ETL = Extract + Transform + Load** (the data ingestion pipeline).
- ✅ **Data Mart** = Subject-specific subset of DW (Sales, HR, Finance).
- ✅ **Metadata** = "Data about data" — schema, lineage, mappings.
- ✅ **Star Schema** = Fact table + Dimension tables (most common DW schema).
- ✅ **Inmon Definition** — Subject-oriented, Integrated, Time-variant, Non-volatile.
- ✅ **Three-tier is preferred** due to **scalability, performance, and separation of concerns**.
- ✅ **Staging Area** = Temporary buffer between source and DW.
- ✅ **CDC (Change Data Capture)** = Method to extract only changed records (incremental).
- ✅ **Cube Operations:** Roll-up, Drill-down, Slice, Dice, Pivot.
- ✅ **Modern Variants (Awareness Level):** Data Lakehouse, Cloud DW (Snowflake, BigQuery), Lambda Architecture.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Mantra:**
> *"Architecture answers HOW. Components answer WHAT. ETL answers WHEN & WHERE data moves. OLAP answers HOW users analyze it."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Warehouse Implementation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT524 - DATA MINING AND WAREHOUSING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Data Warehouse Implementation ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Data Warehouse Implementation** is the systematic, phased process of translating a logical data warehouse design into a physical, operational system. It encompasses hardware and software provisioning, **schema instantiation (Star, Snowflake, Fact Constellation)**, **ETL pipeline development (Extract, Transform, Load)**, **OLAP server configuration (ROLAP / MOLAP / HOLAP)**, indexing strategy selection, metadata repository deployment, and the staged go-live (roll-out) of data marts aligned with business priorities. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus framing, implementation is the bridge between *Data Warehouse Modelling* (the architectural blueprint) and *Data Mining* (the analytical payload). It is governed by the **CIF (Corporate Information Factory)** and **Dimensional Modelling** paradigms, primarily the methodologies advocated by **Bill Inmon** (top-down) and **Ralph Kimball** (bottom-up). ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Centralised Library" > [!NOTE] > **Think of a Data Warehouse as a CENTRAL LIBRARY of a state.** > > - **Sources** (villages, towns, government offices) keep generating raw records (births, sales, taxes). > - **Staging Area** is the **loading dock** of the library where books are checked, repaired, and sorted. > - **ETL** is the **librarian** who cleans, translates, and catalogues every book before shelving. > - **Data Warehouse Core** is the **main reading hall** — clean, indexed, organized. > - **Data Marts** are **subject-specific reading rooms** (Medical, Legal, History). > - **OLAP** is the **research desk** that allows a reader to "roll up" yearly trends or "drill down" to a specific district. > - **Front-End Tools** are the **public enquiry systems** (kiosks) that citizens use. Without the librarian (ETL) and proper shelving (implementation), the library would just be a dusty warehouse of unsorted papers — which is precisely what the term *Data Warehouse* warns against! ### 1.3 Why Implementation is a Distinct Module > [!TIP] > **Three high-yield distinctions the examiner expects:** > 1. **Design ≠ Implementation**: A perfect logical model can fail due to poor indexing, wrong partitioning keys, or unmonitored ETL jobs. > 2. **Implementation is staged, not monolithic**: It follows the **SDLC** (Systems Development Life Cycle) — Planning → Analysis → Design → Implementation → Testing → Deployment → Maintenance. > 3. **Performance Engineering** (indexing, partitioning, materialized views, denormalization trade-offs) is the heart of implementation. ### 1.4 Core Implementation Components — Quick Glance | Component | Role | KTU Keyword | |---|---|---| | **Source Systems** | Operational DBs, flat files, APIs, logs | OLTP feeds | | **Staging Area** | Landing zone for raw extracts | Temporary, non-queryable | | **ETL Engine** | Cleansing, transformation, loading | Informatica, Talend, SSIS | | **Data Warehouse DB** | Integrated, time-variant, subject-oriented | Star/Snowflake schema | | **Data Marts** | Departmental slices | Sales DM, HR DM | | **OLAP Server** | Multi-dimensional query engine | ROLAP, MOLAP, HOLAP | | **Front-End Tools** | Reporting, dashboards, mining | Tableau, Power BI, SPSS | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Two Principal Implementation Methodologies #### A. Top-Down Approach — Bill Inmon (CIF Methodology) > [!NOTE] > Inmon defines the Data Warehouse as a **centralised, normalised (3NF) enterprise-wide repository**, from which departmental data marts are derived. **Stepwise Logic:** 1. Build the **Enterprise Data Warehouse (EDW)** first in normalised form. 2. Build **Data Marts** (dimensional) on top of the EDW for departments. 3. Use **CIF (Corporate Information Factory)** as the architectural reference. **Pros:** Single source of truth, low redundancy, enterprise integration. **Cons:** High upfront cost, slow ROI, long implementation timeline. #### B. Bottom-Up Approach — Ralph Kimball (Dimensional Lifecycle) > [!NOTE] > Kimball advocates building **dimensional data marts first** and integrating them via a **conformed dimension bus architecture**. **Stepwise Logic:** 1. Identify a business process (e.g., Sales). 2. Build the **dimensional model (Star Schema)**. 3. Load data marts incrementally. 4. Standardise shared dimensions (Customer, Product, Date) across marts. 5. Over time, the union of data marts forms the warehouse. **Pros:** Fast ROI, iterative, business-driven. **Cons:** Risk of inconsistency, integration challenges. ### 2.2 The Phased Implementation Roadmap (KTU Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **The 7-Phase Implementation Model** (Adapted from Kimball's Dimensional Lifecycle): > > **Phase 1 — Project Planning** > Define scope, sponsors, deliverables, risk register. > > **Phase 2 — Business Requirements Definition** > Interviews, KPIs, grain selection. > > **Phase 3 — Dimensional Modelling** > Identify Facts (measures) and Dimensions (contexts). Choose schema. > > **Phase 4 — Physical Design** > Storage sizing, partitioning strategy, index selection, compression. > > **Phase 5 — ETL Design & Development** > Extract → Stage → Transform → Load. Data quality rules. > > **Phase 6 — Deployment & OLAP Configuration** > Build aggregations, materialized views, ROLAP/MOLAP cubes. > > **Phase 7 — Maintenance, Growth & Tuning** > Index tuning, query rewrite, partition archival. ### 2.3 Schema Choices — The Big Three > [!TIP] > **Schema selection is a 14-mark favourite on KTU boards.** | Schema | Structure | Pros | Cons | |---|---|---|---| | **Star** | 1 Fact table + N Denormalised Dimension tables | Fast joins, intuitive | Data redundancy | | **Snowflake** | Normalised dimensions (sub-dimensions) | Storage efficient | More joins, slower | | **Fact Constellation (Galaxy)** | Multiple fact tables sharing dimensions | Models complex business | Complexity in ETL | ### 2.4 ETL — The Backbone of Implementation > [!IMPORTANT] > **ETL is the data movement and quality engine.** Without ETL, the warehouse is just an empty shell. - **Extract** — Pull data from heterogeneous sources (DB2, Oracle, MySQL, XML, JSON, CSV). - **Transform** — Apply business rules: cleansing, deduplication, standardization, surrogate key assignment, slowly changing dimensions (SCD Type 1/2/3). - **Load** — Bulk insert into target warehouse tables using partition exchange, bulk-loader utilities, or change-data-capture (CDC) mechanisms. > [!NOTE] > **SCD (Slowly Changing Dimensions)** handling is a high-yield topic: > - **SCD Type 1**: Overwrite (no history kept). > - **SCD Type 2**: Add new row with effective date range (full history). > - **SCD Type 3**: Add new column for previous value (limited history). ### 2.5 OLAP Server Flavours | Type | Storage | Strength | Weakness | |---|---|---|---| | **ROLAP** (Relational OLAP) | Relational DB | Scalability, flexibility | Slower aggregations | | **MOLAP** (Multidimensional OLAP) | Proprietary cube | Lightning fast | Limited cube size | | **HOLAP** (Hybrid OLAP) | Relational + Cube | Balance | Architectural complexity | ### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Storage & Sizing Estimations for Data Warehouse Implementation** | Symbol | Formula | Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | $T_R$ | $T_R = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (C_i \cdot L_i \cdot P_i)$ | Total rows = sum across tables | rows | | $S_F$ | $S_F = R \cdot \sum_{j=1}^{k} (M_j \cdot B_j)$ | Fact table size | bytes | | $S_D$ | $S_D = \sum_{d=1}^{D} (R_d \cdot C_d \cdot B_d)$ | Dimension table size | bytes | | $S_{DW}$ | $S_{DW} = S_F + \sum_{d=1}^{D} S_{D_d}$ | Total DW size | bytes | | $S_{C}$ | $S_{C} = \dfrac{S_{DW}}{CR}$ | Compressed size | bytes | | $S_{I}$ | $S_{I} = R \cdot (K_{size} + P_{size})$ | B-Tree index size | bytes | | $T_{ETL}$ | $T_{ETL} = \dfrac{V}{R_t \cdot (1 - P_{err})}$ | ETL throughput | rows/s | | $R_{agg}$ | $R_{agg} = \dfrac{R_{fact}}{g_1 \cdot g_2 \cdots g_n}$ | Aggregation reduction ratio | unitless | | $Q_{latency}$ | $Q_{latency} = T_{scan} + T_{join} + T_{agg}$ | Query latency | seconds | | $A_{gain}$ | $A_{gain} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_{with\,agg}}{Q_{without\,agg}}$ | Aggregation speed-up | fraction | **Variable Glossary:** - $C_i, L_i, P_i$ = columns, length, rows for table $i$ - $M_j, B_j$ = measure column $j$ size, type width - $CR$ = compression ratio - $V$ = total source volume (rows) - $R_t$ = raw extraction rate - $P_{err}$ = error/parity overhead fraction - $g_1, g_2, \ldots, g_n$ = grouping cardinalities ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility Data Warehouse implementation patterns are foundational in: - **Banking** — Basel III regulatory reporting. - **Retail** — Walmart, Amazon sales analysis. - **Healthcare** — Patient outcome analytics. - **Telecom** — CDR (Call Detail Record) warehousing. - **IoT** — Time-series sensor data lakes. - **E-Governance** — Smart city dashboards (relevant to KTU Kerala context — KSEB, KSRTC dashboards). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Problem 1 — Sizing a Star Schema Data Warehouse > [!NOTE] > **Scenario:** A retail company designs a Star Schema with: > - **Fact_Sales** with **10 million rows**. > - Measures: `sales_amount` (DECIMAL(10,2)) and `quantity` (INT) — 2 measures. > - Foreign keys: 4 surrogate keys (each 4 bytes INT). > - Date dimension: 5 years × 365 days = **1,825 rows**, 15 columns × 50 bytes avg. > - Product dimension: **50,000 rows**, 20 columns × 50 bytes avg. > - Store dimension: **500 rows**, 10 columns × 50 bytes avg. > - Customer dimension: **1,000,000 rows**, 25 columns × 50 bytes avg. > - Compression ratio (CR) = 3.5 > > Find: (a) Raw fact size, (b) Total dimension size, (c) Compressed DW size. #### Step-by-Step Derivation **(a) Fact Table Raw Size $S_F$:** $$ S_F = R \cdot \sum_{j=1}^{k} (M_j \cdot B_j) $$ $$ S_F = 10{,}000{,}000 \cdot \big[(10 \cdot 2) + (4 \cdot 1) + (4 \cdot 4)\big] $$ $$ S_F = 10{,}000{,}000 \cdot \big[20 + 4 + 16\big] = 10{,}000{,}000 \cdot 40 $$ $$ S_F = 400{,}000{,}000 \text{ bytes} \approx 381.5 \text{ MB} $$ **Logic:** The fact table holds 2 measure columns (sales_amount = 10 bytes, quantity = 4 bytes) plus 4 surrogate key columns of 4 bytes each. Sum of column widths = 40 bytes per row. **(b) Total Dimension Size $S_D$:** $$ S_D = \sum_{d=1}^{D} (R_d \cdot C_d \cdot B_d) $$ $$ S_D = (1825 \cdot 15 \cdot 50) + (50000 \cdot 20 \cdot 50) + (500 \cdot 10 \cdot 50) + (1000000 \cdot 25 \cdot 50) $$ $$ S_D = 1{,}368{,}750 + 50{,}000{,}000 + 250{,}000 + 1{,}250{,}000{,}000 $$ $$ S_D = 1{,}301{,}618{,}750 \text{ bytes} \approx 1.212 \text{ GB} $$ **Logic:** Compute each dimension separately, then sum. The Customer dimension dominates (1 GB+). **(c) Compressed Warehouse Size $S_{DW}^{comp}$:** $$ S_{DW} = S_F + S_D = 400{,}000{,}000 + 1{,}301{,}618{,}750 $$ $$ S_{DW} = 1{,}701{,}618{,}750 \text{ bytes} \approx 1.585 \text{ GB} $$ $$ S_{C} = \dfrac{S_{DW}}{CR} = \dfrac{1.585 \text{ GB}}{3.5} \approx 0.453 \text{ GB} \approx 463 \text{ MB} $$ **Logic:** Compression ratio of 3.5 reduces storage by factor of 3.5. This is realistic for columnar compression in Parquet/ORC formats. --- ### 3.2 Problem 2 — ETL Throughput Calculation > [!NOTE] > **Scenario:** A bank's nightly ETL must process 500 million records. Extraction rate $R_t$ = 50,000 rows/sec. Error/validation overhead $P_{err}$ = 0.04 (4% records need retransformation). Find the effective ETL throughput and total time. #### Derivation $$ T_{ETL} = \dfrac{V}{R_t \cdot (1 - P_{err})} $$ $$ T_{ETL} = \dfrac{500{,}000{,}000}{50{,}000 \cdot (1 - 0.04)} = \dfrac{500{,}000{,}000}{50{,}000 \cdot 0.96} $$ $$ T_{ETL} = \dfrac{500{,}000{,}000}{48{,}000} \approx 10{,}416.67 \text{ rows/sec} $$ $$ \text{Total Time} = \dfrac{V}{T_{ETL}} = \dfrac{500{,}000{,}000}{10{,}416.67} \approx 48{,}000 \text{ sec} \approx 13.33 \text{ hours} $$ **Logic:** 4% of records consume extra validation cycles, reducing effective throughput. If the nightly window is < 13 hours, the team must parallelise or optimise ETL. --- ### 3.3 Problem 3 — Aggregation Speed-up via Materialized Views > [!NOTE] > **Scenario:** Base fact table has 100M rows. Aggregation by (Year, Region, ProductCategory) reduces rows to 50,000. Without aggregation, a query takes 240 sec. With the materialized aggregate, it takes 3 sec. Find speed-up fraction. #### Derivation $$ A_{gain} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_{with\,agg}}{Q_{without\,agg}} $$ $$ A_{gain} = 1 - \dfrac{3}{240} = 1 - 0.0125 = 0.9875 $$ $$ \boxed{A_{gain} = 98.75\%} $$ **Logic:** The aggregation reduces scanned data by 2000× (100M → 50K), translating to a ~98.75% latency reduction. This justifies the storage cost of maintaining materialized views. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Schema Selection | Criterion | Star Schema | Snowflake | Fact Constellation | |---|---|---|---| | **Query Performance** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **Storage Efficiency** | ⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ | | **ETL Complexity** | ⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐ | | **Business Adaptability** | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **Maintenance Ease** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ | > [!TIP] > **Examiner Heuristic:** For OLAP, prefer **Star**; for storage-critical cold storage, prefer **Snowflake**; for complex multi-process warehouses, use **Galaxy**. --- ### 3.5 B-Tree Index Size Derivation > [!NOTE] > For 1M rows, INT key (4 bytes), pointer (6 bytes typical for ROWID): $$ S_I = R \cdot (K_{size} + P_{size}) = 1{,}000{,}000 \cdot (4 + 6) = 10{,}000{,}000 \text{ bytes} \approx 9.54 \text{ MB} $$ **Logic:** Leaf nodes store key+pointer pairs. With 70% page fill factor, actual on-disk size may inflate by ~1.4×. --- ### 3.6 KTU Analytical Reasoning Pattern > [!IMPORTANT] > **Implementation Evaluation Framework (used in 14-mark questions):** > 1. **Grain Definition** — What does one fact row represent? > 2. **Conformed Dimensions** — Are Customer/Product/Date shared? > 3. **Surrogate Keys** — Are natural keys replaced? > 4. **Partitioning Key** — Date? Region? Hash? > 5. **ETL SLA** — Time window, freshness, error rate. > 6. **Index Strategy** — B-Tree, Bitmap, Columnar. > 7. **Aggregation Refresh** — On-write vs. on-read. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Three-Tier Data Warehouse Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB node1["SOURCE TIER
OLTP Databases
Flat Files
APIs and Logs"] node2["STAGING TIER
Landing Zone
Raw Extracts
Temporary Tables"] node3["ETL ENGINE TIER
Extract Transform Load
Data Cleansing
SCD Handler"] node4["INTEGRATION TIER
Enterprise Data Warehouse
Normalised 3NF
Conformed Dimensions"] node5["DATA MARTS TIER
Sales Mart
Finance Mart
HR Mart"] node6["OLAP TIER
ROLAP MOLAP HOLAP
Cubes and Aggregates"] node7["PRESENTATION TIER
BI Dashboards
Reports
Data Mining Tools"] node8["METADATA LAYER
Technical Metadata
Business Metadata
Process Metadata"] node1 --> node2 node2 --> node3 node3 --> node4 node4 --> node5 node5 --> node6 node6 --> node7 node8 -.governs.-> node3 node8 -.governs.-> node4 node8 -.governs.-> node5 ``` **Reading the diagram:** - **Bottom-up flow** (Sources → Presentation) is the data movement. - **Side-channel (Metadata)** cuts across all tiers — it is the *control plane* of the warehouse. --- ### 4.2 Star Schema — Sales Data Mart ```mermaid flowchart LR fact1["FACT_SALES
PK: sale_id
FK: date_key
FK: product_key
FK: store_key
FK: customer_key
Measures: sales_amount, quantity, profit"] dim1["DIM_DATE
PK: date_key
day, month, quarter, year
fiscal_week, holiday_flag"] dim2["DIM_PRODUCT
PK: product_key
name, category, brand
supplier, launch_date"] dim3["DIM_STORE
PK: store_key
name, city, state, region
manager, opened_date"] dim4["DIM_CUSTOMER
PK: customer_key
name, segment, age_band
loyalty_tier, join_date"] dim1 --> fact1 dim2 --> fact1 dim3 --> fact1 dim4 --> fact1 ``` **Reading the diagram:** Each dimension is **denormalised** (a flat wide table). The fact table is the centre of the "star." --- ### 4.3 Snowflake Schema — Normalised Product Dimension ```mermaid flowchart LR fact2["FACT_SALES
measures: sales, qty"] dimP["DIM_PRODUCT
product_key
name, list_price"] dimC["DIM_CATEGORY
category_key
category_name"] dimB["DIM_BRAND
brand_key
brand_name"] dimS["DIM_SUPPLIER
supplier_key
supplier_name"] fact2 --> dimP dimP --> dimC dimP --> dimB dimP --> dimS ``` **Reading the diagram:** Product is normalised into 3 sub-dimensions (Category, Brand, Supplier), forming a *snowflake* pattern. --- ### 4.4 ETL Process Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD s1["STEP A: EXTRACT
Identify source systems
Perform full or incremental pull
Use CDC change data capture"] s2["STEP B: STAGE
Land raw data in staging schema
Preserve source fidelity
Avoid transformations here"] s3["STEP C: TRANSFORM
Apply cleansing rules
Deduplicate records
Standardize formats
Assign surrogate keys
Resolve SCD Type 2 history"] s4["STEP D: LOAD
Bulk load fact tables
Update dimensions
Rebuild indexes
Refresh aggregations"] s5["STEP E: AUDIT AND NOTIFY
Log row counts
Reconcile with source
Email SLA dashboard"] s1 --> s2 --> s3 --> s4 --> s5 ``` **Reading the diagram:** Strictly sequential — each step has a defined input/output contract. --- ### 4.5 Implementation Lifecycle (Kimball) ```mermaid flowchart LR p1["P1: PROGRAM
Scope and charter"] p2["P2: REQUIREMENTS
Business KPIs"] p3["P3: DATA MODEL
Dimensional design"] p4["P4: PHYSICAL DESIGN
Storage and indexing"] p5["P5: ETL DESIGN
Pipeline build"] p6["P6: DEPLOYMENT
Cube and roll out"] p7["P7: GROWTH
Tuning and maintenance"] p1 --> p2 --> p3 --> p4 --> p5 --> p6 --> p7 p7 -.feedback loop.-> p1 ``` **Reading the diagram:** Sequential but with a **feedback loop** — insights from growth phase feed into new program cycles (next data mart). ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Explain the three-tier architecture of a Data Warehouse with a neat diagram.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The three-tier architecture of a Data Warehouse consists of: 1. **Bottom Tier (Data Source / Staging Layer):** Contains the source OLTP systems, flat files, and external feeds. Raw data is extracted into a staging area without transformation. 2. **Middle Tier (Integration / Data Warehouse Server):** This is the core of the warehouse, where data is cleaned, integrated, transformed, and stored using either a relational (ROLAP) or multidimensional (MOLAP) engine. The enterprise data warehouse resides here, normalised in 3NF (Inmon) or denormalised into star schemas (Kimball). 3. **Top Tier (Front-End / Presentation Layer):** Houses OLAP servers, reporting tools, dashboards, and data mining applications used by analysts and decision-makers. > [!NOTE] > **Markers' Note:** Always draw the diagram (1 mark) and label tiers with examples (2 marks). Mentioning the metadata repository spanning all tiers gets extra credit. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Differentiate between ROLAP, MOLAP, and HOLAP. (Any three points)* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Criterion | ROLAP | MOLAP | HOLAP | |---|---|---|---| | **Storage** | Relational DB | Multidimensional Cube | Both | | **Query Speed** | Slower | Fastest | Moderate | | **Scalability** | High | Limited by cube size | High | | **Data Freshness** | Real-time | Periodic refresh | Hybrid | ROLAP executes SQL against the fact table at query time; MOLAP pre-aggregates into cubes; HOLAP stores detail in RDBMS and aggregations in cubes. --- ### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Style]** **(a)** Explain the Kimball Dimensional Lifecycle for Data Warehouse Implementation with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of **conformed dimensions** in integrating multiple data marts. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A retail company is implementing a Sales Data Warehouse. The **Fact_Sales** table has **20 million rows** with 3 measures (`sales_amount` = 8 bytes, `discount` = 4 bytes, `tax` = 4 bytes) and 5 foreign keys (each 4 bytes). The dimensions have the following sizes: - Date: 1,826 rows × 12 columns × 40 bytes - Product: 30,000 rows × 18 columns × 50 bytes - Store: 800 rows × 15 columns × 50 bytes - Customer: 2,000,000 rows × 22 columns × 50 bytes Compute: (i) Fact table raw size, (ii) Total dimension size, (iii) Total DW size, (iv) Compressed size assuming CR = 4.0. **(7 Marks)** --- ##### **Model Solution for Question A:** **(a) Kimball Dimensional Lifecycle (7 Marks):** The Kimball Lifecycle is a **bottom-up methodology** that prioritises rapid delivery of analytical value through iterative data mart construction. **Phases:** 1. **Program/Project Planning:** Define scope, sponsors, and ROI. 2. **Business Requirements:** Conduct interviews, identify KPIs, fix grain. 3. **Dimensional Modelling:** Design Star/Snowflake schemas; identify facts (numeric measures) and dimensions (descriptive contexts). 4. **Physical Design:** Storage allocation, indexing, partitioning. 5. **ETL Design:** Build extract-transform-load pipelines with data quality rules. 6. **Deployment:** Configure OLAP cubes, security, end-user training. 7. **Growth & Maintenance:** Performance tuning, partitioning archival, additional marts. **Role of Conformed Dimensions:** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conformed dimensions are the "glue" of the Kimball architecture.** They are dimensions (e.g., Customer, Product, Date, Geography) that are **shared identically** across multiple data marts. **Benefits:** - **Cross-process analysis:** A user can join Sales and Inventory facts on the same Date and Product keys. - **Drill-across queries:** Compare metrics across departments without reconciliation logic. - **Single source of truth:** Eliminates dimension redundancy and inconsistency. - **Bus architecture:** Forms the *dimensional bus matrix* that orchestrates enterprise rollout. **Example:** The `Date` dimension in the Sales Mart and the Finance Mart must have identical rows, surrogate keys, and hierarchies for the bus matrix to function. **(b) Numerical Computation (7 Marks):** **(i) Fact Table Size:** $$ S_F = 20{,}000{,}000 \cdot \big[(8 + 4 + 4) + (5 \cdot 4)\big] = 20{,}000{,}000 \cdot (16 + 20) = 20{,}000{,}000 \cdot 36 $$ $$ \boxed{S_F = 720{,}000{,}000 \text{ bytes} \approx 686.6 \text{ MB}} $$ **(ii) Total Dimension Size:** $$ S_D = (1826 \cdot 12 \cdot 40) + (30000 \cdot 18 \cdot 50) + (800 \cdot 15 \cdot 50) + (2000000 \cdot 22 \cdot 50) $$ $$ S_D = 876{,}480 + 27{,}000{,}000 + 600{,}000 + 2{,}200{,}000{,}000 $$ $$ \boxed{S_D = 2{,}228{,}476{,}480 \text{ bytes} \approx 2.076 \text{ GB}} $$ **(iii) Total DW Size:** $$ S_{DW} = S_F + S_D = 720{,}000{,}000 + 2{,}228{,}476{,}480 = 2{,}948{,}476{,}480 \text{ bytes} $$ $$ \boxed{S_{DW} \approx 2.747 \text{ GB}} $$ **(iv) Compressed Size:** $$ S_C = \dfrac{S_{DW}}{CR} = \dfrac{2.747 \text{ GB}}{4.0} \approx 0.687 \text{ GB} \approx 703.4 \text{ MB} $$ **Final Answer:** Compressed Data Warehouse Size ≈ **703 MB**. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]** **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **ETL process** in Data Warehouse Implementation. Discuss the three types of **Slowly Changing Dimensions (SCD)** with one real-world example each. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** An ETL pipeline processes 1.2 billion records nightly. The extraction rate is 80,000 rows/sec. The error/validation overhead is 6%. Compute: (i) Effective ETL throughput, (ii) Total ETL time, (iii) Suggest **two techniques** to reduce this time by 40%. **(7 Marks)** --- ##### **Model Solution for Question B:** **(a) ETL Process & SCD Types (7 Marks):** **ETL Diagram & Phases:** 1. **Extract:** Pull data from heterogeneous sources — RDBMS (Oracle, MySQL), NoSQL, XML/JSON, log files, APIs. Strategies: Full Extract vs. Incremental Extract (using CDC, timestamps, or triggers). 2. **Transform:** Apply business rules: - Data cleansing (remove nulls, fix typos). - Standardisation (date formats, currency conversion). - Deduplication (using fuzzy matching). - Surrogate key generation. - Business logic application (e.g., profit = revenue − cost). 3. **Load:** Bulk insert into target warehouse tables. Techniques include partition exchange loading, direct-path inserts, and bulk-loader utilities (e.g., `bcp`, `COPY`). **SCD Types:** | Type | Behaviour | Real-World Example | |---|---|---| | **SCD Type 1** | Overwrites old value with new | Updating a customer's phone number | | **SCD Type 2** | Adds new row with effective date | Tracking a customer's address change history | | **SCD Type 3** | Adds new column for previous value | Storing both current and previous job title | > [!TIP] > **SCD Type 2 is the most common in DW** because full history is required for trend analysis. **(b) ETL Throughput Computation (7 Marks):** **(i) Effective Throughput:** $$ T_{ETL} = \dfrac{V}{R_t \cdot (1 - P_{err})} = \dfrac{1{,}200{,}000{,}000}{80{,}000 \cdot 0.94} $$ $$ T_{ETL} = \dfrac{1{,}200{,}000{,}000}{75{,}200} \approx 15{,}957.45 \text{ rows/sec} $$ **(ii) Total ETL Time:** $$ \text{Time} = \dfrac{V}{T_{ETL}} = \dfrac{1{,}200{,}000{,}000}{15{,}957.45} \approx 75{,}200 \text{ sec} \approx 20.89 \text{ hours} $$ **(iii) Two Techniques to Reduce Time by 40%:** 1. **Parallelism & Partitioning:** Split the source data into N parallel streams. With N = 4 parallel workers, throughput scales nearly linearly → time drops to ~5.2 hours. 2. **Bulk Loading + Direct Path Inserts:** Bypass SQL parsing by using native loaders (e.g., Oracle `SQL*Loader`, PostgreSQL `COPY`, Snowflake `PUT`). Reduces per-row overhead by 5–10×. 3. *(Bonus)* Use **incremental loads with CDC** instead of full extracts — only process changed rows. 4. *(Bonus)* Pre-aggregate at source using **change-data-capture streams (Kafka, Kinesis)** for near-real-time ingestion. **Final Target Time:** 20.89 × 0.6 ≈ **12.5 hours** (achievable easily with the above techniques). --- > [!WARNING] > **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing SCD types** — Students frequently swap Type 2 and Type 3. Memorise: **Type 2 = New Row**, **Type 3 = New Column**. > 2. **Forgetting units in sizing problems** — Always mention **bytes / KB / MB / GB** explicitly. Marks are deducted for unit omission. > 3. **Not drawing the diagram** in ETL questions — A missing diagram = 2-mark penalty minimum. > 4. **Mixing up Inmon and Kimball** — Inmon = top-down + 3NF EDW; Kimball = bottom-up + dimensional. **Do NOT interchange.** > 5. **Ignoring Metadata** — Every implementation answer should mention the metadata repository as a *cross-cutting layer*. > 6. **Confusing Fact and Dimension** — Facts hold *numeric measures*; Dimensions hold *descriptive attributes*. A common error is to model measures as dimensions. > 7. **Compression ratio placement** — Divide by CR to get compressed size, not multiply. > 8. **ETL ≠ ELT** — Note that cloud warehouses (Snowflake, BigQuery) sometimes prefer **ELT** (load raw, transform in-place) for scalability — this distinction can fetch bonus marks. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **⚡ RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Data Warehouse Implementation** > > ✅ **Core Phases:** Plan → Requirements → Model → Physical → ETL → Deploy → Maintain. > > ✅ **Inmon vs Kimball:** Top-down vs Bottom-up; 3NF EDW vs Dimensional Marts. > > ✅ **Three-Tier Architecture:** Source → Integration → Presentation (+ Metadata across all). > > ✅ **Schemas:** Star (fast), Snowflake (compact), Galaxy (complex business). > > ✅ **ETL:** Extract → Stage → Transform → Load → Audit. SCD Type 1/2/3 handling. > > ✅ **OLAP Types:** ROLAP (scalable, slower), MOLAP (fast cubes), HOLAP (hybrid). > > ✅ **Sizing Formulas:** $S_F$, $S_D$, $S_{DW}$, $S_C = S_{DW}/CR$. > > ✅ **Indexing:** B-Tree (range), Bitmap (low-cardinality), Columnar (analytics). > > ✅ **Partitioning:** Date-range, Hash, List. Improves prune efficiency. > > ✅ **Aggregations:** Materialized views reduce query time by 10×–1000×. > > ✅ **Metadata Types:** Technical (schema, ETL logs), Business (KPIs, glossary), Process (run times). > > ✅ **Real-World Tools:** Informatica, Talend, SSIS (ETL); Snowflake, Teradata, Redshift (DW); Tableau, Power BI (BI). > > ✅ **Grain Declaration:** Always state *what one fact row represents* — this is non-negotiable in dimensional modelling. > > ✅ **Conformed Dimensions:** The cornerstone of Kimball's bus architecture. > > ✅ **Implementation Risks:** Scope creep, data quality issues, poor indexing, ETL failures, user adoption resistance. > [!TIP] > **Top 3 High-Yield Topics for KTU 2024 Exams:** > 1. Schema comparison (Star vs Snowflake) — 7 to 14 marks guaranteed. > 2. ETL with SCD types — 7 marks very likely. > 3. Numerical sizing of fact/dimension tables — always appears in Part B.

topics.title: From Data Warehousing to Data Mining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT524 - DATA MINING AND WAREHOUSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** From Data Warehousing to Data Mining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Data Warehousing** is the systematic process of constructing and maintaining a centralized repository (the *data warehouse*) that consolidates data from multiple heterogeneous, distributed, and autonomous operational sources for the purpose of enabling efficient **Online Analytical Processing (OLAP)**, strategic reporting, and knowledge discovery.

**Data Mining**, in contrast, is the computational process of uncovering previously unknown, valid, non-trivial, and potentially useful patterns, associations, anomalies, and predictive relationships from large volumes of stored data using techniques drawn from statistics, machine learning, and database systems.

The transition phrase **"From Data Warehousing to Data Mining"** refers to the evolutionary pipeline in which an organization's *integrated, cleaned, time-variant, subject-oriented* data warehouse becomes the **primary substrate** upon which intelligent analytical mining algorithms are executed. The warehouse does not perform mining itself; rather, it prepares the *right data, in the right shape* so that mining algorithms can extract *right knowledge*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Inmon-style):**
> A Data Warehouse is a **subject-oriented, integrated, time-variant, and non-volatile** collection of data in support of management's decision-making process.

| Property | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Subject-Oriented** | Organized around major business subjects (sales, customers, products) rather than operational transactions |
| **Integrated** | Unifies data from heterogeneous sources with consistent naming, units, and encoding |
| **Time-Variant** | Every datum is tied to a specific time horizon (5–10 years), unlike operational data which is current-value only |
| **Non-Volatile** | Once loaded, data is stable — no update/delete operations, only inserts and bulk loads |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Library and the Researcher Analogy**
> 
> Imagine a **National Library** that collects books, magazines, newspapers, and manuscripts from every district, language, and era. It catalogues them, dust-jackets them, repairs torn pages, places them in a single air-conditioned hall, and indexes them by author, year, and subject — this is the **Data Warehouse**.
> 
> Now imagine a **sociologist** who walks into that library. She does not care about the library's shelves — she wants to answer the question: *"Across 50 years of regional newspapers, which months saw the highest spike in agricultural distress reports, and is there a correlation with monsoon delays?"* She reads thousands of documents, cross-references dates, applies statistical tests, and produces a thesis. **That researcher is the Data Mining engine.**
> 
> The library (warehouse) is useless without the researcher (mining), and the researcher cannot work without the library (warehouse). This is the *evolution from warehousing to mining*.

## 1.3 Why the Evolution Occurs — The Motivation Pipeline

1. **Stage 1 — Operational Bottleneck:** Operational databases (OLTP) are tuned for fast, atomic transactions (insert/update/delete). Running analytical queries on them causes locking conflicts and crashes.
2. **Stage 2 — Data Warehousing:** A separate analytical store is built, decoupled from OLTP, optimized for read-heavy, complex, multi-dimensional queries (OLAP).
3. **Stage 3 — Need for Discovery:** OLAP answers *known* questions ("What were Q3 sales in Kerala?"). But managers ask *unknown* questions ("Which customers are likely to churn next quarter?"). This demands **proactive pattern discovery** — i.e., Data Mining.
4. **Stage 4 — Convergence:** Modern DW systems embed DM engines (in-database mining); DM algorithms consume OLAP cubes directly (OLAP-mining integration).

## 1.4 Key Metrics and Constants

- **Storage Time Horizon:** typically **5 to 10 years** of historical data.
- **Refresh Cycle:** Near-real-time (continuous) → Daily → Weekly → Monthly.
- **Data Granularity:** Fact tables store measures at the lowest grain (transaction level); aggregates are pre-computed in OLAP cubes.
- **Dimensional Cardinality Rule:** A cube with $d$ dimensions has $2^d$ cuboids (including the apex and base cuboid).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Multi-Dimensional Data Model — Foundation of Both DW and DM

The bridge between warehousing and mining is the **multi-dimensional data model**, which views data as a **data cube**.

A **data cube** consists of:
- **Dimensions:** the *axes* of analysis (e.g., `Time`, `Location`, `Product`).
- **Measures:** the *numeric values* being analyzed (e.g., `sales_amount`, `units_sold`).

### 2.1.1 Lattice of Cuboids

For $d$ dimensions, the cube consists of a **lattice of cuboids**. The **apex cuboid** (0-D) holds the single grand-total measure. The **base cuboid** ($d$-D) holds the lowest-level fact data.

$$ \text{Total Cuboids} = 2^d $$

For $d = 3$ dimensions: $2^3 = 8$ cuboids.

### 2.1.2 OLAP Operations on the Data Cube

| Operation | Description | Intuition |
|---|---|---|
| **Roll-up (Drill-up)** | Aggregates data by climbing up a concept hierarchy (e.g., city → state → country) | Zoom out on a map |
| **Drill-down** | Reverse of roll-up; navigates from less detailed to more detailed data | Zoom into the map |
| **Slice** | Selects a single value for one dimension (a thin slab of the cube) | Cut a vertical slice of bread |
| **Dice** | Selects a sub-cube by specifying value ranges on two or more dimensions | Cut a 3D dice from a block |
| **Pivot (Rotate)** | Rotates the cube axes to provide a different presentation | Turn the cube to see another face |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The data cube is the *structural enabler* that allows both OLAP queries and DM algorithms (clustering, classification, association) to operate on the same pre-aggregated, multi-dimensional substrate efficiently.

## 2.2 Multi-Dimensional Data Model — Schema Patterns

### 2.2.1 Star Schema
A central **fact table** (containing measures and foreign keys to dimensions) surrounded by **dimension tables** (each representing one axis). Denormalized.

### 2.2.2 Snowflake Schema
A refinement of star schema where dimension tables are **normalized** into multiple related tables (e.g., `City` table connected to `State` table). Reduces redundancy but increases join cost.

### 2.2.3 Fact Constellations (Galaxy Schema)
Multiple fact tables sharing dimension tables — used for complex enterprise warehouses.

## 2.3 Data Warehouse Architecture — Four-Tier Reference Model

| Tier | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Bottom** | Operational/Source DBs + External Sources | Raw data origin |
| **Tier 1** | Data Staging Area (ETL server) | Extract, Transform, Load, Cleaning, Integration |
| **Tier 2** | Data Warehouse Server (RDBMS/OLAP) | Stores cleaned, integrated, multi-dimensional data |
| **Tier 3** | OLAP Servers + Front-End Tools | Provides multi-dimensional view, query, mining |
| **Top** | Clients (Analysts, Managers, Mining Tools) | Decision-making and knowledge discovery |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Metric | Expression | Purpose / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Number of Cuboids in Cube | $N_{cuboids} = 2^d$ | $d$ = number of dimensions |
| Number of Non-Apex Cuboids | $N_{non-apex} = 2^d - 1$ | Excludes the empty 0-D cell |
| Star Schema Join Cost | $\text{Joins} = d$ | Fact table joins $d$ dimension tables |
| Snowflake Schema Join Cost | $\text{Joins} = d + \sum_{i=1}^{d}(h_i - 1)$ | $h_i$ = hierarchy levels in dimension $i$ |
| Storage for Full Materialization | $S = \sum_{k=0}^{d} \binom{d}{k} \cdot T_k$ | $T_k$ = average tuples in a $k$-D cuboid |
| Minimum Materialization (Iceberg) | Cells with measure $\geq \text{min\_support}$ | Only "above waterline" cuboids stored |
| ETL Throughput Target | $\geq 10^6$ rows/hour | Industry-standard warehouse loading |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Every modern BI platform — *Snowflake, Amazon Redshift, Google BigQuery, Microsoft Azure Synapse* — is built on this exact multi-dimensional DW + DM pipeline. Understanding the "warehouse → mining" transition is foundational for ETL engineering, BI architecture, and applied ML systems.

## 2.5 Concept Hierarchies

A **concept hierarchy** defines a sequence of mappings from a set of low-level concepts to higher-level (more general) concepts. They enable roll-up.

Example for `Location` dimension:

$$ \text{street} < \text{city} < \text{state} < \text{country} < \text{region} $$

Example for `Time` dimension:

$$ \text{day} < \text{month} < \text{quarter} < \text{year} < \text{decade} $$

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Number of Cuboids in a Data Cube

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Given a data cube with $d$ distinct dimensions, derive the total number of cuboids in its complete lattice.

### 3.1.2 Logical Foundation

A cuboid is uniquely identified by the **subset of dimensions** it aggregates over. If a cuboid is the aggregation over $k$ specific dimensions (and holds measures at that level of grouping), then there are $\binom{d}{k}$ such cuboids in the lattice — one for every possible choice of those $k$ dimensions.

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Derivation

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{cuboids} &= \sum_{k=0}^{d} \binom{d}{k} \\
&= \binom{d}{0} + \binom{d}{1} + \binom{d}{2} + \dots + \binom{d}{d}
\end{aligned}$$

Using the **Binomial Theorem** (a standard identity in combinatorics):

$$(x + y)^d = \sum_{k=0}^{d} \binom{d}{k} x^{d-k} y^k$$

Substituting $x = 1$ and $y = 1$:

$$(1 + 1)^d = \sum_{k=0}^{d} \binom{d}{k} (1)^{d-k} (1)^k$$

$$2^d = \sum_{k=0}^{d} \binom{d}{k}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{N_{cuboids} = 2^d}$$

### 3.1.4 Numerical Verification for $d = 3$

| $k$ (group-by count) | $\binom{3}{k}$ | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 1 | Apex (grand total) |
| 1 | 3 | Each single dimension |
| 2 | 3 | Each pair of dimensions |
| 3 | 1 | Base cuboid (no aggregation) |

$$\text{Total} = 1 + 3 + 3 + 1 = 8 = 2^3 \;\checkmark$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Star vs Snowflake Join Cost

### 3.2.1 Star Schema
A fact table $F$ with $d$ foreign keys references $d$ separate denormalized dimension tables $D_1, D_2, \dots, D_d$.

To answer a query touching all $d$ dimensions, we need:

$$J_{star} = d \;\text{joins}$$

### 3.2.2 Snowflake Schema
Each dimension $D_i$ is normalized into $h_i$ hierarchy tables. To traverse dimension $i$ to its highest level requires $h_i - 1$ additional joins.

$$J_{snowflake} = d + \sum_{i=1}^{d} (h_i - 1)$$

### 3.2.3 Worked Example

Suppose $d = 3$ with hierarchy levels `Location = 4` (street, city, state, country), `Time = 3` (day, month, year), `Product = 2` (item, category).

$$\begin{aligned}
J_{snowflake} &= 3 + (4-1) + (3-1) + (2-1) \\
&= 3 + 3 + 2 + 1 \\
&= 9 \;\text{joins}
\end{aligned}$$

While $J_{star} = 3$. This demonstrates the **denormalization trade-off**: star is faster for queries; snowflake saves storage.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Data Warehouse vs Data Mining

| Parameter | Data Warehousing (OLAP) | Data Mining |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Goal** | Enable fast, multi-dimensional queries | Discover hidden, predictive patterns |
| **Question Type** | Known, predefined ("What happened?") | Unknown, exploratory ("Why? What next?") |
| **Data Orientation** | Subject-oriented, historical | Modeled on patterns across data |
| **Output** | Reports, dashboards, cubes | Rules, clusters, trees, models |
| **User** | Decision-makers, analysts | Data scientists, algorithms |
| **System Type** | OLAP (read-optimized) | DM engines (compute-optimized) |
| **Time Horizon** | Historical + current | Pattern extraction from history |
| **Technique** | SQL, MDX, roll-up/drill-down | Classification, clustering, association, regression |
| **Synergy** | Provides clean, integrated substrate | Consumes cube to find insights |
| **Example Tool** | Snowflake, Redshift | Weka, RapidMiner, Python scikit-learn |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A]`

**Explain any three properties of a Data Warehouse that distinguish it from a traditional OLTP database.**

**Model Answer:**

A Data Warehouse is distinguished from an OLTP system by four defining properties (Inmon's framework). Three are explained below:

1. **Subject-Oriented:** While OLTP databases are organized around *applications* (payroll, billing, inventory), the data warehouse is organized around *major business subjects* such as customers, products, sales, and suppliers. This subject-centric view enables holistic analysis across an entire enterprise rather than isolated operational workflows.

2. **Integrated:** OLTP databases typically contain heterogeneous data with inconsistent naming conventions, units of measurement, and encoding schemes (e.g., `M`/`F` vs `Male`/`Female` vs `1`/`0` for gender). The data warehouse integrates these by applying a unified, consistent set of codes, naming standards, and measurement units, making cross-source analysis reliable.

3. **Time-Variant:** OLTP data stores only the *current value* of a record (e.g., current address of customer). The data warehouse stores data over a **5 to 10 year** horizon, capturing how values evolve over time. Every record carries an implicit or explicit time-key, enabling trend analysis and historical comparison.

*(Optional 4th property for completeness: Non-Volatile — once data is loaded, it is not deleted or updated; only new loads occur.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Set B]`

**Differentiate between OLAP and OLTP in terms of any three characteristics.**

**Model Answer:**

| Characteristic | OLTP (Online Transaction Processing) | OLAP (Online Analytical Processing) |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Handle day-to-day operational transactions (insert/update/delete) | Support complex analytical and decision-support queries |
| **Data Nature** | Current, up-to-the-second, detailed | Historical, summarized, multi-dimensional |
| **Query Type** | Simple, short, predefined transactions (e.g., `UPDATE account SET balance = ...`) | Complex, long-running, ad-hoc aggregations (e.g., `SUM(sales) GROUP BY region, quarter`) |
| **Users** | Clerks, front-line staff, customers | Managers, analysts, executives |
| **Schema Design** | Highly normalized (3NF) to minimize redundancy | Denormalized (star/snowflake) to optimize joins |
| **Workload** | Read + Write, mixed, high-concurrency | Read-heavy, low-write |
| **Performance Metric** | Transactions per second (TPS) | Query response time for multi-dimensional scans |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Question (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the architecture of a Data Warehouse with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of ETL in detail.**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** A Data Warehouse architecture is a structured, multi-tier framework that defines how data flows from operational sources, through transformation, into a central repository, and finally to analytical and mining tools.

**Architecture Tiers (Top-Down):**

**1. Operational/Source Databases & External Sources (Bottom Tier):**
These include legacy OLTP systems (e.g., ERP, CRM), flat files, web logs, sensor data, and external feeds. The data here is heterogeneous in format (relational, XML, JSON, CSV) and often contains errors, duplicates, and inconsistencies.

**2. Data Staging Area — ETL Server (Tier 1):**
This is the *transformation engine*. The ETL process performs:

- **Extract:** Pulling raw data from source systems via ODBC/JDBC connectors, APIs, or file ingestion.
- **Transform:** Applying *data cleansing* (handling missing values, removing duplicates), *data integration* (resolving schema conflicts, unifying units), *data derivation* (computing new fields like `profit = revenue - cost`), and *data aggregation* (pre-aggregating for performance).
- **Load:** Inserting transformed data into the warehouse tables using bulk-loading utilities.

**3. Data Warehouse Server (Tier 2):**
Stores the integrated, historical, subject-oriented data. Implemented using RDBMS (Teradata, Oracle Exadata) or columnar MPP databases (Snowflake, Redshift). Contains fact tables and dimension tables in star/snowflake schema.

**4. OLAP Servers (Tier 3):**
Provide a multi-dimensional view of warehouse data. Two main types:
- **ROLAP (Relational OLAP):** Performs multi-dimensional operations via extended SQL on relational tables.
- **MOLAP (Multidimensional OLAP):** Stores data in sparse-array multi-dimensional arrays for fast pre-aggregation.

**5. Front-End Clients (Top Tier):**
BI tools (Tableau, Power BI), data mining engines (Weka, SAS), query/report builders, and executive dashboards.

**Role of ETL in Detail:**

| ETL Sub-task | Function | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Profiling** | Statistical analysis of source to detect anomalies | Discovering that 5% of `customer_age` values are NULL |
| **Data Cleansing** | Fixing or removing dirty records | Replacing `999-999-9999` with NULL phone numbers |
| **Data Standardization** | Enforcing consistent units/codes | Converting all dates to `YYYY-MM-DD` |
| **Deduplication** | Identifying and merging duplicates | Merging "ABC Corp" and "A.B.C. Corporation" |
| **Slowly Changing Dimensions** | Handling changes in dimension attributes over time | Storing historical addresses using SCD Type 2 |
| **Surrogate Key Assignment** | Replacing natural keys with warehouse keys | Assigning `cust_id = 10023` to a new customer |
| **Load Auditing** | Logging rows extracted, transformed, rejected | Reporting "1,200,000 rows extracted, 8,400 rejected" |

ETL is the **most critical and resource-intensive** part of warehousing — typically consuming 60–80% of DW project effort.

---

**Question (b) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the multi-dimensional data model with a suitable example. List the various OLAP operations supported by a data cube.**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** The multi-dimensional data model views enterprise data as a **data cube**, where data is organized along multiple *dimensions* (axes of analysis) with associated *measures* (numeric facts to be analyzed).

**Example:** Consider a retail sales scenario.
- **Dimensions:** `Time` (day, month, quarter, year), `Location` (street, city, state, country), `Product` (item, category, brand).
- **Measure:** `sales_amount` (in INR) and `units_sold`.

The base cuboid `(Time, Location, Product)` contains the lowest-level fact data. Aggregating along `Time` (roll-up) yields `(Quarter, Location, Product)`. The apex cuboid `()` holds the grand total sales across all dimensions.

**Lattice Structure for 3 Dimensions:**

| Cuboid | Dimensions Aggregated | Granularity |
|---|---|---|
| 0-D (Apex) | () | Grand Total |
| 1-D | (Time) | Per Time period |
| 1-D | (Location) | Per Location |
| 1-D | (Product) | Per Product |
| 2-D | (Time, Location) | Time × Location |
| 2-D | (Time, Product) | Time × Product |
| 2-D | (Location, Product) | Location × Product |
| 3-D (Base) | (Time, Location, Product) | All combinations |

Total cuboids = $2^3 = 8$.

**OLAP Operations:**

1. **Roll-up (Drill-up):** Aggregates data by climbing up a concept hierarchy. Example: Sum sales from `(Day, City, Item)` → `(Month, State, Item)` → `(Year, Country, Category)`. Reduces dimensionality or granularity.
