**Smoothed likelihoods for "Spam"** (denominator $= 3 + 2 = 5$):

$$P(\text{Free}=\text{Yes} \mid \text{Spam}) = \frac{2 + 1}{3 + 2} = \frac{3}{5} = 0.6$$

$$P(\text{Offer}=\text{No} \mid \text{Spam}) = \frac{1 + 1}{3 + 2} = \frac{2}{5} = 0.4$$

$$P(\text{Money}=\text{Yes} \mid \text{Spam}) = \frac{1 + 1}{3 + 2} = \frac{2}{5} = 0.4$$

$$P(\text{Click}=\text{Yes} \mid \text{Spam}) = \frac{1 + 1}{3 + 2} = \frac{2}{5} = 0.4$$

**Smoothed likelihoods for "Not Spam"** (denominator $= 3 + 2 = 5$):

$$P(\text{Free}=\text{Yes} \mid \text{Not Spam}) = \frac{0 + 1}{3 + 2} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.2$$

$$P(\text{Offer}=\text{No} \mid \text{Not Spam}) = \frac{2 + 1}{3 + 2} = \frac{3}{5} = 0.6$$

$$P(\text{Money}=\text{Yes} \mid \text{Not Spam}) = \frac{1 + 1}{3 + 2} = \frac{2}{5} = 0.4$$

$$P(\text{Click}=\text{Yes} \mid \text{Not Spam}) = \frac{2 + 1}{3 + 2} = \frac{3}{5} = 0.6$$

### Step 6: Compute Smoothed Posteriors

**Spam posterior:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P(\text{Spam} \mid X)_{\text{smooth}} &\propto 0.5 \times 0.6 \times 0.4 \times 0.4 \times 0.4 \\
&= 0.5 \times 0.0384 \\
&\propto 0.0192
\end{aligned}$$

**Not Spam posterior:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P(\text{Not Spam} \mid X)_{\text{smooth}} &\propto 0.5 \times 0.2 \times 0.6 \times 0.4 \times 0.6 \\
&= 0.5 \times 0.0288 \\
&\propto 0.0144
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 7: Normalize to Get Valid Probabilities

Total unnormalized mass:

$$Z = 0.0192 + 0.0144 = 0.0336$$

$$P(\text{Spam} \mid X) = \frac{0.0192}{0.0336} \approx 0.5714 \; (57.14\%)$$

$$P(\text{Not Spam} \mid X) = \frac{0.0144}{0.0336} \approx 0.4286 \; (42.86\%)$$

### Step 8: Final Classification (MAP Decision)

$$c^* = \arg\max_{c} P(c \mid X) = \arg\max \{0.5714, \; 0.4286\}$$

$$\boxed{c^* = \text{Spam}}$$

**Conclusion:** The test email is classified as **Spam** with a confidence of $\approx 57.14\%$.

### Step 9: Log-Probability Verification (Numerical Stability Check)

For very large documents, products of small probabilities underflow to zero. The **log-sum** form is preferred:

$$\log P(\text{Spam} \mid X) \propto \log(0.5) + \log(0.6) + \log(0.4) + \log(0.4) + \log(0.4)$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\log P(\text{Spam} \mid X) &\propto -0.6931 + (-0.5108) + 3 \times (-0.9163) \\
&= -0.6931 - 0.5108 - 2.7489 \\
&= -3.9528
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\log P(\text{Not Spam} \mid X) &\propto \log(0.5) + \log(0.2) + \log(0.6) + \log(0.4) + \log(0.6) \\
&= -0.6931 + (-1.6094) + (-0.5108) + (-0.9163) + (-0.5108) \\
&= -4.2404
\end{aligned}$$

Since $-3.9528 > -4.2404$, the same classification is obtained. Log-form avoids underflow. ✓

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Naive Bayes Conceptual Map (Bayes' Theorem Components)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Prior P of C"] --> nodeE["Posterior P of C given X"]
    nodeB["Likelihood P of X given C"] --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF["MAP Decision argmax"]
    nodeC["Evidence P of X"] --> nodeE
    nodeD["Conditional Independence Assumption"] --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeG["Product over all features"]
    nodeG --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeH["Predicted Class Label"]
```

## 4.2 Naive Bayes Training and Prediction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph training ["PHASE 1 TRAINING"]
        t1["Input Labelled Training Dataset"] --> t2["Compute Prior P of C for each class"]
        t2 --> t3["Compute Likelihood P of x_i given C for every feature value"]
        t3 --> t4{"Apply Laplace Smoothing if any count equals zero"}
        t4 --> t5["Store Likelihood Table and Prior Probabilities"]
    end
    subgraph prediction ["PHASE 2 PREDICTION"]
        p1["New Test Instance X with n features"] --> p2["For each class compute product of P of C times product of P of x_i given C"]
        p2 --> p3["Apply log transformation for numerical stability"]
        p3 --> p4["Select class with maximum posterior probability"]
        p4 --> p5["Output Predicted Class Label"]
    end
    t5 --> p1
```

## 4.3 Zero-Frequency Problem Resolution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["Test Instance Enters Classifier"] --> nodeCheck{"Any Likelihood P of x_i given C equals zero"}
    nodeCheck -- YES --> nodeLaplace["Apply Laplace Smoothing add alpha to numerator and alpha times vocabulary to denominator"]
    nodeCheck -- NO --> nodeDirect["Use Raw MLE Estimates"]
    nodeLaplace --> nodeCompute["Compute Smoothed Posteriors"]
    nodeDirect --> nodeCompute
    nodeCompute --> nodeArgMax["Argmax over all classes"]
    nodeArgMax --> nodeFinal["Return Best Class"]
```

## 4.4 Naive Bayes Family Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph input ["Input Feature Type"]
        i1["Discrete Counts Words"]
        i2["Binary Presence or Absence"]
        i3["Continuous Real Valued"]
    end
    input --> nbFamily
    subgraph nbFamily ["Naive Bayes Variants"]
        v1["Multinomial NB"]
        v2["Bernoulli NB"]
        v3["Gaussian NB"]
    end
    nbFamily --> apps
    subgraph apps ["Engineering Applications"]
        a1["Spam Filtering and Document Classification"]
        a2["Sentiment Analysis on Short Text"]
        a3["Medical Diagnosis and Sensor Data Mining"]
    end
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**State and explain Bayes' theorem. Define each component with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Bayes' theorem expresses the posterior probability of a hypothesis $C$ given evidence $X$ in terms of prior probability and likelihood:

$$P(C \mid X) = \frac{P(X \mid C) \cdot P(C)}{P(X)}$$

- $P(C)$ — **Prior**: Initial belief about class $C$ (e.g., 1% of all emails are phishing).
- $P(X \mid C)$ — **Likelihood**: Probability of observing evidence $X$ in class $C$ (e.g., probability of a suspicious link being present given a phishing email).
- $P(X)$ — **Evidence**: Overall probability of observing $X$ regardless of class.
- $P(C \mid X)$ — **Posterior**: Updated probability after observing $X$ (e.g., revised belief that the email is phishing given a suspicious link).

**(1 mark** for theorem, **1 mark** for definitions, **1 mark** for example.)

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**What is the "naive" assumption in Naive Bayes? Why is the model still effective despite this assumption?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The naive assumption is the **conditional independence of features given the class label** — that is, for any two features $x_i$ and $x_j$:

$$P(x_i, x_j \mid C) = P(x_i \mid C) \cdot P(x_j \mid C)$$

This means the model assumes that the presence (or value) of one feature does not affect another feature, given the class. It is "naive" because in reality, features are almost always correlated (e.g., in spam detection, "free" and "offer" co-occur).

Despite this oversimplification, Naive Bayes performs well because: **(i)** the classification decision depends only on the **rank ordering** of class probabilities, not their exact values, and **(ii)** the decoupled likelihoods reduce the data requirements drastically, avoiding the curse of dimensionality. Empirically, it is competitive with far more complex models in text mining and document classification.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - 2024 Model, 14 Marks]`

**(a)** Derive the Naive Bayes classification rule from Bayes' theorem. Explain how the conditional independence assumption simplifies the computation, and show the role of the MAP hypothesis in final classification. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given the following training data for a weather-based play decision:

| Day | Outlook | Temperature | Humidity | Wind | **Play** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sunny | Hot | High | Weak | No |
| 2 | Sunny | Hot | High | Strong | No |
| 3 | Overcast | Hot | High | Weak | Yes |
| 4 | Rain | Mild | High | Weak | Yes |
| 5 | Rain | Cool | Normal | Weak | Yes |
| 6 | Rain | Cool | Normal | Strong | No |
| 7 | Overcast | Cool | Normal | Strong | Yes |
| 8 | Sunny | Mild | High | Weak | No |
| 9 | Sunny | Cool | Normal | Weak | Yes |
| 10 | Rain | Mild | Normal | Weak | Yes |

Classify the test instance **(Outlook=Sunny, Temperature=Cool, Humidity=High, Wind=Strong)** using Naive Bayes with **Laplace smoothing** ($\alpha = 1$). **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Bayes' Theorem (2 marks):**

$$P(C \mid X) = \frac{P(X \mid C) \cdot P(C)}{P(X)}$$

where $X = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$ is a feature vector and $C$ is the class variable.

**Step 2 — Joint likelihood without independence (1 mark):**
The exact likelihood $P(X \mid C) = P(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n \mid C)$ requires the full joint distribution — exponential in $n$, infeasible for large feature sets.

**Step 3 — Apply conditional independence (2 marks):**

$$P(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n \mid C) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C)$$

Substituting:

$$P(C \mid X) = \frac{P(C) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C)}{P(X)}$$

**Step 4 — MAP Hypothesis (2 marks):**
Since $P(X)$ is class-independent, drop it and select the class with maximum posterior:

$$c^* = \arg\max_{c \in C} P(c) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid c)$$

This is the **Naive Bayes classification rule**.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Prior Probabilities (1 mark)**

$$P(\text{Yes}) = \frac{5}{10} = 0.5, \quad P(\text{No}) = \frac{5}{10} = 0.5$$

**Step 2: Apply Laplace Smoothing Formula (1 mark)**

$$P(x_i \mid c) = \frac{\text{count}(x_i, c) + 1}{\text{count}(c) + \vert V \vert}$$

For this dataset: $\vert V_{\text{Outlook}} \vert = 3$, $\vert V_{\text{Temp}} \vert = 3$, $\vert V_{\text{Humidity}} \vert = 2$, $\vert V_{\text{Wind}} \vert = 2$.

**Step 3: Likelihoods for "Yes" class (count=5) (1.5 marks)**

$$P(\text{Sunny} \mid \text{Yes}) = \frac{1 + 1}{5 + 3} = \frac{2}{8} = 0.25$$

$$P(\text{Cool} \mid \text{Yes}) = \frac{2 + 1}{5 + 3} = \frac{3}{8} = 0.375$$

$$P(\text{High} \mid \text{Yes}) = \frac{1 + 1}{5 + 2} = \frac{2}{7} \approx 0.2857$$

$$P(\text{Strong} \mid \text{Yes}) = \frac{2 + 1}{5 + 2} = \frac{3}{7} \approx 0.4286$$

**Step 4: Likelihoods for "No" class (count=5) (1.5 marks)**

$$P(\text{Sunny} \mid \text{No}) = \frac{2 + 1}{5 + 3} = \frac{3}{8} = 0.375$$

$$P(\text{Cool} \mid \text{No}) = \frac{0 + 1}{5 + 3} = \frac{1}{8} = 0.125$$

$$P(\text{High} \mid \text{No}) = \frac{3 + 1}{5 + 2} = \frac{4}{7} \approx 0.5714$$

$$P(\text{Strong} \mid \text{No}) = \frac{2 + 1}{5 + 2} = \frac{3}{7} \approx 0.4286$$

**Step 5: Posterior Calculation (2 marks)**

$$P(\text{Yes} \mid X) \propto 0.5 \times 0.25 \times 0.375 \times 0.2857 \times 0.4286 \approx 0.005738$$

$$P(\text{No} \mid X) \propto 0.5 \times 0.375 \times 0.125 \times 0.5714 \times 0.4286 \approx 0.005741$$

Since $P(\text{No} \mid X) > P(\text{Yes} \mid X)$, the predicted class is:

$$\boxed{c^* = \text{No (Do Not Play)}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the MAP rule** — students often compute both posteriors but forget to apply $\arg\max$. KTU deducts 1 full mark.
> 2. **Using MLE instead of Laplace smoothing** — even one zero count will collapse the posterior. Always state the smoothing parameter $\alpha$ explicitly.
> 3. **Confusing normalization** — raw posteriors can be compared *without* dividing by $P(X)$ as long as you compare them consistently. If a question asks for "actual probabilities", you must normalize.
> 4. **Wrong vocabulary size** — $\vert V \vert$ in Laplace smoothing is the number of **distinct values the feature can take**, NOT the number of instances.

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - 2024 Model, 14 Marks]`

**(a)** Explain the **three main variants of Naive Bayes classifiers** — Multinomial, Bernoulli, and Gaussian — with their likelihood functions, feature types, and at least one real-world application for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **challenges and limitations** of the Naive Bayes classifier. In particular, explain the **zero-frequency problem**, the **independence assumption violation**, and how **log probabilities** help in numerical stability. Show with a small example how taking logarithms converts a product of small probabilities into a numerically stable sum. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B (a) — 7 Marks**

**1. Multinomial Naive Bayes (2 marks)**
- **Feature type:** Discrete counts (e.g., word frequencies in a document).
- **Likelihood:** $P(x_i \mid c) = \frac{\text{count of feature } i \text{ in class } c}{\text{total count of all features in class } c}$
- **Application:** Document classification, spam filtering, news article categorization.

**2. Bernoulli Naive Bayes (2 marks)**
- **Feature type:** Binary / Boolean (presence or absence of a feature).
- **Likelihood:** $P(x_i \mid c) = p_i^{x_i} (1 - p_i)^{1 - x_i}$ where $p_i = P(\text{feature } i \text{ present} \mid c)$.
- **Application:** Short-text sentiment classification (positive/negative word presence), image classification with binary tags.

**3. Gaussian Naive Bayes (2 marks)**
- **Feature type:** Continuous (real-valued).
- **Likelihood:** $P(x_i \mid c) = \frac{1}{\sigma_c \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{(x_i - \mu_c)^2}{2\sigma_c^2}\right)$
- **Application:** Medical diagnosis (e.g., predicting disease from continuous patient vitals), Iris-flower classification, sensor-based predictions.

**4. Comparison and choice (1 mark):**
- Use **Multinomial NB** for text with term frequencies.
- Use **Bernoulli NB** for binary bag-of-words or short texts.
- Use **Gaussian NB** whenever features are real-valued and approximately normally distributed within each class.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B (b) — 7 Marks**

**1. Zero-Frequency Problem (2.5 marks)**
If a categorical feature value never occurs in training data for a particular class, the MLE estimate becomes:

$$P(x_i \mid c) = \frac{0}{\text{count}(c)} = 0$$

This single zero, when multiplied into the Naive Bayes product, annihilates the entire posterior for that class. The classifier becomes over-confident and assigns zero probability. **Solution:** Apply **Laplace (additive) smoothing**:

$$P(x_i \mid c) = \frac{\text{count}(x_i, c) + \alpha}{\text{count}(c) + \alpha \cdot \vert V \vert}$$

with $\alpha = 1$ (or other positive constant).

**2. Independence Assumption Violation (2.5 marks)**
Naive Bayes assumes features are conditionally independent given the class — but in reality, features are often correlated (e.g., "rain" and "cloudy" in weather data; "free" and "offer" in spam). Despite this violation, the classifier still ranks classes correctly in most cases because the MAP decision only requires the **relative ordering** of posteriors, not their exact values. This robustness is a major reason Naive Bayes remains a baseline in modern NLP pipelines.

**3. Log-Probability for Numerical Stability (2 marks)**
Products of many small probabilities (e.g., 100 word features each with probability $10^{-3}$) underflow to zero in floating-point arithmetic. Converting to **log-space** transforms products into sums:

$$\log\left(\prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid c)\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(x_i \mid c)$$

**Example:** Suppose $P(x_1 \mid c) = 0.01$, $P(x_2 \mid c) = 0.02$, $P(x_3 \mid c) = 0.05$.

- **Direct product:** $0.01 \times 0.02 \times 0.05 = 10^{-5}$ — at risk of underflow.
- **Log-sum:** $\log(0.01) + \log(0.02) + \log(0.05) = -4.605 + -3.912 + -2.996 = -11.513$

Both yield equivalent information, but the log form is numerically stable for high-dimensional inputs.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision Checklist — Naive Bayes**

- ✅ **Bayes' Theorem** is the foundation: $P(C \mid X) = \frac{P(X \mid C) \cdot P(C)}{P(X)}$ — memorize the variable meanings.
- ✅ The **"naive" assumption** is **conditional independence** of features given the class.
- ✅ The classification rule is the **MAP hypothesis**: $c^* = \arg\max P(c) \cdot \prod P(x_i \mid c)$.
- ✅ **Laplace Smoothing** formula: $\dfrac{\text{count}(x_i, c) + \alpha}{\text{count}(c) + \alpha \cdot \vert V \vert}$ with $\alpha = 1$ by default.
- ✅ **Vocabulary size** $\vert V \vert$ = number of **distinct values** a feature can take (not the number of training samples).
- ✅ Use **log-probabilities** in high-dimensional text problems to avoid floating-point underflow.
- ✅ Three main variants: **Multinomial** (counts), **Bernoulli** (binary), **Gaussian** (continuous).
- ✅ The **zero-frequency problem** can collapse a class posterior to 0 — always check for unseen feature-class combinations.
- ✅ $P(X)$ (evidence) is **class-independent** and can be dropped during comparison — but include it if the question asks for **actual probabilities**.
- ✅ Naive Bayes is the **baseline classifier** in text mining, spam filtering, and sentiment analysis — frequently used in KTU viva questions.
- ✅ **Key advantage:** Fast training, handles high-dimensional data, works with small training sets, probabilistic output (not just labels).
- ✅ **Key disadvantage:** Strong independence assumption rarely holds in real data; poor probability calibration; zero-frequency issue requires smoothing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 🎯 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN) Algorithm:**
> *k-Nearest Neighbors is a fundamental **non-parametric, instance-based (lazy) supervised learning algorithm** used in data mining for both classification and regression tasks. The algorithm classifies a new, unknown data point by computing its **distance** to all training instances, identifying the **k closest neighbors** in the feature space, and assigning the class label based on the **majority vote** (classification) or computing the **average** of the neighbor values (regression).*

The term **"k"** represents a user-defined positive integer (typically odd to avoid tie-breaking) that determines the number of neighboring training samples considered for the prediction.

---

## 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — The "Voting Neighbors" Intuition

Imagine you have just moved into a new neighborhood and want to find a good restaurant 🍽️. You don't know the area, so you ask your **3 nearest neighbors** (k = 3) for recommendations.

- **Neighbour A** says: "Italian 🇮🇹"
- **Neighbour B** says: "Italian 🇮🇹"
- **Neighbour C** says: "Chinese 🇨🇳"

The **majority vote (2 out of 3)** decides that an **Italian restaurant** is your best bet!

**KNN works the exact same way:**
- The "neighbors" are points in a multi-dimensional **feature space**.
- The "distance" is the mathematical separation (Euclidean, Manhattan, etc.).
- The "vote" is the class label that appears most often among the k-closest training points.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is KNN called "Lazy Learning"?**
> Because the algorithm **does NOT build a model during training**. It simply **memorizes** the entire training dataset. All the computational work is deferred to the **query (prediction) time** — hence the name "lazy." This is in stark contrast to "eager learners" like Decision Trees or SVMs.

---

## 📌 Key Terminology (KTU Board-Exam Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Instance-Based Learning** | Learning by storing the entire training set; no generalization happens during training. |
| **Feature Space** | The n-dimensional space where each dimension corresponds to a feature/attribute. |
| **Distance Metric** | A mathematical function that quantifies dissimilarity between two data points. |
| **Majority Voting** | The classification rule where the most frequent class among k neighbors wins. |
| **Hyperparameter `k`** | The number of neighbors to consult; selected via cross-validation. |
| **Curse of Dimensionality** | The phenomenon where distance measures become less meaningful in high-dimensional spaces. |

---

## 🏗️ Where KNN Fits in the Data Mining Pipeline

KNN is one of the **simplest yet powerful** algorithms in the data mining toolkit. It is heavily used in:

- 🏥 **Medical Diagnosis** (predicting disease based on patient similarity)
- 💳 **Credit Scoring** (classifying loan defaulters)
- 🛒 **Recommendation Systems** (similar user preferences)
- 🖼️ **Image Recognition** (similar pixel patterns)
- 📈 **Anomaly Detection** (outliers have large distances to all neighbors)

# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🔬 Algorithmic Operational Breakdown

The KNN algorithm can be decomposed into **four logical phases**:

### **Phase 1: Data Preparation (Preprocessing)**
1. **Handle Missing Values:** Impute or remove records with null entries.
2. **Feature Scaling:** Apply **Min-Max Normalization** or **Z-Score Standardization** to ensure no single feature dominates the distance calculation due to scale differences.
3. **Encode Categorical Variables:** Use one-hot encoding or label encoding.

### **Phase 2: Distance Computation**
For a query point $\mathbf{x}_q$ and a training point $\mathbf{x}_i$ in $n$-dimensional space:

$$\mathbf{x}_q = (x_{q1}, x_{q2}, \ldots, x_{qn}), \quad \mathbf{x}_i = (x_{i1}, x_{i2}, \ldots, x_{in})$$

The algorithm computes a **distance metric** $d(\mathbf{x}_q, \mathbf{x}_i)$ for **every training instance**.

### **Phase 3: Neighbor Selection**
- Sort all training points by their computed distance to $\mathbf{x}_q$ in **ascending order**.
- Select the **top-k points** with the smallest distances — these form the neighborhood set $\mathcal{N}_k(\mathbf{x}_q)$.

### **Phase 4: Decision Rule (Prediction)**
- **For Classification (Majority Voting):**

$$\hat{y}_q = \arg\max_{c \in C} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}_k(\mathbf{x}_q)} \mathbb{1}(y_i = c)$$

where $\mathbb{1}(\cdot)$ is the **indicator function** (returns 1 if true, 0 otherwise), and $C$ is the set of all class labels.

- **For Regression (Averaging):**

$$\hat{y}_q = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}_k(\mathbf{x}_q)} y_i$$

- **For Weighted KNN (Advanced):**

$$\hat{y}_q = \frac{\sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}_k(\mathbf{x}_q)} w_i \cdot y_i}{\sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}_k(\mathbf{x}_q)} w_i}, \quad \text{where} \quad w_i = \frac{1}{d(\mathbf{x}_q, \mathbf{x}_i)^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inverse Distance Weighting** gives closer neighbors a higher say in the prediction, making the algorithm more robust to noise and outliers.

---

## 📐 KTU 2024 High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Key Property |
|---|---------|----------------------|--------------|
| 1 | **Euclidean Distance** (L2 norm) | $d_E = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{n}(x_{qj} - x_{ij})^2}$ | Most common; sensitive to scale |
| 2 | **Manhattan Distance** (L1 norm) | $d_M = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert x_{qj} - x_{ij} \vert$ | Robust to outliers |
| 3 | **Minkowski Distance** (Lp norm) | $d_p = \left( \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert x_{qj} - x_{ij} \vert^p \right)^{1/p}$ | Generalization: $p=1$ → Manhattan, $p=2$ → Euclidean |
| 4 | **Chebyshev Distance** (L∞ norm) | $d_C = \max_{j} \vert x_{qj} - x_{ij} \vert$ | Used in chessboard-like grids |
| 5 | **Cosine Similarity** | $\cos(\theta) = \frac{\mathbf{x}_q \cdot \mathbf{x}_i}{\Vert\mathbf{x}_q\Vert \cdot \Vert\mathbf{x}_i\Vert}$ | For text/high-dimensional sparse data |
| 6 | **Min-Max Normalization** | $x' = \frac{x - \min(x)}{\max(x) - \min(x)}$ | Scales to [0, 1] |
| 7 | **Z-Score Standardization** | $z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Zero mean, unit variance |
| 8 | **Majority Vote Classification** | $\hat{y} = \text{mode}(y_i : i \in \mathcal{N}_k)$ | Most frequent class |
| 9 | **Time Complexity (Brute Force)** | $O(n \cdot d)$ per query | $n$ = samples, $d$ = dimensions |
| 10 | **Space Complexity** | $O(n \cdot d)$ | Entire training set stored |

---

## ⚙️ The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why scale features?** → A feature "Income" in ₹ (range: 10,000–1,000,000) would dominate a feature "Age" (range: 0–100) in Euclidean distance, making KNN **misclassify** based on irrelevant scale differences.

- **Why choose an odd `k`?** → An odd value ensures there are no ties in binary classification (e.g., 2 vs 2 becomes 3 vs 2 with k=5).

- **Why is `k=1` a special case?** → It is the **Nearest Neighbor** algorithm — highly susceptible to noise and overfitting. `k=1` decision boundary is a **Voronoi tessellation**.

- **Why use KD-Trees/Ball-Trees?** → To reduce query time from $O(nd)$ to approximately $O(\log n)$, making KNN practical for large datasets.

---

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 📊 Worked-Out Numerical Problem (KTU 2024 Exam Pattern)

### **Problem Statement:**
Given the following 2D training dataset with two classes (C1 = 🟦, C2 = 🟥), classify the new query point **Q = (3, 4)** using **k-NN with k = 3** and **Euclidean Distance**.

| Point ID | x₁ | x₂ | Class (C) |
|----------|----|----|-----------|
| P1 | 1 | 1 | C1 |
| P2 | 2 | 3 | C1 |
| P3 | 2 | 1 | C2 |
| P4 | 5 | 4 | C2 |
| P5 | 4 | 5 | C2 |
| P6 | 1 | 0 | C1 |

---

### **Step 1: Define the Query and Training Points**

The query point in vector form:

$$\mathbf{x}_q = (3, 4)$$

A generic training point:

$$\mathbf{x}_i = (x_{i1}, x_{i2})$$

---

### **Step 2: Apply the Euclidean Distance Formula**

The squared Euclidean distance (we can use squared distance to avoid square root — it preserves ordering):

$$d^2(\mathbf{x}_q, \mathbf{x}_i) = (x_{q1} - x_{i1})^2 + (x_{q2} - x_{i2})^2$$

---

### **Step 3: Compute Distance to Every Training Point**

**For P1 = (1, 1), Class = C1:**

$$d^2(Q, P1) = (3-1)^2 + (4-1)^2 = 2^2 + 3^2 = 4 + 9 = 13$$

**For P2 = (2, 3), Class = C1:**

$$d^2(Q, P2) = (3-2)^2 + (4-3)^2 = 1^2 + 1^2 = 1 + 1 = 2$$

**For P3 = (2, 1), Class = C2:**

$$d^2(Q, P3) = (3-2)^2 + (4-1)^2 = 1^2 + 3^2 = 1 + 9 = 10$$

**For P4 = (5, 4), Class = C2:**

$$d^2(Q, P4) = (3-5)^2 + (4-4)^2 = (-2)^2 + 0^2 = 4 + 0 = 4$$

**For P5 = (4, 5), Class = C2:**

$$d^2(Q, P5) = (3-4)^2 + (4-5)^2 = (-1)^2 + (-1)^2 = 1 + 1 = 2$$

**For P6 = (1, 0), Class = C1:**

$$d^2(Q, P6) = (3-1)^2 + (4-0)^2 = 2^2 + 4^2 = 4 + 16 = 20$$

---

### **Step 4: Build the Sorted Distance Table**

| Rank | Point | Distance² | Class |
|------|-------|-----------|-------|
| 1 | P2 | 2 | **C1** |
| 2 | P5 | 2 | **C2** |
| 3 | P4 | 4 | **C2** |
| 4 | P3 | 10 | C2 |
| 5 | P1 | 13 | C1 |
| 6 | P6 | 20 | C1 |

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that P2 and P5 are at the **same distance** (distance² = 2). In KTU exams, if not specified, you can either break the tie arbitrarily or use the natural ordering. The **top-3** are: P2 (C1), P5 (C2), P4 (C2).

---

### **Step 5: Apply the Majority Vote (k = 3)**

Count the votes among the top-3 neighbors:

$$\text{Vote}(C1) = 1 \quad (\text{from P2})$$

$$\text{Vote}(C2) = 2 \quad (\text{from P5, P4})$$

---

### **Step 6: Final Classification**

$$\hat{y}_Q = \arg\max_{c} \text{Vote}(c) = \arg\max(1, 2) = C2$$

> ✅ **Final Answer:** The query point **Q = (3, 4)** is classified as **Class C2** (🟥).

---

## 🔁 Variation: Weighted KNN for the Same Problem

Using inverse distance squared weights $w_i = 1/d_i^2$:

$$w_{P2} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5, \quad w_{P5} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5, \quad w_{P4} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25$$

Weighted scores:

$$\text{Score}(C1) = 0.5, \quad \text{Score}(C2) = 0.5 + 0.25 = 0.75$$

Still classifies as **C2**, but weighted KNN provides **more nuanced confidence scores**.

---

## 🧮 Analytical Matrix: Impact of `k` on the Same Dataset

| Value of k | Neighbors Considered | Class Distribution | Predicted Class | Bias-Variance Trade-off |
|------------|----------------------|--------------------|-----------------|-------------------------|
| k = 1 | P2 | 100% C1 | C1 | High variance, low bias |
| k = 3 | P2, P5, P4 | 1 C1, 2 C2 | C2 | Balanced |
| k = 5 | P2, P5, P4, P3, P1 | 2 C1, 3 C2 | C2 | Smoother boundary |
| k = 6 | All 6 points | 3 C1, 3 C2 | **TIE** | Highest smoothing (underfitting risk) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This matrix is GOLD for KTU exams** — examiners love asking about the effect of k on the decision boundary and bias-variance trade-off.

---

## 🔄 Effect of Distance Metric — Comparison Table

| Property | Euclidean (L2) | Manhattan (L1) | Minkowski (Lp) | Cosine |
|----------|---------------|----------------|----------------|--------|
| Formula | $\sqrt{\sum(x_q - x_i)^2}$ | $\sum \vert x_q - x_i \vert$ | $\left(\sum \vert x_q - x_i \vert^p\right)^{1/p}$ | $\frac{\mathbf{x}_q \cdot \mathbf{x}_i}{\Vert\mathbf{x}_q\Vert \Vert\mathbf{x}_i\Vert}$ |
| Best for | Continuous, low-dim data | High-dim, sparse data | Tunable (parameter p) | Text, document similarity |
| Sensitivity to outliers | High | Moderate | Depends on p | Low (uses angle only) |
| Computational cost | Moderate (involves square root) | Lowest | Highest (parameter tuning) | Lowest (for sparse vectors) |


## 📋 Textual Schematic: Voronoi Diagram Explanation

For **k = 1 (Nearest Neighbor)**, the 2D feature space is partitioned into **Voronoi cells** — one cell per training point. Every point within a cell is assigned the class of its enclosing training point. This produces a **jagged, complex decision boundary** that perfectly separates the training data but generalizes poorly.

For **k > 1**, the decision boundary becomes **smoother** as predictions are averaged over multiple neighbors, trading off exact training fit for **better generalization** on unseen data.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently draw Voronoi diagrams in the question paper. Always be prepared to **sketch and label** them in viva or written exams.

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define the k-Nearest Neighbors algorithm. Why is it called a "lazy learner"?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN) is a **non-parametric, supervised learning algorithm** that classifies a query instance based on the **majority class** among its *k* closest training examples in the feature space, where closeness is measured using a **distance metric** (e.g., Euclidean distance).

> **Why "Lazy Learner"?** The algorithm does not construct any explicit generalization model (like a decision tree or neural network) during the **training phase**. It simply **stores the entire training dataset in memory**. All computations — distance calculations, sorting, voting — are deferred to the **query (testing) phase**. Hence, the algorithm "lazily" waits until a prediction is requested before doing any work, earning the name *instance-based* or *lazy learning*. This is in contrast to **eager learners** (e.g., Decision Trees, Naive Bayes) that eagerly build a model at training time.

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Explain the role of the distance metric in k-NN. List any three commonly used distance metrics.**

**Model Answer:**

The **distance metric** in k-NN quantifies the **similarity or dissimilarity** between two data points in the feature space. It is the **core engine** of the algorithm, directly determining which training points become the "neighbors" of a query instance. The choice of metric can drastically alter classification outcomes.

**Three Commonly Used Distance Metrics:**

1. **Euclidean Distance (L2 norm):** $d = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{n}(x_{qj} - x_{ij})^2}$ — the straight-line distance in n-dimensional space.

2. **Manhattan Distance (L1 norm):** $d = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert x_{qj} - x_{ij} \vert$ — the sum of absolute differences, like navigating a city grid.

3. **Minkowski Distance (Lp norm):** $d = \left( \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert x_{qj} - x_{ij} \vert^p \right)^{1/p}$ — a generalized form where $p=1$ gives Manhattan and $p=2$ gives Euclidean.

---

## 📝 PART B: Long Answer Questions — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Describe the k-NN algorithm with a neat flowchart. Explain the step-by-step procedure for classifying a new instance using k-NN. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following 2D training data with two classes C1 and C2. Use **k = 5** and **Euclidean distance** to classify the query point Q = (4, 3):

| Point | x₁ | x₂ | Class |
|-------|----|----|-------|
| A | 2 | 2 | C1 |
| B | 3 | 1 | C1 |
| C | 3 | 4 | C1 |
| D | 5 | 5 | C2 |
| E | 4 | 4 | C2 |
| F | 6 | 3 | C2 |
| G | 1 | 5 | C1 |

**(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Part (a):

The k-NN algorithm executes the following **sequential steps**:

1. **Step 1 — Input:** Receive the training dataset $D = \{(\mathbf{x}_1, y_1), (\mathbf{x}_2, y_2), \ldots, (\mathbf{x}_n, y_n)\}$ and a new query point $\mathbf{x}_q$.

2. **Step 2 — Preprocessing:** Normalize/standardize the features to ensure equal contribution.

3. **Step 3 — Distance Computation:** For every training instance $\mathbf{x}_i$, compute:

$$d(\mathbf{x}_q, \mathbf{x}_i) = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{m}(x_{qj} - x_{ij})^2}$$

4. **Step 4 — Sorting:** Sort the distances in **ascending order** $d(\mathbf{x}_q, \mathbf{x}_{(1)}) \leq d(\mathbf{x}_q, \mathbf{x}_{(2)}) \leq \cdots$

5. **Step 5 — Top-k Selection:** Select the first *k* instances — these form the neighborhood $\mathcal{N}_k$.

6. **Step 6 — Voting:** Apply majority voting:

$$\hat{y}_q = \arg\max_{c} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}_k} \mathbb{1}(y_i = c)$$

7. **Step 7 — Output:** Return $\hat{y}_q$ as the predicted class.

**Flowchart** (textual representation for exam):

```
START → Preprocess Data → [Loop: i=1 to n]
     → Compute d(xq, xi) → Store in array
     → End Loop → Sort array ascending
     → Pick top-k → Majority Vote → Output Label → END
```

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Part (b):

**Query Point:** $Q = (4, 3)$

**Step 1: Compute Euclidean Distances**

For A = (2, 2), C1:
$$d^2 = (4-2)^2 + (3-2)^2 = 4 + 1 = 5$$

For B = (3, 1), C1:
$$d^2 = (4-3)^2 + (3-1)^2 = 1 + 4 = 5$$

For C = (3, 4), C1:
$$d^2 = (4-3)^2 + (3-4)^2 = 1 + 1 = 2$$

For D = (5, 5), C2:
$$d^2 = (4-5)^2 + (3-5)^2 = 1 + 4 = 5$$

For E = (4, 4), C2:
$$d^2 = (4-4)^2 + (3-4)^2 = 0 + 1 = 1$$

For F = (6, 3), C2:
$$d^2 = (4-6)^2 + (3-3)^2 = 4 + 0 = 4$$

For G = (1, 5), C1:
$$d^2 = (4-1)^2 + (3-5)^2 = 9 + 4 = 13$$

**Step 2: Sort Ascending**

| Rank | Point | d² | Class |
|------|-------|----|----|
| 1 | E | 1 | C2 |
| 2 | C | 2 | C1 |
| 3 | F | 4 | C2 |
| 4 | A | 5 | C1 |
| 5 | B | 5 | C1 |
| 5 | D | 5 | C2 |
| 7 | G | 13 | C1 |

**Step 3: Top-5 Neighbors (Tie-breaking by natural order at rank 5)**

Top-5: **E(C2), C(C1), F(C2), A(C1), B(C1)**

- **C1 votes:** 3 (from C, A, B)
- **C2 votes:** 2 (from E, F)

**Step 4: Final Classification**

$$\hat{y}_Q = \arg\max(3, 2) = \boxed{C1}$$

> ✅ **Answer:** The query point Q = (4, 3) is classified as **Class C1**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Discuss the effect of choosing different values of *k* on the performance of the k-NN algorithm. Explain the bias-variance trade-off with a suitable diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Weighted k-NN** and compare it with the standard k-NN algorithm. How does feature scaling affect k-NN performance? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Part (a):

**Effect of k on KNN Performance:**

The value of *k* is a critical hyperparameter that controls the **complexity of the decision boundary** and directly influences the **bias-variance trade-off**.

| k Value | Effect | Boundary | Risk |
|---------|--------|----------|------|
| **k = 1** | Most flexible, captures local patterns | Jagged, irregular | **High Variance / Overfitting** |
| **Small k (3-7)** | Good local sensitivity | Moderately smooth | Moderate variance |
| **Moderate k (10-20)** | Balanced | Smooth | Balanced |
| **Large k (close to n)** | Over-simplified | Almost linear/constant | **High Bias / Underfitting** |
| **k = n** | Predicts majority class globally | No boundary (single region) | Maximum bias |

**Bias-Variance Trade-off Explanation:**

- **Bias** = Error from wrong assumptions. High bias → the model is too simple (e.g., k = n predicts the global majority class for everything).
- **Variance** = Error from sensitivity to training data fluctuations. High variance → the model is too complex (e.g., k = 1 memorizes every training point, including noise).

As **k increases**, the model becomes **simpler** → **bias increases** and **variance decreases**.
As **k decreases**, the model becomes **more complex** → **bias decreases** and **variance increases**.

The **optimal k** minimizes the total error (sum of bias² + variance + irreducible noise), typically found via **k-fold cross-validation** on a validation set.

**Diagram (for exam — describe in words):**

```
Error
  ^           Total Error
  |          /         \
  |         /  Bias^2   \
  |        /  ------     \   Variance
  |       / /            \  /
  |      / /              \/
  |     / /              /\
  |    / /              /  \
  |___/_/______________/____\____> k
       1   OPTIMAL    Large k
```

The **minimum of the total error curve** corresponds to the **optimal k**.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Part (b):

**Weighted k-NN Algorithm:**

In standard k-NN, **all k neighbors contribute equally** to the final prediction. However, intuitively, a **closer neighbor** should have **more influence** than a distant one. **Weighted k-NN** addresses this by assigning **weights inversely proportional to distance**.

**Standard k-NN (Majority Vote):**

$$\hat{y}_q = \arg\max_{c} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}_k} \mathbb{1}(y_i = c)$$

**Weighted k-NN (Inverse Distance Weighting):**

$$\hat{y}_q = \arg\max_{c} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}_k} w_i \cdot \mathbb{1}(y_i = c), \quad \text{where} \quad w_i = \frac{1}{d(\mathbf{x}_q, \mathbf{x}_i)^2}$$

For regression:

$$\hat{y}_q = \frac{\sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}_k} w_i \cdot y_i}{\sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}_k} w_i}$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Standard k-NN | Weighted k-NN |
|--------|---------------|---------------|
| Neighbor influence | Equal for all k | Inversely proportional to distance |
| Robustness to noise | Moderate | Higher (distant noisy points get low weight) |
| Tie-breaking | Random or arbitrary | Naturally resolved by weights |
| Computational cost | Lower (no extra division) | Slightly higher (weight computation) |
| Accuracy (typical) | Good baseline | Often 3-8% better |

**Effect of Feature Scaling on KNN:**

Since KNN relies entirely on **distance calculations**, features with **larger numerical ranges** will **dominate** the distance metric, making the algorithm biased toward those features.

> [!WARNING]
> **Concrete Example:** Consider two features:
> - Feature 1: **Age** (range: 0–100)
> - Feature 2: **Annual Income** (range: 0–1,000,000 ₹)
>
> Without scaling, the **income difference** will completely swamp the **age difference** in Euclidean distance. A 30-year-old earning ₹5,00,000 will be "closer" to a 30-year-old earning ₹10,00,000 than to a 31-year-old earning ₹5,00,000 — even though age-wise they are identical.

**Solution — Apply Min-Max Normalization:**

$$x' = \frac{x - x_{\min}}{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}$$

This rescales all features to **[0, 1]**, ensuring **equal contribution** to the distance computation. **Always scale features before applying KNN.**

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Students Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to normalize features** → Loss of 2-3 marks in numerical questions. **Always scale first!**
>
> 2. **Choosing k = 1 blindly** → Examiners interpret this as lack of depth. Always justify the choice of k using **cross-validation** or **odd-k-for-binary-classification** logic.
>
> 3. **Ignoring ties in distance** → If two neighbors have the same distance (as in our P2 and P5 case), clearly state your **tie-breaking strategy** (e.g., "we select the first one encountered in the dataset order").
>
> 4. **Confusing regression vs classification formulas** → Classification uses **mode (majority vote)**, regression uses **mean (averaging)**. Mixing them up costs 1 full mark easily.
>
> 5. **Not mentioning the "Curse of Dimensionality"** → For high-dimensional data, KNN fails. Always mention this as a limitation in long answers.
>
> 6. **Writing the flowchart in words only** → While a text flowchart is acceptable, examiners prefer **numbered procedural steps with a labeled diagram** for full marks.

---

> 📌 **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

- ✅ KNN is a **supervised, non-parametric, lazy, instance-based** learning algorithm.
- ✅ **k must be a positive integer** — prefer **odd values** for binary classification to avoid ties.
- ✅ **Distance metrics:** Euclidean (default), Manhattan, Minkowski, Cosine, Chebyshev.
- ✅ **Classification:** majority vote; **Regression:** arithmetic mean; **Weighted KNN:** inverse-distance weighting.
- ✅ **Always normalize/standardize features** before computing distances.
- ✅ **Brute-force time complexity:** $O(nd)$ per query; use **KD-Tree / Ball-Tree** to optimize.
- ✅ **Small k** → low bias, high variance (overfitting); **Large k** → high bias, low variance (underfitting).
- ✅ **Optimal k** is found via **k-fold cross-validation**.
- ✅ **Curse of Dimensionality:** KNN degrades severely in high-dimensional spaces — consider PCA or feature selection as preprocessing.
- ✅ **Voronoi diagram** is the decision boundary for k = 1.
- ✅ KNN requires **storing the entire training set** in memory — high space complexity $O(nd)$.
- ✅ **Weighted KNN** outperforms standard KNN in most practical scenarios by giving closer neighbors higher influence.
- ✅ For KTU exam: Always show **complete numerical work** — every distance must be **explicitly computed**, never skipped.
- ✅ KNN has **zero training time** but **high prediction time** — a key viva question.
- ✅ KNN is **sensitive to noisy training data and irrelevant features** — apply data cleaning and feature selection.

> **All the best for your KTU 2024 Examinations! 🎓🚀**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Support Vector Machines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT524 - DATA MINING AND WAREHOUSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Support Vector Machines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Support Vector Machine (SVM)** is a **supervised machine learning algorithm** used primarily for **classification** (and regression as SVR) that constructs an **optimal separating hyperplane** in a high-dimensional feature space to maximize the **margin** between two or more classes. The boundary decision surface is determined exclusively by the most critical training examples known as **support vectors**.

In its canonical form, given a labeled training dataset:

$$
\mathcal{D} = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i) \mid i = 1, 2, \dots, N\}, \quad \mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^d, \quad y_i \in \{-1, +1\}
$$

SVM seeks a hyperplane $H: \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = 0$ such that:
1. All points are correctly classified: $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1$
2. The **margin** $\dfrac{2}{\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert}$ is **maximized** (or equivalently $\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert^2$ is minimized).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology — KTU Syllabus Highlights**
> - **Hyperplane:** A decision boundary of dimension $d-1$ in $d$-dimensional input space.
> - **Margin:** The perpendicular distance between the separating hyperplane and the nearest data point from either class.
> - **Support Vectors:** Training samples lying exactly on the margin boundaries; they alone define the hyperplane.
> - **Kernel Function:** A function $\mathcal{K}(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ that implicitly computes inner products in a higher-dimensional space without explicit mapping.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Widest Road Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: Drawing the Widest Road Between Two Villages**
>
> Imagine two villages (Class $+1$ and Class $-1$) sitting on opposite banks of a river. You, as a city planner, must build a road (the **hyperplane**) that separates the two villages cleanly. The question is — *where exactly do you lay the road*?
>
> - If you draw it too close to one village, you'll annoy its residents (the model becomes **sensitive to noise** and generalizes poorly).
> - The smart strategy is to place the road **as far as possible** from **both** villages — i.e., maximize the **margin**.
> - The houses that are closest to the riverbank (and hence constrain where the road can go) are the **support vectors**. Move even a single one of them, and the road must shift!
>
> But what if the villages are tangled together (non-linearly separable)? You build a **bridge (kernel trick)** that lifts them into a higher dimension where a clean flat road can be drawn — and then project the road back to the original map.

This analogy maps directly to SVM:

| Real-World Element | SVM Concept |
|---|---|
| Two villages | Two classes $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$ |
| Riverbank houses | Support vectors |
| Width of the road | Geometric margin $\dfrac{2}{\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert}$ |
| The road itself | Optimal separating hyperplane $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = 0$ |
| Building a bridge to the sky | Kernel-induced feature mapping $\phi(\mathbf{x})$ |

---

## 1.3 Historical Context & Engineering Significance

- **Introduced by** Vapnik & Chervonenkis (1963, linear) and Cortes & Vapnik (1995, soft-margin).
- Foundation rooted in **Statistical Learning Theory** (VC dimension theory).
- Used in: **handwritten digit recognition (MNIST)**, **bioinformatics (cancer classification)**, **face detection**, **text categorization**, **anomaly detection**, and **time-series forecasting**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bold Standard Metric:** The expected generalization error of SVM is bounded by a quantity proportional to the ratio of the VC dimension to the number of training samples — making it exceptionally robust on **small-to-medium** datasets.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Linear Separable SVM — The Hard Margin Formulation

When the data is **linearly separable**, the **Hard-Margin SVM** finds the unique hyperplane that maximizes the geometric margin.

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Define the hyperplane:**

$$
\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = 0
$$

where $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is the weight vector (normal to the hyperplane) and $b \in \mathbb{R}$ is the bias.

2. **State the classification constraint:**

$$
y_i (\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1 \quad \forall i \in \{1, \dots, N\}
$$

The value **1** is the canonical scaling (because $\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert$ is free to scale).

3. **Define the margin:**

The **functional margin** for sample $i$ is:

$$
\hat{\gamma}_i = y_i (\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b)
$$

The **geometric margin** (true Euclidean distance) is:

$$
\gamma_i = \frac{\hat{\gamma}_i}{\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert}
$$

4. **Formulate the primal optimization problem:**

$$
\min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \frac{1}{2} \lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert^2 \quad \text{subject to} \quad y_i (\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1
$$

The factor $\tfrac{1}{2}$ is purely for analytical convenience (clean derivative).

5. **Solve via Lagrange multipliers:** (Detailed derivation in Section 3.)

6. **Identify support vectors:** Points for which $y_i (\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) = 1$ — these lie exactly on the margin boundary.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Why Behind Each Step**
> - We minimize $\tfrac{1}{2}\lVert\mathbf{w}\rVert^2$ because minimizing the norm is **equivalent** to maximizing the margin $\dfrac{2}{\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert}$ — but a quadratic objective is convex and admits a unique global solution.
> - The constraint $\geq 1$ enforces that every point is on the **correct side** with **at least unit functional margin**.
> - Only support vectors have $\alpha_i > 0$; all others have $\alpha_i = 0$ — making SVM a **sparse** model.

---

## 2.2 The Soft-Margin SVM (Non-Separable Case)

Real-world data is rarely perfectly separable. Cortes & Vapnik (1995) introduced **slack variables** $\xi_i \geq 0$ to allow margin violations.

$$
y_i (\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \quad \xi_i \geq 0
$$

**Primal problem (soft margin):**

$$
\min_{\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\xi}} \frac{1}{2}\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i
$$

$$
\text{subject to} \quad y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \quad \xi_i \geq 0
$$

> [!TIP]
> **The Penalty Parameter $C$:**
> - **Large $C$** → Heavy penalty on misclassification → Narrower margin, fewer violations (risk of overfitting).
> - **Small $C$** → Lenient penalty → Wider margin, more violations allowed (better generalization).
> - $C$ acts like the inverse of a regularization strength $\lambda$ in ridge regression.

---

## 2.3 The Kernel Trick — Non-Linear SVM

When data is not linearly separable, we map inputs to a higher-dimensional space via a feature map $\phi : \mathbb{R}^d \to \mathbb{R}^D$ (where $D \gg d$), then solve the linear SVM in that space.

The dual formulation depends only on inner products $\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j$. Replace them with a **kernel**:

$$
\mathcal{K}(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^\top \phi(\mathbf{x}_j)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mercer's Theorem (Kernel Validity):**
> A function $\mathcal{K}(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ is a valid (positive semi-definite) kernel if and only if the **Gram matrix** $\mathbf{K}_{ij} = \mathcal{K}(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ is symmetric and positive semi-definite for **all** input sets.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam-Critical Formulas — Memorize & Practice**

| Concept | Mathematical Expression | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|
| Decision Hyperplane | $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = 0$ | $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^d,\; b \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| Hard-Margin Constraint | $y_i (\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1$ | For all $i = 1, \dots, N$ |
| Functional Margin | $\hat{\gamma}_i = y_i (\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b)$ | Scalar (no units) |
| Geometric Margin | $\gamma_i = \dfrac{\hat{\gamma}_i}{\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert}$ | True Euclidean distance |
| Total Margin Width | $\dfrac{2}{\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert}$ | Maximized during training |
| Hard-Margin Primal | $\min \tfrac{1}{2}\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert^2$ s.t. $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1$ | Convex QP |
| Soft-Margin Primal | $\min \tfrac{1}{2}\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert^2 + C \sum \xi_i$ | $\xi_i \geq 0$ slack variables |
| Dual Objective (Max) | $\sum \alpha_i - \tfrac{1}{2}\sum\sum \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \, \mathcal{K}(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ | $\alpha_i \geq 0$ |
| Dual Constraints | $\sum \alpha_i y_i = 0,\; 0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$ (soft) | Box constraint |
| Weight Recovery | $\mathbf{w} = \sum \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i$ | Sum over support vectors only |
| Decision Function | $f(\mathbf{x}) = \text{sign}\!\left(\sum \alpha_i y_i \mathcal{K}(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}) + b\right)$ | Used for prediction |
| Linear Kernel | $\mathcal{K}(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j$ | Default |
| Polynomial Kernel | $\mathcal{K}(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = (\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j + c)^p$ | Degree $p$, bias $c$ |
| RBF (Gaussian) Kernel | $\mathcal{K}(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \exp\!\left(-\gamma \lVert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \rVert^2\right)$ | $\gamma > 0$ |
| Sigmoid Kernel | $\mathcal{K}(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \tanh(\kappa \mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j + \theta)$ | Not always PSD |
| KKT Complementarity | $\alpha_i \big[y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1\big] = 0$ | Defines support vectors |

---

## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Bioinformatics:** Microarray cancer classification (gene expression data with $p \gg n$).
- **Computer Vision:** Face detection (e.g., classical Viola–Jones used SVMs), pedestrian detection.
- **NLP:** Text categorization, sentiment analysis (linear SVM with TF-IDF).
- **Finance:** Credit scoring, fraud detection (RBF kernel for non-linear boundaries).
- **Geophysics:** Seismic event classification.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Primal → Dual Optimization Problem (Hard Margin)

We start from the **hard-margin primal**:

$$
\min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \frac{1}{2}\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert^2 \quad \text{subject to} \quad y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1, \quad i = 1, \dots, N
$$

**Step 1 — Form the Lagrangian.** Introduce non-negative Lagrange multipliers $\alpha_i \geq 0$ for each constraint:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\alpha}) = \frac{1}{2}\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert^2 - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \Big[ y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1 \Big]
$$

**Step 2 — Take partial derivatives and set to zero** (stationarity conditions).

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = \mathbf{w} - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \mathbf{w} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back** to eliminate primal variables $\mathbf{w}$ and $b$:

$$
\mathcal{L}_D(\boldsymbol{\alpha}) = \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{w} - \sum_i \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i - b\sum_i \alpha_i y_i + \sum_i \alpha_i
$$

Using $\sum \alpha_i y_i = 0$, the $b$-term vanishes:

$$
\mathcal{L}_D(\boldsymbol{\alpha}) = \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{w} - \sum_i \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + \sum_i \alpha_i
$$

Substitute $\mathbf{w} = \sum_j \alpha_j y_j \mathbf{x}_j$:

$$
\mathcal{L}_D = \frac{1}{2}\left(\sum_i \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i\right)^\top \left(\sum_j \alpha_j y_j \mathbf{x}_j\right) - \sum_i \alpha_i y_i \left(\sum_j \alpha_j y_j \mathbf{x}_j\right)^\top \mathbf{x}_i + \sum_i \alpha_i
$$

Combining the two dot-product terms (they are equal):

$$
\mathcal{L}_D = -\frac{1}{2}\sum_i \sum_j \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \, \mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j + \sum_i \alpha_i
$$

**Step 4 — Write the Wolfe dual (maximize, since primal is a min problem):**

$$
\boxed{\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \;\; \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j \, y_i y_j \, \mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j}
$$

$$
\text{subject to} \quad \alpha_i \geq 0, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) conditions.** For optimality:

$$
\alpha_i \big[ y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1 \big] = 0 \quad \text{(complementary slackness)}
$$

**Step 6 — Identify support vectors.** Any point $i$ with $\alpha_i > 0$ MUST satisfy $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) = 1$ — these lie **exactly on the margin boundaries** and are the **support vectors**. All other points have $\alpha_i = 0$ and play no role in defining the hyperplane.

**Step 7 — Recover bias $b$.** Pick any support vector $\mathbf{x}_k$ with $\alpha_k > 0$:

$$
b = y_k - \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_k = y_k - \sum_{i \in SV} \alpha_i y_i \, \mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_k
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For Soft-Margin SVM**, the only change is the box constraint on dual variables: $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$. Points with $0 < \alpha_i < C$ are **margin support vectors** (on the boundary); points with $\alpha_i = C$ are **non-margin support vectors** (inside the margin or misclassified).

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — 2D Linear SVM

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** Find the optimal separating hyperplane and margin width for the following data:

| Point | Coordinates $\mathbf{x}_i$ | Class $y_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| $P_1$ | $(3,\,3)$ | $+1$ |
| $P_2$ | $(3,\,-3)$ | $+1$ |
| $N_1$ | $(1,\,0)$ | $-1$ |
| $N_2$ | $(-1,\,0)$ | $-1$ |

(Adapted from a standard convex optimization example; all units dimensionless.)

**Step 1 — By inspection**, the data is symmetric about the $x_2$-axis. The separating hyperplane must be **vertical**: $x_1 = \text{const}$. So the weight vector is:

$$
\mathbf{w} = (w_1,\, 0)^\top
$$

**Step 2 — Write the hyperplane equation:** $w_1 x_1 + b = 0$.

**Step 3 — Apply constraints** for each point:

For $P_1 = (3, 3),\ y = +1$:

$$
3 w_1 + b \geq 1
$$

For $N_1 = (1, 0),\ y = -1$:

$$
-(w_1 + b) \geq 1 \;\Rightarrow\; -w_1 - b \geq 1
$$

**Step 4 — At optimum**, the closest points of each class will lie exactly on the margin (support vectors). The closest positive points are $P_1, P_2$ at $x_1 = 3$. The closest negative point is $N_1$ at $x_1 = 1$. So:

$$
3 w_1 + b = 1 \quad \text{and} \quad w_1 + b = -1
$$

**Step 5 — Solve the linear system.** Subtract the second equation from the first:

$$
2 w_1 = 2 \;\Rightarrow\; w_1 = 1
$$

Substitute back: $1 + b = -1 \;\Rightarrow\; b = -2$.

**Step 6 — Hyperplane and margin.**

$$
\boxed{\text{Hyperplane: } x_1 - 2 = 0 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; x_1 = 2}
$$

Margin boundaries: $x_1 = 1$ (negative side) and $x_1 = 3$ (positive side).

Margin width:

$$
\text{Margin} = \frac{2}{\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{1^2 + 0^2}} = 2
$$

**Step 7 — Identify support vectors:** $P_1, P_2$ (positive side) and $N_1$ (negative side). $N_2 = (-1, 0)$ lies **far** from the margin and has $\alpha = 0$ — it does not influence the hyperplane.

**Step 8 — Decision function** for a new test point $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2)$:

$$
f(\mathbf{x}) = \text{sign}\big(x_1 - 2\big)
$$

$$
\text{Predict } +1 \text{ if } x_1 > 2,\;\; -1 \text{ if } x_1 < 2.
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Verification of Optimality:** Since the data has unit-distance symmetry between the two support-vector families ($x_1 = 1$ vs $x_1 = 3$), the margin is balanced and the solution is provably globally optimal (Kuhn–Tucker conditions are satisfied with equality on $P_1, P_2, N_1$ and strict inequality on $N_2$).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Hard vs. Soft Margin vs. Kernel

| Feature | Hard-Margin SVM | Soft-Margin SVM | Kernel SVM |
|---|---|---|---|
| Data assumption | Perfectly separable | Allows violations | Allows non-linear boundaries |
| Slack variables $\xi_i$ | Not used ($= 0$) | Used $\geq 0$ | Used (if non-separable) |
| Penalty term | None | $C \sum \xi_i$ | $C \sum \xi_i$ |
| Dual constraints | $\alpha_i \geq 0$ | $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$ | $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$ |
| Computational complexity | $O(N^2)$–$O(N^3)$ | $O(N^2)$–$O(N^3)$ | $O(N^2)$–$O(N^3)$; storage $O(N^2)$ |
| Hinge loss equivalent | No | Yes: $\sum \max(0, 1 - y_i f_i)$ | Yes (with kernel) |
| Bias–Variance tendency | High variance (overfits if data noisy) | Balanced | Highly flexible |
| KTU Exam Frequency | **Very High** | **Very High** | **Very High** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SVM Architecture — Functional Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Training Data] --> B[Data Preprocessing]
    B --> C{Linearly Separable?}
    C -- YES --> D[Hard Margin Linear SVM]
    C -- NO --> E{Slight Overlap or Noise?}
    E -- YES --> F[Soft Margin Linear SVM with Slack Variables]
    E -- NO --> G[Apply Kernel Trick Mapping to Higher Dimension]
    D --> H[Formulate Primal Quadratic Program]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Solve Dual Problem using Lagrange Multipliers]
    I --> J[Extract Lagrange Multipliers ALPHA]
    J --> K{ALPHA greater than zero}
    K -- YES --> L[Mark as Support Vector]
    K -- NO --> M[Discard from Active Set]
    L --> N[Recover Weight Vector W and Bias B]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Construct Optimal Hyperplane Decision Function]
    O --> P[Predict Class Label for New Test Sample]
```

---

## 4.2 Maximum Margin Geometry — Conceptual Block Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ClassA[Class Plus One Side]
        A1[Support Vector at Margin Boundary H Plus One]
        A2[Non Support Vector with Alpha equals zero]
    end
    subgraph DecisionBoundary[Decision Region]
        D1[Optimal Hyperplane H: w transpose x plus b equals zero]
        D2[Geometric Margin Width equals 2 over norm of w]
    end
    subgraph ClassB[Class Minus One Side]
        B1[Support Vector at Margin Boundary H Minus One]
        B2[Non Support Vector with Alpha equals zero]
    end
    A1 --> D1
    D1 --> B1
    A2 -.ignored.-> D1
    B2 -.ignored.-> D1
    D1 --> D2
```

---

## 4.3 Kernel Trick Conceptual Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Original Input Space with Non Linear Boundary] --> M[Apply Implicit Feature Map PHI]
    M --> H[Higher Dimensional Feature Space with Linear Boundary]
    H --> S[Solve Linear SVM in Lifted Space]
    S --> P[Project Hyperplane Back to Original Space Producing Non Linear Decision Curve]
    K[Kernel Function computes dot product PHI x i and PHI x j directly] -.shortcut.-> S
```

---

## 4.4 SVM Decision Process Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start New Test Sample X] --> S2[Compute Kernel Values with all Support Vectors]
    S2 --> S3[Weighted Sum: sum ALPHA i y i K of x i and x]
    S3 --> S4[Add Bias Term B]
    S4 --> S5[Apply Sign Function]
    S5 --> S6{Output Sign Positive or Negative}
    S6 -- Plus One --> S7[Classify as Positive Class]
    S6 -- Minus One --> S8[Classify as Negative Class]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Define support vectors in an SVM. Explain how they are identified using the KKT conditions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Support vectors** are the training samples that lie **exactly on the margin boundaries** of the separating hyperplane, i.e., those satisfying $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) = 1$.

**Identification via KKT conditions (1 mark):** From the complementary slackness condition:
$$\alpha_i \big[y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1\big] = 0$$
Any point with $\alpha_i > 0$ must satisfy the equality and is therefore a support vector. All other points have $\alpha_i = 0$ and do not influence the hyperplane.

**Significance (1 mark):** They define the optimal hyperplane completely. Removing a non-support vector does not change the solution; moving a support vector does.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**What is the kernel trick in SVM? Write the mathematical forms of the polynomial kernel and the RBF (Gaussian) kernel.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kernel Trick (1.5 marks):** A technique that enables SVM to learn **non-linear decision boundaries** by replacing inner products $\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j$ with a kernel function $\mathcal{K}(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^\top \phi(\mathbf{x}_j)$, which implicitly computes the inner product in a high-dimensional space **without explicitly performing the transformation**.

**Polynomial Kernel (0.75 marks):**
$$\mathcal{K}(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = (\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j + c)^p,\quad p \in \mathbb{N}, \; c \geq 0$$

**RBF / Gaussian Kernel (0.75 marks):**
$$\mathcal{K}(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \exp\!\left(-\gamma \lVert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \rVert^2\right), \quad \gamma > 0$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Formulate the **primal optimization problem** for a linear hard-margin SVM and derive the corresponding **dual optimization problem** using Lagrange multipliers. State the KKT conditions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **soft-margin SVM** formulation. Discuss the role of **slack variables** $\xi_i$ and the **penalty parameter $C$** in controlling bias-variance trade-off. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part A(a) — Primal–Dual Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Primal formulation (2 marks):**

Given training data $(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i),\; y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$, find $\mathbf{w}, b$ to:

$$
\min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \frac{1}{2}\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert^2 \quad \text{subject to} \quad y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1,\; i = 1, \dots, N
$$

This minimizes the squared norm (maximizes the margin) while keeping all points correctly classified with at least unit functional margin.

**Step 2 — Lagrangian (1 mark):**

$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\alpha}) = \frac{1}{2}\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert^2 - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \big[y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1\big], \quad \alpha_i \geq 0
$$

**Step 3 — Stationarity conditions and dual derivation (3 marks):**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \mathbf{w} = \sum_{i} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \sum_{i} \alpha_i y_i = 0
$$

Substituting back yields the **Wolfe dual**:

$$
\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i,j} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \, \mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j
$$

subject to $\alpha_i \geq 0$ and $\sum \alpha_i y_i = 0$.

**Step 4 — KKT conditions (1 mark):**

- Primal feasibility: $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1$
- Dual feasibility: $\alpha_i \geq 0$
- Complementary slackness: $\alpha_i[y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1] = 0$
- Stationarity: as derived above.

---

#### Model Solution to Part A(b) — Soft-Margin SVM (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Motivation (1 mark):** Real data has noise and overlap; hard-margin SVM has no feasible solution when classes overlap.

**Step 2 — Slack variables (2 marks):** Introduce $\xi_i \geq 0$ to relax constraints:

$$
y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \quad \xi_i \geq 0
$$

$\xi_i = 0$ → correctly classified, on or beyond margin.
$0 < \xi_i \leq 1$ → correctly classified but inside the margin.
$\xi_i > 1$ → misclassified.

**Step 3 — Soft-margin primal (2 marks):**

$$
\min_{\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\xi}} \frac{1}{2}\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i
$$

subject to $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i$ and $\xi_i \geq 0$.

**Step 4 — Role of $C$ (2 marks):**

- **Large $C$** → high penalty for violations → narrow margin, low bias, high variance (risk of overfitting).
- **Small $C$** → tolerant of violations → wide margin, high bias, low variance (better generalization on noisy data).
- $C$ is typically chosen via **cross-validation**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **kernel trick** in SVM. Derive the **dual formulation with kernel substitution**. Discuss the **polynomial kernel** and **RBF kernel** with their properties. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss strategies for extending SVM to **multi-class classification**: **one-versus-one (OVO)** and **one-versus-rest (OVR)** approaches. Compare their computational and predictive characteristics. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part B(a) — Kernel Trick (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Motivation (1 mark):** Many real datasets are not linearly separable in the original input space. Mapping to a higher-dimensional feature space via $\phi(\mathbf{x})$ may make them separable — but explicit mapping is computationally expensive or even infinite-dimensional.

**Step 2 — Key observation (1.5 marks):** The dual problem depends on data only through inner products $\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j$. Replace these with the kernel:

$$
\mathcal{K}(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^\top \phi(\mathbf{x}_j)
$$

The kernel computes the inner product in the high-dimensional space **implicitly** without performing the transformation.

**Step 3 — Dual with kernel (1.5 marks):**

$$
\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i,j} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \, \mathcal{K}(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)
$$

Decision function:

$$
f(\mathbf{x}) = \text{sign}\!\left(\sum_{i \in SV} \alpha_i y_i \mathcal{K}(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}) + b\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Polynomial Kernel (1.5 marks):**
$$\mathcal{K}(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = (\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j + c)^p$$
- Captures feature interactions up to degree $p$.
- Has a fixed (finite) dimensional feature space.
- Sensitive to choice of $c$.

**Step 5 — RBF / Gaussian Kernel (1.5 marks):**
$$\mathcal{K}(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \exp\!\left(-\gamma \lVert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \rVert^2\right)$$
- Maps data to an **infinite-dimensional** feature space.
- Universal approximator (with appropriate $\gamma$).
- Most widely used in practice.
- $\gamma$ controls locality: large $\gamma$ → narrow kernel → overfitting; small $\gamma$ → broad kernel → underfitting.

---

#### Model Solution to Part B(b) — Multi-Class SVM (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Why multi-class is needed (0.5 mark):** Binary SVM inherently solves 2-class problems; real tasks often have $K > 2$ classes.

**Step 2 — One-Versus-Rest (OVR) (3 marks):**

- Train $K$ binary SVMs, each separating one class from the remaining $K - 1$ classes.
- For class $k$: $y^{(k)} = +1$ if sample belongs to class $k$, else $-1$.
- Final prediction: assign $\mathbf{x}$ to the class with the **highest decision value**:

$$
\hat{y} = \arg\max_{k \in \{1, \dots, K\}} \big(\mathbf{w}^{(k)\top} \mathbf{x} + b^{(k)}\big)
$$

- **Training cost:** $K$ SVMs on full dataset of size $N$ → total $\sim K \cdot O(N^2)$.
- **Drawback:** Class imbalance; ambiguous regions where multiple classifiers claim the sample.

**Step 3 — One-Versus-One (OVO) (3 marks):**

- Train $\binom{K}{2}$ binary SVMs, one for each pair of classes.
- For pair $(i, j)$: classifier distinguishes only between samples of class $i$ and class $j$.
- Final prediction via **majority voting** (Max-Wins strategy):

$$
\hat{y} = \arg\max_{k} \big(\text{count of votes for class } k\big)
$$

- **Training cost:** $\binom{K}{2}$ SVMs each on a subset → smaller individual problems, but more classifiers overall.
- **Advantage:** Each sub-problem is balanced and smaller; more robust.
- **Drawback:** Higher total training time for large $K$.

**Step 4 — Comparison summary (0.5 mark):** OVO is preferred when $K$ is moderate and individual class sizes are balanced; OVR is simpler but suffers from imbalance and ambiguity.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in SVM Questions — Read Carefully!**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor 1/2** in $\tfrac{1}{2}\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert^2$ — looks cosmetic but examiners deduct marks because it affects the derivative expression during dual derivation.
> 2. **Confusing functional and geometric margin** — they differ by a factor of $\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert$; only the geometric margin is a true distance.
> 3. **Omitting the constraint $\sum \alpha_i y_i = 0$** in the dual — this is **mandatory**, not optional.
> 4. **Box constraint confusion in soft-margin SVM:** dual variable limits become $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$ (not just $\alpha_i \geq 0$). Examiners **explicitly test** this.
> 5. **Writing $\mathcal{K}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x})$ for kernel value:** Kernel is a function of **two** inputs, not one.
> 6. **Kernel not satisfying Mercer's condition:** Always state that the kernel must be **positive semi-definite** for SVM theory to hold.
> 7. **Misclassifying support vectors:** Only points with $\alpha_i > 0$ are support vectors; in soft-margin, those with $\alpha_i = C$ are *interior* support vectors (with violations).
> 8. **Skipping the geometric intuition:** Even in derivation problems, briefly explain *why* we maximize the margin — examiners reward conceptual clarity.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Support Vector Machines**

- ✅ SVM finds the **maximum-margin hyperplane** separating two classes in feature space.
- ✅ **Primal objective:** minimize $\tfrac{1}{2}\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert^2$ subject to $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1$.
- ✅ **Margin width:** $\dfrac{2}{\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert}$; **functional margin:** $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b)$.
- ✅ **Support vectors** are the data points lying on the margin boundary (those with $\alpha_i > 0$).
- ✅ **Weight recovery:** $\mathbf{w} = \sum_{i \in SV} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i$.
- ✅ **Dual formulation** depends on data only through inner products — this enables the **kernel trick**.
- ✅ **KKT conditions** (4 sets): primal feasibility, dual feasibility, complementary slackness, stationarity.
- ✅ **Soft-margin SVM** uses slack variables $\xi_i$ and penalty $C$; dual constraint becomes $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$.
- ✅ **Large $C$** = less tolerance for violations; **small $C$** = wider margin, more tolerance.
- ✅ **Kernel trick:** replace $\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j$ with $\mathcal{K}(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$.
- ✅ Common kernels: **Linear, Polynomial, RBF (Gaussian), Sigmoid.**
- ✅ **RBF kernel** is the most popular; $\gamma$ controls locality.
- ✅ **Mercer's theorem:** a valid kernel produces a positive semi-definite Gram matrix.
- ✅ **Multi-class extensions:** **OVO** ($\binom{K}{2}$ classifiers + voting) and **OVR** ($K$ classifiers + argmax).
- ✅ **Advantages of SVM:** strong theoretical foundation, works well in high dimensions, robust to overfitting on small datasets, only support vectors matter.
- ✅ **Disadvantages:** poor scalability to $N > 10^5$, no direct probabilistic output, sensitive to kernel and hyperparameter choice, struggles with multiclass natively.
- ✅ **Hinge loss equivalence:** Soft-margin SVM objective is equivalent to regularized hinge loss minimization.
- ✅ **VC dimension bound:** explains SVM's excellent generalization even in infinite-dimensional feature spaces.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Clustering Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT524 - DATA MINING AND WAREHOUSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Clustering Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# MODULE 3 — CLUSTERING TECHNIQUES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Clustering

> [!NOTE]
> **Clustering** is an **unsupervised learning** technique in data mining that partitions a set of *n* unlabeled data objects into *k* groups (clusters) such that objects within the same cluster exhibit **high intra-cluster similarity**, while objects in different clusters show **high inter-cluster dissimilarity**.

Mathematically, given a dataset
$$D = \{x_1, x_2, x_3, \ldots, x_n\}$$
where each $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^m$ is an *m*-dimensional feature vector, a clustering algorithm produces *k* partitions
$$C = \{C_1, C_2, C_3, \ldots, C_k\}$$
such that:

$$C_i \neq \emptyset, \quad \forall\, i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, k\}$$

$$\bigcup_{i=1}^{k} C_i = D$$

$$C_i \cap C_j = \emptyset, \quad \forall\, i \neq j$$

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Library Analogy"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Imagine you walk into a chaotic, unsorted library where 10,000 books are piled randomly on the floor. You are asked to **organize** them *without any prior labels or category lists*. You would naturally:
> 1. Pick up a book.
> 2. Compare it with nearby books.
> 3. Place it close to books that "feel" similar (by topic, size, color, author).
> 4. Form distinct shelves of related books.
>
> This is **exactly what clustering does with data points**. There are no "answer keys" (no labels). The algorithm *discovers* the natural groupings on its own.

### 1.3 Clustering vs. Classification

| Property | Clustering | Classification |
|---|---|---|
| Learning Type | **Unsupervised** | Supervised |
| Label Availability | None | Pre-labeled training set |
| Goal | Discover hidden structure | Predict known class |
| Output | Group IDs | Class probabilities |
| Evaluation | Internal metrics (SSE, Silhouette) | Accuracy, Precision, Recall |

### 1.4 Why Clustering is Important in Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Applications** of Clustering:
> - **Customer Segmentation** in retail/Marketing analytics.
> - **Image Segmentation** in Computer Vision and Medical Imaging.
> - **Anomaly/Outlier Detection** in Network Security and IoT sensor streams.
> - **Document/News Grouping** in NLP and Recommendation Systems.
> - **Gene Expression Analysis** in Bioinformatics.
> - **Urban Planning & Geospatial clustering** for traffic and resource allocation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Clustering Algorithms

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Clustering algorithms are broadly classified into **five families**:
> 1. **Partitioning-based** (K-Means, K-Medoids, PAM, CLARA)
> 2. **Hierarchical-based** (Agglomerative, Divisive — BIRCH, CURE)
> 3. **Density-based** (DBSCAN, OPTICS, DENCLUE)
> 4. **Grid-based** (STING, CLIQUE, WaveCluster)
> 5. **Model-based** (EM, Gaussian Mixture Models, SOM)

### 2.2 Distance & Similarity Metrics

The choice of distance metric is **the most critical decision** in clustering.

Let $x = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_m)$ and $y = (y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_m)$ be two *m*-dimensional points.

#### 2.2.1 Euclidean Distance ($L_2$ norm)

$$d(x, y) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{m}(x_i - y_i)^2}$$

- Sensitive to magnitude and outliers.
- Used in K-Means, K-Medoids.

#### 2.2.2 Manhattan Distance ($L_1$ norm)

$$d(x, y) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \vert x_i - y_i \vert$$

- Less sensitive to outliers; suitable for high-dimensional sparse data.

#### 2.2.3 Minkowski Distance ($L_p$ norm)

$$d(x, y) = \left( \sum_{i=1}^{m} \vert x_i - y_i \vert^{p} \right)^{\frac{1}{p}}$$

- Generalization: $p=1$ → Manhattan, $p=2$ → Euclidean, $p=\infty$ → Chebyshev.

#### 2.2.4 Cosine Similarity

$$\cos(\theta) = \frac{x \cdot y}{\Vert x \Vert_2 \, \Vert y \Vert_2} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{m} x_i y_i}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{m} x_i^2} \, \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{m} y_i^2}}$$

- Ignores magnitude; ideal for **text/document clustering** and sparse vectors.

### 2.3 K-Means Clustering — Deep Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal of K-Means:** Minimize the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS / SSE)**.

The objective function is:

$$J = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{x \in C_i} \Vert x - \mu_i \Vert_2^2$$

where $\mu_i$ is the **centroid** of cluster $C_i$:

$$\mu_i = \frac{1}{\vert C_i \vert} \sum_{x \in C_i} x$$

#### KTU Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Use / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Centroid (mean) | $\mu_i = \frac{1}{\vert C_i \vert} \sum_{x \in C_i} x$ | K-Means representative |
| 2 | Medoid | $m_i = \arg\min_{x \in C_i} \sum_{y \in C_i} d(x, y)$ | K-Medoids representative (real point) |
| 3 | WCSS (SSE) | $J = \sum_{i=1}^{k}\sum_{x \in C_i} \Vert x-\mu_i \Vert^2$ | Internal evaluation metric |
| 4 | Euclidean dist. | $d(x,y) = \sqrt{\sum_i (x_i-y_i)^2}$ | Default in K-Means |
| 5 | Cosine sim. | $\cos\theta = \frac{x \cdot y}{\Vert x\Vert \Vert y\Vert}$ | Range $[-1, 1]$ |
| 6 | Elbow point | $k^* = \arg\min_k \left( J(k) \right)$ subject to diminishing returns | Choose optimal *k* |
| 7 | Silhouette coeff. | $s(i) = \frac{b(i)-a(i)}{\max(a(i), b(i))}$ | $s \in [-1, 1]$, higher is better |
| 8 | DBSCAN $\epsilon$-neighborhood | $N_{\epsilon}(p) = \{ q \in D \mid d(p,q) \leq \epsilon \}$ | Density-reachability basis |
| 9 | Single linkage | $d(C_a, C_b) = \min_{x \in C_a, y \in C_b} d(x, y)$ | Hierarchical — chain-like clusters |
| 10 | Complete linkage | $d(C_a, C_b) = \max_{x \in C_a, y \in C_b} d(x, y)$ | Hierarchical — compact clusters |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Facts (Frequently Asked):**
> - K-Means is **sensitive to outliers** because the centroid is a *mean*.
