> - K-Medoids is **robust to outliers** because the medoid is an *actual data point*.
> - The K-Means objective $J$ is **NP-hard** to minimize globally; the Lloyd's algorithm uses an *iterative local-minimization* heuristic.
> - K-Means **always converges** but may converge to a *local minimum*.

## 2.4 Hierarchical Clustering — Linkage Criteria

| Linkage | Formula | Behaviour | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Single | $\min_{x\in C_i, y\in C_j} d(x,y)$ | Tends to form long, chain-like clusters | Non-elliptical shapes |
| Complete | $\max_{x\in C_i, y\in C_j} d(x,y)$ | Forms compact, spherical clusters | Balanced groups |
| Average | $\frac{1}{\vert C_i \vert \vert C_j \vert}\sum\sum d(x,y)$ | Compromise between single & complete | General purpose |
| Ward's | $\Delta(A\cup B) = \frac{\vert A \vert \vert B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert} \Vert \mu_A - \mu_B \Vert^2$ | Minimizes total within-cluster variance | Numerical, balanced data |

## 2.5 DBSCAN — Density-Based Spatial Clustering

> [!NOTE]
> DBSCAN requires **two parameters**: $\epsilon$ (neighborhood radius) and **MinPts** (minimum points to form a dense region).

A point $p$ is classified as one of three types:

1. **Core Point**: $\vert N_{\epsilon}(p) \vert \geq \text{MinPts}$
2. **Border Point**: $\vert N_{\epsilon}(p) \vert < \text{MinPts}$ but lies within $\epsilon$ of a core point.
3. **Noise Point**: Neither core nor border — an **outlier**.

**Density-Reachability:** $q$ is *directly density-reachable* from $p$ if $q \in N_{\epsilon}(p)$ and $p$ is a core point.
**Density-Connectivity:** $p$ and $q$ are density-connected if there exists a point $o$ such that both are density-reachable from $o$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: K-Means on a 2-D Toy Dataset

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently tests this as a **14-mark derivation problem**. Learn to show the **centroid recalculation** at *each iteration* explicitly.

**Given Data Points** ($n = 6$):
$$D = \{(2,10),\ (2,5),\ (8,4),\ (5,8),\ (7,5),\ (6,4)\}$$

Choose $k = 2$ with initial centroids:
$$\mu_1^{(0)} = (2, 10), \quad \mu_2^{(0)} = (5, 8)$$

### Iteration 1 — Assignment Step

For every point, compute distance to both centroids and assign to the *nearest*.

$$
\begin{aligned}
d\big((2,10), \mu_1\big) &= \sqrt{(2-2)^2 + (10-10)^2} = 0.00 \\
d\big((2,10), \mu_2\big) &= \sqrt{(2-5)^2 + (10-8)^2} = \sqrt{9+4} = 3.61 \\[4pt]
d\big((2,5), \mu_1\big) &= \sqrt{0+25} = 5.00 \\
d\big((2,5), \mu_2\big) &= \sqrt{9+9} = 4.24 \\[4pt]
d\big((8,4), \mu_1\big) &= \sqrt{36+36} = 8.49 \\
d\big((8,4), \mu_2\big) &= \sqrt{9+16} = 5.00 \\[4pt]
d\big((5,8), \mu_1\big) &= \sqrt{9+4} = 3.61 \\
d\big((5,8), \mu_2\big) &= 0.00 \\[4pt]
d\big((7,5), \mu_1\big) &= \sqrt{25+25} = 7.07 \\
d\big((7,5), \mu_2\big) &= \sqrt{4+9} = 3.61 \\[4pt]
d\big((6,4), \mu_1\big) &= \sqrt{16+36} = 7.21 \\
d\big((6,4), \mu_2\big) &= \sqrt{1+16} = 4.12
\end{aligned}
$$

**Assignments after Iteration 1:**
$C_1 = \{(2,10)\}$ ; $\quad C_2 = \{(2,5),\ (5,8),\ (8,4),\ (7,5),\ (6,4)\}$

### Iteration 1 — Update Step (Recompute Centroids)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_1^{(1)} &= (2, 10) \\
\mu_2^{(1)} &= \left( \frac{2+5+8+7+6}{5}, \frac{5+8+4+5+4}{5} \right) = \left( \frac{28}{5}, \frac{26}{5} \right) = (5.6, 5.2)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Iteration 2 — Assignment Step

$$
\begin{aligned}
d\big((2,10), \mu_1\big) &= 0 \\
d\big((2,10), \mu_2\big) &= \sqrt{3.6^2 + 4.8^2} = 6.00 \\
&\Rightarrow (2,10) \to C_1 \\[4pt]
d\big((2,5), \mu_1\big) &= 5.00, \quad d\big((2,5), \mu_2\big) = \sqrt{3.6^2+0.2^2} = 3.61 \\
&\Rightarrow (2,5) \to C_2 \\[4pt]
d\big((8,4), \mu_1\big) &= 8.49, \quad d\big((8,4), \mu_2\big) = \sqrt{2.4^2+1.2^2} = 2.68 \\
&\Rightarrow (8,4) \to C_2 \\[4pt]
d\big((5,8), \mu_1\big) &= 3.61, \quad d\big((5,8), \mu_2\big) = \sqrt{0.6^2+2.8^2} = 2.86 \\
&\Rightarrow (5,8) \to C_2 \\[4pt]
d\big((7,5), \mu_1\big) &= 7.07, \quad d\big((7,5), \mu_2\big) = \sqrt{1.4^2+0.2^2} = 1.41 \\
&\Rightarrow (7,5) \to C_2 \\[4pt]
d\big((6,4), \mu_1\big) &= 7.21, \quad d\big((6,4), \mu_2\big) = \sqrt{0.4^2+1.2^2} = 1.28 \\
&\Rightarrow (6,4) \to C_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Assignments after Iteration 2:** $C_1 = \{(2,10)\}$, $C_2$ contains the remaining 5 points. Centroids are identical to Iteration 1 → **Algorithm has converged**.

### Final SSE Computation

$$
\begin{aligned}
J &= \sum_{x \in C_1} \Vert x - \mu_1 \Vert^2 + \sum_{x \in C_2} \Vert x - \mu_2 \Vert^2 \\
&= 0 + \big[3.61^2 + 2.86^2 + 2.68^2 + 1.41^2 + 1.28^2\big] \\
&= 0 + \big[13.03 + 8.18 + 7.18 + 1.99 + 1.64\big] \\
&= 32.02
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the K-Means Convergence Guarantee

> [!NOTE]
> The Lloyd's algorithm for K-Means **always converges in finite steps** because the objective $J$ is monotonically non-increasing at each iteration.

**Proof Sketch:**

1. **Assignment Step:** Fix centroids $\{\mu_i\}$. Assign each $x$ to the cluster $C_i^*$ that minimizes $\Vert x - \mu_i \Vert^2$. This *minimizes* $J$ over assignments.
2. **Update Step:** Fix assignments $\{C_i\}$. The centroid that minimizes $\sum_{x \in C_i}\Vert x - c\Vert^2$ is the **mean** $\mu_i = \frac{1}{\vert C_i \vert}\sum x$. (This is the standard least-squares result obtained by setting $\partial J / \partial c = 0$.)
3. Since $J$ is bounded below by $0$ and the number of possible assignments is finite, $J$ must converge. $\blacksquare$

**Caveat (KTU Pitfall):** Convergence is to a *local minimum*, not necessarily the *global minimum*. The solution depends on the initial centroid selection.

## 3.3 Derivation of Ward's Linkage

The Ward criterion merges the two clusters $A, B$ that produce the *minimum increase* in total SSE:

$$
\Delta(A, B) = \frac{\vert A \vert \cdot \vert B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert} \, \Vert \mu_A - \mu_B \Vert^2
$$

This is derived from the *Lance–Williams recurrence* and represents the loss of information when merging $A$ and $B$ into a single cluster.

## 3.4 Silhouette Coefficient — Full Derivation

For each data point $i$:

- $a(i)$ = average distance from $i$ to all other points in the **same** cluster.
- $b(i)$ = minimum over all other clusters of the average distance from $i$ to points in that cluster.

$$
s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i),\, b(i)\}}
$$

- $s(i) \approx 1$ → point is well-clustered.
- $s(i) \approx 0$ → point lies on the border between two clusters.
- $s(i) < 0$ → point may have been assigned to the wrong cluster.

The **average silhouette score** $\bar{S} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} s(i)$ is a global measure of clustering quality.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Taxonomy of Clustering Algorithms

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Clustering Techniques] --> B[Partitioning Based]
    A --> C[Hierarchical Based]
    A --> D[Density Based]
    A --> E[Grid Based]
    A --> F[Model Based]

    B --> B1[K Means]
    B --> B2[K Medoids]
    B --> B3[PAM and CLARA]

    C --> C1[Agglomerative]
    C --> C2[Divisive]
    C1 --> C1a[Single Linkage]
    C1 --> C1b[Complete Linkage]
    C1 --> C1c[Average Linkage]
    C1 --> C1d[Wards Linkage]

    D --> D1[DBSCAN]
    D --> D2[OPTICS]
    D --> D3[DENCLUE]

    E --> E1[STING]
    E --> E2[CLIQUE]
    E --> E3[WaveCluster]

    F --> F1[Expectation Maximization]
    F --> F2[Gaussian Mixture Models]
    F --> F3[Self Organizing Maps]
```

## 4.2 K-Means Algorithm — Block-Level Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Input dataset D and choose k] --> S2[Step 2: Initialize k centroids randomly or via K Means plus plus]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: ASSIGNMENT step - For each data point compute distance to every centroid]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Assign point to cluster of nearest centroid]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: UPDATE step - Recompute each centroid as the mean of its assigned points]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Check CONVERGENCE - Have centroids stopped moving or J stopped changing]
    S6 --> S7{Converged?}
    S7 -- No --> S3
    S7 -- Yes --> S8[Step 7: Output final clusters and centroids]
```

## 4.3 DBSCAN Density Reachability Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Point P0]:::core
    P1[Point P1]:::core
    P2[Point P2]:::core
    P3[Point P3]:::border
    P4[Point P4]:::noise
    P5[Point P5]:::border
    P6[Point P6]:::core
    P7[Point P7]:::core
    P8[Point P8]:::noise

    P0 --- P1
    P0 --- P2
    P1 --- P2
    P1 --- P3
    P6 --- P7
    P6 --- P5
    P0 --- P6

    classDef core fill:#7AC74F,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef border fill:#FFD966,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef noise fill:#E06666,stroke:#333,color:#FFF
```

**Legend:**
- **Green** = Core points ($\geq \text{MinPts}$ in their $\epsilon$-neighborhood).
- **Yellow** = Border points (within $\epsilon$ of a core point but not dense themselves).
- **Red** = Noise / Outlier points.

## 4.4 Agglomerative Hierarchical Clustering — Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H1[Step 1: Start with n singleton clusters one per point] --> H2[Step 2: Compute pairwise distance matrix n by n]
    H2 --> H3[Step 3: Merge the two closest clusters using the chosen linkage criterion]
    H3 --> H4[Step 4: Update the distance matrix using the Lance Williams formula]
    H4 --> H5[Step 5: Repeat Steps 3 and 4 until a single cluster remains or k clusters are reached]
    H5 --> H6[Step 6: Cut the dendrogram at the desired similarity level to obtain final clusters]
```

## 4.5 K-Medoids (PAM) vs K-Means — Functional Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[K Means] --> A1[Centroid is the mean of all points in cluster]
    A1 --> A2[Sensitive to outliers and noise]
    A2 --> A3[Works only on numerical data]

    B[K Medoids] --> B1[Medoid is the most centrally located actual data point]
    B1 --> B2[Robust to outliers and noise]
    B2 --> B3[Can work with arbitrary distance metrics]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define clustering. List and briefly explain the major categories of clustering methods.**  `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Clustering** is the process of organizing a collection of unlabeled data points into groups (clusters) such that intra-cluster similarity is maximized and inter-cluster similarity is minimized.

**Major Categories:**
1. **Partitioning-based** — Divides data into *k* partitions (e.g., K-Means, K-Medoids). Each partition must contain at least one point.
2. **Hierarchical-based** — Produces a nested tree (dendrogram) of clusters. Two sub-types: **agglomerative** (bottom-up) and **divisive** (top-down).
3. **Density-based** — Forms clusters as dense regions separated by sparse regions. Handles arbitrary shapes and noise (e.g., DBSCAN, OPTICS).
4. **Grid-based** — Quantizes the space into a finite cell grid and performs clustering on the grid (e.g., STING, CLIQUE).
5. **Model-based** — Assumes a generative statistical model and uses EM to fit it (e.g., Gaussian Mixture Models).

### **Q2. What is the difference between a centroid and a medoid? Why is K-Medoids more robust to outliers than K-Means?**  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A **centroid** is the *mean vector* of all points in a cluster, which may or may not correspond to an actual data point. A **medoid** is the *most centrally located actual data point* in the cluster, i.e., the point that minimizes the total distance to all other points in the same cluster.

**Why K-Medoids is more robust:**
- A centroid is the arithmetic *mean* — a single extreme outlier can **drastically shift** the centroid.
- A medoid is always a *real data point*, so an outlier cannot "move" it; an outlier remains an outlier in the periphery.
- Consequently, K-Medoids produces **stable clusters** in noisy data, whereas K-Means can be severely distorted.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Do NOT skip the distance computation table** in K-Means problems. Showing every $d(x_i, \mu_j)$ is mandatory for full marks.
> - Always **show two iterations minimum** and explicitly state **"Algorithm has converged because centroids are unchanged"**.
> - For DBSCAN questions, **students confuse core, border, and noise points**. Memorize the definitions of MinPts and $\epsilon$ cold.
> - For hierarchical clustering, the **dendrogram cut** determines the final *k* — do not pre-decide *k* unless asked.
> - In formula derivations, **write $\Vert x - \mu \Vert^2$, not $d$ alone**; the squared form is what the objective function uses.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — K-Means Deep Dive**  `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **K-Means clustering algorithm** with its objective function. Show that the K-Means objective $J = \sum_{i=1}^{k}\sum_{x \in C_i} \Vert x - \mu_i \Vert^2$ is minimized when each centroid is the **mean** of its cluster points. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**K-Means Algorithm Steps:**
1. Choose $k$ and initialize $k$ centroids.
2. **Repeat** until convergence:
   - **Assignment:** For each $x \in D$, assign $x$ to the cluster with the nearest centroid.
   - **Update:** Recompute each centroid as the mean of points in its cluster.

**Proof that the mean minimizes within-cluster SSE:**

Consider cluster $C_i$ with $n_i$ points. The contribution to $J$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
J_i &= \sum_{x \in C_i} \Vert x - c \Vert^2 = \sum_{x \in C_i} (x - c)^T(x - c)
\end{aligned}
$$

Take the derivative with respect to $c$ and set to zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial J_i}{\partial c} &= -2 \sum_{x \in C_i}(x - c) = 0 \\
\Rightarrow \sum_{x \in C_i} x &= n_i \cdot c \\
\Rightarrow c &= \frac{1}{n_i} \sum_{x \in C_i} x = \mu_i \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

The second derivative $\partial^2 J_i / \partial c^2 = 2 n_i I > 0$ confirms a minimum.

**(b)** Apply the **K-Means algorithm** to the dataset $D = \{(2,10), (2,5), (8,4), (5,8), (7,5), (6,4)\}$ with $k=2$ and initial centroids $\mu_1 = (2,10)$, $\mu_2 = (5,8)$. Show all iterations until convergence and compute the **final SSE**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The complete worked solution is presented in **Section 3.1** of these notes. Key results to write in the exam:

| Iter | $C_1$ | $C_2$ | $\mu_1$ | $\mu_2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | — | — | (2, 10) | (5, 8) |
| 1 | $\{(2,10)\}$ | $\{(2,5), (5,8), (8,4), (7,5), (6,4)\}$ | (2, 10) | (5.6, 5.2) |
| 2 | $\{(2,10)\}$ | $\{(2,5), (5,8), (8,4), (7,5), (6,4)\}$ | (2, 10) | (5.6, 5.2) |

**Convergence:** Centroids unchanged after Iteration 2.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SSE} &= 0 + \big[3.61^2 + 2.86^2 + 2.68^2 + 1.41^2 + 1.28^2\big] \\
&= 13.03 + 8.18 + 7.18 + 1.99 + 1.64 = \mathbf{32.02}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Hierarchical & Density-Based Clustering**  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **agglomerative hierarchical clustering** algorithm. Compare **single linkage** and **complete linkage** criteria with suitable diagrams (proximity matrices). Illustrate with a small dataset. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Agglomerative Algorithm (Bottom-Up):**
1. Start with $n$ singleton clusters.
2. Compute the $n \times n$ proximity (distance) matrix.
3. **Repeat:**
   - Merge the two closest clusters based on the linkage criterion.
   - Update the proximity matrix using the Lance-Williams formula.
4. Stop when a single cluster remains (or $k$ clusters are desired).

**Single Linkage:** Distance between clusters = *minimum* distance between any pair of points.
$$d_{\text{single}}(C_i, C_j) = \min_{x \in C_i, y \in C_j} d(x, y)$$

- **Pros:** Can handle non-elliptical shapes.
- **Cons:** Suffers from the *chaining effect* — long, straggly clusters.

**Complete Linkage:** Distance = *maximum* distance between any pair of points.
$$d_{\text{complete}}(C_i, C_j) = \max_{x \in C_i, y \in C_j} d(x, y)$$

- **Pros:** Produces compact, balanced clusters.
- **Cons:** Sensitive to outliers; biased toward spherical clusters.

**Illustration (Proximity Matrix):** Consider 4 points: A=(1,1), B=(1,2), C=(5,4), D=(6,5). Initially each is a singleton. Compute Euclidean distances and iteratively merge.

| Pair | A | B | C | D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 0.00 | 1.00 | 5.00 | 6.40 |
| **B** | 1.00 | 0.00 | 4.47 | 5.83 |
| **C** | 5.00 | 4.47 | 0.00 | 1.41 |
| **D** | 6.40 | 5.83 | 1.41 | 0.00 |

**Merging order (single linkage):** (A,B) → (C,D) → (A,B,C,D).

**(b)** Explain the **DBSCAN algorithm**. Define **core, border, and noise points**. State and explain the algorithm. Mention any **two advantages** of DBSCAN over K-Means. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**DBSCAN (Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise):**

**Parameters:** $\epsilon$ (neighborhood radius), MinPts (minimum number of points in a neighborhood).

**Definitions:**

1. **$\epsilon$-neighborhood of point $p$:** $N_{\epsilon}(p) = \{q \in D \mid d(p, q) \leq \epsilon\}$
2. **Core Point:** $p$ such that $\vert N_{\epsilon}(p) \vert \geq \text{MinPts}$.
3. **Border Point:** Not a core point, but lies within $\epsilon$ of some core point.
4. **Noise Point:** Neither a core nor a border point — an outlier.

**Algorithm:**
1. For each unvisited point $p$, find its $\epsilon$-neighborhood.
2. If $\vert N_{\epsilon}(p) \vert \geq \text{MinPts}$, mark $p$ as a **core** and form a new cluster. Recursively add all density-reachable points.
3. If $p$ is a border point, mark it and move to the next point.
4. If $p$ is **noise**, label it as an outlier.

**Two Advantages of DBSCAN over K-Means:**
1. **No need to pre-specify $k$** — the algorithm discovers the number of clusters automatically based on density.
2. **Can discover clusters of arbitrary shape** (e.g., crescent, ring) and is **robust to outliers/noise**, whereas K-Means assumes spherical clusters and is sensitive to noise.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 3: Clustering Techniques**

- **Clustering = Unsupervised Learning.** No labels used.
- **K-Means** minimizes **WCSS / SSE** $J = \sum_{i}\sum_{x \in C_i}\Vert x - \mu_i \Vert^2$.
- **K-Means Steps:** (i) Choose $k$, (ii) Initialize centroids, (iii) Assign, (iv) Update, (v) Repeat until convergence.
- **K-Means is sensitive to outliers**; **K-Medoids is robust** (uses actual data points as representatives).
- **K-Means++ initialization** chooses the first centroid randomly and subsequent centroids with probability proportional to $D(x)^2$ — better than pure random.
- **Hierarchical Clustering** produces a **dendrogram**. Cut at any level to obtain *k* clusters.
- **Agglomerative (Bottom-Up) vs. Divisive (Top-Down)** — Agglomerative is more common.
- **Linkage Criteria:** Single (min), Complete (max), Average (mean), Ward's (variance increase).
- **DBSCAN Parameters:** $\epsilon$ and MinPts.
- **DBSCAN Point Types:** Core, Border, Noise.
- **DBSCAN Pros:** No need for $k$, finds arbitrary shapes, handles noise.
- **DBSCAN Cons:** Struggles with varying densities, sensitive to parameter choice.
- **Distance Metrics:** Euclidean (default), Manhattan, Minkowski, Cosine (for text).
- **Cosine Similarity** ranges in $[-1, +1]$; **Cosine Distance** = $1 - \cos\theta$.
- **Silhouette Coefficient** $s(i) \in [-1, 1]$: Higher is better; $\bar{S} > 0.5$ is generally considered good.
- **Elbow Method:** Plot $J$ vs. $k$; choose $k$ at the "elbow" where the marginal gain drops sharply.
- **Hierarchical clustering is $O(n^2)$ memory** (distance matrix) — not scalable to huge datasets.
- **K-Means is $O(n \cdot k \cdot I \cdot m)$** where $I$ is iterations and $m$ is dimensionality.
- **Cluster Validity Metrics:** Internal (SSE, Silhouette, Davies-Bouldin Index) vs External (Adjusted Rand Index, Fowlkes-Mallows) vs Relative (Elbow, Gap Statistic).
- **STING (Statistical Information Grid-based)** — multi-resolution grid structure.
- **EM (Expectation-Maximization)** — soft clustering, computes *probabilistic* assignments.
- **BIRCH** uses **CF-Tree (Clustering Feature Tree)** for incremental clustering.
- **CURE** uses multiple representative points to handle outliers and non-spherical shapes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Golden Rule for KTU 2024 Answer Writing:** Always **draw the Mermaid-style tree/flow** (or a hand-drawn equivalent) and the **distance computation table** in K-Means problems. Examiners reward **clarity of presentation** and **explicit convergence statements** with up to 2 extra marks per question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: k-Means
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT524 - DATA MINING AND WAREHOUSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** k-Means

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **k-Means Clustering** is a prototype-based, partitional, unsupervised clustering algorithm that aims to partition a dataset of *n* observations into **k** pre-defined, non-overlapping subgroups (clusters), where each observation belongs to the cluster with the nearest **centroid** (mean of points in that cluster). It is formally known as the **Lloyd's Algorithm** (1957) in its iterative batch form.

The objective is to minimize the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)**, also called the **inertia** or **distortion**, formally expressed as:

$$J = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{x \in C_i} \Vert x - \mu_i \Vert^2$$

where:
- $C_i$ is the $i^{th}$ cluster
- $\mu_i$ is the centroid (mean) of cluster $C_i$
- $\Vert x - \mu_i \Vert^2$ is the squared Euclidean distance between data point *x* and centroid $\mu_i$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sorting Classroom" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> 🏫 **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a school teacher with 30 students standing randomly in a playground. The teacher wants to form **k = 3** teams for a sports event based on their heights. 
> 
> 1. The teacher **picks 3 students at random** to be the "team leaders" (initial centroids).
> 2. Every other student **walks to the leader closest in height** to them (assignment step).
> 3. Once teams are formed, the teacher **recalculates the average height** of each team and places a new flag at that average spot (update step).
> 4. Some students now find that a different team leader is closer — they **switch teams** (re-assignment).
> 5. The teacher repeats this until **no student wants to switch teams anymore** (convergence).
> 
> This is exactly how k-Means works — except the "height" is a multi-dimensional feature vector, and "closeness" is measured by Euclidean distance.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Glossary

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Centroid** | The arithmetic mean of all feature vectors assigned to a cluster; the geometric center of the cluster. |
| **Cluster** | A subset of data points grouped together due to mutual similarity. |
| **Inertia / WCSS** | Sum of squared distances of samples to their closest cluster center. Lower is better. |
| **k** | The number of clusters — a user-defined **hyperparameter**, determined *a priori*. |
| **Convergence** | The state when cluster assignments no longer change between iterations. |
| **Hard Assignment** | Each point belongs to *exactly one* cluster (no probabilistic overlap). |

## 1.4 Why k-Means Matters in KTU Syllabus

> [!IMPORTANT]
> According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** for **PEITT524 — Data Mining and Warehousing**, Module 3 covers **Clustering Analysis**. k-Means is the **flagship algorithm** of this module and forms the foundation for advanced topics like k-Medoids, DBSCAN, and Hierarchical Clustering that appear in Part B 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Algorithmic Intuition — "Why" and "How"

k-Means solves an **NP-hard optimization problem** in its general form, but the iterative greedy heuristic achieves a local optimum efficiently. Let us break it down:

### 🔹 Step 1: Initialization
- **Why?** We need a starting point for the centroids; without it, the algorithm has no reference.
- **How?** Choose *k* initial centroids. Common methods:
  - **Random Initialization** — pick *k* random data points.
  - **Forgy Method** — randomly assign each point to one of *k* clusters, then take the mean.
  - **k-Means++** (Arthur & Vassilvitskii, 2007) — *intelligently* picks initial centroids spread far apart to improve convergence and quality.

### 🔹 Step 2: Assignment Step
- **Why?** Data points must be allocated to the nearest cluster.
- **How?** For each data point $x_j$, compute the Euclidean distance to every centroid $\mu_i$, and assign $x_j$ to the cluster whose centroid is nearest:

$$C^{(t)}(x_j) = \arg\min_{i \in \{1,2,\ldots,k\}} \Vert x_j - \mu_i^{(t)} \Vert^2$$

### 🔹 Step 3: Update Step
- **Why?** The current centroids no longer represent the "true" centers of their new clusters.
- **How?** Recompute each centroid as the **mean of all points assigned to its cluster**:

$$\mu_i^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{\vert C_i \vert} \sum_{x_j \in C_i} x_j$$

where $\vert C_i \vert$ is the cardinality (count) of cluster $C_i$.

### 🔹 Step 4: Convergence Check
- **Why?** To know when to stop iterating.
- **How?** Terminate if any of these conditions hold:
  - Cluster assignments do not change.
  - Centroids stabilize (movement < tolerance $\epsilon$).
  - A maximum iteration count is reached.
  - The WCSS *J* improvement falls below a threshold.

## 2.2 Distance Metrics — The Heart of k-Means

> [!NOTE]
> k-Means is fundamentally a **distance-based** algorithm. The default and most common metric is the **Euclidean Distance** because it is mathematically compatible with the mean-update formula.

| Metric | Formula | When Used |
|--------|---------|-----------|
| **Euclidean (L2)** | $d(x,y) = \sqrt{\sum_{d=1}^{D}(x_d - y_d)^2}$ | Default for continuous numerical data |
| **Squared Euclidean** | $d^2(x,y) = \sum_{d=1}^{D}(x_d - y_d)^2$ | Optimization trick (avoids square root) |
| **Manhattan (L1)** | $d(x,y) = \sum_{d=1}^{D} \vert x_d - y_d \vert$ | Used in **k-Medians** variant, not standard k-Means |
| **Cosine Distance** | $d(x,y) = 1 - \frac{x \cdot y}{\Vert x \Vert \Vert y \Vert}$ | For sparse or high-dimensional text data |

## 2.3 🎯 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Compilation** — Memorize these for KTU ESE 2024 examination.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Notation |
|---|---------|---------|----------|
| 1 | $J = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{x \in C_i} \Vert x - \mu_i \Vert^2$ | Objective function (WCSS) to minimize | $J$ — total inertia |
| 2 | $\mu_i = \frac{1}{n_i} \sum_{x_j \in C_i} x_j$ | Centroid recomputation formula | $n_i$ — size of cluster $C_i$ |
| 3 | $d(x_j, \mu_i) = \sqrt{\sum_{d=1}^{D}(x_{jd} - \mu_{id})^2}$ | Euclidean distance between point and centroid | $D$ — number of features |
| 4 | $C(x_j) = \arg\min_i \Vert x_j - \mu_i \Vert^2$ | Cluster assignment rule | Returns cluster index |
| 5 | $\text{SSE} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{x \in C_i}(x - \mu_i)^2$ | Sum of Squared Errors (alternate WCSS name) | Same as *J* |
| 6 | $k_{opt} = \arg\min_k \left( J(k) \right)$ subject to elbow rule | Optimal k from Elbow Method | Tradeoff curve |

## 2.4 Methods to Determine Optimal *k*

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently tests how to choose the value of **k**. Three popular methods:

### 2.4.1 The Elbow Method
- Plot WCSS (*J*) vs. *k*. The "elbow" point where the rate of decrease sharply shifts is the optimal *k*.

### 2.4.2 The Silhouette Score
$$s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}$$

- $a(i)$ = mean intra-cluster distance (how similar point *i* is to its own cluster).
- $b(i)$ = mean nearest-cluster distance (how similar point *i* is to the next closest cluster).
- $s(i) \in [-1, +1]$. Values near **+1** indicate well-clustered points.

### 2.4.3 Gap Statistic
- Compares the WCSS of the clustering to its expected value under a null reference distribution.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Field | Application |
|-------|-------------|
| **Image Compression** | Vector quantization — reduces color palette to *k* representative colors |
| **Customer Segmentation** | Marketing teams segment buyers into personas |
| **Anomaly Detection** | Points far from any centroid are flagged as outliers |
| **Document Clustering** | News articles grouped by topic similarity |
| **Sensor Networks** | IoT devices grouped by spatial proximity for routing |
| **Bioinformatics** | Gene expression analysis, protein structure grouping |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — KTU Board Pattern

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **classic KTU 14-mark problem type**. Given a small dataset, manually execute the k-Means algorithm across all iterations until convergence.

### 📊 Given Dataset (2-D Points, k = 2)

| Point | $x_1$ | $x_2$ |
|-------|-------|-------|
| A | 2 | 10 |
| B | 2 | 5 |
| C | 8 | 4 |
| D | 5 | 8 |
| E | 7 | 5 |
| F | 6 | 4 |

**Initial Centroids (assume Forgy/Manual):**
- $C_1^{(0)}$ = A = (2, 10)
- $C_2^{(0)}$ = F = (6, 4)

### 🔁 Iteration 1

**Step 1.1: Compute Squared Euclidean Distances from each point to both centroids**

For Point A = (2, 10):

$$d^2(A, C_1) = (2-2)^2 + (10-10)^2 = 0 + 0 = 0$$

$$d^2(A, C_2) = (2-6)^2 + (10-4)^2 = 16 + 36 = 52$$

For Point B = (2, 5):

$$d^2(B, C_1) = (2-2)^2 + (5-10)^2 = 0 + 25 = 25$$

$$d^2(B, C_2) = (2-6)^2 + (5-4)^2 = 16 + 1 = 17$$

For Point C = (8, 4):

$$d^2(C, C_1) = (8-2)^2 + (4-10)^2 = 36 + 36 = 72$$

$$d^2(C, C_2) = (8-6)^2 + (4-4)^2 = 4 + 0 = 4$$

For Point D = (5, 8):

$$d^2(D, C_1) = (5-2)^2 + (8-10)^2 = 9 + 4 = 13$$

$$d^2(D, C_2) = (5-6)^2 + (8-4)^2 = 1 + 16 = 17$$

For Point E = (7, 5):

$$d^2(E, C_1) = (7-2)^2 + (5-10)^2 = 25 + 25 = 50$$

$$d^2(E, C_2) = (7-6)^2 + (5-4)^2 = 1 + 1 = 2$$

For Point F = (6, 4):

$$d^2(F, C_1) = (6-2)^2 + (4-10)^2 = 16 + 36 = 52$$

$$d^2(F, C_2) = (6-6)^2 + (4-4)^2 = 0 + 0 = 0$$

**Step 1.2: Distance Matrix Summary**

| Point | $d^2$ to $C_1$ | $d^2$ to $C_2$ | Assigned Cluster |
|-------|---------------|---------------|-----------------|
| A | **0** | 52 | Cluster 1 |
| B | 25 | **17** | Cluster 2 |
| C | 72 | **4** | Cluster 2 |
| D | **13** | 17 | Cluster 1 |
| E | 50 | **2** | Cluster 2 |
| F | 52 | **0** | Cluster 2 |

**Cluster 1** = {A, D}, **Cluster 2** = {B, C, E, F}

**Step 1.3: Recompute Centroids**

For Cluster 1 (points A = (2,10), D = (5,8)):

$$\mu_1^{(1)} = \left( \frac{2+5}{2}, \frac{10+8}{2} \right) = (3.5, 9.0)$$

For Cluster 2 (points B = (2,5), C = (8,4), E = (7,5), F = (6,4)):

$$\mu_2^{(1)} = \left( \frac{2+8+7+6}{4}, \frac{5+4+5+4}{4} \right) = \left( \frac{23}{4}, \frac{18}{4} \right) = (5.75, 4.5)$$

**Step 1.4: Compute WCSS after Iteration 1**

$$J^{(1)} = (0 + 13) + (17 + 4 + 2 + 0) = 13 + 23 = 36$$

### 🔁 Iteration 2

**New centroids:** $C_1 = (3.5, 9.0)$, $C_2 = (5.75, 4.5)$

Recompute all distances (showing the critical changes only):

For Point B = (2, 5):

$$d^2(B, C_1) = (2-3.5)^2 + (5-9)^2 = 2.25 + 16 = 18.25$$

$$d^2(B, C_2) = (2-5.75)^2 + (5-4.5)^2 = 14.0625 + 0.25 = 14.3125 \quad \leftarrow \text{still Cluster 2}$$

For Point D = (5, 8):

$$d^2(D, C_1) = (5-3.5)^2 + (8-9)^2 = 2.25 + 1 = 3.25$$

$$d^2(D, C_2) = (5-5.75)^2 + (8-4.5)^2 = 0.5625 + 12.25 = 12.8125 \quad \leftarrow \text{still Cluster 1}$$

| Point | $d^2$ to $C_1$ | $d^2$ to $C_2$ | Assigned Cluster |
|-------|---------------|---------------|-----------------|
| A | **0.25** | 50.3125 | Cluster 1 |
| B | 18.25 | **14.3125** | Cluster 2 |
| C | 72.5625 | **6.8125** | Cluster 2 |
| D | **3.25** | 12.8125 | Cluster 1 |
| E | 50.25 | **2.0625** | Cluster 2 |
| F | 52.5625 | **0.3125** | Cluster 2 |

**Cluster assignments unchanged → ALGORITHM HAS CONVERGED.**

$$J^{(2)} = (0.25 + 3.25) + (14.3125 + 6.8125 + 2.0625 + 0.3125) = 3.5 + 23.5 = 27.0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Notice:** $J^{(2)} = 27.0 < J^{(1)} = 36.0$ — WCSS has *decreased*, confirming the algorithm's monotonic improvement property.

## 3.2 Mathematical Derivation — Why Mean Minimizes WCSS

This is a critical KTU question: *"Prove that the mean is the best representative (centroid) of a cluster."*

**Claim:** For a fixed set of points in cluster $C_i = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\}$, the point $\mu$ that minimizes $\sum_{j=1}^{n} \Vert x_j - \mu \Vert^2$ is the arithmetic mean.

**Proof:**

$$\text{Let } f(\mu) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \Vert x_j - \mu \Vert^2 = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \sum_{d=1}^{D} (x_{jd} - \mu_d)^2$$

To minimize, take the partial derivative w.r.t. $\mu_d$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{\partial f(\mu)}{\partial \mu_d} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} 2(x_{jd} - \mu_d)(-1) = -2 \sum_{j=1}^{n} (x_{jd} - \mu_d) = 0$$

Solving:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} x_{jd} - n\mu_d = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mu_d = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} x_{jd}$$

This holds for every dimension $d = 1, 2, \ldots, D$, proving:

$$\mu = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} x_j \qquad \blacksquare$$

**Second derivative test (to confirm minimum):**

$$\frac{\partial^2 f(\mu)}{\partial \mu_d^2} = 2n > 0$$

Since the second derivative is positive, $f$ is strictly convex, and the critical point is a **global minimum**. This mathematically justifies the centroid update formula.

## 3.3 Analytical Complexity Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU may ask about time/space complexity. Memorize this table.

| Aspect | Complexity | Explanation |
|--------|-----------|-------------|
| **Time per Iteration** | $O(n \cdot k \cdot D)$ | n points × k centroids × D dimensions |
| **Total Time** | $O(n \cdot k \cdot D \cdot I)$ | I = number of iterations until convergence |
| **Space Complexity** | $O(n \cdot D + k \cdot D)$ | Storage for dataset + centroids |
| **Convergence** | Polynomial (local optimum) | Guaranteed to converge but not to global optimum |
| **Scalability** | Excellent | Linear in *n* — works on millions of points |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — k-Means Algorithm Execution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Load Dataset X of n points] --> B[Choose k and initialize k centroids mu_1 to mu_k]
    B --> C{Iteration Limit Reached?}
    C -- No --> D[Assignment Step: For each point x_j compute d squared x_j to mu_i]
    D --> E[Assign x_j to nearest cluster C_i]
    E --> F[Update Step: Recompute mu_i as mean of points in C_i]
    F --> G{Convergence Achieved?}
    G -- No --> C
    G -- Yes --> H[Output Final Clusters and Centroids]
    C -- Yes --> H
    H --> I[END]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Input[Input Dataset X] --> Pre[Preprocessing: Normalization and Scaling]
    Pre --> Init[Initialization Module: k-Means Plus Plus or Random]
    Init --> Assign[Assignment Engine: Distance Computation]
    Assign --> Cluster[Cluster Membership Table]
    Cluster --> Update[Centroid Update Module]
    Update --> Check{Convergence Check}
    Check -- Not Converged --> Assign
    Check -- Converged --> Out[Output: Final k Clusters]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Iterative Cluster Evolution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0[Iteration 0: Two random centroids placed] --> R1[Iteration 1: Points split by nearest centroid, centroids shift toward true cluster centers]
    R1 --> R2[Iteration 2: Refined boundary between clusters, centroids move further to equilibrium]
    R2 --> R3[Iteration 3: Centroids stabilize, no reassignments, algorithm terminates]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid — Elbow Method Visualization (Conceptual Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Compute WCSS for k equals 1] --> B[Compute WCSS for k equals 2]
    B --> C[Compute WCSS for k equals 3]
    C --> D[Compute WCSS for k equals 4]
    D --> E[Compute WCSS for k equals 5]
    E --> F[Plot WCSS versus k curve]
    F --> G[Locate the Elbow point - optimal k]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define k-Means clustering. Write its objective function.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **k-Means clustering** is a partitional clustering technique that partitions *n* data points into *k* non-overlapping clusters, where each point belongs to the cluster with the nearest centroid. The **objective function** (WCSS) is:
> 
> $$J = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{x \in C_i} \Vert x - \mu_i \Vert^2$$
> 
> where $\mu_i$ is the centroid of cluster $C_i$. The algorithm iteratively minimizes *J* using two alternating steps: **assignment** and **update**. *(3 marks: Definition 1 + Formula 1 + Brief explanation 1)*

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List any two limitations of the k-Means algorithm.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> 1. **Sensitivity to Initialization** — Different initial centroid choices can lead to different final clusters and suboptimal local minima.
> 2. **Requirement to Pre-specify k** — The number of clusters must be fixed before execution; poor choice of *k* degrades quality.
> 3. **Sensitivity to Outliers** — Outliers heavily distort the mean and hence the centroid position. *(3 marks: Any two with 1.5 each)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### 📝 Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

**Q. (a)** Explain the k-Means clustering algorithm with a neat flowchart. Discuss the role of the objective function in clustering. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**k-Means Algorithm Steps:**

1. **Input:** Dataset $X = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\}$ and desired number of clusters *k*.
2. **Initialize** *k* centroids $\{\mu_1, \mu_2, \ldots, \mu_k\}$ either randomly or using k-Means++.
3. **Repeat** until convergence:
   - **Assignment Step:** For each data point $x_j$, assign it to the cluster with the nearest centroid:
   $$C(x_j) = \arg\min_{i} \Vert x_j - \mu_i \Vert^2$$
   - **Update Step:** Recompute each centroid as the mean of points in its cluster:
   $$\mu_i = \frac{1}{n_i} \sum_{x_j \in C_i} x_j$$
4. **Output:** Final clusters $C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_k$ and their centroids.

**Flowchart:** Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes.

**Role of Objective Function:**
- The objective function $J = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{x \in C_i} \Vert x - \mu_i \Vert^2$ mathematically quantifies cluster compactness.
- k-Means is essentially an **optimization procedure** that minimizes *J* through iterative refinement.
- Lower WCSS indicates tighter, more compact clusters.
- The objective function provides a **stopping criterion** — when *J* stops decreasing significantly, the algorithm has converged.

---

**Q. (b)** Given the following 5 two-dimensional data points, perform k-Means clustering with **k = 2** until convergence. Take initial centroids as P1 and P4. **(7 marks)**

| Point | X | Y |
|-------|-----|-----|
| P1 | 1 | 1 |
| P2 | 2 | 1 |
| P3 | 4 | 3 |
| P4 | 5 | 4 |
| P5 | 3 | 2 |

**Model Solution:**

**Initial centroids:** $\mu_1^{(0)} = (1, 1)$ [P1], $\mu_2^{(0)} = (5, 4)$ [P4]

**Iteration 1 — Distances:**

For P2 = (2, 1):

$$d^2(P2, \mu_1) = (2-1)^2 + (1-1)^2 = 1$$

$$d^2(P2, \mu_2) = (2-5)^2 + (1-4)^2 = 9 + 9 = 18 \quad \rightarrow \text{Cluster 1}$$

For P3 = (4, 3):

$$d^2(P3, \mu_1) = (4-1)^2 + (3-1)^2 = 9 + 4 = 13$$

$$d^2(P3, \mu_2) = (4-5)^2 + (3-4)^2 = 1 + 1 = 2 \quad \rightarrow \text{Cluster 2}$$

For P5 = (3, 2):

$$d^2(P5, \mu_1) = (3-1)^2 + (2-1)^2 = 4 + 1 = 5$$

$$d^2(P5, \mu_2) = (3-5)^2 + (2-4)^2 = 4 + 4 = 8 \quad \rightarrow \text{Cluster 1}$$

**Iteration 1 Result:** Cluster 1 = {P1, P2, P5}, Cluster 2 = {P3, P4}

**Update Centroids:**

$$\mu_1^{(1)} = \left(\frac{1+2+3}{3}, \frac{1+1+2}{3}\right) = (2.0, 1.33)$$

$$\mu_2^{(1)} = \left(\frac{4+5}{2}, \frac{3+4}{2}\right) = (4.5, 3.5)$$

**Iteration 2 — Distances from new centroids:**

For P5 = (3, 2) — the borderline point:

$$d^2(P5, \mu_1) = (3-2)^2 + (2-1.33)^2 = 1 + 0.4489 = 1.4489$$

$$d^2(P5, \mu_2) = (3-4.5)^2 + (2-3.5)^2 = 2.25 + 2.25 = 4.5 \quad \rightarrow \text{Cluster 1 (unchanged)}$$

All other points also retain their assignments → **CONVERGED**.

**Final Clusters:**
- Cluster 1: {P1, P2, P5} with centroid (2.0, 1.33)
- Cluster 2: {P3, P4} with centroid (4.5, 3.5)

**Final WCSS:**

$$J = [(0 + 1 + 1.4489)] + [(2 + 0)] = 2.4489 + 2.0 = 4.4489$$

---

### 📝 Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q. (a)** Explain the **Elbow Method** for determining the optimal number of clusters in k-Means. How does the **Silhouette Score** complement this approach? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Elbow Method:**
- Run k-Means for a range of *k* values (typically $k = 1$ to $k = 10$).
- For each *k*, compute the WCSS:
$$J(k) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{x \in C_i} \Vert x - \mu_i \Vert^2$$
- Plot *J(k)* against *k*. The curve monotonically decreases.
- The **elbow point** — where the rate of decrease sharply transitions — represents the optimal *k*. Beyond this, adding clusters yields diminishing returns.

**Silhouette Score Method:**
- For each point $i$, compute:
  - $a(i)$ = mean distance to all other points in the *same* cluster.
  - $b(i)$ = mean distance to all points in the *nearest* cluster.
- Silhouette coefficient:
$$s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}$$
- Average $s(i)$ over all points gives the overall Silhouette Score.
- Score range: $[-1, +1]$. Higher is better. Choose *k* that maximizes this score.

**Complementary Role:**
- The **Elbow Method** is intuitive but subjective — the elbow is not always visually obvious.
- The **Silhouette Score** provides an objective, quantitative measure of cluster quality.
- Together, they give both *visual intuition* and *numerical validation* for the optimal *k*.

---

**Q. (b)** Discuss the **k-Means++ initialization** strategy. How does it improve over random initialization? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Random Initialization Problem:**
- Random selection may place initial centroids very close to each other.
- This causes slow convergence or convergence to a poor local minimum.
- Different random seeds can produce dramatically different final clusters.

**k-Means++ Algorithm (Arthur & Vassilvitskii, 2007):**

1. Choose the first centroid $\mu_1$ uniformly at random from the dataset.
2. For each remaining data point $x_j$, compute its squared distance $D(x_j)^2$ to the *nearest already-chosen* centroid.
3. Select the next centroid $\mu_i$ with probability proportional to $D(x_j)^2$ — points far from existing centroids are *more likely* to be chosen.
4. Repeat steps 2–3 until *k* centroids are selected.
5. Proceed with standard k-Means.

**Mathematical Intuition:**
- The probability of selecting $x_j$ as the next centroid is:
$$P(x_j) = \frac{D(x_j)^2}{\sum_{m=1}^{n} D(x_m)^2}$$
- This ensures centroids are well-spread across the data space.

**Improvements over Random Initialization:**
- ✅ **Faster convergence** — typically fewer iterations are needed.
- ✅ **Better cluster quality** — provably $O(\log k)$-competitive with the optimal clustering.
- ✅ **More deterministic** — reduces variance in final results.
- ✅ **Mitigates poor local minima** — strategic spread avoids stuck configurations.

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Forgetting to show distance calculations** — Students often write the algorithm in words but skip the actual numerical table. *Always* present a **distance matrix** with the formula and computed values for each point.
> 2. **Not computing the final WCSS** — Even if not asked explicitly, calculating the final *J* value earns extra credit and shows complete understanding.
> 3. **Confusing the iteration count** — KTU values correct identification of *convergence* over arbitrary iteration limits. Clearly state the stopping condition used.
> 4. **Misapplying the centroid formula** — The centroid is the *mean* of points, NOT the *median* or the *midpoint of extremes*. The mean is justified by the WCSS minimization proof.
> 5. **Ignoring scaling** — Real datasets need feature normalization (StandardScaler/MinMax) before k-Means. A complete answer should mention this.

---

## 5.3 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE 2024**

- 🔹 k-Means is a **partitional, prototype-based, unsupervised** clustering algorithm.
- 🔹 **Objective function:** $J = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{x \in C_i} \Vert x - \mu_i \Vert^2$ (WCSS/Inertia).
- 🔹 **Two steps per iteration:** Assignment (assign to nearest centroid) and Update (recompute centroid as mean).
- 🔹 **Centroid formula:** $\mu_i = \frac{1}{n_i} \sum_{x_j \in C_i} x_j$ (proven by setting $\partial J / \partial \mu = 0$).
- 🔹 **Default distance metric:** Squared Euclidean distance (avoids square root for speed).
- 🔹 **Time complexity per iteration:** $O(n \cdot k \cdot D)$.
- 🔹 **Convergence:** Guaranteed to a local minimum (NOT global), in finite iterations.
- 🔹 **Limitations:** Sensitive to initialization, outliers, requires pre-specified *k*, assumes spherical/convex clusters, sensitive to feature scaling.
- 🔹 **k-Means++:** Intelligent initialization that spreads initial centroids for better results.
- 🔹 **Choosing k:** Elbow Method (visual), Silhouette Score (quantitative), Gap Statistic.
- 🔹 **Variants to know:** k-Medoids (uses actual data points as centers), Bisecting k-Means (hierarchical top-down), Mini-Batch k-Means (scalable).
- 🔹 **Algorithm converges monotonically:** $J^{(t+1)} \leq J^{(t)}$ always holds.
- 🔹 **Preprocessing:** Always **normalize/standardize** features before applying k-Means.
- 🔹 **Empty cluster handling:** If a cluster becomes empty, reinitialize its centroid (e.g., to the farthest point from any centroid).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hierarchical Clustering and DBSCAN
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT524 - DATA MINING AND WAREHOUSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hierarchical Clustering and DBSCAN

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Hierarchical Clustering — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hierarchical Clustering** is an unsupervised machine learning technique that builds a hierarchy (tree-like structure) of clusters. Unlike partitioning methods (e.g., *k*-means), it does **not** require the user to pre-specify the number of clusters. The output is a **Dendrogram** — a tree diagram that records the sequence of merges (or splits) and the distance at which each occurred.

Hierarchical clustering comes in two top-down flavors:

1. **Agglomerative (Bottom-Up / AGNES — Agglomerative Nesting)**
   * Starts with every data point as its own singleton cluster.
   * At each step, the two *closest* clusters are merged.
   * Continues until only one cluster (or a stopping criterion) remains.

2. **Divisive (Top-Down / DIANA — Divisive Analysis)**
   * Starts with all points in one single cluster.
   * At each step, the *least cohesive* cluster is split into two.
   * Continues until every point is its own cluster.

> [!NOTE]
> Agglomerative methods are **far more common** in KTU exams and industry because they are computationally simpler and supported by well-defined linkage formulas.

### 1.2 DBSCAN — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DBSCAN (Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise)** is a density-based clustering algorithm proposed by Ester, Kriegel, Sander & Xu (1996). It groups together points that are **closely packed** (high-density regions) and marks points lying alone in **low-density regions** as **outliers (noise)**. DBSCAN does **not** require the number of clusters in advance and can discover clusters of **arbitrary shape**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Analogy 1 — Hierarchical Clustering (Family Tree / Dendrogram)**
Imagine organizing a family reunion. You start with individual cousins. The closest siblings pair up first (smallest distance). Then those small pairs combine with each other (because they share grandparents). Eventually, all the cousins merge into one giant family tree. The "height" at which two branches join tells you how distant their relationship is. That is exactly what a **dendrogram** shows for data.

**Analogy 2 — DBSCAN (Starry Night Sky)**
Imagine a dark night sky. Some stars are clustered tightly together (a constellation — these form a *cluster*). Some stars sit near a constellation but not quite inside it (a *border* star). A few stars are isolated, far from any group — those are **noise points** (anomalies). DBSCAN's job is to identify the constellations, the border stars, and the lone outliers, just by examining how tightly stars are packed.

### 1.4 Key Metrics Used in KTU 2024 Scheme

> [!NOTE]
> The following terms are **standard KTU syllabus jargon** and must be memorized verbatim:
> - **Eps (ε)** — radius of the neighborhood around a point.
> - **MinPts** — minimum number of points required inside an Eps-neighborhood for a point to be considered a *core point*.
> - **Distance Metric** — typically **Euclidean**, but Manhattan/Minkowski are acceptable.
> - **Linkage Criterion** — defines how inter-cluster distance is computed (single, complete, average, Ward's).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Agglomerative Clustering — Operational Logic

The agglomerative algorithm proceeds through the following structured steps:

1. **Initialization**: Treat each of the *n* data points as a separate cluster. Construct an *n × n* symmetric **distance matrix** *D* where $D(i,j)$ is the distance between points $i$ and $j$.
2. **Merge Step**: Find the pair of clusters $(C_i, C_j)$ that are **closest** according to the chosen linkage criterion. Merge them into a new cluster $C_{ij}$.
3. **Update Step**: Recompute the distance from the newly formed cluster $C_{ij}$ to every other cluster $C_k$ using the linkage formula.
4. **Repeat**: Continue steps 2–3 until only one cluster remains, or until the desired number of clusters is reached.
5. **Dendrogram Construction**: Plot each merge as a horizontal line at the height equal to the distance at which it occurred.

### 2.2 Linkage Criteria — The Four Core Methods

Let $C_a$ and $C_b$ be two clusters, $p \in C_a$ and $q \in C_b$. The four primary linkage criteria are:

* **Single Linkage (MIN):** distance between the *closest* pair of points.
* **Complete Linkage (MAX):** distance between the *farthest* pair of points.
* **Average Linkage:** mean distance between all cross-pair points.
* **Ward's Linkage:** minimizes the total within-cluster variance.

### 2.3 DBSCAN — Three Point Classifications

DBSCAN classifies every data point into exactly one of three categories:

1. **Core Point**: A point $p$ is a core point if its **Eps-neighborhood** contains at least **MinPts** points (including $p$ itself).
2. **Border Point**: A point that is *not* a core point, but lies within the Eps-neighborhood of a core point.
3. **Noise Point (Outlier)**: A point that is neither a core point nor a border point.

Two foundational concepts underpin DBSCAN:

* **Directly Density-Reachable**: Point $q$ is directly density-reachable from $p$ if $q \in N_{Eps}(p)$ and $p$ is a core point.
* **Density-Connected**: Points $p$ and $q$ are density-connected if there exists a point $o$ such that both $p$ and $q$ are density-reachable from $o$.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The following table contains every formula you will need to solve KTU 2024 Scheme exam questions on this topic.

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Typical Use |
|---------|---------------------|-------------|
| Euclidean Distance | $d(p,q) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(p_i - q_i)^2}$ | Default metric for both methods |
| Manhattan Distance | $d(p,q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert p_i - q_i \vert$ | Grid/road networks |
| Minkowski Distance | $d(p,q) = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert p_i - q_i \vert^{r}\right)^{1/r}$ | Generalized metric (r = 1, 2, …) |
| Single Linkage | $d(C_a, C_b) = \min_{p \in C_a, q \in C_b} d(p,q)$ | Finds elongated, chain-like clusters |
| Complete Linkage | $d(C_a, C_b) = \max_{p \in C_a, q \in C_b} d(p,q)$ | Finds compact, spherical clusters |
| Average Linkage | $d(C_a, C_b) = \frac{1}{\vert C_a \vert \vert C_b \vert}\sum_{p \in C_a}\sum_{q \in C_b} d(p,q)$ | Balanced trade-off |
| Ward's Linkage | $d(C_a, C_b) = \sqrt{\frac{2 \vert C_a \vert \vert C_b \vert}{\vert C_a \vert + \vert C_b \vert}} \, \Vert \bar{x}_{C_a} - \bar{x}_{C_b} \Vert$ | Minimizes within-cluster variance |
| Eps-Neighborhood | $N_{Eps}(p) = \{ q \in D \mid d(p,q) \le Eps \}$ | DBSCAN neighborhood |
| Core Point | $\vert N_{Eps}(p) \vert \ge MinPts$ | DBSCAN classification |
| Time Complexity (Agglomerative) | $O(n^3)$ naïve, $O(n^2 \log n)$ with heap | Scalability bottleneck |
| Time Complexity (DBSCAN) | $O(n \log n)$ with spatial index | Highly scalable |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

* **Hierarchical Clustering**: Used in **bioinformatics** (gene-expression phylogenetics), **taxonomy** (biological classification), **document clustering** in NLP, and **social network analysis** for community detection at multiple resolutions.
* **DBSCAN**: Used in **anomaly detection** (fraud, network intrusion), **geospatial clustering** (earthquake epicenters, crime hotspots), **image segmentation**, and **sensor-data analytics** in IoT systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Agglomerative Clustering with Single Linkage

**Given:** Five 2-D points

$$P_1 = (2,10), \quad P_2 = (2,5), \quad P_3 = (8,4), \quad P_4 = (5,8), \quad P_5 = (7,5)$$

**Distance Metric:** Euclidean

**Linkage Criterion:** Single Linkage (MIN)

**Step 1 — Compute the Initial Distance Matrix**

Using $d(p,q) = \sqrt{(p_x - q_x)^2 + (p_y - q_y)^2}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(P_1, P_2) &= \sqrt{(2-2)^2 + (10-5)^2} = \sqrt{25} = 5.00 \\
d(P_1, P_3) &= \sqrt{(2-8)^2 + (10-4)^2} = \sqrt{72} = 8.49 \\
d(P_1, P_4) &= \sqrt{(2-5)^2 + (10-8)^2} = \sqrt{13} = 3.61 \\
d(P_1, P_5) &= \sqrt{(2-7)^2 + (10-5)^2} = \sqrt{50} = 7.07 \\
d(P_2, P_3) &= \sqrt{(2-8)^2 + (5-4)^2} = \sqrt{37} = 6.08 \\
d(P_2, P_4) &= \sqrt{(2-5)^2 + (5-8)^2} = \sqrt{18} = 4.24 \\
d(P_2, P_5) &= \sqrt{(2-7)^2 + (5-5)^2} = \sqrt{25} = 5.00 \\
d(P_3, P_4) &= \sqrt{(8-5)^2 + (4-8)^2} = \sqrt{25} = 5.00 \\
d(P_3, P_5) &= \sqrt{(8-7)^2 + (4-5)^2} = \sqrt{2} = 1.41 \\
d(P_4, P_5) &= \sqrt{(5-7)^2 + (8-5)^2} = \sqrt{13} = 3.61 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Initial 5 × 5 Distance Matrix (in the lower triangle):**

| | P1 | P2 | P3 | P4 | P5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| P1 | 0 | 5.00 | 8.49 | 3.61 | 7.07 |
| P2 | | 0 | 6.08 | 4.24 | 5.00 |
| P3 | | | 0 | 5.00 | **1.41** |
| P4 | | | | 0 | 3.61 |
| P5 | | | | | 0 |

**Step 2 — First Merge**
The minimum distance is $d(P_3, P_5) = 1.41$. **Merge $P_3$ and $P_5$ into cluster $C_{35}$** at height **1.41**.

**Step 3 — Update Distance Matrix (Single Linkage Rule)**

For each remaining cluster $C_k$, the distance is:

$$d(C_{35}, C_k) = \min(d(P_3, C_k), \, d(P_5, C_k))$$

Applying this:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(C_{35}, P_1) &= \min(d(P_3, P_1), d(P_5, P_1)) = \min(8.49, 7.07) = 7.07 \\
d(C_{35}, P_2) &= \min(d(P_3, P_2), d(P_5, P_2)) = \min(6.08, 5.00) = 5.00 \\
d(C_{35}, P_4) &= \min(d(P_3, P_4), d(P_5, P_4)) = \min(5.00, 3.61) = 3.61 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Updated 4 × 4 Matrix:**

| | P1 | P2 | P4 | C35 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| P1 | 0 | 5.00 | **3.61** | 7.07 |
| P2 | | 0 | 4.24 | 5.00 |
| P4 | | | 0 | 3.61 |
| C35 | | | | 0 |

**Step 4 — Second Merge**
A tie exists at **3.61** between $(P_1, P_4)$ and $(C_{35}, P_4)$. By KTU convention, we merge the *leftmost-occurring* pair. **Merge $P_1$ and $P_4$ into cluster $C_{14}$** at height **3.61**.

**Step 5 — Update Distance Matrix Again**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(C_{14}, P_2) &= \min(d(P_1, P_2), d(P_4, P_2)) = \min(5.00, 4.24) = 4.24 \\
d(C_{14}, C_{35}) &= \min(d(P_1, C_{35}), d(P_4, C_{35})) = \min(7.07, 3.61) = 3.61 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Updated 3 × 3 Matrix:**

| | P2 | C14 | C35 |
|---|---|---|---|
| P2 | 0 | 4.24 | 5.00 |
| C14 | | 0 | **3.61** |
| C35 | | | 0 |

**Step 6 — Third Merge**
Minimum is $d(C_{14}, C_{35}) = 3.61$. **Merge $C_{14}$ and $C_{35}$ into $C_{1345}$** at height **3.61**.

**Step 7 — Final Merge**
Only two clusters remain. $d(C_{1345}, P_2) = \min(d(P_1, P_2), d(P_4, P_2), d(P_3, P_2), d(P_5, P_2)) = \min(5.00, 4.24, 6.08, 5.00) = 4.24$. **Merge everything into the root at height 4.24.**

### 3.2 Worked Example — DBSCAN Point Classification

**Given:** A 2-D dataset with 8 points, parameters **Eps = 1.5**, **MinPts = 3**.

| Point | (x, y) |
|-------|--------|
| A | (1, 1) |
| B | (1, 2) |
| C | (2, 1) |
| D | (2, 2) |
| E | (8, 8) |
| F | (9, 8) |
| G | (8, 9) |
| H | (15, 15) |

**Step 1 — Compute Eps-Neighborhoods (using Euclidean distance, threshold ≤ 1.5):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N(A) &= \{A, B, C, D\} \Rightarrow |N(A)| = 4 \ge 3 \quad \Rightarrow \textbf{CORE} \\
N(B) &= \{A, B, C, D\} \Rightarrow |N(B)| = 4 \ge 3 \quad \Rightarrow \textbf{CORE} \\
N(C) &= \{A, B, C, D\} \Rightarrow |N(C)| = 4 \ge 3 \quad \Rightarrow \textbf{CORE} \\
N(D) &= \{A, B, C, D\} \Rightarrow |N(D)| = 4 \ge 3 \quad \Rightarrow \textbf{CORE} \\
N(E) &= \{E, F, G\} \Rightarrow |N(E)| = 3 \ge 3 \quad \Rightarrow \textbf{CORE} \\
N(F) &= \{E, F, G\} \Rightarrow |N(F)| = 3 \ge 3 \quad \Rightarrow \textbf{CORE} \\
N(G) &= \{E, F, G\} \Rightarrow |N(G)| = 3 \ge 3 \quad \Rightarrow \textbf{CORE} \\
N(H) &= \{H\} \Rightarrow |N(H)| = 1 < 3 \quad \Rightarrow \textbf{NOISE} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify Border Points**
A border point is a non-core point lying inside a core point's neighborhood. Since all of A–G have $\vert N \vert \ge 3$, there are **no border points** in this dataset.

**Step 3 — Form Clusters via Density-Connectivity**

* **Cluster 1** = {A, B, C, D} — all mutually density-reachable through the chain A → B → D → C.
* **Cluster 2** = {E, F, G} — similar dense triplet.
* **Noise** = {H} — isolated outlier.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Hierarchical Clustering | DBSCAN |
|----------|----------------------|--------|
| Number of Clusters | Not required (cut dendrogram) | Not required |
| Cluster Shape | Tends toward convex | **Arbitrary shapes** |
| Handles Noise | **No** (every point assigned) | **Yes** (explicit noise class) |
| Parameter Sensitivity | Linkage function | **Eps, MinPts** critical |
| Scalability | $O(n^2 \log n)$ to $O(n^3)$ | $O(n \log n)$ |
| Determinism | Deterministic | Deterministic for fixed params |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Agglomerative Clustering Operational Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Load n data points] --> B[Treat each point as a singleton cluster]
    B --> C[Compute initial n x n distance matrix]
    C --> D{Find minimum-distance pair}
    D --> E[Merge pair into new cluster]
    E --> F[Update distance matrix using linkage criterion]
    F --> G{Only 1 cluster remains?}
    G -- No --> D
    G -- Yes --> H[Build Dendrogram from merge history]
    H --> I[Cut dendrogram at desired height k to obtain k clusters]
    I --> J[End]
```

### 4.2 DBSCAN Operational Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Initialize all points as UNVISITED] --> B{Pick an unvisited point p}
    B --> C[Mark p as VISITED]
    C --> D[Compute Eps-neighborhood N of p]
    D --> E{Is N size >= MinPts?}
    E -- No --> F[Mark p as NOISE temporarily]
    E -- Yes --> G[Mark p as CORE point and create new cluster C]
    G --> H[Add all points in N to seed set S of C]
    H --> I{Pop point q from S}
    I --> J{Mark q as VISITED if not already}
    J --> K[Compute N-eps of q]
    K --> L{Is N-eps size >= MinPts?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Add N-eps points to S]
    L -- No --> N[Mark q as BORDER if not core]
    M --> I
    N --> O{Any unvisited points left?}
    F --> O
    O -- Yes --> B
    O -- No --> P[Output: Core, Border, Noise labels per cluster]
    P --> Q[End]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Cluster Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT LAYER]
        D1[Raw Dataset D]
    end

    subgraph HC[HIERARCHICAL CLUSTERING]
        H1[Distance Matrix Builder]
        H2[Linkage Engine: Single/Complete/Average/Ward]
        H3[Dendrogram Generator]
    end

    subgraph DB[DBSCAN ENGINE]
        E1[Eps-Neighborhood Query Module]
        E2[Core Point Classifier]
        E3[Border and Noise Classifier]
        E4[Density Connectivity Graph Builder]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT LAYER]
        O1[Hierarchical Tree Visualization]
        O2[Flat Cluster Assignment with Noise Labels]
    end

    D1 --> H1 --> H2 --> H3 --> O1
    D1 --> E1 --> E2 --> E4 --> O2
    E2 --> E3 --> O2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define hierarchical clustering. Differentiate between agglomerative and divisive hierarchical clustering.*

**Model Answer:**
Hierarchical clustering is an unsupervised technique that organizes data into a tree-like hierarchy of clusters, visualized as a dendrogram, without requiring the number of clusters in advance.

| Feature | Agglomerative | Divisive |
|---------|--------------|----------|
| Direction | Bottom-Up | Top-Down |
| Initial State | Each point is its own cluster | All points in one cluster |
| Operation | Repeatedly merges closest pairs | Repeatedly splits least-cohesive cluster |
| Complexity | Lower | Higher (often exponential) |
| Usage | More common in practice | Less common |

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for comparison table)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List and briefly define the three types of points identified by DBSCAN.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Core Point**: A point whose Eps-neighborhood contains at least MinPts points, i.e., $\vert N_{Eps}(p) \vert \ge MinPts$. It lies in the interior of a dense region.
2. **Border Point**: A point that is not a core point but lies within the Eps-neighborhood of some core point. It forms the outer edge of a cluster.
3. **Noise Point (Outlier)**: A point that is neither a core point nor a border point. It does not belong to any cluster.

*(3 marks: 1 mark per category)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### Question A — Agglomerative Clustering Worked Problem [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**Q. (a)** *Explain the agglomerative hierarchical clustering algorithm in detail. Discuss any four linkage methods with their formulas.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**
Agglomerative clustering follows a bottom-up strategy:

1. Begin with *n* singleton clusters.
2. Build an $n \times n$ distance matrix.
3. Iteratively merge the two closest clusters based on a linkage rule.
4. Update the distance matrix after each merge.
5. Stop when a single cluster remains or the desired cluster count is reached.

**Four Linkage Methods:**

* **Single Linkage (MIN):** $d(C_a, C_b) = \min_{p \in C_a, q \in C_b} d(p,q)$ — favors chain-like clusters but sensitive to noise.
* **Complete Linkage (MAX):** $d(C_a, C_b) = \max_{p \in C_a, q \in C_b} d(p,q)$ — favors compact, equal-sized clusters.
* **Average Linkage:** $d(C_a, C_b) = \frac{1}{\vert C_a \vert \cdot \vert C_b \vert} \sum_{p \in C_a} \sum_{q \in C_b} d(p,q)$ — balanced, less sensitive to outliers.
* **Ward's Linkage:** $d(C_a, C_b) = \sqrt{\frac{2 \vert C_a \vert \vert C_b \vert}{\vert C_a \vert + \vert C_b \vert}} \Vert \bar{x}_a - \bar{x}_b \Vert$ — minimizes total within-cluster SSE.

**Q. (b)** *Apply agglomerative clustering with single linkage to the dataset below using Euclidean distance. Show all intermediate distance matrices and draw the dendrogram.*

| Point | X | Y |
|-------|---|---|
| A | 1 | 1 |
| B | 1 | 4 |
| C | 4 | 1 |
| D | 4 | 4 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Initial Distance Matrix:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(A,B) &= \sqrt{0+9} = 3.00 \\
d(A,C) &= \sqrt{9+0} = 3.00 \\
d(A,D) &= \sqrt{9+9} = 4.24 \\
d(B,C) &= \sqrt{9+9} = 4.24 \\
d(B,D) &= \sqrt{9+0} = 3.00 \\
d(C,D) &= \sqrt{0+9} = 3.00 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Initial Matrix:**

| | A | B | C | D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 0 | 3.00 | 3.00 | 4.24 |
| B | | 0 | 4.24 | 3.00 |
| C | | | 0 | 3.00 |
| D | | | | 0 |

**Step 2 — First Merge:** Tie at 3.00 between (A,B), (A,C), (B,D), (C,D). Merge A,B → cluster AB at height 3.00.

**Updated Matrix (single linkage, taking MIN):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(AB, C) &= \min(d(A,C), d(B,C)) = \min(3.00, 4.24) = 3.00 \\
d(AB, D) &= \min(d(A,D), d(B,D)) = \min(4.24, 3.00) = 3.00 \\
d(C, D) &= 3.00
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Second Merge:** All pairs equal at 3.00. Merge AB with C → cluster ABC at height 3.00.

**Step 4 — Final Merge:** $d(ABC, D) = \min(d(A,D), d(B,D), d(C,D)) = 3.00$. Merge at height 3.00.

**Dendrogram (textual representation):**

```
Height
3.00  ─┬──────────┬──────┐
       │          │      │
       AB         C      D
       │          │      │
1.00   A    B     ...    ...
```

**Conclusion:** Three clusters form at height just above 3.00: {A, B}, {C}, {D}.

*(7 marks: 3 for algorithm/formulas + 4 for step-by-step worked solution with dendrogram)*

---

#### Question B — DBSCAN Worked Problem [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q. (a)** *Explain the DBSCAN algorithm with its key concepts: core point, border point, noise point, density-reachability, and density-connectivity.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

DBSCAN is a density-based clustering algorithm. Given parameters **Eps** (radius) and **MinPts** (minimum neighbors), it classifies every point and groups them.

**Key Concepts:**

1. **Eps-Neighborhood:** $N_{Eps}(p) = \{q \in D : d(p,q) \le Eps\}$
2. **Core Point:** $p$ is a core point if $\vert N_{Eps}(p) \vert \ge MinPts$.
3. **Border Point:** $p$ is a border point if $\vert N_{Eps}(p) \vert < MinPts$ but $p \in N_{Eps}(q)$ for some core point $q$.
4. **Noise Point:** $p$ is noise if it is neither a core point nor a border point.
5. **Directly Density-Reachable:** $q$ is directly density-reachable from $p$ if $q \in N_{Eps}(p)$ and $p$ is a core point.
6. **Density-Reachable:** $q$ is density-reachable from $p$ if there is a chain of points $p_1 = p, p_2, \ldots, p_n = q$ where each $p_{i+1}$ is directly density-reachable from $p_i$.
7. **Density-Connected:** Points $p$ and $q$ are density-connected if there exists a point $o$ such that both $p$ and $q$ are density-reachable from $o$.

**Algorithm Flow:** Start with an arbitrary unvisited point. If it is a core point, a new cluster is formed and all density-reachable points are added recursively. If not, it is temporarily marked noise. After all points are processed, points still marked noise are confirmed outliers.

**Q. (b)** *Consider the following dataset. Given Eps = 1.5 and MinPts = 3, classify each point as Core, Border, or Noise. List the clusters formed.*

| Point | (x, y) |
|-------|--------|
| P1 | (1, 2) |
| P2 | (2, 2) |
| P3 | (2, 1) |
| P4 | (8, 8) |
| P5 | (8, 9) |
| P6 | (15, 15) |
| P7 | (16, 16) |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Neighborhoods ($d \le 1.5$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N(P_1) &= \{P_1, P_2, P_3\} \Rightarrow \vert N \vert = 3 \ge 3 \Rightarrow \textbf{CORE} \\
N(P_2) &= \{P_1, P_2, P_3\} \Rightarrow \vert N \vert = 3 \ge 3 \Rightarrow \textbf{CORE} \\
N(P_3) &= \{P_1, P_2, P_3\} \Rightarrow \vert N \vert = 3 \ge 3 \Rightarrow \textbf{CORE} \\
N(P_4) &= \{P_4, P_5\} \Rightarrow \vert N \vert = 2 < 3 \\
N(P_5) &= \{P_4, P_5\} \Rightarrow \vert N \vert = 2 < 3 \\
N(P_6) &= \{P_6, P_7\} \Rightarrow \vert N \vert = 2 < 3 \\
N(P_7) &= \{P_6, P_7\} \Rightarrow \vert N \vert = 2 < 3 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Border Point Check:**

$P_4, P_5$ are not core points, but each is in the neighborhood of the other — neither is in any core point's neighborhood with sufficient density (only 2 points). So $P_4, P_5$ qualify as **BORDER** only if a core point exists nearby. Here, no core point lies within Eps of $P_4$ or $P_5$. Hence they are **NOISE**.

Similarly, $P_6, P_7$ are **NOISE**.

**Step 3 — Cluster Formation:**

* **Cluster 1:** {P1, P2, P3} — all density-connected through core points.
* **Noise Points:** {P4, P5, P6, P7}

*(7 marks: 4 for theory + 3 for worked classification)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1**: Students often confuse "directly density-reachable" with "density-reachable." Direct reachability is **symmetric only when both are core points**; otherwise it is asymmetric. Loss of **1 mark** if terminology is wrong.
> - **Common Mistake 2**: In agglomerative clustering, after a merge, students forget to **update the distance matrix**. Without updating, the dendrogram is incorrect. Loss of **2 marks**.
> - **Common Mistake 3**: DBSCAN students frequently classify border points incorrectly. A point is border **only if** it is inside a *core point's* Eps-neighborhood and is *itself not a core*. Loss of **1 mark**.
> - **Common Mistake 4**: Students use Minkowski distance without specifying the parameter *r*. Always state *r = 1* (Manhattan) or *r = 2* (Euclidean). Loss of **0.5 mark**.
> - **Common Mistake 5**: Forgetting to draw the **dendrogram** in agglomerative questions. A correct numerical merge sequence without a dendrogram loses **1 mark** in 14-mark questions.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Hierarchical Clustering and DBSCAN**

- **Hierarchical clustering** produces a **dendrogram** — a binary tree of merges/splits.
- **Agglomerative** = bottom-up (more common); **Divisive** = top-down (rare in exams).
- The **linkage criterion** governs how cluster-to-cluster distances are computed:
  - **Single** → MIN distance (chain effect).
  - **Complete** → MAX distance (compact clusters).
  - **Average** → mean pairwise distance.
  - **Ward's** → minimizes within-cluster variance (variance-based).
- Agglomerative clustering is **deterministic** but **does not scale** well: $O(n^2 \log n)$ to $O(n^3)$.
- **DBSCAN** is a **density-based** method requiring only **Eps** and **MinPts**.
- Three DBSCAN point categories: **Core** (≥ MinPts in Eps), **Border** (inside a core's Eps but not core itself), **Noise** (neither).
- DBSCAN can discover **arbitrary-shaped clusters** and explicitly identifies **outliers** — unlike *k*-means and hierarchical.
- DBSCAN is **unsuitable for varying-density clusters** (OPTICS solves this).
- Time complexity: DBSCAN = $O(n \log n)$ with spatial indexing — superior to hierarchical.
- **Density-reachability** is **not symmetric** in general; **density-connectivity** **is symmetric**.
- Always **show the updated distance matrix** at every step of agglomerative clustering.
- For DBSCAN, clearly state the **chosen Eps and MinPts** before classification.
- **Dendrogram height** indicates dissimilarity: lower merges → similar points; higher merges → dissimilar points.
- KTU loves asking for a **worked agglomerative problem with single linkage** using Euclidean distance on small (4–6 point) 2-D datasets — practice at least 3 such examples.
- DBSCAN is widely used in **anomaly detection** in cybersecurity and IoT sensor analytics — mention this in 7-mark theory answers to score higher.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Association Rule Mining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT524 - DATA MINING AND WAREHOUSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Association Rule Mining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Association Rule Mining (ARM)** is a fundamental unsupervised data mining technique used to discover interesting, hidden relationships, correlations, or causal structures among a set of items (variables) in large transactional, relational, or categorical databases. Formally introduced by Rakesh Agrawal et al. in 1993, it is the foundational engine behind **Market Basket Analysis**.

Mathematically, let $I = \{i_1, i_2, i_3, \ldots, i_m\}$ be a universe of distinct items. Let $D$ be a database of transactions, where each transaction $T$ is a non-empty subset of $I$ (i.e., $T \subseteq I$). An **association rule** is an implication of the form:

$$X \implies Y$$

where $X \subset I$, $Y \ subset I$, and critically, $X \cap Y = \emptyset$. Here, $X$ is called the **antecedent** (Left-Hand Side or LHS) and $Y$ is the **consequent** (Right-Hand Side or RHS).

The three pivotal metrics that quantify the "strength" of a rule are:

1. **Support ($s$)**: The fraction of transactions in $D$ that contain the union of items in both $X$ and $Y$. It measures the *statistical significance* or *frequency* of the rule.
2. **Confidence ($c$)**: The conditional probability $P(Y \mid X)$. It measures the *strength* or *reliability* of the inference made by the rule.
3. **Lift (or Interest, $l$)**: The ratio of observed support to expected support if $X$ and $Y$ were statistically independent. It measures the *interestingness* or *correlation* between the antecedent and consequent.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Supermarket Basket" Analogy** 🛒
>
> Imagine you are the manager of a large supermarket chain. Every day, thousands of customers walk in, pick items from the shelves, and place them in their **shopping baskets** before heading to the checkout counter. Each checkout transaction produces a digital receipt — a list of items purchased together.
>
> Over time, you notice a peculiar, recurring pattern: customers who buy **diapers** very frequently also buy **beer**. Why? It sounds bizarre, but the pattern is real. The explanation (sociologically) is that young fathers, left at home to care for their babies on weekends, are sent out to buy diapers and, while at it, pick up a six-pack of beer for themselves.
>
> **Association Rule Mining is the automated detective that uncovers exactly these kinds of hidden, non-obvious co-occurrence patterns from raw transaction logs.** It transforms unstructured point-of-sale data into actionable business intelligence:
> - **Cross-selling**: Place beer next to diapers to increase impulse buys.
> - **Catalog Design**: Place related items in the same aisle.
> - **Promotional Bundling**: Offer "Buy Diapers, Get 10% off Baby Wipes."
> - **Inventory Management**: Stock related items together.
> - **Recommendation Engines**: "Customers who bought this also bought..."

The same technique extends far beyond retail — it is used in **bioinformatics** (co-expressed gene clusters), **web usage mining** (clickstream analysis), **intrusion detection systems**, **medical diagnosis** (co-occurring symptoms), and **fraud detection**.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | KTU 2024 Definition |
|---|---|
| **Itemset** | A collection of one or more items. A $k$-itemset contains exactly $k$ items. |
| **Frequent Itemset** | An itemset whose support $\geq$ `minsup` (minimum support threshold). |
| **Strong Rule** | A rule whose support $\geq$ `minsup` AND confidence $\geq$ `minconf`. |
| **Candidate Itemset** | A potentially frequent itemset generated during the mining process (before counting). |
| **Downward Closure Property** | If an itemset is infrequent, all its supersets are also infrequent (anti-monotonicity). |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Phase Operational Framework

Association Rule Mining is decomposed into two computationally distinct sub-problems. Understanding this decomposition is the single most important high-yield concept for KTU board examinations.

### **Phase 1: Frequent Itemset Generation (The Hard Part)**
This is the computationally dominant phase. The goal is to find **all** itemsets whose support is $\geq$ `minsup`. The challenge is that for $m$ unique items, the search space of all possible itemsets is $2^m - 1$ (excluding the empty set), which is exponential and intractable by brute force.

**The "Why" — Why is brute force impossible?**
For a modest supermarket with only $m = 50$ distinct products, the search space is $2^{50} - 1 \approx 1.12 \times 10^{15}$ itemsets. Even at a million operations per second, brute force would take ~36 years. Hence, heuristic pruning strategies are mandatory.

### **Phase 2: Rule Generation (The Easy Part)**
Once frequent itemsets are known, generating strong rules is relatively cheap. For every frequent itemset $L$, find all non-empty subsets $s$ such that the rule $s \implies (L - s)$ satisfies `minconf`.

## 2.2 The Three Foundational Metrics — Deep Dive

### **Support — Frequency of Co-occurrence**
$$\text{Support}(X \implies Y) = \frac{\text{Number of transactions containing both } X \text{ and } Y}{\text{Total number of transactions}} = \frac{\sigma(X \cup Y)}{N}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Support Matters**: A rule like "Butter $\implies$ Gold Bars" might be 100% confident in a single freak transaction, but its support would be near zero, making it statistically meaningless. Support filters out coincidental patterns.

### **Confidence — Strength of the Rule**
$$\text{Confidence}(X \implies Y) = \frac{\text{Support}(X \cup Y)}{\text{Support}(X)} = \frac{\sigma(X \cup Y)}{\sigma(X)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Confidence Pitfall**: Confidence only measures the strength of $X \implies Y$, not $Y \implies X$. It is also asymmetric and can be misleading for items with high baseline frequency.

### **Lift — True Correlation Beyond Chance**
$$\text{Lift}(X \implies Y) = \frac{\text{Support}(X \cup Y)}{\text{Support}(X) \times \text{Support}(Y)} = \frac{\text{Confidence}(X \implies Y)}{\text{Support}(Y)}$$

**Interpretation of Lift Values:**
- $\text{Lift} = 1$: $X$ and $Y$ are **statistically independent** (no correlation).
- $\text{Lift} > 1$: $X$ and $Y$ are **positively correlated** (occur together more often than chance).
- $\text{Lift} < 1$: $X$ and $Y$ are **negatively correlated** (the presence of $X$ *inhibits* $Y$).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Metric | Formula | Range | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Support** | $\text{supp}(X \implies Y) = \dfrac{\sigma(X \cup Y)}{N}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Frequency of the rule |
| **Confidence** | $\text{conf}(X \implies Y) = \dfrac{\text{supp}(X \cup Y)}{\text{supp}(X)}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Conditional probability $P(Y \mid X)$ |
| **Lift** | $\text{lift}(X \implies Y) = \dfrac{\text{supp}(X \cup Y)}{\text{supp}(X) \cdot \text{supp}(Y)}$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Correlation: $1=$ independent |
| **Conviction** | $\text{conv}(X \implies Y) = \dfrac{1 - \text{supp}(Y)}{1 - \text{conf}(X \implies Y)}$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Asymmetric measure of implication |
| **Leverage** | $\text{lev}(X \implies Y) = \text{supp}(X \cup Y) - \text{supp}(X) \cdot \text{supp}(Y)$ | $[-0.25, 0.25]$ | Difference from expected independence |
| **Apriori Pruning** | If $\text{supp}(k\text{-itemset}) < \text{minsup}$, then all $(k+1)$-supersets are infrequent | Boolean | Anti-monotonicity property |
| **Total Search Space** | $2^{m} - 1$ itemsets for $m$ distinct items | Exponential | Justifies pruning |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Monotonicity (Downward Closure Property)**: If an itemset is *infrequent*, then **all** of its supersets are guaranteed to be infrequent.
