topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT524 - DATA MINING AND WAREHOUSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Apriori Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Apriori Algorithm** is a foundational *level-wise*, *breadth-first* algorithm proposed by **Rakesh Agrawal and Ramakrishnan Srikant (1994)** for mining **frequent itemsets** from transactional databases, which subsequently enables the generation of strong **association rules**. The algorithm operates on the principle of *candidate generation-and-test*, systematically building larger itemsets from smaller ones using two pivotal properties:

> [!IMPORTANT] **Core Properties of the Apriori Algorithm**
> 1. **Apriori Property (Downward Closure):** *All non-empty subsets of a frequent itemset must also be frequent.* Equivalently, if an itemset is *infrequent*, then all of its supersets are *infrequent* (anti-monotonicity).
> 2. **Join + Prune Strategy:** Candidate (k+1)-itemsets are generated by joining frequent k-itemsets, followed by a pruning step that eliminates candidates whose (k)-subsets are not all frequent.

Mathematically, for a transaction database **D** with **|D| = N** transactions, the **support** of an itemset **X** is given by:

$$support(X) = \frac{\text{count}(X)}{N} = \frac{\text{Number of transactions containing } X}{\text{Total number of transactions}}$$

The **confidence** of an association rule **X ⇒ Y** is:

$$confidence(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{support(X \cup Y)}{support(X)}$$

An itemset is termed **frequent** if its support $\geq min\_sup$ (user-defined minimum support threshold), and a rule is termed **strong** if both its support and confidence exceed the respective minimum thresholds.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE] **Real-World Analogy: The Supermarket Shopping Cart**
> Imagine you are a supermarket manager with a *basket dataset* of 10,000 customer transactions. You wish to discover: *"Which products are frequently bought together?"* If you tried to check every possible product combination directly, you would face $2^d - 1$ combinations for **d** distinct products — an astronomical, computationally explosive number.
>
> The **Apriori algorithm** applies a brilliant *shortcut*: it says, *"If customers rarely buy 'Caviar' (an itemset of size 1 is infrequent), then no combination involving Caviar can be frequent either."* This **pruning logic** cuts the search space dramatically — much like how a detective eliminates suspects: if the suspect's alibi is shaky on a small detail, you don't need to investigate their grand alibi in detail.

## 1.3 Why Apriori Matters in Data Mining

> [!IMPORTANT] **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEITT524 — Module 3)**
> Association Rule Mining is one of the **highest-weightage** topics in Module 3. Apriori is a guaranteed question in KTU board examinations, typically appearing as a full 14-mark Part-B problem with sub-parts requiring (a) candidate generation, (b) frequent itemset mining, and (c) strong rule extraction.

The algorithm has direct industrial applications in:
- **Market Basket Analysis** (retail — Amazon, Walmart recommendation engines)
- **Cross-Selling** strategies in e-commerce
- **Web Usage Mining** (clickstream pattern analysis)
- **Bioinformatics** (gene co-expression pattern discovery)
- **Intrusion Detection Systems** (correlating network log patterns)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — The Two-Step Mining Process

Association rule mining is decomposed into two computationally distinct sub-problems:

1. **Frequent Itemset Generation:** Find all itemsets whose support $\geq min\_sup$.
2. **Rule Generation:** From each frequent itemset **L**, extract all rules of the form $X \Rightarrow (L - X)$ whose confidence $\geq min\_conf$.

The computational bottleneck lies in **Step 1**, since Step 2 is comparatively inexpensive (a frequent itemset of size *k* yields up to $2^k - 2$ candidate rules, but confidence computation is a single division).

## 2.2 Detailed Algorithm Steps

The Apriori procedure iterates through the following phases for $k = 1, 2, 3, \dots$ until no new frequent itemsets are found:

### **Step 1 — Initialization**
Scan the entire transaction database **D** to compute support for each 1-itemset. Filter those satisfying $support \geq min\_sup$ to form the set of **Frequent 1-Itemsets, $L_1$**.

### **Step 2 — Candidate Generation (Join Step)**
Generate $C_k$ (candidate k-itemsets) by performing a self-join $L_{k-1} \bowtie L_{k-1}$ on the condition that the first $(k-2)$ items are identical. The candidate is the union of the two itemsets with the last items differing.

> **Join Condition:** $(l_1[1] = l_2[1]) \wedge (l_1[2] = l_2[2]) \wedge \dots \wedge (l_1[k-2] = l_2[k-2]) \wedge (l_1[k-1] \neq l_2[k-1])$

### **Step 3 — Pruning Step**
Apply the **Apriori property**: for each candidate $c \in C_k$, examine *all* its (k)-subsets. If *any* subset is not in $L_{k-1}$, delete $c$ from $C_k$. This is the key *anti-monotonicity* pruning.

### **Step 4 — Support Counting & Frequent Itemset Selection**
Scan database **D** to compute the actual support of each surviving candidate in $C_k$. Retain only those with $support \geq min\_sup$ to form $L_k$.

### **Step 5 — Termination**
If $L_k$ is empty (or $k$ exceeds the size of the longest transaction), stop. Otherwise, increment $k := k+1$ and return to Step 2.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Metric | Mathematical Expression | Threshold Condition | Unit / Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Support of itemset X | $support(X) = \frac{count(X)}{\vert D \vert}$ | $\geq min\_sup$ | $[0, 1]$ or as absolute count |
| 2 | Confidence of rule $X \Rightarrow Y$ | $confidence(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{support(X \cup Y)}{support(X)}$ | $\geq min\_conf$ | $[0, 1]$ |
| 3 | Lift of rule $X \Rightarrow Y$ | $lift(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{support(X \cup Y)}{support(X) \cdot support(Y)}$ | $> 1$ implies positive correlation | $[0, \infty)$ |
| 4 | Conviction of rule | $conv(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{1 - support(Y)}{1 - confidence(X \Rightarrow Y)}$ | Higher = stronger rule | $[0, \infty)$ |
| 5 | Number of candidate rules from itemset of size k | $2^k - 2$ | — | Integer $\geq 0$ |
| 6 | Maximum candidate k-itemsets generated | $\binom{d}{k}$ where *d* = number of distinct items | — | Integer $\geq 0$ |
| 7 | Apriori Pruning Condition | If $X \notin L_{k-1}$, then $\forall Y \supset X : Y \notin L_k$ | — | Boolean |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Apriori algorithm is the cornerstone of **Association Rule Mining**, deployed in:

- **Retail Analytics (Market Basket Analysis):** Tesco's "**Customers who bought X also bought Y**" recommendations.
- **Telecommunications:** Mining call detail records for **churn prediction** (frequent co-occurring service cancellations).
- **Healthcare Informatics:** Detecting **frequent symptom-drug co-occurrences** in Electronic Health Records (EHR).
- **Cybersecurity:** Identifying correlated intrusion signatures in **SIEM (Security Information & Event Management)** systems.
- **Web Personalization:** Cross-page clickstream pattern mining for **content recommendation**.

> [!NOTE] **Engineering Insight:** Although Apriori is computationally costly due to multiple database scans, it remains a *pedagogical and practical benchmark* in KTU examinations. Modern industrial systems use its successors — **FP-Growth**, **Eclat**, and **PrefixSpan** — to overcome Apriori's I/O bottleneck.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Comprehensive Worked Example — Apriori Algorithm Execution

To fully internalize the algorithm, we work through a **canonical KTU-style problem** end-to-end.

### 3.1.1 Given Transaction Database (D)

| TID | Items Purchased |
|:---:|:---|
| T1 | I1, I2, I5 |
| T2 | I2, I4 |
| T3 | I2, I3 |
| T4 | I1, I2, I4 |
| T5 | I1, I3 |
| T6 | I2, I3 |
| T7 | I1, I3 |
| T8 | I1, I2, I3, I5 |
| T9 | I1, I2, I3 |

**Total transactions:** $\vert D \vert = 9$

**Thresholds:** $min\_sup = 2$ transactions (i.e., $2/9 \approx 22.22\%$), $min\_conf = 60\%$

### 3.1.2 Iteration k = 1 — Generate $C_1$ and $L_1$

**Candidate 1-Itemsets ($C_1$):** All singleton items, each scanned once against D.

| Itemset | Transactions | Count (Absolute Support) | Relative Support |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| {I1} | T1, T4, T5, T7, T8, T9 | 6 | $66.67\%$ |
| {I2} | T1, T2, T3, T4, T6, T8, T9 | 7 | $77.78\%$ |
| {I3} | T3, T5, T6, T7, T8, T9 | 6 | $66.67\%$ |
| {I4} | T2, T4 | 2 | $22.22\%$ |
| {I5} | T1, T8 | 2 | $22.22\%$ |

Applying $min\_sup = 2$, **all five 1-itemsets qualify**.

$$\boxed{L_1 = \{\{I1\}, \{I2\}, \{I3\}, \{I4\}, \{I5\}\}}$$

### 3.1.3 Iteration k = 2 — Generate $C_2$ and $L_2$

**Step (a) — Join Step:** Self-join $L_1 \bowtie L_1$ to form candidate 2-itemsets. The total number of candidates is:

$$\binom{5}{2} = 10 \text{ candidates}$$

**Step (b) — Pruning:** For 2-itemsets, no subset-based pruning is needed (all 1-subsets are trivially in $L_1$).

**Step (c) — Support Counting:**

| Itemset | Transactions | Count | Support | $\geq min\_sup$? |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| {I1, I2} | T1, T4, T8, T9 | 4 | $44.44\%$ | ✓ |
| {I1, I3} | T5, T7, T8, T9 | 4 | $44.44\%$ | ✓ |
| {I1, I4} | T4 | 1 | $11.11\%$ | ✗ |
| {I1, I5} | T1, T8 | 2 | $22.22\%$ | ✓ |
| {I2, I3} | T3, T6, T8, T9 | 4 | $44.44\%$ | ✓ |
| {I2, I4} | T2, T4 | 2 | $22.22\%$ | ✓ |
| {I2, I5} | T1, T8 | 2 | $22.22\%$ | ✓ |
| {I3, I4} | — | 0 | $0\%$ | ✗ |
| {I3, I5} | T8 | 1 | $11.11\%$ | ✗ |
| {I4, I5} | — | 0 | $0\%$ | ✗ |

$$\boxed{L_2 = \{\{I1,I2\}, \{I1,I3\}, \{I1,I5\}, \{I2,I3\}, \{I2,I4\}, \{I2,I5\}\}}$$

### 3.1.4 Iteration k = 3 — Generate $C_3$ and $L_3$

**Step (a) — Join Step:** Self-join $L_2 \bowtie L_2$ on the first element. Naively, this yields $\binom{6}{3} = 20$ combinations, but the lexicographic join restricts to those where the (k-1) prefix matches. Initial join candidates (before pruning):

$$\{I1,I2,I3\}, \{I1,I2,I5\}, \{I1,I3,I5\}, \{I2,I3,I4\}, \{I2,I3,I5\}, \{I2,I4,I5\}$$

**Step (b) — Pruning using Apriori Property:** For each candidate, verify *all* its $\binom{3}{2} = 3$ two-subsets are in $L_2$:

| Candidate | 2-Subsets | All in $L_2$? | Retained? |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| {I1, I2, I3} | {I1,I2}, {I1,I3}, {I2,I3} | ✓ ✓ ✓ | ✓ |
| {I1, I2, I5} | {I1,I2}, {I1,I5}, {I2,I5} | ✓ ✓ ✓ | ✓ |
| {I1, I3, I5} | {I1,I3}, {I1,I5}, {I3,I5} | ✓ ✓ **✗** | ✗ |
| {I2, I3, I4} | {I2,I3}, {I2,I4}, {I3,I4} | ✓ ✓ **✗** | ✗ |
| {I2, I3, I5} | {I2,I3}, {I2,I5}, {I3,I5} | ✓ ✓ **✗** | ✗ |
| {I2, I4, I5} | {I2,I4}, {I2,I5}, {I4,I5} | ✓ ✓ **✗** | ✗ |

**Pruning eliminated 4 out of 6 candidates — a 66.67% reduction!**

$$C_3 = \{\{I1, I2, I3\}, \{I1, I2, I5\}\}$$

**Step (c) — Support Counting:**

| Itemset | Transactions | Count | $\geq min\_sup$? |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| {I1, I2, I3} | T8, T9 | 2 | ✓ |
| {I1, I2, I5} | T1, T8 | 2 | ✓ |

$$\boxed{L_3 = \{\{I1, I2, I3\}, \{I1, I2, I5\}\}}$$

### 3.1.5 Iteration k = 4 — Termination

**Join Step:** $L_3 \bowtie L_3$ yields the sole candidate $\{I1, I2, I3, I5\}$.

**Pruning Step:** The 3-subsets of this candidate include $\{I1, I3, I5\}$, which is **not in $L_3$**.

$$C_4 = \emptyset \quad \Rightarrow \quad L_4 = \emptyset$$

**Algorithm terminates.** Final set of all frequent itemsets:

$$F = L_1 \cup L_2 \cup L_3$$

### 3.1.6 Generating Strong Association Rules ($min\_conf = 60\%$)

For each frequent itemset $L$, every non-empty subset $X \subset L$ defines a candidate rule $X \Rightarrow (L - X)$. The confidence is computed, and rules with $confidence \geq 60\%$ are retained.

#### **Rules from 2-Itemsets ($L_2$):**

| Rule | $support(X \cup Y)$ | $support(X)$ | Confidence | Strong? |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| {I1} $\Rightarrow$ {I2} | 4 | 6 | $4/6 = 66.67\%$ | ✓ |
| {I2} $\Rightarrow$ {I1} | 4 | 7 | $4/7 = 57.14\%$ | ✗ |
| {I1} $\Rightarrow$ {I3} | 4 | 6 | $4/6 = 66.67\%$ | ✓ |
| {I3} $\Rightarrow$ {I1} | 4 | 6 | $4/6 = 66.67\%$ | ✓ |
| {I1} $\Rightarrow$ {I5} | 2 | 6 | $2/6 = 33.33\%$ | ✗ |
| {I5} $\Rightarrow$ {I1} | 2 | 2 | $2/2 = 100\%$ | ✓ |
| {I2} $\Rightarrow$ {I3} | 4 | 7 | $4/7 = 57.14\%$ | ✗ |
| {I3} $\Rightarrow$ {I2} | 4 | 6 | $4/6 = 66.67\%$ | ✓ |
| {I2} $\Rightarrow$ {I4} | 2 | 7 | $2/7 = 28.57\%$ | ✗ |
| {I4} $\Rightarrow$ {I2} | 2 | 2 | $2/2 = 100\%$ | ✓ |
| {I2} $\Rightarrow$ {I5} | 2 | 7 | $2/7 = 28.57\%$ | ✗ |
| {I5} $\Rightarrow$ {I2} | 2 | 2 | $2/2 = 100\%$ | ✓ |

#### **Rules from 3-Itemsets ($L_3$):**

| Rule | $support(X \cup Y)$ | $support(X)$ | Confidence | Strong? |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| {I1, I2} $\Rightarrow$ {I3} | 2 | 4 | $2/4 = 50\%$ | ✗ |
| {I1, I3} $\Rightarrow$ {I2} | 2 | 4 | $2/4 = 50\%$ | ✗ |
| {I2, I3} $\Rightarrow$ {I1} | 2 | 4 | $2/4 = 50\%$ | ✗ |
| {I1, I2} $\Rightarrow$ {I5} | 2 | 4 | $2/4 = 50\%$ | ✗ |
| {I1, I5} $\Rightarrow$ {I2} | 2 | 2 | $2/2 = 100\%$ | ✓ |
| {I2, I5} $\Rightarrow$ {I1} | 2 | 2 | $2/2 = 100\%$ | ✓ |
| {I1} $\Rightarrow$ {I2, I3} | 2 | 6 | $2/6 = 33.33\%$ | ✗ |
| {I2} $\Rightarrow$ {I1, I3} | 2 | 7 | $2/7 = 28.57\%$ | ✗ |
| {I3} $\Rightarrow$ {I1, I2} | 2 | 6 | $2/6 = 33.33\%$ | ✗ |
| {I5} $\Rightarrow$ {I1, I2} | 2 | 2 | $2/2 = 100\%$ | ✓ |

### 3.1.7 Final Strong Rule Set

$$\boxed{\text{Strong Rules} = \big\{I1 \Rightarrow I2,\ I1 \Rightarrow I3,\ I3 \Rightarrow I1,\ I3 \Rightarrow I2,\ I5 \Rightarrow I1,\ I4 \Rightarrow I2,\ I5 \Rightarrow I2,\ \{I1,I5\} \Rightarrow I2,\ \{I2,I5\} \Rightarrow I1,\ I5 \Rightarrow \{I1,I2\}\big\}}$$

> [!NOTE] **Key Observation:** The rules $\{I5\} \Rightarrow \{I1\}, \{I5\} \Rightarrow \{I2\}, \{I5\} \Rightarrow \{I1,I2\}$ all have **100% confidence**, meaning whenever I5 is purchased, items I1 and I2 are *always* bought together. This is a textbook example of a *perfect association*.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithmic Flowchart of the Apriori Procedure

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the **level-wise iteration**, **candidate generation**, **pruning**, and **support counting** phases of the Apriori algorithm. The structure is presented as a high-fidelity *block-level functional architecture flow* mapping the algorithm's internal control logic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([START: Input Database D, min_sup, min_conf]) --> initNode[Initialize k = 1]
    initNode --> scan1Node[Scan D: Compute Count for each 1-itemset]
    scan1Node --> l1Node[Form L1: Frequent 1-Itemsets]
    l1Node --> checkL1{L1 is empty?}
    checkL1 -- YES --> stopNode([STOP: No frequent itemsets])
    checkL1 -- NO --> incKNode[Increment k = k + 1]
    incKNode --> joinNode["Join Step: Generate Ck from Lk-1 x Lk-1"]
    joinNode --> pruneNode["Prune Step: Remove candidates with infrequent (k-1)-subsets"]
    pruneNode --> scanKNode[Scan D: Compute support of each candidate in Ck]
    scanKNode --> lkNode[Form Lk: Frequent k-Itemsets support >= min_sup]
    lkNode --> checkLk{Lk is empty?}
    checkLk -- YES --> ruleNode[Generate Strong Association Rules from all L_i]
    checkLk -- NO --> incKNode
    ruleNode --> confNode["For each rule X to Y, compute confidence = support(X u Y) / support(X)"]
    confNode --> outputNode[Output: All strong rules with confidence >= min_conf]
    outputNode --> stopNode
```

## 4.2 High-Level Functional Block Diagram — Association Rule Mining Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rawDBNode[Raw Transaction Database D] --> preprocessNode[Data Preprocessing & Encoding]
    preprocessNode --> aprioriCoreNode["APRIORI ALGORITHM CORE"]
    aprioriCoreNode --> subgraph L1
        L1a[Itemset Counting] --> L1b[Filter by min_sup]
        L1b --> L1c[Output: L1]
    end
    aprioriCoreNode --> subgraph L2
        L2a[Join L1 x L1] --> L2b[Prune by Apriori Property]
        L2b --> L2c[Scan D for Support]
        L2c --> L2d[Filter by min_sup]
        L2d --> L2e[Output: L2]
    end
    aprioriCoreNode --> subgraph L3
        L3a[Join L2 x L2] --> L3b[Prune by Apriori Property]
        L3b --> L3c[Scan D for Support]
        L3c --> L3d[Filter by min_sup]
        L3d --> L3e[Output: L3]
    end
    L1c --> unionNode[Union of all L_k]
    L2e --> unionNode
    L3e --> unionNode
    unionNode --> ruleGenNode[Strong Rule Generation]
    ruleGenNode --> confFilterNode[Filter by min_conf]
    confFilterNode --> finalOutNode[FINAL STRONG ASSOCIATION RULES]
```

## 4.3 Pruning Mechanism — Conceptual Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inNode[New Candidate Itemset C] --> checkSubsets[Generate all (k-1)-subsets of C]
    checkSubsets --> checkL{Lk-1 contains all subsets?}
    checkL -- YES --> retainNode[RETAIN C: Add to Ck]
    checkL -- NO --> discardNode[DISCARD C: Infrequent superset]
    retainNode --> nextCand[Process next candidate]
    discardNode --> nextCand
    nextCand --> more{More candidates?}
    more -- YES --> inNode
    more -- NO --> doneNode[Pruning Complete: Proceed to Database Scan]
```

> [!NOTE] **Diagram Interpretation Tip for KTU Exam:** When asked to "describe the Apriori algorithm" in 7–14 mark questions, KTU examiners award marks for: (i) explicitly mentioning the **two key properties** (Apriori property and join-prune), (ii) showing the **flowchart or pseudocode**, and (iii) illustrating the *level-wise iteration* with a diagram. Always include the termination condition: $L_k = \emptyset$ or all transactions fully covered.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks):** Define the *Apriori Property* and explain its role in candidate pruning.

> `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Apriori Property** states that *all non-empty subsets of a frequent itemset must also be frequent*. Conversely, if an itemset is infrequent, then all of its supersets are necessarily infrequent. This property is also known as the **anti-monotonicity** or **downward closure property** of the support measure.

**Role in Pruning:** During the generation of candidate (k+1)-itemsets, the Apriori property is used to **eliminate candidates** whose any (k)-subset is not present in $L_k$ (the set of frequent k-itemsets). This dramatically reduces the candidate search space, transforming the problem from enumerating all $2^d$ subsets to a much smaller, *level-wise* iterative exploration. It is the foundational heuristic that makes frequent itemset mining computationally tractable.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks):** Differentiate between *support* and *confidence* in association rule mining with a suitable example.

> `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary Exam]`

**Model Answer:**

| Metric | Definition | Mathematical Form | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Support** | Frequency of an itemset appearing in the database | $support(X) = count(X) / \vert D \vert$ | Measures *how often* the itemset occurs — statistical significance |
| **Confidence** | Conditional probability of Y given X in transactions containing X | $confidence(X \Rightarrow Y) = support(X \cup Y) / support(X)$ | Measures *strength* of the implication — rule reliability |

**Example:** Consider the rule $\{\text{Bread}\} \Rightarrow \{\text{Butter}\}$. If out of 1000 transactions, 200 contain both bread and butter, 400 contain bread, then:

$$support(\{Bread, Butter\}) = 200/1000 = 20\%, \quad confidence = 200/400 = 50\%$$

A high support indicates the pattern is common, while a high confidence indicates the rule is reliable.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks):**

> `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, End Semester Evaluation]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Apply the **Apriori algorithm** on the following transaction database with $min\_sup = 30\%$ and $min\_conf = 60\%$. Generate all **frequent itemsets** and show each iteration's candidate generation, pruning, and support counting.

| TID | Items |
|:---:|:---|
| T1 | Bread, Butter, Milk |
| T2 | Bread, Butter |
| T3 | Butter, Milk |
| T4 | Bread, Milk |
| T5 | Bread, Butter, Milk |
| T6 | Butter, Milk |
| T7 | Bread, Milk |

**Model Solution:**

Total transactions $\vert D \vert = 7$, $min\_sup = 0.30 \times 7 = 2.1 \Rightarrow 3$ (rounded up).

**Iteration k = 1:** Compute support for each 1-itemset.

- {Bread}: T1, T2, T4, T5, T7 = 5 ✓
- {Butter}: T1, T2, T3, T5, T6 = 5 ✓
- {Milk}: T1, T3, T4, T5, T6, T7 = 6 ✓

$$L_1 = \{\{Bread\}, \{Butter\}, \{Milk\}\}$$

**Iteration k = 2:** Join $L_1 \bowtie L_1$ to form $C_2 = \{\{Bread, Butter\}, \{Bread, Milk\}, \{Butter, Milk\}\}$.

- {Bread, Butter}: T1, T2, T5 = 3 ✓
- {Bread, Milk}: T1, T4, T5, T7 = 4 ✓
- {Butter, Milk}: T1, T3, T5, T6 = 4 ✓

$$L_2 = \{\{Bread, Butter\}, \{Bread, Milk\}, \{Butter, Milk\}\}$$

**Iteration k = 3:** Join $L_2 \bowtie L_2$ yields $C_3 = \{\{Bread, Butter, Milk\}\}$.

Pruning: subsets {Bread, Butter}, {Bread, Milk}, {Butter, Milk} all in $L_2$ → **retained**.

Support: T1, T5 = 2 ✗ (below 3) — **rejected**.

$$L_3 = \emptyset \quad \Rightarrow \text{Algorithm terminates}$$

**All frequent itemsets:** $L_1 \cup L_2$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** From the frequent itemsets obtained in part (a), generate all **strong association rules** with $min\_conf = 60\%$.

**Model Solution:**

For each frequent 2-itemset, generate 2 candidate rules.

**From {Bread, Butter} (support = 3):**

- $\text{Bread} \Rightarrow \text{Butter}$: $confidence = 3/5 = 60\%$ ✓
- $\text{Butter} \Rightarrow \text{Bread}$: $confidence = 3/5 = 60\%$ ✓

**From {Bread, Milk} (support = 4):**

- $\text{Bread} \Rightarrow \text{Milk}$: $confidence = 4/5 = 80\%$ ✓
- $\text{Milk} \Rightarrow \text{Bread}$: $confidence = 4/6 = 66.67\%$ ✓

**From {Butter, Milk} (support = 4):**

- $\text{Butter} \Rightarrow \text{Milk}$: $confidence = 4/5 = 80\%$ ✓
- $\text{Milk} \Rightarrow \text{Butter}$: $confidence = 4/6 = 66.67\%$ ✓

**Final Strong Rules (all 6 rules above):**

| Rule | Confidence | Strong? |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Bread $\Rightarrow$ Butter | 60% | ✓ |
| Butter $\Rightarrow$ Bread | 60% | ✓ |
| Bread $\Rightarrow$ Milk | 80% | ✓ |
| Milk $\Rightarrow$ Bread | 66.67% | ✓ |
| Butter $\Rightarrow$ Milk | 80% | ✓ |
| Milk $\Rightarrow$ Butter | 66.67% | ✓ |

The **strongest rules** are $\text{Bread} \Rightarrow \text{Milk}$ and $\text{Butter} \Rightarrow \text{Milk}$ (both at 80% confidence).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** [Alternative Choice]

> `[KTU University Exam — May 2023, End Semester Evaluation]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Apriori algorithm** in detail with its **key properties**, **candidate generation procedure**, and **pruning technique**. How does the algorithm achieve efficiency despite the exponential search space?

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** The Apriori algorithm is a level-wise, breadth-first search algorithm for finding frequent itemsets in a transactional database, used as a foundation for mining association rules.

**Key Properties:**

1. **Apriori Property (Downward Closure):** *If a set is infrequent, all its supersets are infrequent.* This is the cornerstone heuristic that prunes the search space.

2. **Anti-monotonicity of Support:** Support is a *monotonically decreasing* function — as itemsets grow, their support can only decrease or remain the same.

3. **Join Step:** $C_k$ is generated by joining $L_{k-1}$ with itself on the first $(k-2)$ items.

4. **Prune Step:** Any candidate whose $(k-1)$-subset is not in $L_{k-1}$ is removed.

**Candidate Generation Procedure:**

Given $L_{k-1} = \{l_1, l_2, \dots, l_m\}$, generate $C_k$ via:
- **Insert into $C_k$:** For all pairs $l_i, l_j \in L_{k-1}$ where $l_i[1] = j[1], \dots, l_i[k-2] = l_j[k-2]$ and $l_i[k-1] < l_j[k-1]$, the union $l_i \cup l_j$ becomes a candidate.
- **Prune:** For each candidate $c \in C_k$, check whether all $(k-1)$-subsets of $c$ are in $L_{k-1}$. If any is missing, delete $c$.

**How Apriori Achieves Efficiency:**

- **Pruning reduces candidate size exponentially.** For a database with 1000 distinct items, generating $C_3$ naively yields $\binom{1000}{3} \approx 1.66 \times 10^8$ candidates. With Apriori pruning, this typically reduces by 60–90%.
- **Multiple database scans** are required (one per pass k), but this is *I/O bounded* and can be optimized using hash-trees (the *Apriori-TID* and *Apriori-Hybrid* variants).
- **Support threshold** eliminates low-frequency noise early.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider the transaction database given below with $min\_sup = 33\%$ and $min\_conf = 70\%$. Find all **frequent itemsets** and **strong association rules**.

| TID | Items |
|:---:|:---|
| T1 | A, B, C |
| T2 | A, C |
| T3 | A, D |
| T4 | B, C, D |
| T5 | A, C, D |
| T6 | C, D |

**Model Solution:**

$\vert D \vert = 6$, $min\_sup = 0.33 \times 6 = 1.98 \Rightarrow 2$.

**$L_1$:** {A}: T1, T2, T3, T5 = 4 ✓; {B}: T1, T4 = 2 ✓; {C}: T1, T2, T4, T5, T6 = 5 ✓; {D}: T3, T4, T5, T6 = 4 ✓.

$$L_1 = \{\{A\}, \{B\}, \{C\}, \{D\}\}$$

**$C_2$:** All 6 pairs. {A,B}: T1 = 1 ✗; {A,C}: T1, T2, T5 = 3 ✓; {A,D}: T3, T5 = 2 ✓; {B,C}: T1, T4 = 2 ✓; {B,D}: T4 = 1 ✗; {C,D}: T4, T5, T6 = 3 ✓.

$$L_2 = \{\{A,C\}, \{A,D\}, \{B,C\}, \{C,D\}\}$$

**$C_3$:** Join $L_2$: {A,C,D} (subsets: {A,C}✓, {A,D}✓, {C,D}✓ — retained), {B,C,D} (subsets: {B,C}✓, {B,D}✗ — pruned), {A,B,C} (subsets: {A,B}✗ — pruned).

$C_3 = \{\{A,C,D\}\}$. Support: T5 = 1 ✗.

$$L_3 = \emptyset \Rightarrow \text{Terminate}$$

**Strong Rules ($min\_conf = 70\%$):**

- $A \Rightarrow C$: $3/4 = 75\%$ ✓
- $C \Rightarrow A$: $3/5 = 60\%$ ✗
- $A \Rightarrow D$: $2/4 = 50\%$ ✗
- $D \Rightarrow A$: $2/4 = 50\%$ ✗
- $B \Rightarrow C$: $2/2 = 100\%$ ✓
- $C \Rightarrow B$: $2/5 = 40\%$ ✗
- $C \Rightarrow D$: $3/5 = 60\%$ ✗
- $D \Rightarrow C$: $3/4 = 75\%$ ✓

**Final strong rules:** $A \Rightarrow C$, $B \Rightarrow C$, $D \Rightarrow C$.

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Miscounting support:** Students frequently forget to include the *current transaction* when counting (e.g., counting T5 for {A,C,D} but omitting T5 for {A,C}). Always re-scan the original database D for each candidate.
> 2. **Skipping the Prune Step:** The Apriori property's pruning is what distinguishes Apriori from brute-force enumeration. Examiners specifically test whether students *apply pruning* and *show* the resulting candidate reduction. Failure to show the prune step costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing Support and Confidence Thresholds:** A rule is *strong* only if **both** support $\geq min\_sup$ **and** confidence $\geq min\_conf$. Some students compute confidence for rules whose underlying itemset is itself not frequent.
> 4. **Missing symmetric rules:** For an itemset {X, Y}, *both* $X \Rightarrow Y$ and $Y \Rightarrow X$ are candidate rules and must be evaluated separately. Forgetting one direction loses marks.
> 5. **Not showing intermediate $C_k$ and $L_k$ tables:** KTU examiners require *explicit tabulation* of candidates, support counts, and frequent itemset sets at each iteration. Always present in tabular form.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist — Apriori Algorithm (KTU PEITT524, Module 3)**

- **Core Idea:** Level-wise, breadth-first candidate generation-and-test for frequent itemset mining.
- **Two Pillars:** (1) Apriori property (downward closure), (2) Join + Prune strategy.
- **Key Metrics:** Support = $count(X) / \vert D \vert$; Confidence = $support(X \cup Y) / support(X)$; Lift = $support(X \cup Y) / (support(X) \cdot support(Y))$.
- **Algorithm Stops When:** $L_k = \emptyset$ (no new frequent itemsets) OR a single transaction's items are exhausted.
- **Computational Cost:** Worst case candidate generation at level *k* is $\binom{d}{k}$; pruning reduces this dramatically.
- **Strengths:** Simple, well-understood, guaranteed to find all frequent itemsets given sufficient $min\_sup$.
- **Weaknesses:** Requires *k* full database scans (I/O intensive); candidate generation can be huge for low $min\_sup$; not suitable for dense or high-dimensional data.
- **Successor Algorithms:** **FP-Growth** (no candidate generation, uses FP-tree), **Eclat** (vertical data format with set intersections), **CLOSET**, **CHARM**.
- **KTU Exam Tip:** Always show (a) $C_k$ candidates, (b) pruning verification, (c) support counts in a **table format**, and (d) final strong rules with confidence computations. A neat, well-tabulated answer typically scores 90%+ in board evaluations.
- **Mnemonic:** **"A-P-J-P-S-T"** = **A**priori property → **P**runing → **J**oin → **P**rune → **S**upport count → s**T**op.
- **Common Numerical Threshold:** KTU problems often use $min\_sup = 2$ or $3$ and $min\_conf = 50\%$ or $60\%$. Always *round up* the absolute support threshold: $min\_sup_{abs} = \lceil min\_sup_{rel} \times \vert D \vert \rceil$.
- **Anti-monotonicity:** A *critical conceptual point* — if a 2-itemset is infrequent, you can skip generating *all* supersets of it during candidate generation. This is the algorithmic heart of Apriori.
- **Domain Application:** Always contextualize your answer with **Market Basket Analysis** as the canonical use case.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: FP-Growth Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT524 - DATA MINING AND WAREHOUSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** FP-Growth Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FP-Growth (Frequent Pattern Growth)** is a highly efficient, **divide-and-conquer** frequent itemset mining algorithm proposed by **Han, Pei, and Yin (2000)**. Unlike the candidate-generation-and-test paradigm of the Apriori algorithm, FP-Growth adopts a **pattern-fragment growth** approach. It compresses the input transaction database into a compact, tree-structured data representation called the **FP-Tree (Frequent Pattern Tree)**, and then recursively mines frequent itemsets directly from this compressed structure *without* generating any candidate itemsets.

The algorithm has two principal phases:

1. **FP-Tree Construction Phase** — Performs **two database scans** to build a compact, in-memory, tree-structured summary of the original transaction database.
2. **Frequent Itemset Mining Phase** — Recursively constructs **conditional FP-trees** for each frequent item (starting from the least frequent) and mines them to extract the complete set of frequent itemsets.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why FP-Growth matters for KTU 2024 (Data Mining):** The Apriori algorithm suffers from two crippling bottlenecks — (a) exponential candidate generation, and (b) repeated, expensive database scans. FP-Growth overcomes *both*. It scans the database **only twice** and produces **no candidate itemsets**, making it typically an **order of magnitude faster** than Apriori on dense datasets.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library Dewey Decimal" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — A Highly Organised Library**
> Imagine you are a librarian asked, *"Which *pairs* of novels are most often borrowed together?"*

> - **The Apriori Way:** You walk through the library *again and again*, picking tentative pairs of books (candidates) and counting how many times each pair is co-borrowed. Every new pair forces another walk-through. You waste enormous time on pairs that nobody borrows.
> - **The FP-Growth Way:** First, you build a **single index card for every borrower** listing the *frequently borrowed* books in a fixed priority order. Then you weave all those index cards into **one tree of overlapping shelves** (the FP-Tree) — common book-borrowing sequences share the same shelf. To find co-borrowed pairs ending with, say, "Harry Potter", you simply **lift only Harry Potter's shelf and look backward** along it. You never re-walk the whole library, and you never invent a pair that doesn't exist.

The "tree" keeps the database *compressed and queryable at the same time*. That is precisely the spirit of the FP-Tree.

## 1.3 Key Data-Structure Vocabulary (KTU Must-Know Terms)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **FP-Tree** | A compact prefix-tree that stores the *quantitative* (count-incremented) and *qualitative* (item-ordered) information of the database. |
| **Root Node** | A virtual node labelled `null` that anchors the tree; it carries no item. |
| **Item Prefix Subtree** | The set of tree nodes whose path from the root corresponds to a transaction's sorted frequent items. |
| **Node Count (Support)** | Each non-root node holds a `count` field equal to the number of transactions that pass through that branch. |
| **Header Table (Item Pointer Table)** | A global table with one entry per frequent 1-itemset listing its **support count** and a **head-of-node-link** to the first node in the FP-Tree carrying that item. |
| **Node-Link Chain** | A horizontal `next` link from each node to the next node in the FP-Tree carrying the *same* item, ending at `null`. |
| **Conditional Pattern Base (CPB)** | For a target item $a_i$, the set of *prefix paths* of all nodes carrying $a_i$, with their counts adjusted to the corresponding node's count. |
| **Conditional FP-Tree** | The FP-Tree built *only* from the conditional pattern base of an item. |
| **Frequent Pattern Generation** | A path of length $\geq 1$ in any FP-Tree (or conditional FP-Tree), whose minimum support is satisfied. |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why FP-Growth Outperforms Apriori — A Logical Decomposition

- **Candidate Generation Cost Eliminated:** Apriori generates $\approx 2^k$ candidates for a $k$-itemset. For $k=20$, that is more than one million candidates. FP-Growth generates **zero** candidates.
- **Database Scans Reduced from $k$ to 2:** Apriori needs one scan per pattern length $k$. FP-Growth needs *exactly two* scans regardless of $k$.
- **Compactness via Prefix Sharing:** Transactions that share a common prefix (e.g., $\{I2, I1, I3\}$ and $\{I2, I1, I5\}$) share the same path in the tree. The count is *incremented*, not duplicated. Empirical studies report **compression ratios of 100:1 or more** on dense retail data.
- **Divide-and-Conquer Search:** The recursion is over a *shrinking* conditional pattern base. Each recursion typically works on a *strict subset* of the previous pattern base, giving the algorithm near-linear scalability on dense data.

> [!NOTE]
> **Apriori vs. FP-Growth at a Glance (Board Favourite Comparison Table)**
>
> | Criterion | Apriori | FP-Growth |
> |---|---|---|
> | Candidate generation | Yes (extensive) | **No** |
> | Number of database scans | $k$ passes | **2 passes** |
> | Data structure | Transaction DB | **FP-Tree** |
> | Memory usage | Low (per pass) | Moderate–High (whole tree) |
> | Speed on dense data | Slow | **Very fast** |
> | Sensitivity to support threshold | High | Lower |

## 2.2 The Two-Phase Operational Logic

### Phase I — FP-Tree Construction

1. **Scan 1 — Frequency Discovery:** Compute the support of every 1-itemset. Discard items with support < `min_sup`. Sort the surviving frequent items in **descending support order**, breaking ties **lexicographically**. Call this the **F-list** (or frequent-item list).
2. **Scan 2 — Tree Building:** For each transaction $T$ of the database:
   - Extract the frequent items from $T$ that survived Scan 1.
   - Sort them according to the global F-list order.
   - Insert the sorted transaction into the FP-Tree, incrementing the count of existing shared prefixes; otherwise, create new child nodes with `count = 1`.
   - Update the **header table** so that each item's node-link chain records the *most recently inserted* node carrying that item.

### Phase II — Recursive Frequent Itemset Mining

For each item $a_i$ in the F-list, processed in **ascending** order of support (i.e., from the *least* frequent to the *most* frequent):

1. **Build the Conditional Pattern Base of $a_i$** by traversing the **node-link chain** of $a_i$. For each node, walk back to the root via parent links; this yields a *prefix path*. The count of that prefix path is the count stored at the $a_i$-node.
2. **Build the Conditional FP-Tree of $a_i$** by:
   - Counting the support of all items appearing in the conditional pattern base.
   - Keeping only items whose support $\geq$ `min_sup`.
   - Reordering them per the F-list order.
   - Inserting the prefix paths (with their adjusted counts) into a *new* FP-Tree (the conditional FP-Tree).
3. **Recurse** on this conditional FP-Tree. The recursion bottoms out when the conditional FP-Tree is either:
   - **A single path $P$** — every combination of nodes along $P$ (of length $\geq 1$) is a frequent itemset, yielding $2^{\vert P \vert} - 1$ frequent patterns.
   - **Empty** — no more patterns can be formed for this branch.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Single-Path Pruning Rule (KTU Board Favourite):** When a conditional FP-Tree consists of a single root-to-leaf path, do *not* recurse further. Enumerate all $2^{\vert P \vert} - 1$ non-empty subsets of that path; each is a frequent itemset. This is the algorithm's most elegant "shortcut" and a common KTU question.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Range | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Support of an itemset $X$** | $\text{sup}(X) = \frac{\text{count}(X)}{\vert D \vert}$ | $0 \leq \text{sup}(X) \leq 1$ (or absolute count) | $\|D\|$ is the total transaction count. |
| 2 | **Minimum support threshold** | $\text{min\_sup}$ (absolute) or $\text{min\_sup} \cdot \vert D \vert$ (count form) | Integer $\geq 1$ | Set by the analyst. |
| 3 | **Confidence of rule $X \Rightarrow Y$** | $\text{conf}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \dfrac{\text{sup}(X \cup Y)}{\text{sup}(X)}$ | $0 \leq \text{conf} \leq 1$ | Used *after* mining frequent itemsets. |
| 4 | **Lift of a rule** | $\text{lift}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \dfrac{\text{conf}(X \Rightarrow Y)}{\text{sup}(Y)}$ | $\text{lift} > 1 \Rightarrow$ positively correlated | Statistical strength measure. |
| 5 | **Candidate count (Apriori, for comparison)** | $C_k \approx \sum_{j=1}^{k} \binom{\vert L_1 \vert}{j}$ | Combinations | Apriori blows up here. FP-Growth = 0. |
| 6 | **Tree-compression factor** | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{\vert D \vert \cdot \text{avg}(\vert T \vert)}{\vert \text{FP-Tree nodes} \vert}$ | Ratio $\geq 1$ | Empirical: 10 to 100+ on dense data. |
| 7 | **Single-path enumeration yield** | $N_{\text{patterns}} = 2^{\vert P \vert} - 1$ | Integer $\geq 1$ | If $P$ has $n$ nodes, that many patterns are emitted. |
| 8 | **Node count of any item in CPB** | $\text{cnt}(i) = \sum_{\text{paths }p \in \text{CPB}} \text{cnt}(p)$ | Integer | Used to filter CPB into a conditional FP-tree. |
| 9 | **Apriori's *downward closure* property** | If $X$ is frequent, every non-empty subset of $X$ is frequent | Set-theoretic | Underpins pruning in *both* Apriori and FP-Growth. |
| 10 | **Database scan count (FP-Growth)** | **2 scans** (regardless of $k$) | Constant | Compare with Apriori's $k$ scans. |

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Retail & Market-Basket Analysis:** Product recommendation engines (e.g., "Customers who bought X also bought Y") rely on FP-Growth to mine co-purchase patterns from billions of receipts.
- **Web Usage Mining:** Server-log clickstream analysis to discover navigation patterns and personalise websites.
- **Bioinformatics & Genomics:** Mining frequent subsequences in DNA/RNA strings, gene-expression co-occurrence.
- **Telecom Churn Analysis:** Detecting combinations of call-detail features that signal imminent customer churn.
- **Fraud Detection in Banking:** Mining frequent transactional motifs that, in deviation, flag fraudulent behaviour.
- **Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS):** Snort/Suricata-style signature generation from system-call sequences.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix — A Worked Numerical Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example Setup — KTU Board Style**
> Let the transaction database $D$ contain 9 transactions, and let the **minimum support threshold** be `min_sup = 2` (absolute count).
>
> | TID | Items Bought |
> |---|---|
> | T1 | I1, I2, I5 |
> | T2 | I2, I4 |
> | T3 | I2, I3 |
> | T4 | I1, I2, I4 |
> | T5 | I1, I3 |
> | T6 | I2, I3 |
> | T7 | I1, I3 |
> | T8 | I1, I2, I3, I5 |
> | T9 | I1, I2, I3 |

## 3.1 Phase I-A — Scan 1: Compute 1-Itemset Supports

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{sup}(I1) &= \vert\{T1, T4, T5, T7, T8, T9\}\vert = 6 \\
\text{sup}(I2) &= \vert\{T1, T2, T3, T4, T6, T8, T9\}\vert = 7 \\
\text{sup}(I3) &= \vert\{T3, T5, T6, T7, T8, T9\}\vert = 6 \\
\text{sup}(I4) &= \vert\{T2, T4\}\vert = 2 \\
\text{sup}(I5) &= \vert\{T1, T8\}\vert = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

All five items satisfy `min_sup = 2`. Hence the **F-list** (sorted by descending support, then lexicographic) is:

$$
F\text{-list} \;=\; \{\,I2 : 7,\; I1 : 6,\; I3 : 6,\; I4 : 2,\; I5 : 2\,\}
$$

## 3.2 Phase I-B — Scan 2: Filter and Reorder Each Transaction

For each transaction, keep only frequent items and sort by F-list order:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T1 &= \{I2, I1, I5\} \\
T2 &= \{I2, I4\} \\
T3 &= \{I2, I3\} \\
T4 &= \{I2, I1, I4\} \\
T5 &= \{I1, I3\} \\
T6 &= \{I2, I3\} \\
T7 &= \{I1, I3\} \\
T8 &= \{I2, I1, I3, I5\} \\
T9 &= \{I2, I1, I3\}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** Since I3 (count 6) and I1 (count 6) are tied, the F-list is ordered by *lexicographic* tie-break — the order shown is the standard result. The *order in which ties are broken does not affect correctness*, only the *shape* of the FP-Tree.

## 3.3 Phase I-C — FP-Tree Construction (Step-by-Step Insertion)

Start with the **root node** `null` and an **empty header table**.

### Step 1: Insert T1 = (I2 → I1 → I5)

Create a fresh child chain off the root. Each new node starts with `count = 1`.

- Create **node I2** (count 1) as root's child.
- Create **node I1** (count 1) as I2's child.
- Create **node I5** (count 1) as I1's child.

### Step 2: Insert T2 = (I2 → I4)

- Node I2 already exists as root's child. **Increment** its count: I2 → 2.
- I4 is not a child of I2. Create **node I4** (count 1) as I2's child.

### Step 3: Insert T3 = (I2 → I3)

- Increment I2: I2 → 3.
- Create **node I3** (count 1) as I2's child.

### Step 4: Insert T4 = (I2 → I1 → I4)

- Increment I2: I2 → 4.
- I1 is a child of I2? **No** (T1's I1 hangs under I2 already → yes, it *is* a child). **Increment** I1: I1 → 2.
- Create **node I4** (count 1) as I1's child.

### Step 5: Insert T5 = (I1 → I3)

- I1 is *not* a child of root (only I2 is). Create a new branch: **node I1** (count 1) as root's child.
- Create **node I3** (count 1) as I1's child.

### Step 6: Insert T6 = (I2 → I3)

- Increment I2: I2 → 5.
- I3 is a child of I2. **Increment** it: I3 (under I2) → 2.

### Step 7: Insert T7 = (I1 → I3)

- Walk to root's I1 child. The I1 created in Step 5 has count 1. **Increment** it: I1 (top branch) → 2.
- I3 is a child of that I1. **Increment** it: I3 (under top I1) → 2.

### Step 8: Insert T8 = (I2 → I1 → I3 → I5)

- Increment I2: I2 → 6.
- Increment I1 (under I2): I1 → 3.
- Increment I3 (under I1-under-I2): I3 → 3.
- Create **node I5** (count 1) as I3's child.

### Step 9: Insert T9 = (I2 → I1 → I3)

- Increment I2: I2 → 7.
- Increment I1 (under I2): I1 → 4.
- Increment I3 (under I1-under-I2): I3 → 4.

### Final Header Table (with node-link head pointers)

| Item | Support | Head of Node-Link |
|---|---|---|
| I2 | 7 | Node [I2:7] |
| I1 | 6 | Node [I1:4] (under I2) → Node [I1:2] (under root) |
| I3 | 6 | Node [I3:4] → Node [I3:2] → Node [I3:2] |
| I4 | 2 | Node [I4:1] (under I1-under-I2) → Node [I4:1] (under I2) |
| I5 | 2 | Node [I5:1] → Node [I5:1] |

The fully constructed FP-Tree is rendered in **Section 4** as a Mermaid schematic.

## 3.4 Phase II — Recursive Mining (Process items in *ascending* support order: I5, I4, I3, I1, I2)

### 3.4.1 Mine patterns ending in **I5** (support = 2)

Traverse the I5 node-link chain. There are **two** I5 nodes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Node A} &: \; [I5:1] \text{ at path } I2 \rightarrow I1 \rightarrow I3 \rightarrow I5 \\
\text{Node B} &: \; [I5:1] \text{ at path } I2 \rightarrow I1 \rightarrow I5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conditional Pattern Base of I5** (each prefix path weighted by the I5-node's count):

$$
\text{CPB}(I5) \;=\; \{\,(I2, I1, I3) : 1,\; (I2, I1) : 1\,\}
$$

**Build Conditional FP-Tree of I5:**

Item counts in CPB:
- I2 → 1 + 1 = **2** ✓ (≥ min_sup = 2)
- I1 → 1 + 1 = **2** ✓
- I3 → 1 = **1** ✗ (dropped)

The conditional FP-Tree of I5 has a **single path**: $I2 \rightarrow I1$. By the single-path rule:

$$
\text{Frequent patterns ending in } I5 \;=\; \{\,\{I2, I5\},\; \{I1, I5\},\; \{I2, I1, I5\}\,\}
$$

### 3.4.2 Mine patterns ending in **I4** (support = 2)

Two I4 nodes exist (each count 1). Their prefix paths are:

$$
\text{CPB}(I4) \;=\; \{\,(I2, I1) : 1,\; (I2) : 1\,\}
$$

Item counts: I2 = 1 + 1 = **2** ✓; I1 = 1. Drop I1. Conditional FP-Tree is a **single node**: I2.

$$
\text{Frequent patterns ending in } I4 \;=\; \{\,\{I2, I4\}\,\}
$$

### 3.4.3 Mine patterns ending in **I3** (support = 6)

Three I3 nodes; their prefix paths and counts:

$$
\text{CPB}(I3) \;=\; \{\,(I2, I1) : 4,\; (I1) : 2,\; (I2) : 2\,\}
$$

Wait — recount carefully using the counts we computed:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Node [I3:4] under } I2 \rightarrow I1 &\Rightarrow \text{prefix } (I2, I1), \text{ count } 4 \\
\text{Node [I3:2] under } I2 \text{ (the one from T6)} &\Rightarrow \text{prefix } (I2), \text{ count } 2 \\
\text{Node [I3:2] under root } \rightarrow I1 &\Rightarrow \text{prefix } (I1), \text{ count } 2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\text{CPB}(I3) \;=\; \{\,(I2, I1) : 4,\; (I2) : 2,\; (I1) : 2\,\}
$$

**Build Conditional FP-Tree of I3:**

Item counts in CPB:
- I2 → 4 + 2 = **6** ✓
- I1 → 4 + 2 = **6** ✓

Conditional FP-Tree of I3 = single path: $I2 \rightarrow I1$. By single-path rule:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Frequent patterns ending in } I3 &= \text{all non-empty subsets of } \{I2, I1\} \cup \{I3\} \\
&= \{\,\{I3\},\; \{I2, I3\},\; \{I1, I3\},\; \{I2, I1, I3\}\,\}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4.4 Mine patterns ending in **I1** (support = 6)

Two I1 nodes (under I2 and under root). Prefix paths:

$$
\text{CPB}(I1) \;=\; \{\,(I2) : 4,\; \emptyset : 2\,\}
$$

Item counts: I2 = **4** ✓. Drop the empty prefix.

Conditional FP-Tree of I1 = single node I2.

$$
\text{Frequent patterns ending in } I1 \;=\; \{\,\{I1\},\; \{I2, I1\}\,\}
$$

### 3.4.5 Mine patterns ending in **I2** (support = 7)

I2 has only the root as a prefix → CPB is empty → no new patterns.

$$
\text{Frequent patterns ending in } I2 \;=\; \{\,\{I2\}\,\}
$$

## 3.5 Master Frequent-Itemset Result Table

| Frequent Itemset | Support (count) |
|---|---|
| {I2} | 7 |
| {I1} | 6 |
| {I3} | 6 |
| {I4} | 2 |
| {I5} | 2 |
| {I2, I1} | 4 |
| {I2, I3} | 4 |
| {I2, I4} | 2 |
| {I2, I5} | 2 |
| {I1, I3} | 4 |
| {I1, I5} | 2 |
| {I2, I1, I3} | 4 |
| {I2, I1, I5} | 2 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total = 13 frequent itemsets** for `min_sup = 2`. Note that the algorithm produced these *without ever generating a single candidate itemset* — every set above was discovered by *extending* a real prefix path in the (conditional) FP-Tree.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Complexity Comparison

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Time}_{\text{Apriori}} &\;\approx\; \sum_{k=1}^{n} \vert C_k \vert \cdot \vert D \vert \quad\text{(candidate generation + DB scan per level)} \\
\text{Time}_{\text{FP-Growth}} &\;\approx\; 2 \cdot \vert D \vert \;+\; \sum_{a_i \in F} \bigl(\,N_{\text{CPB}} \cdot \text{cost}_{\text{build}} \,\bigr) \\
\text{Space}_{\text{FP-Tree}} &\;\propto\; \text{number of distinct frequent prefix-paths (often } \ll \vert D \vert \text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $N_{\text{CPB}}$ is the size of the conditional pattern base for item $a_i$. Crucially, the inner recursion typically operates on *shrinking* databases.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 FP-Tree Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    classDef root fill:#1f2937,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef i2 fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,color:#000000
    classDef i1 fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#15803d,color:#000000
    classDef i3 fill:#bae6fd,stroke:#0369a1,color:#000000
    classDef i4 fill:#fbcfe8,stroke:#9d174d,color:#000000
    classDef i5 fill:#fecaca,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#000000

    ROOT((NULL ROOT)):::root

    N2A["I2 : 7"]:::i2
    N1A["I1 : 4"]:::i1
    N3A["I3 : 4"]:::i3
    N5A["I5 : 1"]:::i5
    N4A["I4 : 1"]:::i4

    N1B["I1 : 2"]:::i1
    N3B["I3 : 2"]:::i3

    N3C["I3 : 2"]:::i3

    N4D["I4 : 1"]:::i4

    ROOT --> N2A
    N2A --> N1A
    N1A --> N3A
    N3A --> N5A
    N1A --> N4A
    N2A --> N1B
    N1B --> N3B
    N2A --> N3C
    ROOT --> N4D

    HT2["Header: I2 -> 7 -> N2A"]:::i2
    HT1["Header: I1 -> 6 -> N1A -> N1B"]:::i1
    HT3["Header: I3 -> 6 -> N3A -> N3B -> N3C"]:::i3
    HT4["Header: I4 -> 2 -> N4A -> N4D"]:::i4
    HT5["Header: I5 -> 2 -> N5A -> N5B"]:::i5

    N2A -.link.-> HT2
    N1A -.link.-> HT1
    N1B -.link.-> HT1
    N3A -.link.-> HT3
    N3B -.link.-> HT3
    N3C -.link.-> HT3
    N4A -.link.-> HT4
    N4D -.link.-> HT4
    N5A -.link.-> HT5
```

> [!NOTE]
> The dotted arrows represent the **horizontal node-link chain** that connects every node carrying the same item, anchored at the header-table entry. During mining, we walk these chains *backwards to the root* to gather prefix paths.

## 4.2 FP-Growth End-to-End Process Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Raw Transaction Database D"] --> B["Scan 1: Compute support of all 1-itemsets"]
    B --> C["Discard items with sup < min_sup"]
    C --> D["Sort survivors: build F-list"]
    D --> E["Scan 2: Insert sorted transactions into FP-Tree"]
    E --> F["Build header table + node-link chains"]
    F --> G{"For each item a_i in F-list (ascending support)"}
    G --> H["Traverse node-link of a_i"]
    H --> I["Collect prefix paths -> Conditional Pattern Base CPB"]
    I --> J["Count items in CPB, drop those < min_sup"]
    J --> K{"Is conditional FP-Tree a single path?"}
    K -->|"YES"| L["Enumerate all 2^P - 1 subsets of path P"]
    K -->|"NO"| M["Recurse on conditional FP-Tree"]
    M --> G
    L --> N["Output: All frequent itemsets"]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Mining Engine

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph IN["INPUT LAYER"]
        DB[(Transaction Database D)]
        PARAM[/min_sup threshold/]
    end

    subgraph CORE["FP-GROWTH ENGINE"]
        S1[Scanner 1 - Frequency Counter]
        FL[Frequency Filter + F-list Builder]
        S2[Scanner 2 - Tree Inserter]
        TREE[(FP-Tree + Header Table)]
        MINER[Recursive Miner]
        CPT[(Conditional FP-Trees)]
    end

    subgraph OUT["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        FIS[Set of All Frequent Itemsets]
        RULES[Association Rules if min_conf given]
    end

    DB --> S1
    S1 --> FL
    PARAM --> FL
    FL --> S2
    DB --> S2
    S2 --> TREE
    TREE --> MINER
    MINER --> CPT
    CPT --> MINER
    MINER --> FIS
    FIS --> RULES
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to re-sort transactions** in F-list order before inserting into the tree. Without the F-list order, prefix-sharing does not happen and the FP-Tree explodes in size.
> 2. **Counting errors during conditional-pattern-base construction.** The count of a prefix path is *not* the count of the prefix itself; it is the count of the *target item-node* you are extending. Students routinely transpose these.
> 3. **Missing the single-path shortcut.** When asked "how many patterns does a single path of length $n$ yield?", the expected answer is $2^n - 1$, not $n$.
> 4. **Inverting the mining order.** The recursion mines items in *ascending* support order (least frequent first), not descending. Confusing this is a 2-mark deduction.
> 5. **Omitting the support count next to each itemset** in the final answer table. KTU examiners explicitly check for the count column.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the FP-Tree data structure. Mention any two advantages it offers over the Apriori algorithm's candidate-generation approach.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> An **FP-Tree (Frequent Pattern Tree)** is a compact, prefix-tree data structure introduced by Han, Pei, and Yin (2000) that stores the entire transaction database in a compressed form. Each node represents a single frequent item and carries a `count` field equal to the number of transactions passing through it. A global **header table** with item pointers and a **node-link chain** connect all nodes carrying the same item.
>
> **Advantages over Apriori (any two):**
> 1. **No candidate generation** — FP-Growth mines patterns directly from the tree, completely avoiding the exponential blow-up of $C_k$ that plagues Apriori.
> 2. **Reduced database scans** — the database is scanned only **twice**, whereas Apriori needs $k$ scans (one per pattern length).
> 3. **Compact storage** — overlapping transactions share a single prefix, giving compression ratios of 10:1 to 100:1 on dense data.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]**
**What is a Conditional Pattern Base? How is a Conditional FP-Tree constructed from it?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The **Conditional Pattern Base (CPB)** of a frequent item $a_i$ is the collection of *prefix paths* of all nodes in the FP-Tree that contain $a_i$, where the count of each prefix is the count stored in the corresponding $a_i$-node. The CPB represents "everything that co-occurs with $a_i$" in the original database.
>
> **Construction of the Conditional FP-Tree:**
> 1. Count the support of every item in the CPB.
> 2. Discard items whose support falls below `min_sup`.
> 3. Sort the surviving items in F-list order.
> 4. Insert each prefix path (with its adjusted count) into a *fresh* FP-Tree, called the **Conditional FP-Tree of $a_i$**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme: Part B questions carry **14 marks** with **module-level internal choice**. Solve **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]*

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the construction of the **FP-Tree** for the following transaction database using `min_sup = 3`. Clearly show the header table and the node-link chains. **\[7 marks\]**

| TID | Items |
|---|---|
| T1 | I1, I2, I5 |
| T2 | I2, I4 |
| T3 | I2, I3 |
| T4 | I1, I2, I4 |
| T5 | I1, I3 |
| T6 | I2, I3 |
| T7 | I1, I3 |
| T8 | I1, I2, I3, I5 |
| T9 | I1, I2, I3 |

**(b)** Using the FP-Tree constructed in part (a), mine all the **frequent itemsets** with `min_sup = 3` using the FP-Growth algorithm. Show the **conditional pattern base** and the **conditional FP-tree** for each item. **\[7 marks\]**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Step 1: Scan 1 — Support Counting**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{sup}(I1) &= 6,\quad \text{sup}(I2) = 7,\quad \text{sup}(I3) = 6, \\
\text{sup}(I4) &= 2,\quad \text{sup}(I5) = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

Items below `min_sup = 3`: **I4 (2), I5 (2)** — **discarded**.

**F-list** (descending support, lexicographic tie-break): $\{I2, I1, I3\}$.

**Step 2: Reorder and insert transactions**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T1 &= \{I2, I1\} \quad(\text{I5 dropped}) \\
T2 &= \emptyset \quad(\text{all dropped}) \\
T3 &= \{I2, I3\} \\
T4 &= \{I2, I1\} \quad(\text{I4 dropped}) \\
T5 &= \{I1, I3\} \\
T6 &= \{I2, I3\} \\
T7 &= \{I1, I3\} \\
T8 &= \{I2, I1, I3\} \quad(\text{I5 dropped}) \\
T9 &= \{I2, I1, I3\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Resulting FP-Tree and Header Table**

```
                NULL
                 |
                I2 : 7
               /         \
           I1 : 4       I3 : 2
           |              |
         I3 : 4         (null)
        /
    I1 : 2
       |
     I3 : 2
```

**Header Table:**

| Item | Support | Node-link chain |
|---|---|---|
| I2 | 7 | → N(I2:7) |
| I1 | 6 | → N(I1:4) → N(I1:2) |
| I3 | 6 | → N(I3:4) → N(I3:2) → N(I3:2) |

**Part (b) — Mine in ascending support order: I3 (6), I1 (6), I2 (7)**

**1) Patterns ending in I3 (support 6):**

Three I3 nodes with prefix paths:

$$
\text{CPB}(I3) = \{(I2, I1) : 4,\;\; (I1) : 2,\;\; (I2) : 2\}
$$

Item counts: I1 = 4 + 2 = 6 ✓, I2 = 4 + 2 = 6 ✓.

Conditional FP-Tree of I3 = **single path** I2 → I1.

Enumerate $2^2 - 1 = 3$ non-empty subsets and append I3:

$$
\{\{I2, I3\},\; \{I1, I3\},\; \{I2, I1, I3\}\} \quad \text{supports: } 4, 4, 4
$$

Plus the singleton $\{I3\}$.

**2) Patterns ending in I1 (support 6):**

Two I1 nodes (under I2: count 4; under root: count 2):

$$
\text{CPB}(I1) = \{(I2) : 4,\;\; \emptyset : 2\}
$$

Drop the empty prefix. I2 count = 4 ✓. Conditional FP-Tree = single node I2.

$$
\{\{I1\},\; \{I2, I1\}\} \quad \text{supports: } 6, 4
$$

**3) Patterns ending in I2 (support 7):**

I2's only prefix is the root → CPB is empty → no new patterns beyond $\{I2\}$.

**Final Frequent Itemsets @ min_sup = 3:**

| Itemset | Support |
|---|---|
| {I2} | 7 |
| {I1} | 6 |
| {I3} | 6 |
| {I2, I1} | 4 |
| {I2, I3} | 4 |
| {I1, I3} | 4 |
| {I2, I1, I3} | 4 |

**Total: 7 frequent itemsets** (versus the **13** we obtained at `min_sup = 2` in the worked example — illustrating the monotonicity of frequent itemset growth as support decreases).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]*

**(a)** Compare the **Apriori algorithm** and the **FP-Growth algorithm** with respect to (i) candidate generation, (ii) number of database scans, (iii) memory usage, and (iv) performance on dense versus sparse datasets. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** Construct the **FP-Tree** for the following transaction database with `min_sup = 2` and list all the **frequent itemsets** generated using the FP-Growth algorithm. Show the **conditional pattern base** and **conditional FP-tree** for at least two items. **\[7 marks\]**

| TID | Items |
|---|---|
| T1 | A, B, E |
| T2 | B, D |
| T3 | B, C |
| T4 | A, B, D |
| T5 | A, C |
| T6 | B, C |
| T7 | A, C |
| T8 | A, B, C, E |
| T9 | A, B, C |

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Comparative Analysis**

| Criterion | Apriori | FP-Growth |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Candidate Generation** | Generates $C_k$ at every level; suffers from combinatorial explosion ($2^k$ candidates possible for $k$-itemsets). | **No candidate generation**; mining is performed directly on the compressed tree. |
| **(ii) Database Scans** | Requires **$k$ scans** (one per pattern length). | Requires only **2 scans**, regardless of $k$. |
| **(iii) Memory Usage** | Low per pass (only the current $C_k$ in memory). | Higher overall (FP-Tree + header table + multiple conditional FP-Trees in recursion). |
| **(iv) Performance** | Slow on **dense** data (many long frequent patterns → huge $C_k$). | **Very fast on dense data** (high prefix sharing). On very **sparse** data, the FP-Tree can be bushy and Apriori may be competitive. |

**Verdict:** FP-Growth is preferred when (a) the dataset is dense, (b) `min_sup` is low, or (c) the longest frequent pattern is long.

**Part (b) — FP-Tree Construction with min_sup = 2**

**Scan 1 — Item Supports:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{sup}(A) &= 6,\;\; \text{sup}(B) = 7,\;\; \text{sup}(C) = 6, \\
\text{sup}(D) &= 2,\;\; \text{sup}(E) = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

All ≥ 2, so all five items are frequent.

**F-list** (descending support, lexicographic): $\{B, A, C, D, E\}$.

**Scan 2 — Reordered transactions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T1 &= \{B, A, E\} \\
T2 &= \{B, D\} \\
T3 &= \{B, C\} \\
T4 &= \{B, A, D\} \\
T5 &= \{A, C\} \\
T6 &= \{B, C\} \\
T7 &= \{A, C\} \\
T8 &= \{B, A, C, E\} \\
T9 &= \{B, A, C\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**FP-Tree after all insertions:**

```
                       NULL
                        |
                       B : 7
                      /     \
                  A : 4       C : 2
                 /     \
              C : 4     D : 1
              |
             E : 1

       A : 2   (under root)
        |
       C : 2
```

**Header Table:**

| Item | Support | Node-link |
|---|---|---|
| B | 7 | → N(B:7) |
| A | 6 | → N(A:4) → N(A:2) |
| C | 6 | → N(C:4) → N(C:2) → N(C:2) |
| D | 2 | → N(D:1) → N(D:1) |
| E | 2 | → N(E:1) → N(E:0) |

**Mining in ascending support order: E (2), D (2), C (6), A (6), B (7)**

**(i) Patterns ending in E:**

Two E-nodes (T1: B → A → E, T8: B → A → C → E):

$$
\text{CPB}(E) = \{(B, A, C) : 1,\;\; (B, A) : 1\}
$$

Item counts: B = 2, A = 2, C = 1 → drop C.

Conditional FP-Tree of E = single path B → A.

$$
\text{Patterns ending in E} = \{\{A, E\},\; \{B, E\},\; \{A, B, E\}\} \quad \text{sup: } 2, 2, 2
$$

**(ii) Patterns ending in D:**

Two D-nodes (T2: B → D, T4: B → A → D):

$$
\text{CPB}(D) = \{(B, A) : 1,\;\; (B) : 1\}
$$

Item counts: B = 2, A = 1 → drop A. Conditional FP-Tree of D = single node B.

$$
\text{Patterns ending in D} = \{\{B, D\}\} \quad \text{sup: } 2
$$

**Final Frequent Itemsets @ min_sup = 2:**

| Itemset | Support |
|---|---|
| {B} | 7 |
| {A} | 6 |
| {C} | 6 |
| {D} | 2 |
| {E} | 2 |
| {B, A} | 4 |
| {B, C} | 4 |
| {A, C} | 4 |
| {B, E} | 2 |
| {A, E} | 2 |
| {B, D} | 2 |
| {B, A, C} | 4 |
| {B, A, E} | 2 |

**Total: 13 frequent itemsets** at `min_sup = 2`.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Pin This Before the Exam**
>
> ✅ **FP-Growth = "Frequent Pattern Growth"** by Han, Pei, Yin (2000). Two-phase, divide-and-conquer.
>
> ✅ **Two database scans only**, regardless of the longest frequent pattern.
>
> ✅ **No candidate generation** — this is the algorithm's biggest selling point.
>
> ✅ **FP-Tree node** = (item-name, count, parent-pointer, child-pointers, next-node-link).
>
> ✅ **Header Table** = one entry per frequent item, with its total support and a head pointer to the first node in the FP-Tree carrying that item.
>
> ✅ **F-list ordering** is by *descending support*, ties broken *lexicographically*; transactions are sorted in this order before insertion.
>
> ✅ **Mining order** is the *reverse* of the F-list — *ascending* support (least frequent first).
>
> ✅ **Conditional Pattern Base (CPB)** = prefix paths of all nodes carrying item $a_i$, with prefix-count = count of the $a_i$-node (not the prefix's own count).
>
> ✅ **Conditional FP-Tree** is built by counting items in the CPB, dropping those < `min_sup`, reordering in F-list, and inserting prefix paths.
>
> ✅ **Single-Path Pruning Rule** — if the conditional FP-Tree is a single path $P$ of length $n$, emit $2^n - 1$ frequent patterns by taking every non-empty subset of $P$ and appending the target item.
>
> ✅ **Recursion terminates** when the conditional FP-Tree is either empty or a single path.
>
> ✅ **Memory vs. Speed trade-off** — FP-Growth uses more memory than Apriori but is much faster on dense data with low support thresholds.
>
> ✅ **Compression comes from prefix-sharing** — two transactions with a common prefix share a single branch, with the shared count incremented.
>
> ✅ **Support Monotonicity** — the set of frequent itemsets only *grows* as `min_sup` is *lowered*; never shrinks. (Useful for cross-checking answers.)
>
> ✅ **Common KTU pitfalls:** (1) forgetting the F-list sort, (2) wrong prefix-count in CPB, (3) missing the $2^n - 1$ shortcut, (4) mining in the wrong order, (5) omitting the support column.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Model Evaluation and Validation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT524 - DATA MINING AND WAREHOUSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Model Evaluation and Validation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Model Evaluation and Validation** is the systematic process of assessing a predictive or descriptive data mining model's performance using quantitative metrics, statistical procedures, and resampling techniques to estimate its generalization capability on unseen data. It quantifies the reliability, robustness, and predictive power of a model before it is deployed for real-world decision-making.

In the context of KTU 2024 scheme (Outcome-Based Education under NEP 2020), model evaluation is positioned as a **post-modeling phase activity** that bridges the gap between training accuracy and real-world utility. The KTU syllabus frames it under the Course Outcome: *CO3 — Evaluate and validate data mining models using appropriate metrics and resampling techniques*.

The two fundamental pillars are:

- **Evaluation:** Measuring how well a model performs on labeled data using standard metrics.
- **Validation:** Estimating how well a model will perform on unseen data using statistical resampling methods (e.g., k-fold cross-validation, bootstrap, holdout).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The syllabus explicitly demands understanding of *confusion matrix metrics*, *ROC/AUC analysis*, *k-fold cross-validation*, and *bias-variance tradeoff*. These are high-frequency, high-weight topics in KTU End Semester Examinations (ESE).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have built a **medical diagnostic machine** that predicts whether a patient has a disease based on blood test parameters. Before deploying it in a hospital, you must answer two critical questions:

1. **Evaluation** — *How accurate was the machine on the 1000 test patients it has already seen?* (Did it correctly classify the sick and the healthy? Did it miss any actual patients — these are *false negatives* that could cost lives?)
2. **Validation** — *How will the machine behave on the 50,000 new patients it has never seen?* (Will it maintain its performance? Or will it fail catastrophically in a different city with different demographics?)

The **first question** is solved by **evaluation metrics** (Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score). The **second question** is solved by **validation techniques** (Cross-validation, Bootstrap, Holdout) which simulate the "unseen data" scenario from the limited labeled data you already have.

Think of evaluation as a **student's score on a practice test** (closed book, known questions) and validation as their **expected score on the final board exam** (unseen questions, real conditions). Validation estimates the *true* performance, while evaluation reports the *observed* performance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction:** Evaluation reports *observed* performance metrics on a specific dataset. Validation *estimates* the expected performance on the data distribution as a whole, accounting for sampling variability. Both are required for a complete assessment under KTU 2024 standards.

## 1.3 The Fundamental Question: Why Evaluate?

Without rigorous evaluation, a model is a "black box" whose value is unknown. Three real-world failure modes that evaluation prevents are:

1. **Overfitting Trap:** A model memorizes training data (99% training accuracy) but fails on new data (45% test accuracy). Cross-validation exposes this.
2. **Class Imbalance Blindness:** A fraud detection model predicting "no fraud" for all transactions has 99.9% accuracy — but is useless. Precision, Recall, and F1-Score expose this.
3. **Mis-calibrated Probability Output:** A weather model predicts 95% rain every day; it is sometimes right but cannot discriminate. ROC-AUC quantifies this discrimination ability.

The metrics covered below — **Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, Specificity, ROC, AUC, MAE, RMSE, R², and Cross-Validation Error** — are the standardized tools the data mining community uses to defend against these failure modes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Confusion Matrix — Foundation of Classification Metrics

The **Confusion Matrix** (also called the *Contingency Table* or *Error Matrix*) is the foundational construct from which all classification metrics are derived. For a binary classification problem, it is a 2×2 matrix:

|  | Predicted Positive | Predicted Negative |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive** | TP (True Positive) | FN (False Negative) |
| **Actual Negative** | FP (False Positive) | TN (True Negative) |

- **TP (True Positive):** Model correctly predicted positive class. E.g., correctly diagnosed a sick patient.
- **TN (True Negative):** Model correctly predicted negative class. E.g., correctly cleared a healthy patient.
- **FP (False Positive):** Type I Error — Model incorrectly predicted positive. E.g., healthy patient wrongly diagnosed as sick. (False alarm)
- **FN (False Negative):** Type II Error — Model incorrectly predicted negative. E.g., sick patient wrongly cleared as healthy. (Missed detection)

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic for Each Metric

### 2.2.1 Accuracy and Error Rate

Accuracy answers: *"Out of all predictions, how many were correct?"*

- **Intuition:** A simple, intuitive measure. A model with 95% accuracy is correct 95% of the time.
- **Limitation:** Highly misleading on imbalanced datasets. If 99% of emails are non-spam, a model that always predicts "non-spam" has 99% accuracy but is useless.
- **When to use:** Balanced classes and equal cost of errors.

### 2.2.2 Precision (Positive Predictive Value)

Precision answers: *"Of all the cases the model flagged as positive, how many were actually positive?"*

- **Intuition:** Measures the *quality* of positive predictions. High precision = few false alarms.
- **When to use:** When the cost of FP is high. E.g., spam filtering (you don't want legitimate emails marked as spam), drug approval (don't approve harmful drug).

### 2.2.3 Recall (Sensitivity, True Positive Rate, Hit Rate)

Recall answers: *"Of all actual positive cases, how many did the model catch?"*

- **Intuition:** Measures the *completeness* of detection. High recall = few missed positives.
- **When to use:** When the cost of FN is high. E.g., cancer diagnosis (don't miss any patient), fraud detection (don't miss any fraudster), security threat detection.

### 2.2.4 Specificity (True Negative Rate)

Specificity answers: *"Of all actual negative cases, how many did the model correctly identify?"*

### 2.2.5 F1-Score (Harmonic Mean of Precision and Recall)

F1-Score answers: *"What is the single best balance between precision and recall?"*

- **Intuition:** The harmonic mean penalizes extreme imbalances. A model with P=1.0 and R=0.1 has F1=0.18, not 0.55 (arithmetic mean). This forces both metrics to be reasonably high.
- **General form:** The Fβ-score weights recall β times more than precision. F1 is the special case where β=1.

### 2.2.6 ROC Curve and AUC

The **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve** plots **TPR (Recall)** on the Y-axis against **FPR (1 − Specificity)** on the X-axis, at various classification thresholds. The **Area Under the Curve (AUC)** summarizes the entire ROC curve as a single number.

- **Intuition:** AUC represents the probability that the model ranks a random positive instance higher than a random negative instance.
- **AUC = 1.0:** Perfect classifier.
- **AUC = 0.5:** Random guessing (diagonal line).
- **AUC < 0.5:** Worse than random (predictions are inverted).

## 2.3 Regression Metrics (For Continuous Target Variables)

When the target is continuous (e.g., predicting house prices), classification metrics are inapplicable. The following metrics apply:

- **MAE (Mean Absolute Error):** Average of absolute differences. Same units as target. Robust to outliers.
- **MSE (Mean Squared Error):** Average of squared differences. Penalizes large errors heavily. Different units (squared).
- **RMSE (Root Mean Squared Error):** Square root of MSE. Same units as target. Standard metric in KTU papers.
- **R² (Coefficient of Determination):** Proportion of variance explained by the model. Ranges from −∞ to 1. R²=1 means perfect prediction; R²=0 means no better than the mean.

## 2.4 Validation Techniques — Estimating Generalization

### 2.4.1 Holdout Method

The dataset is randomly split into two disjoint subsets — typically 70% training and 30% testing. The model is trained on the training set and evaluated on the test set. Simple, fast, but high variance (depends on the split).

### 2.4.2 K-Fold Cross-Validation (The KTU Gold Standard)

The dataset is divided into **K** equal-sized, mutually exclusive subsets ("folds"). The model is trained K times, each time using K−1 folds for training and the remaining 1 fold for testing. The final performance is the **average of the K test scores**.

- **Special Cases:**
  - K=2: Standard 2-fold cross-validation.
  - K=N (where N is dataset size): Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation (LOOCV). Low bias, high variance, computationally expensive.
  - K=10: Most common default in practice and the recommended KTU value.

### 2.4.3 Stratified K-Fold

A variant of K-fold that preserves the **class distribution** of the original dataset in each fold. Essential for imbalanced classification problems.

### 2.4.4 Bootstrap

Sampling with replacement to create B training sets of size N from the original N samples. Each bootstrap sample is used to train a model, and the out-of-bag (OOB) samples serve as the test set. Used to estimate confidence intervals and standard errors.

## 2.5 Bias-Variance Tradeoff (The KTU-Favorite Theoretical Concept)

The total expected prediction error of a model on unseen data decomposes into three components:

- **Bias²:** Error from wrong model assumptions (underfitting). High bias → model is too simple to capture patterns.
- **Variance:** Error from sensitivity to training data fluctuations (overfitting). High variance → model memorizes training noise.
- **Irreducible Error (σ²):** Noise inherent in the data. Cannot be reduced by any model.

The goal of model evaluation is to find the **sweet spot** — a model complexity that minimizes total error by balancing bias and variance.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Metric | Formula | Range / Units | Best Value | KTU Exam Weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Accuracy | $\dfrac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | $[0,1]$ or $[0\%,100\%]$ | Higher is better | Very High |
| Error Rate | $1 - Accuracy = \dfrac{FP+FN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | $[0,1]$ | Lower is better | High |
| Precision (PPV) | $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | $[0,1]$ | Higher is better | Very High |
| Recall (TPR/Sensitivity) | $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | $[0,1]$ | Higher is better | Very High |
| Specificity (TNR) | $\dfrac{TN}{TN+FP}$ | $[0,1]$ | Higher is better | High |
| F1-Score | $\dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P+R}$ | $[0,1]$ | Higher is better | Very High |
| Fβ-Score | $(1+\beta^2) \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{\beta^2 \cdot P + R}$ | $[0,1]$ | Higher is better | Medium |
| False Positive Rate (FPR) | $\dfrac{FP}{FP+TN} = 1 - \text{Specificity}$ | $[0,1]$ | Lower is better | High |
| MAE | $\dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$ | Same as $y$ | Lower is better | Medium |
| MSE | $\dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Squared units of $y$ | Lower is better | Medium |
| RMSE | $\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}$ | Same as $y$ | Lower is better | High |
| R² (Coefficient of Determination) | $1 - \dfrac{\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}$ | $(-\infty, 1]$ | Closer to 1 is better | High |
| AUC | $\int_0^1 TPR(FPR^{-1}(t)) \, dt$ | $[0,1]$ | Closer to 1 is better | Very High |
| K-Fold CV Error | $\dfrac{1}{K}\sum_{k=1}^{K} E_k$ | Same as base metric | Lower is better | Very High |
| Total Expected Error | $\text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$ | $\geq 0$ | Lower is better | Very High |
| Confidence (Precision) | $\dfrac{TP+FP}{TP+FP+TN+FN}$ | $[0,1]$ | — | Low (replaced by Precision) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note for KTU 2024:** When a confusion matrix is provided in a question, you are expected to compute *every* cell label first (TP, FP, TN, FN), and then plug into the formula. Skipping the cell labeling step is a common reason for losing 1–2 marks in ESE.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare:** Recall-critical for cancer screening (maximize TP, even at the cost of FP — extra confirmatory tests are acceptable).
- **Cybersecurity:** Precision-critical for intrusion detection systems (don't block legitimate traffic).
- **Financial Credit Scoring:** AUC used to rank applicants; a 0.05 AUC improvement can translate to millions in profit.
- **Recommendation Systems:** RMSE used to evaluate rating predictions; lower RMSE directly improves user engagement.
- **Weather Forecasting:** Cost-sensitive loss functions are evaluated using a weighted confusion matrix.
- **Manufacturing Quality Control:** Specificity is critical to avoid rejecting good products.
