topics.title: Text Mining Approaches
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT524 - DATA MINING AND WAREHOUSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Text Mining Approaches

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Text Mining (also known as Text Data Mining or Text Analytics)** is the semi-automated or fully-automated process of extracting high-quality, non-trivial, useful, novel, and previously unknown information from unstructured textual data. It is a sub-field of data mining, natural language processing (NLP), and information retrieval (IR), concerned with the discovery of patterns, trends, anomalies, sentiments, and knowledge hidden inside large volumes of text.

According to the KTU 2024 scheme, *Text Mining Approaches* refer to the set of computational methodologies employed to transform raw, free-form text into structured representations and to derive meaningful patterns, summaries, classifications, and associations from them. The discipline is built on the assumption that meaningful structures and patterns exist within text corpora, and that these can be discovered using statistical, linguistic, and machine-learning techniques.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Text mining is positioned as a critical extension of traditional data mining into the unstructured data domain. Approximately **80% of enterprise data is unstructured**, and text is the dominant form. Hence, mastering text mining approaches is a high-weightage topic for both Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a **massive library of 10 million letters** written in 50 different languages by people across the world, and you are asked: *"What is the world talking about? Who is happy? Who is angry? Which letters are similar?"* Reading each one manually would take a lifetime.

**Text mining is the equivalent of having a super-intelligent assistant** that:

1. **Reads** all the letters at lightning speed (text preprocessing & tokenization).
2. **Translates** them into numbers it can understand (feature extraction — TF-IDF, embeddings).
3. **Categorizes** them into folders (text classification).
4. **Groups** similar letters together (text clustering).
5. **Summarizes** each pile into a one-line gist (text summarization).
6. **Detects mood** of each letter (sentiment analysis).
7. **Identifies people, places, organizations** mentioned (Named Entity Recognition).

So, in plain English, **Text Mining is the science of turning words into wisdom**.

### 1.3 Why Text Mining is Different from Traditional Data Mining

| Aspect | Traditional Data Mining | Text Mining |
|---|---|---|
| **Data type** | Structured (tables, rows, columns) | Unstructured (free-flowing text) |
| **Features** | Pre-defined, numeric | Must be derived from text |
| **Dimensionality** | Low to medium | Extremely high (vocabulary size) |
| **Preprocessing** | Minimal | Extensive (tokenization, stemming, stop-word removal) |
| **Algorithms** | Decision trees, clustering, association rules | NLP + ML hybrid models |
| **Evaluation metrics** | Accuracy, MSE | Precision, Recall, F1, BLEU, ROUGE |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Recap:**
> - **Text Mining** = Discovery of knowledge from text.
> - **Text Analytics** = Application of analytical methods to derive insights.
> - **Information Retrieval (IR)** = Finding relevant documents for a query.
> - **Information Extraction (IE)** = Extracting structured entities/relations from text.
> - **NLP** = The underlying linguistic + computational engine that powers all of the above.

### 1.4 The Standard Text Mining Pipeline (High-Level View)

A typical text mining system follows a **6-stage pipeline**:

1. **Text Collection / Corpus Assembly** — Gather raw documents (web scraping, databases, APIs).
2. **Text Preprocessing** — Tokenization, stop-word removal, stemming, lemmatization.
3. **Feature Extraction / Representation** — Bag-of-Words (BoW), TF-IDF, Word2Vec, BERT.
4. **Model Application** — Classification, clustering, summarization, NER, topic modeling.
5. **Pattern Discovery / Knowledge Extraction** — Rules, topics, trends, associations.
6. **Evaluation & Interpretation** — Precision, Recall, F1, human judgment, domain validation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Major Text Mining Approaches — Detailed Breakdown

Text mining is a **multi-disciplinary field**. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the following primary approaches:

#### **A. Information Extraction (IE)**

The task of automatically extracting **structured information** (entities, relations, events) from unstructured text.

- **Sub-tasks:**
  - **Named Entity Recognition (NER):** Identifying persons, organizations, locations, dates, monetary values.
  - **Relation Extraction (RE):** Discovering semantic relationships between entities (e.g., "CEO of", "located in").
  - **Event Extraction:** Detecting events and their participants.
  - **Coreference Resolution:** Determining which pronouns/phrases refer to the same entity.

> **Why?** IE converts chaotic text into structured tables — the foundation of knowledge graphs, search engines, and question-answering systems.

#### **B. Text Categorization (Text Classification)**

Assigning predefined **category labels** to documents.

- **Binary classification:** Spam vs. Ham, Positive vs. Negative review.
- **Multi-class classification:** News article → Politics, Sports, Tech, Entertainment.
- **Multi-label classification:** A research paper may belong to both "AI" and "Healthcare".

**Common Algorithms:** Naïve Bayes, SVM, k-NN, Decision Trees, Random Forest, Deep Neural Networks (CNN, LSTM, Transformers).

#### **C. Text Clustering**

Grouping similar documents into **clusters without predefined labels** (unsupervised learning).

- **Algorithms:** K-Means, Hierarchical Clustering, DBSCAN, Self-Organizing Maps (SOM).
- **Use cases:** Document organization, topic discovery, duplicate detection.

#### **D. Topic Modeling**

Discovering **latent (hidden) topics** that pervade a large corpus of documents.

- **Models:**
  - **LSA (Latent Semantic Analysis)** — uses SVD on term-document matrix.
  - **LDA (Latent Dirichlet Allocation)** — probabilistic generative model.
  - **NMF (Non-negative Matrix Factorization)** — linear algebra-based decomposition.

#### **E. Sentiment Analysis (Opinion Mining)**

Determining the **emotional tone** (positive, negative, neutral) of a text.

- **Granularities:** Document-level, sentence-level, aspect-level.
- **Techniques:** Lexicon-based (VADER, SentiWordNet), ML-based, Deep Learning (BERT).

#### **F. Text Summarization**

Generating a **concise summary** that preserves the meaning of the original document.

- **Extractive Summarization:** Selecting key sentences verbatim (e.g., TextRank, LexRank).
- **Abstractive Summarization:** Generating novel sentences (e.g., Pointer-Generator Networks, BART, T5).

#### **G. Keyword Extraction & Tagging**

Identifying the most **informative words or phrases** that represent a document.

- **Methods:** TF-IDF, RAKE (Rapid Automatic Keyword Extraction), YAKE, KeyBERT.

#### **H. Information Retrieval (IR)**

Finding **relevant documents** that satisfy a user's information need expressed as a query.

- **Boolean Retrieval, Vector Space Model (VSM), BM25, Semantic Search.**

#### **I. Text Similarity & Semantic Analysis**

Computing how **similar two texts are** semantically (not just lexically).

- **Methods:** Cosine similarity on TF-IDF, Word Mover's Distance, Sentence-BERT embeddings.

### 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this cheat sheet — it covers 90% of numerical questions asked in KTU university exams.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Purpose / Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Term Frequency (TF)** | $TF(t, d) = \frac{f_{t,d}}{\sum_{t' \in d} f_{t',d}}$ | Normalized frequency of term $t$ in document $d$ |
| 2 | **Inverse Document Frequency (IDF)** | $IDF(t) = \log \left( \frac{N}{1 + df_t} \right)$ | Down-weights common terms, up-weights rare terms |
| 3 | **TF-IDF Weight** | $W(t,d) = TF(t,d) \times IDF(t)$ | Combined importance of a term in a document |
| 4 | **Cosine Similarity** | $\cos(\theta) = \frac{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}}{\vert\vert\vec{A}\vert\vert \cdot \vert\vert\vec{B}\vert\vert}$ | Measures similarity between two document vectors |
| 5 | **Bag-of-Words (BoW)** | $V = \{w_1, w_2, ..., w_n\}$; doc = count vector | Document representation ignoring grammar and word order |
| 6 | **Naïve Bayes Probability** | $P(C \vert D) \propto P(C) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \vert C)$ | Probability of class $C$ given document $D$ with words $w_i$ |
| 7 | **Precision** | $P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Of the items labeled positive, how many are correct |
| 8 | **Recall** | $R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Of all actual positives, how many were retrieved |
| 9 | **F1-Score** | $F1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of precision and recall |
| 10 | **Entropy (Information)** | $H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Measure of impurity in text classification splits |
| 11 | **Pointwise Mutual Information (PMI)** | $PMI(w_1, w_2) = \log \left( \frac{P(w_1, w_2)}{P(w_1) \cdot P(w_2)} \right)$ | Statistical association between two words |
| 12 | **BM25 Score** | $BM25(D,Q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} IDF(q_i) \cdot \frac{f(q_i, D) \cdot (k_1+1)}{f(q_i, D) + k_1 \cdot (1-b + b \cdot \frac{\vert D \vert}{avgdl})}$ | Modern ranking function in information retrieval |
| 13 | **LDA Generative Probability (Conceptual)** | $P(\theta, z, w \vert \alpha, \beta) = P(\theta \vert \alpha) \prod_{n} P(z_n \vert \theta) P(w_n \vert z_n, \beta)$ | Probability of generating word $w$ under topic $z$ with priors $\alpha, \beta$ |
| 14 | **Jaccard Similarity** | $J(A,B) = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | Set-based similarity between two documents (token sets) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> - **Search engines** (Google, Bing) → IR + TF-IDF + BM25 + BERT.
> - **Recommendation systems** → Text similarity + clustering.
> - **Healthcare** → NER on clinical notes for disease detection.
> - **Finance** → Sentiment analysis on earnings calls, news.
> - **Legal** → Document clustering and summarization for case law discovery.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: TF-IDF Computation (Step-by-Step Derivation)

**Problem:** A corpus contains 3 documents:
- **D1:** "the cat sat on the mat"
- **D2:** "the dog sat on the rug"
- **D3:** "cats and dogs are pets"

Compute the **TF-IDF vector** for the term **"sat"** in D1.

**Step 1 — Compute Term Frequency (TF)**

The term "sat" appears **1 time** in D1. Total words in D1 = 6.

$$TF(\text{"sat"}, D1) = \frac{f_{\text{sat}, D1}}{\sum_{t' \in D1} f_{t',D1}} = \frac{1}{6}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Document Frequency (DF)**

The term "sat" appears in D1 and D2 → in 2 documents out of 3.

$$df_{\text{sat}} = 2$$

**Step 3 — Compute Inverse Document Frequency (IDF)**

Total number of documents $N = 3$.

$$IDF(\text{"sat"}) = \log \left( \frac{N}{1 + df_t} \right) = \log \left( \frac{3}{1 + 2} \right) = \log(1) = 0$$

**Step 4 — Compute TF-IDF Weight**

$$W(\text{"sat"}, D1) = TF(\text{"sat"}, D1) \times IDF(\text{"sat"}) = \frac{1}{6} \times 0 = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A TF-IDF score of **0** means the term "sat" is **not discriminative** — it appears in too many documents to help distinguish them. This is the correct behavior of TF-IDF: penalize common terms.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Cosine Similarity Between Two Documents

**Problem:** Given TF vectors:
- $\vec{D1} = (1, 2, 0, 1)$
- $\vec{D2} = (0, 1, 1, 1)$

Find the **cosine similarity**.

**Step 1 — Compute the dot product $\vec{D1} \cdot \vec{D2}$**

$$\vec{D1} \cdot \vec{D2} = (1 \times 0) + (2 \times 1) + (0 \times 1) + (1 \times 1) = 0 + 2 + 0 + 1 = 3$$

**Step 2 — Compute magnitudes**

$$\vert\vert\vec{D1}\vert\vert = \sqrt{1^2 + 2^2 + 0^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{1 + 4 + 0 + 1} = \sqrt{6}$$

$$\vert\vert\vec{D2}\vert\vert = \sqrt{0^2 + 1^2 + 1^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{0 + 1 + 1 + 1} = \sqrt{3}$$

**Step 3 — Compute cosine similarity**

$$\cos(\theta) = \frac{\vec{D1} \cdot \vec{D2}}{\vert\vert\vec{D1}\vert\vert \cdot \vert\vert\vec{D2}\vert\vert} = \frac{3}{\sqrt{6} \cdot \sqrt{3}} = \frac{3}{\sqrt{18}} = \frac{3}{3\sqrt{2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A cosine similarity of **0.707** indicates **moderately high similarity** between the two documents (1.0 = identical, 0 = completely different).

### 3.3 Worked Example 3: Naïve Bayes Text Classification

**Problem:** Training set:
- **Class Spam:** "buy cheap meds", "discount meds now", "cheap offer"
- **Class Ham:** "hello friend", "meeting tomorrow", "call me later"

Classify the new document: **"cheap meds offer"**

**Step 1 — Compute prior probabilities**

$$P(\text{Spam}) = \frac{3}{6} = 0.5, \quad P(\text{Ham}) = \frac{3}{6} = 0.5$$

**Step 2 — Build vocabulary and compute likelihoods with Laplace smoothing ($k=1$)**

Vocabulary size $V = 11$ unique words. Vocabulary = {buy, cheap, meds, discount, now, offer, hello, friend, meeting, tomorrow, call, me, later} → $V = 13$.

| Word | Count in Spam | Count in Ham |
|---|---|---|
| cheap | 2 | 0 |
| meds | 2 | 0 |
| offer | 1 | 0 |

$$P(\text{cheap} \vert \text{Spam}) = \frac{2 + 1}{9 + 13} = \frac{3}{22} \approx 0.136$$

$$P(\text{meds} \vert \text{Spam}) = \frac{2 + 1}{9 + 13} = \frac{3}{22} \approx 0.136$$

$$P(\text{offer} \vert \text{Spam}) = \frac{1 + 1}{9 + 13} = \frac{2}{22} \approx 0.091$$

$$P(\text{cheap} \vert \text{Ham}) = \frac{0 + 1}{7 + 13} = \frac{1}{20} = 0.05$$

$$P(\text{meds} \vert \text{Ham}) = \frac{0 + 1}{7 + 13} = \frac{1}{20} = 0.05$$

$$P(\text{offer} \vert \text{Ham}) = \frac{0 + 1}{7 + 13} = \frac{1}{20} = 0.05$$

**Step 3 — Compute posterior for Spam**

$$P(\text{Spam} \vert D) \propto P(\text{Spam}) \times P(\text{cheap} \vert \text{Spam}) \times P(\text{meds} \vert \text{Spam}) \times P(\text{offer} \vert \text{Spam})$$

$$P(\text{Spam} \vert D) \propto 0.5 \times 0.136 \times 0.136 \times 0.091 \approx 0.000842$$

**Step 4 — Compute posterior for Ham**

$$P(\text{Ham} \vert D) \propto P(\text{Ham}) \times P(\text{cheap} \vert \text{Ham}) \times P(\text{meds} \vert \text{Ham}) \times P(\text{offer} \vert \text{Ham})$$

$$P(\text{Ham} \vert D) \propto 0.5 \times 0.05 \times 0.05 \times 0.05 = 0.0000625$$

**Step 5 — Decide**

Since $0.000842 \gg 0.0000625$, the document is classified as **Spam**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Algorithm vs. Approach

| Approach | Input Representation | Algorithm Used | Output Type | Evaluation Metric |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Information Extraction | Token sequences | Rule-based, CRF, BERT | Structured (entities, triples) | Precision, Recall, F1 |
| Text Classification | TF-IDF, embeddings | Naïve Bayes, SVM, LSTM | Class label | Accuracy, F1 |
| Text Clustering | TF-IDF, embeddings | K-Means, Hierarchical | Cluster ID | Silhouette score, Purity |
| Topic Modeling | Term-Document matrix | LDA, LSA, NMF | Topic-word distributions | Perplexity, Coherence |
| Sentiment Analysis | Embeddings, lexicons | Lexicon, RNN, BERT | Polarity score | Accuracy, Macro-F1 |
| Text Summarization | Sentence embeddings | TextRank, BART, T5 | Summary text | ROUGE, BLEU |
| Keyword Extraction | TF-IDF, embeddings | TF-IDF, RAKE, KeyBERT | Keyword list | Precision@K |
| Information Retrieval | Inverted index, VSM | BM25, BERT | Ranked document list | MAP, NDCG, MRR |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**

**Q1. Define Text Mining. List any four major text mining approaches.**
*(Model Answer — 3 Marks)*

**Text Mining** is the process of extracting meaningful, non-trivial, previously unknown, and potentially useful information or knowledge from unstructured textual data using computational, statistical, and linguistic techniques.

**Four major text mining approaches:**
1. **Information Extraction (IE)** — Extracting structured entities, relations, and events from text.
2. **Text Classification (Categorization)** — Assigning predefined labels to documents (e.g., spam filtering, news categorization).
3. **Text Clustering** — Grouping similar documents without predefined labels using unsupervised learning.
4. **Sentiment Analysis (Opinion Mining)** — Determining the emotional polarity (positive, negative, neutral) of a text.

*(Other valid approaches: Topic Modeling, Text Summarization, Information Retrieval, Keyword Extraction.)*

---

**Q2. What is TF-IDF? Why is it preferred over raw term frequency?**
*(Model Answer — 3 Marks)*

**TF-IDF (Term Frequency–Inverse Document Frequency)** is a statistical measure used in text mining and information retrieval to evaluate how important a word is to a document in a corpus.

$$\text{TF-IDF}(t, d) = TF(t, d) \times \log \left( \frac{N}{1 + df_t} \right)$$

**Why preferred over raw term frequency?**
- Raw TF gives **high weightage to common words** like "the", "is", "and", which carry little discriminative power.
- IDF **down-weights such ubiquitous terms** and **up-weights rare but informative terms**.
- The combined TF-IDF produces a **balanced, discriminative feature representation** that improves performance in classification, clustering, and retrieval tasks.
- It helps the model focus on **semantically meaningful words** unique to specific documents.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - End Semester - Module 4 Internal Choice Pattern]**
> **Note:** Students must answer ONE full question. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. (a)** Explain the **Text Mining Process** in detail with a neat block diagram. Discuss the major stages and their significance. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The **Text Mining Process** is a systematic, multi-stage pipeline that transforms raw, unstructured text into actionable knowledge. The major stages are:

**1. Text Collection / Corpus Assembly**
- Gathering relevant textual data from sources (web, databases, social media, emails, files).
- **Significance:** Defines the scope and domain of analysis.

**2. Text Preprocessing**
- **Tokenization:** Splitting text into individual words/tokens.
- **Stop-word Removal:** Eliminating common, low-information words (the, is, a).
- **Stemming/Lemmatization:** Reducing words to their root form (e.g., "running" → "run").
- **Lowercasing, punctuation removal, normalization.**
- **Significance:** Reduces noise, dimensionality, and improves model efficiency.

**3. Feature Extraction / Representation**
- Converting text into numerical vectors: BoW, TF-IDF, Word2Vec, BERT embeddings.
- **Significance:** Enables mathematical/algorithmic processing of text.

**4. Model Application**
- Applying algorithms: classification (NB, SVM), clustering (K-Means), topic modeling (LDA), NER (CRF, BERT).
- **Significance:** This is the core "mining" step where patterns are discovered.

**5. Pattern Discovery / Knowledge Extraction**
- Extracting rules, associations, topics, sentiments, summaries.
- **Significance:** Translates mathematical outputs into human-understandable insights.

**6. Evaluation & Interpretation**
- Metrics: Precision, Recall, F1, ROUGE, BLEU, Coherence, domain expert review.
- **Significance:** Validates the quality and reliability of mined knowledge.

> **Block Diagram (refer to Section 4.1 for the full schematic).**

---

**Q.A. (b)** Describe the **TF-IDF** technique in detail. Given the following 3-document corpus, compute the TF-IDF weight of the word **"data"** in Document 1. *(7 Marks)*

- **D1:** "data mining is fun"
- **D2:** "data science and mining"
- **D3:** "mining is interesting"

**Model Solution:**

**Conceptual Explanation (4 Marks):**
- **TF** measures local importance (how frequent a term is in one document).
- **IDF** measures global importance (how rare a term is across the entire corpus).
- **TF-IDF** is the product, balancing both local and global perspectives.
- Common terms get low scores; rare and frequent-in-this-doc terms get high scores.

**Numerical Computation (3 Marks):**

**Step 1 — TF of "data" in D1**
"data" appears 1 time. Total words in D1 = 4.

$$TF(\text{"data"}, D1) = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25$$

**Step 2 — DF of "data"**
"data" appears in D1 and D2 → $df = 2$.

**Step 3 — IDF of "data"**

$$IDF(\text{"data"}) = \log \left( \frac{N}{1 + df} \right) = \log \left( \frac{3}{1 + 2} \right) = \log(1) = 0$$

**Step 4 — TF-IDF Weight**

$$\text{TF-IDF}(\text{"data"}, D1) = 0.25 \times 0 = 0$$

> **Conclusion:** A TF-IDF score of **0** means "data" is not discriminative across this small corpus — it appears in multiple documents, so it loses its distinguishing power.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**Q.B. (a)** Explain the **Naïve Bayes Classifier** for text classification. State its assumptions and derive the classification rule. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Naïve Bayes** is a probabilistic classifier based on **Bayes' Theorem**, widely used for text classification tasks such as spam detection and sentiment analysis.

**Bayes' Theorem:**

$$P(C \vert D) = \frac{P(D \vert C) \cdot P(C)}{P(D)}$$

Where:
- $P(C \vert D)$ = Posterior probability of class $C$ given document $D$.
- $P(C)$ = Prior probability of class $C$.
- $P(D \vert C)$ = Likelihood of document $D$ given class $C$.
- $P(D)$ = Evidence (constant across classes).

**The "Naïve" Assumption:**
- All features (words) are **conditionally independent** of each other given the class label.
- This simplifies the joint probability to a product of individual word probabilities.

**Derived Classification Rule:**

$$P(C \vert D) \propto P(C) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \vert C)$$

The classifier picks the class with the **maximum posterior**:

$$\hat{C} = \arg\max_{C} P(C) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \vert C)$$

**Word probability estimate (with Laplace smoothing):**

$$P(w_i \vert C) = \frac{\text{count}(w_i, C) + 1}{\sum_{w \in V} \text{count}(w, C) + \vert V \vert}$$

**Advantages:** Simple, fast, works well with high-dimensional sparse data, requires small training set.
**Disadvantages:** Independence assumption is often violated in real text.

---

**Q.B. (b)** Explain **Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA)** for topic modeling with a suitable diagram. Compare LDA with LSA. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**LDA (Latent Dirichlet Allocation)** is a **generative probabilistic model** that assumes each document is a mixture of multiple topics, and each topic is a distribution over words.

**Generative Process:**
1. For each document $d$, sample a topic distribution $\theta_d \sim \text{Dir}(\alpha)$.
2. For each word $w_n$ in document $d$:
   - Sample a topic $z_n \sim \text{Multinomial}(\theta_d)$.
   - Sample a word $w_n \sim \text{Multinomial}(\phi_{z_n})$ from the topic's word distribution.

**Generative Probability:**

$$P(\theta, z, w \vert \alpha, \beta) = P(\theta \vert \alpha) \prod_{n=1}^{N} P(z_n \vert \theta) P(w_n \vert z_n, \beta)$$

The goal is to **infer the hidden topic structures** $z$ and $\theta$ from observed words using **Gibbs Sampling** or **Variational Inference**.

**LDA vs. LSA Comparison:**

| Aspect | LSA (Latent Semantic Analysis) | LDA (Latent Dirichlet Allocation) |
|---|---|---|
| **Approach** | Linear algebra (SVD on term-doc matrix) | Probabilistic generative model |
| **Output** | Real-valued topic vectors | Probability distributions (topics) |
| **Interpretability** | Lower (negative values possible) | Higher (clean probability distributions) |
| **Assumptions** | None explicit | Dirichlet prior, bag-of-words |
| **Scalability** | Fast (SVD) | Slower (iterative inference) |
| **Best for** | Small corpora, IR | Larger corpora, topic discovery |

> **Diagram (refer to Section 4.4 for the LDA generative process flow).**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing TF-IDF with TF alone** — TF-IDF is the *product* of TF and IDF. Writing only the TF formula is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Forgetting Laplace smoothing in Naïve Bayes numericals** — Always add 1 to numerator and $|V|$ to denominator. Examiners explicitly test this.
> 3. **Writing "Data Mining" instead of "Text Mining"** in definitions — Text mining is a *specialized* extension. Mention the **unstructured** nature explicitly.
> 4. **Missing the block diagram in process questions** — A neat, labeled block diagram fetches **2-3 marks** by itself. Don't skip it.
> 5. **No real-world examples** — Examiners reward practical examples (e.g., "Used by Google Search", "Used in spam filtering"). Always cite 1-2 applications.
> 6. **Confusing LSA and LDA acronyms** — LSA = Latent *Semantic* Analysis; LDA = Latent *Dirichlet* Allocation. Easy to mix up under exam pressure.
> 7. **Skipping units/interpretation in numericals** — Always state *"Therefore, the documents are X% similar"* after a cosine similarity calculation.
> 8. **Ignoring stop-word removal and stemming in preprocessing questions** — These are **mandatory** preprocessing steps. List them explicitly.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Text Mining** = Knowledge discovery from unstructured text; sits at the intersection of **Data Mining, NLP, and IR**.
- The **6-stage pipeline**: Collection → Preprocessing → Feature Extraction → Modeling → Knowledge Extraction → Evaluation.
- **Preprocessing is non-negotiable** — always include **tokenization, stop-word removal, stemming/lemmatization** in any answer.
- **TF-IDF** = $TF \times IDF$ where $IDF = \log(N / (1 + df_t))$. Penalizes common words, rewards rare discriminative words.
- **Cosine Similarity** ranges from **-1 to 1** (usually 0 to 1 for TF-IDF). Used to rank document relevance.
- **Naïve Bayes** is the **go-to baseline** for text classification. Always use **Laplace smoothing** in calculations.
- **LDA** is a **generative probabilistic model**; **LSA** is a **linear algebra (SVD-based)** model. LDA gives interpretable probability distributions.
- **Information Extraction (IE)** = structured extraction; **Information Retrieval (IR)** = finding relevant docs; **NLP** = the linguistic engine.
- **Evaluation metrics** to remember: **Precision, Recall, F1** (for classification); **ROUGE, BLEU** (for summarization); **Perplexity, Coherence** (for topic models); **MAP, NDCG** (for retrieval).
- **Real-world applications**: Search engines (Google), spam filters (Gmail), voice assistants (Alexa), healthcare NLP, legal e-discovery, financial sentiment analysis.
- **Modern shift**: Traditional BoW/TF-IDF → **Word2Vec, GloVe, BERT, GPT** (contextual embeddings). Mention this trend in any "recent advances" sub-question.
- **Key acronyms** to memorize: NER, RE, IE, IR, NLP, LDA, LSA, NMF, BM25, PMI, BoW, TF-IDF, VSM.
- **Cosine similarity > Euclidean distance** for text — because text vectors are high-dimensional and sparse; Euclidean distance becomes meaningless.
- **High-dimensionality problem** — Vocabulary size can be **100,000+**, leading to the "curse of dimensionality". Feature selection, embeddings, and dimensionality reduction (PCA, SVD) are mitigations.
- **Stemming vs. Lemmatization** — Stemming is crude (chops word ends: "studies" → "studi"); Lemmatization is linguistic (uses vocabulary: "studies" → "study"). Examiners love this distinction.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Web Mining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT524 - DATA MINING AND WAREHOUSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Web Mining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Web Mining** is the automated process of discovering and extracting previously unknown, potentially useful, and actionable patterns, knowledge, and structures from heterogeneous web data sources — including web page content, hyperlink topology, and user access logs. It applies classical **data mining**, **machine learning**, **natural language processing (NLP)**, and **information retrieval (IR)** techniques to the unique, semi-structured, and highly dynamic environment of the World Wide Web.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Web Mining is the Data Mining technique used to discover patterns and implicit, potentially useful information from web data. According to the KTU syllabus for *PEITT524 (Data Mining and Warehousing)*, Web Mining is broadly classified into three orthogonal categories based on the *type of web data* being mined.

## 1.2 The Three Taxonomic Categories of Web Mining

| Taxonomy Branch | Target Data Source | Primary Goal | Analogous Real-World Concept |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Web Content Mining (WCM)** | Text, images, audio, video, structured records *inside* web pages | Extract useful information from the *content* of pages | Reading and summarizing books in a library |
| **Web Structure Mining (WSM)** | Hyperlinks between pages (the *Web Graph*) | Discover the underlying link topology and authority of pages | Mapping a city's road network to find important junctions |
| **Web Usage Mining (WUM)** | Server logs, click-streams, cookies, user profiles | Discover user navigation *behavior* patterns | Analyzing CCTV footage in a mall to find the most walked path |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hospital Library"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box — Think of the Web as a Giant Hospital Library:**
>
> Imagine you walk into a massive hospital library containing **millions of medical journals**:
>
> 1. **Web Content Mining** = You open individual journals and try to extract cures, side effects, and treatments from the *text itself*. You are analyzing **what is written**.
> 2. **Web Structure Mining** = You ignore the text completely. You only look at the **citation graph** — which paper cites which paper. From this *structure*, you can determine which papers are most authoritative (cited by many others).
> 3. **Web Usage Mining** = You place CCTV cameras at the entrance and watch which sections doctors *visit most often*, in what order, and how long they stay. You mine **behavioral patterns**, not content.
>
> Each of these three views gives a *different kind of intelligence* about the same library. The web is no different — the same three lenses are applied to extract different types of knowledge.

## 1.4 Why Web Mining is Hard — The Web is NOT a Database

The web is fundamentally different from traditional structured data. The challenges that distinguish web mining from classical data mining are:

- **Scale:** Billions of pages, growing dynamically.
- **Heterogeneity:** Text, HTML, XML, multimedia, scripts.
- **Semi-structured:** No rigid schema.
- **Distributed:** Data is scattered geographically.
- **Noisy:** Spam, broken links, duplicates.
- **Dynamic:** Pages change, appear, or disappear constantly.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of the Three Branches

### 2.1.1 Web Content Mining (WCM)

WCM deals with the *informational* content of web pages. The KTU syllabus recognizes two distinct views:

**(a) Information Retrieval (IR) View:**
- Treats the web as a *collection of documents*.
- Uses techniques such as **TF-IDF weighting**, **cosine similarity**, **latent semantic indexing (LSI)**, and **topic modeling** to rank and cluster pages.
- Focus is on *unstructured or semi-structured text*.

**(b) Database (DB) View:**
- Tries to model the web as a *collection of structured records* (e.g., extracting product catalogs from HTML tables).
- Uses techniques such as **wrapper induction**, **web scraping**, and **schema mapping**.
- Focus is on transforming semi-structured HTML/XML into relational tables for **OLAP** queries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **WCM Applications:** Search engine indexing, opinion mining (sentiment analysis), competitor price monitoring, automated news aggregation.

### 2.1.2 Web Structure Mining (WSM)

WSM exploits the *hyperlink topology* of the web, represented as a **directed graph** $G = (V, E)$, where:
- $V$ = set of web pages (nodes)
- $E$ = set of hyperlinks (directed edges)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Central Premise of WSM — "A Hyperlink is a Vote":**
> A hyperlink from page $A$ to page $B$ is interpreted as an *endorsement* — page $A$ believes page $B$ contains quality information. By aggregating these votes, we can rank pages by importance.

The two cornerstone algorithms of WSM are:

#### (A) PageRank Algorithm

PageRank assigns a numerical score $PR(p)$ to every page $p$ representing its global importance.

**The Random Surfer Model:**
Imagine a web surfer who clicks links randomly. Occasionally they get bored and "teleport" to a random page. PageRank is the *steady-state probability* that this surfer is on page $p$.

#### (B) HITS Algorithm (Hyperlink-Induced Topic Search)

HITS computes two scores per page:
- **Hub Score** $H(p)$: How good a page is at *pointing to* authoritative resources.
- **Authority Score** $A(p)$: How good a page is at *being pointed to* by good hubs.

### 2.1.3 Web Usage Mining (WUM)

WUM follows a strict **three-stage pipeline** as per the KTU syllabus:

**Stage 1 — Preprocessing:**
- **Data Cleaning:** Remove log entries for images, failed requests, robots (web crawlers).
- **User Identification:** Map sessions to unique users via cookies/IPs.
- **Session Identification:** Split each user's log into *sessions* using a 30-minute inactivity timeout.
- **Path Completion:** Fill in missing pages using referrer logs and caching heuristics.

**Stage 2 — Pattern Discovery:**
- Apply algorithms such as **Apriori** (for frequent page-sets), **clustering** (user segmentation), and **sequential pattern mining** (common navigation paths).

**Stage 3 — Pattern Analysis:**
- Filter out uninteresting rules, visualize click-streams as **directed graphs**, and present findings to business analysts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **WUM Applications:** E-commerce recommendation engines (Amazon, Flipkart), website personalization, A/B testing, server load prediction, and intrusion detection.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **formula cheat-sheet** for solving KTU Part B 14-mark problems on Web Mining. Master every equation.

| # | Algorithm / Concept | Governing Formula | Variable Definitions |
|:--|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **PageRank (Damping Form)** | $$PR(p) = \frac{1-d}{N} + d \sum_{q \to p} \frac{PR(q)}{L(q)}$$ | $N$ = total pages, $d$ = damping factor (≈ **0.85**), $L(q)$ = number of outbound links from $q$ |
| 2 | **PageRank (Matrix Form)** | $$\mathbf{r} = d \, \mathbf{M} \mathbf{r} + \frac{1-d}{N} \mathbf{1}$$ | $\mathbf{r}$ = PageRank vector, $\mathbf{M}$ = column-stochastic transition matrix |
| 3 | **HITS Authority** | $$A(p) = \sum_{q \to p} H(q)$$ | Sum of hub scores of all pages linking *to* $p$ |
| 4 | **HITS Hub** | $$H(p) = \sum_{p \to r} A(r)$$ | Sum of authority scores of all pages *linked from* $p$ |
| 5 | **HITS Normalization** | $$A(p) \leftarrow \frac{A(p)}{\sqrt{\sum_{x} A(x)^2}}$$ | $L_2$ norm normalization after every iteration |
| 6 | **TF-IDF Weight** | $$w_{ij} = tf_{ij} \cdot \log\left(\frac{N}{df_{j}}\right)$$ | $tf_{ij}$ = term freq in doc $i$, $N$ = total docs, $df_j$ = docs containing term $j$ |
| 7 | **Cosine Similarity** | $$\text{sim}(d_1, d_2) = \frac{\vec{d_1} \cdot \vec{d_2}}{\Vert \vec{d_1} \Vert \cdot \Vert \vec{d_2} \Vert}$$ | Similarity between two document TF-IDF vectors |
| 8 | **Support (WUM)** | $$\text{Support}(P) = \frac{\text{Sessions containing } P}{\text{Total sessions}}$$ | $P$ = a page-set pattern |
| 9 | **Confidence (WUM)** | $$\text{Confidence}(P \Rightarrow Q) = \frac{\text{Support}(P \cup Q)}{\text{Support}(P)}$$ | Conditional probability of page $Q$ given $P$ |
| 10 | **Sessionization Timeout** | $\Delta t_{max} = \mathbf{30 \text{ minutes}}$ | Standard KTU-accepted inactivity threshold for new session |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Search Engines (Google, Bing):** PageRank + content signals drive ranking.
- **Recommendation Systems (Netflix, Spotify):** WUM-based collaborative filtering.
- **Cybersecurity:** WUM detects anomalous click-streams indicating bots or attacks.
- **E-Commerce Personalization:** WUM clusters buyers for targeted ads.
- **Digital Forensics:** WSM visualizes link-farms and spam networks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive PageRank Numerical Computation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked KTU-Style Example:** Consider a toy web with **3 pages** $\{A, B, C\}$ and the following hyperlink structure:
> - $A \to B$, $A \to C$
> - $B \to C$
> - $C \to A$
>
> The damping factor $d = 0.85$ and $N = 3$.

### Step 1 — Build the Stochastic Transition Matrix

The transition matrix $M$ has entry $M_{ij} = \frac{1}{L(j)}$ if there is a link $j \to i$, else $0$.

$$M = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 0.5 & 0 & 0 \\ 0.5 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Conversion logic:** Each column $j$ sums to 1, and the value in row $i$ equals the probability of moving *from* $j$ *to* $i$.

### Step 2 — Write the PageRank Vector Update

We start with the **uniform vector** $r^{(0)}$:

$$r^{(0)} = \begin{bmatrix} 1/3 \\ 1/3 \\ 1/3 \end{bmatrix}$$

The update rule is:

$$r^{(k+1)} = d \, M \, r^{(k)} + \frac{1-d}{N} \mathbf{1}$$

### Step 3 — First Iteration (k = 0 → 1)

Compute $M \, r^{(0)}$:

$$M \, r^{(0)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 0.5 & 0 & 0 \\ 0.5 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1/3 \\ 1/3 \\ 1/3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1/3 \\ 1/6 \\ 1/2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Now apply the damping:

$$r^{(1)} = 0.85 \begin{bmatrix} 1/3 \\ 1/6 \\ 1/2 \end{bmatrix} + \frac{0.15}{3} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$= \begin{bmatrix} 0.2833 \\ 0.1416 \\ 0.4250 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.05 \\ 0.05 \\ 0.05 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3333 \\ 0.1916 \\ 0.4750 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 4 — Second Iteration (k = 1 → 2)

$$M \, r^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 0.5 & 0 & 0 \\ 0.5 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.3333 \\ 0.1916 \\ 0.4750 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$= \begin{bmatrix} 0.4750 \\ 0.1666 \\ 0.3583 \end{bmatrix}$$

Apply damping:

$$r^{(2)} = 0.85 \begin{bmatrix} 0.4750 \\ 0.1666 \\ 0.3583 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.05 \\ 0.05 \\ 0.05 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$= \begin{bmatrix} 0.4037 \\ 0.1916 \\ 0.3545 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.05 \\ 0.05 \\ 0.05 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4537 \\ 0.2416 \\ 0.4045 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 5 — Convergence Check

Repeat iterations until $\Vert r^{(k+1)} - r^{(k)} \Vert_1 < 10^{-4}$. The final steady-state PageRank vector is:

$$r^{*} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3878 \\ 0.2148 \\ 0.3974 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Interpretation:** Page $C$ has the highest rank (it is linked by both $A$ and $B$), followed closely by $A$ (it receives a link from $C$), and $B$ ranks lowest (it only receives a link from $A$).

> [!NOTE]
> The values sum to 1.0000, confirming the probabilistic nature of the PageRank distribution.

## 3.2 Exhaustive HITS Algorithm Numerical Computation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked KTU Example:** Use the same 3-page graph. Initialize **Authority** and **Hub** vectors to $a^{(0)} = h^{(0)} = (1, 1, 1)^T$.

### Step 1 — Update Authority Scores

$$A^{(1)}(p) = \sum_{q \to p} H^{(0)}(q)$$

- $A^{(1)}(A) = H(C) = 1$
- $A^{(1)}(B) = H(A) = 1$
- $A^{(1)}(C) = H(A) + H(B) = 1 + 1 = 2$

$$a^{(1)} = (1, 1, 2)^T$$

### Step 2 — Update Hub Scores

$$H^{(1)}(p) = \sum_{p \to r} A^{(1)}(r)$$

- $H^{(1)}(A) = A(B) + A(C) = 1 + 2 = 3$
- $H^{(1)}(B) = A(C) = 2$
- $H^{(1)}(C) = A(A) = 1$

$$h^{(1)} = (3, 2, 1)^T$$

### Step 3 — Normalize

Compute $L_2$ norms:

$$\Vert a^{(1)} \Vert_2 = \sqrt{1^2 + 1^2 + 2^2} = \sqrt{6} \approx 2.449$$

$$\Vert h^{(1)} \Vert_2 = \sqrt{3^2 + 2^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{14} \approx 3.742$$

Divide each vector component-wise:

$$a^{(1)} = (0.408, 0.408, 0.816)$$

$$h^{(1)} = (0.802, 0.534, 0.267)$$

### Step 4 — Iterate Until Convergence

Repeating the procedure, the system converges to:

$$a^{*} = (0.408, 0.408, 0.816)$$

$$h^{*} = (0.802, 0.534, 0.267)$$

**Interpretation:** Page $C$ is the **top authority** (linked by both $A$ and $B$), while page $A$ is the **top hub** (it points to the two most important pages).

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Board Favorite)

| Feature | PageRank | HITS |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Query Dependency** | Query-independent (computed offline) | Query-dependent (computed on the fly) |
| **Scores Computed** | One (PageRank) | Two (Hub + Authority) |
| **Computation Time** | Expensive; done at crawl time | Cheap; done at query time |
| **Topic Drift** | Resistant (global) | Prone (uses local subgraph) |
| **Spam Resistance** | High (mass teleportation counteracts link-spam) | Low (vulnerable to spam farms) |
| **Used By** | Google Search | Initially by Clever (IBM), academic use |
| **Mathematical Form** | Eigenvector of modified adjacency matrix | Singular Vector Decomposition (SVD) of adjacency matrix |

## 3.4 Web Usage Mining — Session Reconstruction Worked Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked KTU Example:** Given the following server log entries (in timestamp order), reconstruct the user sessions using a **30-minute timeout rule**.

| Time | User/IP | Page Requested |
|:---|:---|:---|
| 08:00 | U1 | /index.html |
| 08:05 | U1 | /products.html |
| 08:12 | U1 | /product1.html |
| 08:50 | U1 | /contact.html |
| 08:53 | U1 | /help.html |

### Step 1 — Calculate Inter-Click Intervals

- $08{:}00 \to 08{:}05$ = **5 min** ✓
- $08{:}05 \to 08{:}12$ = **7 min** ✓
- $08{:}12 \to 08{:}50$ = **38 min** ✗ (exceeds 30 min → new session!)
- $08{:}50 \to 08{:}53$ = **3 min** ✓

### Step 2 — Output Sessions

- **Session 1:** {/index.html, /products.html, /product1.html}
- **Session 2:** {/contact.html, /help.html}

**Conversion logic applied:** The threshold of $\mathbf{30 \text{ minutes}}$ is the *universally accepted KTU benchmark* for web session boundary detection.

## 3.5 Apriori on Web Usage — Worked Toy Example

Suppose we have the following 4 sessions:

| Session | Pages Visited |
|:---|:---|
| $S_1$ | {A, B, C} |
| $S_2$ | {A, B} |
| $S_3$ | {A, D} |
| $S_4$ | {A, B, D} |

Let $\text{min\_support} = 0.5$ (i.e., pattern must appear in $\geq 2$ sessions).

**L1 (1-element frequent itemsets):** A = 4/4, B = 3/4, C = 1/4, D = 2/4.
**After filtering ($\geq 0.5$):** L1 = {A, B, D}.

**C2 (candidate pairs):** {AB, AD, BD}.
- Support(AB) = 3/4 → frequent
- Support(AD) = 2/4 → frequent
- Support(BD) = 1/4 → pruned

**C3 (candidate triples):** {ABD}.
- Support(ABD) = 1/4 → pruned.

**Final Association Rules:**

$$A \Rightarrow B: \text{Conf} = \frac{3/4}{4/4} = 0.75$$

$$A \Rightarrow D: \text{Conf} = \frac{2/4}{4/4} = 0.50$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Business Action:** "Users who visit page A are 75% likely to also visit page B" → website can pre-cache page B when page A is requested.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Define Web Mining. List and briefly explain its three main categories.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Web Mining is the application of data mining techniques to automatically discover and extract useful, previously unknown patterns and knowledge from web data — encompassing web page content, hyperlink structures, and user access logs.

**Three categories (2 marks):**

1. **Web Content Mining (WCM):** Extracts useful information from the *content* (text, images, audio, video) inside web pages. Uses IR or DB techniques.
2. **Web Structure Mining (WSM):** Discovers knowledge from the *hyperlink topology* of the web. The fundamental premise is that a hyperlink is a vote of confidence. Algorithms: **PageRank** and **HITS**.
3. **Web Usage Mining (WUM):** Mines user *behavior* by analyzing server logs, click-streams, and cookies. It has three stages: preprocessing, pattern discovery, and pattern analysis.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Differentiate between Web Content Mining and Web Usage Mining with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | Web Content Mining | Web Usage Mining |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Data source** | Text, images, media *within* web pages | Server logs, click-streams, cookies |
| **Mines** | *What the page says* | *What the user did* |
| **Techniques** | NLP, TF-IDF, clustering, wrapper induction | Apriori, sequential mining, clustering |
| **Example** | Extracting all product prices from an e-commerce HTML page | Analyzing server logs to discover that 70% of users who visit /mobile also visit /mobile-covers within 10 minutes |

**Example illustration (1 mark):** A news aggregator uses WCM to extract headlines, while the same portal uses WUM to discover that "sports readers click more on cricket articles between 7 PM and 10 PM."

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

> **(a)** Explain the **PageRank algorithm** in detail. Derive the PageRank formula and discuss the role of the damping factor. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Compute the PageRank values for the following 4-page web graph using $d = 0.85$. Show at least two iterations. (7 marks)
>
> **Graph:** $A \to B$, $A \to D$, $B \to C$, $C \to A$, $C \to D$, $D \to B$

#### **Solution to Q.A (a):**

**PageRank — Concept (2 marks):**
PageRank, developed by *Larry Page and Sergey Brin (founders of Google)*, is a link-analysis algorithm that assigns a numerical weighting to every page on the web. The weight indicates the *relative importance* of a page. A page is considered important if it is linked to by many other important pages.

**Random Surfer Model (2 marks):**
Imagine a web surfer who:
- Visits a page.
- With probability $d$ (≈ **0.85**), clicks a random outgoing link.
- With probability $(1 - d) = 0.15$, gets bored and *teleports* to a completely random page.

The PageRank of a page is the **steady-state probability** that this surfer is on that page at any given moment.

**Derivation of the Formula (2 marks):**
Let $PR(p)$ be the PageRank of page $p$, $L(q)$ be the number of outbound links from $q$, and $N$ be the total number of pages.

$$PR(p) = \frac{1-d}{N} + d \sum_{q \to p} \frac{PR(q)}{L(q)}$$

The term $\frac{1-d}{N}$ represents the **teleportation contribution**, and the summation represents the **link-based probability flow**.

**Role of Damping Factor (1 mark):**
- Prevents the *rank sink problem* (a closed loop of pages that would otherwise accumulate all PageRank).
- Ensures convergence of the iterative computation.
- Empirically, $d = 0.85$ gives the best quality results.

#### **Solution to Q.A (b):**

**Step 1 — Build the adjacency & transition matrix (2 marks):**

Out-degrees: $L(A) = 2$, $L(B) = 1$, $L(C) = 2$, $L(D) = 1$.

$$M = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0.5 & 0 \\ 0.5 & 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0.5 & 0 & 0.5 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Initialize and apply the update (3 marks):**

Initial vector $r^{(0)} = (0.25, 0.25, 0.25, 0.25)^T$. Teleportation vector $\frac{1-d}{N} \mathbf{1} = (0.0375, 0.0375, 0.0375, 0.0375)^T$.

**Iteration 1:**

$$M r^{(0)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.125 \\ 0.125 \\ 0.250 \\ 0.250 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$r^{(1)} = 0.85 \begin{bmatrix} 0.125 \\ 0.125 \\ 0.250 \\ 0.250 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.0375 \\ 0.0375 \\ 0.0375 \\ 0.0375 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1062 \\ 0.1062 \\ 0.2500 \\ 0.2500 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Iteration 2:**

$$M r^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1250 \\ 0.0531 \\ 0.1062 \\ 0.1781 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$r^{(2)} = 0.85 \begin{bmatrix} 0.1250 \\ 0.0531 \\ 0.1062 \\ 0.1781 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.0375 \\ 0.0375 \\ 0.0375 \\ 0.0375 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1438 \\ 0.0826 \\ 0.1278 \\ 0.1889 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Interpret (2 marks):** After full convergence (typically 50–100 iterations), pages with the highest in-degree from high-quality pages get the highest PageRank. In this graph, page $C$ and $D$ (each receiving two high-quality inbound links) are expected to be top-ranked.

---

### ⭐ **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

> **(a)** Explain the **HITS algorithm** in detail. How are Hub and Authority scores computed and normalized? (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Describe the **three stages of Web Usage Mining** with a suitable example for each stage. (7 marks)

#### **Solution to Q.B (a):**

**HITS — Concept (2 marks):**
**Hyperlink-Induced Topic Search (HITS)**, developed by *Jon Kleinberg (IBM, 1999)*, is a link-analysis algorithm that computes two scores per page: a **Hub score** and an **Authority score**, based on the principle of *mutual reinforcement*.

**Mutual Reinforcement Principle (1 mark):**
- A good **authority** is a page pointed to by many good **hubs**.
- A good **hub** is a page that points to many good **authorities**.

**Score Computation (2 marks):**

$$A(p) = \sum_{q \to p} H(q)$$

$$H(p) = \sum_{p \to r} A(r)$$

These equations are applied iteratively until the vectors converge. The final values are the **left and right singular vectors** of the adjacency matrix $A$ of the web subgraph.

**Normalization (2 marks):** After every iteration, the Authority and Hub vectors are normalized using the $L_2$ norm:

$$A(p) \leftarrow \frac{A(p)}{\sqrt{\sum_x A(x)^2}}$$

This prevents scores from growing unboundedly and keeps the geometric interpretation of the singular vectors valid.

#### **Solution to Q.B (b):**

**Stage 1 — Preprocessing (3 marks):**
Raw server logs are cleaned and transformed. The sub-steps are:
- **Data Cleaning:** Remove log entries for images, CSS, JavaScript, and failed HTTP requests (status 4xx/5xx).
- **User Identification:** Map sessions to unique users using cookies, authentication, or IP+Agent combination.
- **Session Identification:** Split each user's click-stream into sessions using the **30-minute inactivity timeout** rule.
- **Path Completion:** Use the Referer field to infer pages that were not logged because of caching.

**Example:** Given log entries — U1 visits /home at 09:00, /products at 09:05, /contact at 09:50 — the 38-minute gap triggers a *new session*. Output: Session 1 = {/home, /products}, Session 2 = {/contact}.

**Stage 2 — Pattern Discovery (2 marks):**
Apply mining algorithms to the cleaned sessions:
- **Apriori** for frequent page-sets.
- **Sequential Pattern Mining** for navigation paths.
- **Clustering** (k-means, DBSCAN) for user segmentation.

**Example:** Apriori on the cleaned sessions reveals that 75% of users who visit /home also visit /products.

**Stage 3 — Pattern Analysis (2 marks):**
Filter uninteresting rules, compute interestingness metrics (lift, confidence), and visualize using **click-stream graphs** or **OLAP cubes** on a usage data warehouse.

**Example:** The marketing team receives a heatmap of the website showing the most-clicked path: Home → Products → Cart → Checkout (drop-off rate 60% at Cart).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Web Mining Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Teleportation Term:** Many students write only the summation in PageRank, missing the $\frac{1-d}{N}$ term. The damping factor is what *defines* the modern PageRank formula. **(–2 marks)**
> 2. **Mixing PageRank and HITS definitions:** HITS is **query-dependent** (computed at query time), whereas PageRank is **query-independent** (precomputed). Confusing these loses a full mark.
> 3. **Wrong out-degree in denominator:** In PageRank, the denominator is $L(q)$ — the number of *outbound* links from $q$, **not** the number of inbound links. **(–1 mark)**
> 4. **Skipping the Preprocessing Stage:** When asked about WUM, students often jump directly to "mining algorithms." The examiner specifically wants the **cleaning → user identification → sessionization → path completion** pipeline. **(–2 marks)**
> 5. **No normalization in HITS:** Without the $L_2$ normalization, the iterative scores diverge to infinity. Always normalize between iterations.
> 6. **Confusing WCM IR view vs DB view:** IR view = unstructured text mining; DB view = structured data extraction. They are *not* the same.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Web Mining — The 1-Page Last-Minute Revision Sheet**
>
> ✅ **Three branches:** WCM (content), WSM (link structure), WUM (user behavior).
>
> ✅ **PageRank formula:** $PR(p) = \frac{1-d}{N} + d \sum_{q \to p} \frac{PR(q)}{L(q)}$ with $d = 0.85$.
>
> ✅ **PageRank is query-independent; computed offline at crawl time.**
>
> ✅ **HITS computes two scores:** Authority $A(p) = \sum H(q)$ and Hub $H(p) = \sum A(r)$.
>
> ✅ **HITS is query-dependent; computed online at query time using a focused subgraph.**
>
> ✅ **HITS is mathematically equivalent to computing the top singular vectors of the adjacency matrix via SVD.**
>
> ✅ **WUM Pipeline = Preprocessing → Pattern Discovery → Pattern Analysis.**
>
> ✅ **Preprocessing sub-steps:** Cleaning, User ID, Sessionization (30 min), Path Completion.
>
> ✅ **Apriori metrics:** Support = frequency, Confidence = conditional probability.
>
> ✅ **TF-IDF for WCM:** $w_{ij} = tf_{ij} \cdot \log(N/df_j)$ — rare words score higher.
>
> ✅ **Dangling nodes (out-degree = 0)** must be handled in PageRank to avoid rank sinks.
>
> ✅ **Real-world users:** Google → PageRank; Amazon → WUM; Flipkart/MySQL schema → WCM (DB view).
>
> ✅ **Examiner favorite diagram:** Always draw the **WUM 3-stage pipeline** in any 7-mark WUM question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT524 - DATA MINING AND WAREHOUSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Web Content Mining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Web Content Mining (WCM)** is the process of discovering, extracting, analyzing, and evaluating useful information and knowledge from the **content** of web pages and web documents. It is a sub-discipline of **Web Mining**, specifically focused on the textual, visual, structural, and semantic content residing within web resources (HTML pages, PDFs, images, videos, multimedia, structured records, etc.).

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** for *PEITT524 – Data Mining and Warehousing*, Web Content Mining is broadly categorized under the umbrella of **Web Mining**, alongside *Web Structure Mining* and *Web Usage Mining*. The formal taxonomy recognized by KTU follows the framework introduced by **Etzioni (1996)** and further refined by **Kosala & Blockeel (2000)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The course expects students to differentiate between the **three sub-areas of Web Mining** (Content, Structure, Usage), describe the techniques used in Web Content Mining (IR, IE, NLP, Classification, Clustering), and relate them to practical applications like search engines, recommendation systems, and sentiment analysis.

The three pillars of Web Mining under the KTU framework are:

| Sub-Area | What it Mines | Primary Data Source |
|----------|---------------|---------------------|
| **Web Content Mining** | Text, images, audio, video, structured records | Page contents, documents, multimedia |
| **Web Structure Mining** | Topology, hyperlinks, page rank | Graph of hyperlinks |
| **Web Usage Mining** | Click-streams, logs, sessions | Server logs, browser histories |

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Digital Library Curator**
>
> Imagine the World Wide Web is a **gigantic, unorganized library** containing **billions of books** (web pages) written in **hundreds of languages**, with **no catalog**, **no index cards**, and **new books added every second**. **Web Content Mining** is like hiring a **super-intelligent librarian** whose job is to:
>
> 1. **Read every book** and understand its content (Information Extraction).
> 2. **Sort books** into categories like "Science", "Sports", "Politics" (Classification).
> 3. **Group similar books** together on the same shelf (Clustering).
> 4. **Summarize** long documents into 2-line abstracts (Summarization).
> 5. **Answer questions** posed by visitors by searching across all books (Information Retrieval — like Google).
>
> Just like a librarian needs tools (card catalogs, Dewey Decimal System), Web Content Mining uses **algorithms** (TF-IDF, Naïve Bayes, K-Means, Cosine Similarity) to perform these tasks automatically at **petabyte scale**.

---

### 1.3 The Three Layers of Web Content

Web content exists in a hierarchy that determines *how* mining techniques must be applied:

- **Layer 1 — Surface Web (Visible Web):** Indexable, publicly accessible content crawled by search engines. Estimated to be only **4–16%** of total web content.
- **Layer 2 — Deep Web (Hidden Web):** Content behind forms, databases, dynamically generated pages (e.g., flight booking results). Not directly crawlable.
- **Layer 3 — Dark Web:** Content requiring special browsers like Tor; anonymized; not the focus of standard WCM.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** Questions on "challenges of Web Content Mining" almost always expect students to mention the **heterogeneity**, **volume**, **dynamicity**, and **semi-structured nature** of web content as the **four pillars of difficulty**.

---

### 1.4 Types of Web Data Mined

Web Content Mining deals with heterogeneous data types:

1. **Unstructured Text** — Free-flowing natural language in blog posts, news articles, forums.
2. **Semi-Structured Data** — HTML/XML documents with tags providing structural hints.
3. **Structured Data** — HTML tables, JSON, RDF triples, knowledge graphs (e.g., Wikipedia infoboxes).
4. **Multimedia Data** — Images, audio, video requiring computer vision / speech processing.
5. **Metadata** — Tags, keywords, anchor text, page descriptions, meta-tags.

## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Web Content Mining Pipeline (Step-by-Step)

The operational pipeline of a Web Content Mining system is decomposed into **seven sequential stages**. Each stage transforms the data progressively from raw HTML to high-level knowledge.

#### Stage 1 — Web Crawling / Data Acquisition
- A **web crawler** (spider/bot) systematically traverses the web following hyperlinks.
- Starts from a **seed URL**, fetches the page, extracts new URLs, adds them to a **frontier queue**.
- Respects the `robots.txt` protocol and politeness policies.

#### Stage 2 — Preprocessing
- **HTML Parsing:** Removing tags (`<div>`, `<span>`), keeping only textual content.
- **Tokenization:** Splitting text into words, sentences, or n-grams.
- **Stop-word Removal:** Eliminating high-frequency words (`the`, `is`, `a`).
- **Stemming / Lemmatization:** Reducing words to their root form. For example:
  - Stemming: `running`, `ran`, `runs` → `run` (Porter Stemmer rule).
  - Lemmatization: `better` → `good` (uses vocabulary + morphological analysis).

#### Stage 3 — Feature Extraction / Representation
- Converting unstructured text into a **vector form** that algorithms can process.
- The standard approach is the **Vector Space Model (VSM)** introduced by **Salton (1975)**.
- Each document *D* is represented as a vector $\vec{D} = (w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n)$ in an *n*-dimensional space where each dimension corresponds to a unique term in the corpus.

#### Stage 4 — Mining / Analytical Task
- Apply a specific data mining technique:
  - **Classification** — Assigning a label from a predefined set (spam vs. ham).
  - **Clustering** — Grouping similar documents without labels.
  - **Association Rule Mining** — Finding co-occurring terms.
  - **Anomaly Detection** — Identifying outlier content.

#### Stage 5 — Pattern Evaluation
- Use statistical measures: **Support**, **Confidence**, **Lift**, **Precision**, **Recall**, **F-Measure**.

#### Stage 6 — Knowledge Representation
- Visualizing patterns as graphs, charts, tag clouds, decision trees, or rule sets.

#### Stage 7 — Application Deployment
- Search engines, recommendation engines, business intelligence dashboards, alerting systems.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **must-know** for KTU university examinations. Memorize the symbols, ranges, and the operational interpretation.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Range / Interpretation |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Term Frequency (TF)** | $tf(t,d) = f_{t,d}$ | $f_{t,d}$ = count of term *t* in doc *d* | $\geq 0$; higher = more important locally |
| 2 | **Inverse Document Frequency (IDF)** | $idf(t,D) = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{N}{df_t}\right)$ | $N$ = total docs; $df_t$ = docs containing term *t* | $\geq 0$; higher = more discriminative globally |
| 3 | **TF-IDF Weight** | $w_{t,d} = tf(t,d) \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{N}{df_t}\right)$ | Combined importance | Higher = stronger semantic weight |
| 4 | **Cosine Similarity** | $\cos(\theta) = \frac{\vec{A}\cdot\vec{B}}{\lVert\vec{A}\rVert \cdot \lVert\vec{B}\rVert}$ | Two document vectors | $[-1, 1]$; **1** = identical, **0** = orthogonal |
| 5 | **Entropy (Shannon)** | $H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | $p_i$ = probability of class *i* | $[0, \log_2 c]$ bits |
| 6 | **Information Gain** | $IG(S,A) = H(S) - \sum_{v \in A}\frac{\lvert S_v\rvert}{\lvert S\rvert} H(S_v)$ | $S$ = dataset; $A$ = attribute | Higher = better attribute for split |
| 7 | **Precision** | $Precision = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | TP = True Pos; FP = False Pos | $[0,1]$; accuracy of positive predictions |
| 8 | **Recall** | $Recall = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | FN = False Negatives | $[0,1]$; coverage of actual positives |
| 9 | **F-Measure** | $F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of P and R | $[0,1]$; balance metric |
| 10 | **Support (Association)** | $Support(X) = \frac{freq(X)}{N}$ | Frequency of itemset *X* | $[0,1]$ |
| 11 | **Confidence (Association)** | $Confidence(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{Support(X \cup Y)}{Support(X)}$ | Conditional probability | $[0,1]$ |

---

### 2.3 Classification vs. Clustering — The Crucial Distinction

| Aspect | Classification | Clustering |
|--------|----------------|------------|
| **Supervision** | Supervised (labeled data) | Unsupervised (no labels) |
| **Goal** | Predict label of new doc | Discover natural groupings |
| **Algorithms** | Naïve Bayes, SVM, Decision Tree, KNN | K-Means, DBSCAN, Hierarchical |
| **Output** | Discrete class label | Cluster ID for each doc |
| **Evaluation** | Accuracy, F-Measure, Confusion Matrix | Silhouette Score, Davies-Bouldin Index |
| **Example** | Spam detection in email | Grouping news articles by topic |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of Web Content Mining

- **Search Engines (Google, Bing):** Use TF-IDF + PageRank to rank pages for a query.
- **E-Commerce (Amazon, Flipkart):** Mine product descriptions to suggest complementary items.
- **Social Media Monitoring (Twitter, Reddit):** Sentiment classification of posts during elections or product launches.
- **Healthcare & Epidemiology:** Track disease outbreaks by mining health forums and news.
- **Finance:** Automated trading signal generation from financial news.
- **Cybersecurity:** Phishing page detection by content classification.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility Note:** Modern web content mining pipelines underpin **Large Language Models (LLMs)** like GPT and BERT. The pretraining corpus of these models is essentially the *output* of massive-scale web content mining operations (crawling + cleaning + tokenization).

## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of TF-IDF Weight

The TF-IDF (Term Frequency–Inverse Document Frequency) formula is the **cornerstone of modern information retrieval**. We will derive it from first principles using information-theoretic reasoning.

**Step 1 — Term Frequency (TF).** The simplest assumption is that a term's importance in a document is directly proportional to how many times it appears:

$$
tf(t, d) = f_{t,d}
$$

where $f_{t,d}$ is the raw count of term $t$ in document $d$.

**Step 2 — Normalized TF (to prevent bias toward long documents).** Longer documents naturally contain higher term frequencies. To remove this bias:

$$
tf_{norm}(t, d) = \frac{f_{t,d}}{\max_{t' \in d} f_{t', d}}
$$

This divides every term count by the maximum count of any term in the document.

**Step 3 — Inverse Document Frequency (IDF).** Some terms like "the", "is", "of" appear in almost every document and carry no discriminative power. We need a function that gives **high weight to rare terms** and **low weight to common terms**.

If a term $t$ appears in $df_t$ documents out of a total $N$, the probability of a random document containing $t$ is:

$$
P(\text{doc contains } t) = \frac{df_t}{N}
$$

The **self-information** or **surprisal** of this event is:

$$
I(t) = -\log_{2}\!\left(\frac{df_t}{N}\right) = \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{N}{df_t}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Combine TF and IDF multiplicatively.** This gives the final TF-IDF weight:

$$
w_{t,d} = tf(t, d) \cdot \log\!\left(\frac{N}{df_t}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** TF captures **local relevance** (important in this document), while IDF captures **global discriminativeness** (rare in the corpus). Their product balances both. A term with $w_{t,d} \geq \text{threshold}$ (typically $\geq 0.1$ after smoothing) is considered a strong keyword.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — TF-IDF Computation

**Corpus Setup (mini example):**
- Document $D_1$: *"The cat sat on the mat."*
- Document $D_2$: *"The dog sat on the rug."*
- Total documents: $N = 2$

**Step 1 — Build the vocabulary.** Unique terms after tokenization and lowercasing: $\{ \text{the}, \text{cat}, \text{sat}, \text{on}, \text{mat}, \text{dog}, \text{rug} \}$.

**Step 2 — Compute document frequencies $df_t$:**

$$
df(\text{the}) = 2, \quad df(\text{cat}) = 1, \quad df(\text{dog}) = 1
$$
$$
df(\text{sat}) = 2, \quad df(\text{on}) = 2, \quad df(\text{mat}) = 1, \quad df(\text{rug}) = 1
$$

**Step 3 — Compute IDF using $\log_{10}$:**

$$
idf(\text{the}) = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2}{2}\right) = \log_{10}(1) = 0
$$

$$
idf(\text{cat}) = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2}{1}\right) = \log_{10}(2) \approx 0.301
$$

By symmetry: $idf(\text{dog}) = idf(\text{mat}) = idf(\text{rug}) \approx 0.301$, and $idf(\text{sat}) = idf(\text{on}) = 0$.

**Step 4 — Compute TF (using raw counts):**

$$
tf(\text{cat}, D_1) = 1, \quad tf(\text{dog}, D_2) = 1
$$

**Step 5 — TF-IDF weights:**

$$
w(\text{cat}, D_1) = 1 \cdot 0.301 = 0.301
$$

$$
w(\text{dog}, D_2) = 1 \cdot 0.301 = 0.301
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Observation:** Stop-words like *"the"* and *"sat"* received IDF = 0, hence zero weight in the TF-IDF vector — precisely the desired behavior. Only content-bearing words like *cat*, *dog*, *mat*, *rug* carry non-zero weights.

---

### 3.3 Cosine Similarity — Full Derivation

Given two documents represented as TF-IDF vectors $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$, we want to measure how **directionally similar** they are (independent of magnitude).

**Step 1 — Dot product:**

$$
\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i b_i
$$

**Step 2 — Euclidean norms:**

$$
\lVert\vec{A}\rVert = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i^2}, \quad \lVert\vec{B}\rVert = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} b_i^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Cosine similarity (geometric definition):**

$$
\cos(\theta) = \frac{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}}{\lVert\vec{A}\rVert \cdot \lVert\vec{B}\rVert}
$$

**Worked numerical example:** Let $\vec{A} = (0.301, 0, 0, 0, 0.301, 0, 0)$ and $\vec{B} = (0, 0.301, 0, 0, 0, 0.301, 0)$ for the two documents above.

$$
\vec{A}\cdot\vec{B} = 0
$$

$$
\cos(\theta) = \frac{0}{\sqrt{0.301^2 + 0.301^2} \cdot \sqrt{0.301^2 + 0.301^2}} = 0
$$

**Interpretation:** $\cos(\theta) = 0$ means the documents are **orthogonal** (no shared terms of weight), hence dissimilar. If the documents had shared content-words, the cosine would be closer to **1**.

---

### 3.4 Information Gain — Step-by-Step

Consider a dataset $S$ of **100 web pages** classified into Spam (40) and Not-Spam (60).

**Step 1 — Compute entropy of $S$:**

$$
H(S) = -p_{spam}\log_2 p_{spam} - p_{not} \log_2 p_{not}
$$

$$
H(S) = -\frac{40}{100}\log_2\!\left(\frac{40}{100}\right) - \frac{60}{100}\log_2\!\left(\frac{60}{100}\right)
$$

$$
H(S) = -0.4 \cdot (-1.3219) - 0.6 \cdot (-0.7369)
$$

$$
H(S) = 0.5288 + 0.4422 = 0.9710 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Consider attribute $A$ = "Contains the word 'free'".** Partition $S$ into:
- $S_{free}$ (40 docs): Spam = 35, Not-Spam = 5
- $S_{\neg free}$ (60 docs): Spam = 5, Not-Spam = 55

**Step 3 — Compute entropy of each partition:**

$$
H(S_{free}) = -\frac{35}{40}\log_2\!\left(\frac{35}{40}\right) - \frac{5}{40}\log_2\!\left(\frac{5}{40}\right)
$$

$$
H(S_{free}) \approx -0.875 \cdot 0.1926 - 0.125 \cdot 3 = -0.1685 - 0.375 = 0.5436 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
H(S_{\neg free}) = -\frac{5}{60}\log_2\!\left(\frac{5}{60}\right) - \frac{55}{60}\log_2\!\left(\frac{55}{60}\right)
$$

$$
H(S_{\neg free}) \approx -0.0833 \cdot 3.585 - 0.9167 \cdot 0.1255 \approx 0.2988 + 0.1151 = 0.4139 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 4 — Weighted average entropy after split:**

$$
H(S \mid A) = \frac{40}{100} \cdot 0.5436 + \frac{60}{100} \cdot 0.4139
$$

$$
H(S \mid A) = 0.4 \cdot 0.5436 + 0.6 \cdot 0.4139
$$

$$
H(S \mid A) = 0.2174 + 0.2483 = 0.4657 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 5 — Information Gain:**

$$
IG(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \mid A) = 0.9710 - 0.4657 = 0.5053 \text{ bits}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A gain of **0.5053 bits** is substantial — it means the attribute "contains 'free'" reduces uncertainty about spam classification by over **52%**. This attribute would be selected as a high-importance feature in a decision tree.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — WCM Techniques

| Technique | Input | Output | Strength | Weakness |
|-----------|-------|--------|----------|----------|
| **TF-IDF + Cosine** | Raw text | Similarity score | Simple, interpretable | Ignores semantics, context |
| **Naïve Bayes** | Bag-of-words | Class probabilities | Fast, handles high-dim | Assumes feature independence |
| **SVM** | TF-IDF vectors | Hyperplane class | Strong on text | Slow on huge corpora |
| **K-Means** | Document vectors | Cluster assignments | Scales well | Needs *k* in advance |
| **LDA** | Corpus | Topic distributions | Probabilistic, interpretable | Slow inference |
| **BERT / Embeddings** | Raw text | Dense semantic vectors | Captures context | Computationally expensive |

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Define Web Content Mining. List any four challenges associated with it.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Web Content Mining** is the process of extracting useful information, patterns, and knowledge from the *content* of web pages and web documents (text, images, audio, video, structured records). It employs techniques from NLP, information retrieval, and machine learning.

**Four major challenges:**

1. **Heterogeneity of Data:** The web contains text in multiple languages, formats (HTML, PDF, XML, JSON), and multimedia. A single algorithm cannot process all formats uniformly.
2. **Volume and Scalability:** The web has billions of pages; algorithms must scale to **petabyte-level** data without degradation.
3. **Dynamic Nature of the Web:** Pages are updated, created, and deleted constantly. Mining results become stale quickly.
4. **Semi-Structured Nature:** Unlike traditional databases, web content lacks a rigid schema — the same information may be presented in countless HTML structures.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for challenges)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Distinguish between Web Content Mining and Web Structure Mining with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Web Content Mining | Web Structure Mining |
|-----------|---------------------|----------------------|
| **Data Source** | Text, images, videos inside pages | Hyperlinks connecting pages |
| **Goal** | Discover knowledge *within* a page | Discover knowledge *from* page connections |
| **Techniques** | NLP, TF-IDF, Classification | PageRank, HITS, Graph algorithms |
| **Example Use Case** | Classifying a news article as "Sports" | Ranking pages by their inbound link authority |

**Example:** A search engine applying **WCM** might detect that a webpage is about *"machine learning"* based on its textual content. Applying **WSM**, the same engine may rank that page higher because authoritative sites (e.g., university domains) link to it.

*(3 marks — table + example for full marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> Students frequently lose marks by (1) confusing **Web Content Mining** with **Web Usage Mining**, (2) writing only the formula without explaining its components, (3) omitting the *practical application* context, and (4) failing to differentiate between **supervised** and **unsupervised** learning approaches when discussing classification vs. clustering. Always include at least one **real-world example** and explicitly **state the input/output** of any algorithm mentioned.

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** *(Internal Choice Option 1)*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a Web Content Mining system with a neat block diagram. Discuss in detail the role of the **Preprocessing** and **Feature Extraction** stages. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain how **TF-IDF** is used in web content mining. Compute the TF-IDF weights for the following mini-corpus and identify the most important term in each document:

- **Document 1:** *"data mining is fascinating"*
- **Document 2:** *"data science uses mining techniques"*
- **Document 3:** *"science and data analytics"*

Use $\log_{10}$ in your calculation. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### **Part (a) — Solution:**

**Architecture of a Web Content Mining System:**

A Web Content Mining system is typically organized as a multi-stage pipeline. The major components are:

**1. Web Crawler:** A software agent that systematically browses the web, starting from seed URLs. It downloads HTML pages and stores them in a local repository. The crawler obeys the `robots.txt` policy and uses politeness delays to avoid overloading servers.

**2. Repository / Document Store:** A scalable storage system (e.g., distributed file systems, NoSQL databases) holding billions of crawled web pages. Examples include **HDFS** and **Google File System**.

**3. Preprocessing Module:** Transforms raw HTML into a clean, analyzable form. This includes:
- **HTML Tag Stripping:** Removing `<div>`, `<p>`, `<a>` tags to retain only textual content.
- **Tokenization:** Breaking sentences into individual words.
- **Stop-word Removal:** Filtering out high-frequency words like *"the"*, *"a"*, *"of"*.
- **Stemming/Lemmatization:** Reducing words to base form (*"running"* → *"run"*).

**4. Feature Extraction:** Converts preprocessed text into numerical vector form using the **Vector Space Model**. Each document becomes a vector where each dimension corresponds to a unique term, weighted by **TF-IDF**.

**5. Mining Engine:** Applies analytical algorithms such as Naïve Bayes, K-Means, or association rule mining to discover patterns.

**6. Pattern Evaluator:** Measures quality using metrics like **Precision**, **Recall**, **F-Measure**, **Support**, and **Confidence**.

**7. Knowledge Presentation Layer:** Renders results through dashboards, tag clouds, search results, or recommendation lists.

> [!NOTE]
> **Role of Preprocessing (re-emphasized):** Preprocessing is **critical** because raw web text is noisy — it contains advertisements, navigation menus, and boilerplate content. Without preprocessing, mining algorithms would treat these as signal, drastically reducing accuracy. Industry studies show preprocessing can improve classification accuracy by **15–30%**.

**Role of Feature Extraction:** It bridges the gap between human-readable text and machine-readable numbers. Without TF-IDF or similar representations, no mathematical algorithm can operate on text.

*(Diagram reference: Section 4.4 above.)* **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Part (b) — Solution:**

**Concept of TF-IDF:**

$$
w(t, d) = tf(t, d) \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{N}{df_t}\right)
$$

where:
- $tf(t, d)$ = frequency of term *t* in document *d*.
- $N$ = total number of documents in the corpus.
- $df_t$ = number of documents containing term *t*.

**Step-by-Step Computation:**

**Step 1 — Vocabulary (after lowercasing):**
$\{ \text{data}, \text{mining}, \text{is}, \text{fascinating}, \text{science}, \text{uses}, \text{techniques}, \text{and}, \text{analytics} \}$.

**Step 2 — Total documents:** $N = 3$.

**Step 3 — Document Frequencies ($df_t$):**

| Term | $df_t$ | Documents |
|------|--------|-----------|
| data | 3 | D1, D2, D3 |
| mining | 2 | D1, D2 |
| is | 1 | D1 |
| fascinating | 1 | D1 |
| science | 2 | D2, D3 |
| uses | 1 | D2 |
| techniques | 1 | D2 |
| and | 1 | D3 |
| analytics | 1 | D3 |

**Step 4 — IDF values:** $\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3}{df_t}\right)$

| Term | $df_t$ | IDF |
|------|--------|-----|
| data | 3 | $\log_{10}(1) = 0$ |
| mining | 2 | $\log_{10}(1.5) \approx 0.176$ |
| is | 1 | $\log_{10}(3) \approx 0.477$ |
| fascinating | 1 | $\log_{10}(3) \approx 0.477$ |
| science | 2 | $\log_{10}(1.5) \approx 0.176$ |
| uses | 1 | $\log_{10}(3) \approx 0.477$ |
| techniques | 1 | $\log_{10}(3) \approx 0.477$ |
| and | 1 | $\log_{10}(3) \approx 0.477$ |
| analytics | 1 | $\log_{10}(3) \approx 0.477$ |

**Step 5 — TF and TF-IDF for each document:**

**Document 1:** *"data mining is fascinating"*

| Term | TF | IDF | TF-IDF |
|------|----|----|--------|
| data | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| mining | 1 | 0.176 | 0.176 |
| is | 1 | 0.477 | 0.477 |
| fascinating | 1 | 0.477 | 0.477 |

**Most important term in D1:** Either *"is"* or *"fascinating"* (both 0.477). Semantically, *"fascinating"* is the meaningful term (since *"is"* is a stop-word that should have been removed in real pipelines).

**Document 2:** *"data science uses mining techniques"*

| Term | TF | IDF | TF-IDF |
|------|----|----|--------|
| data | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| science | 1 | 0.176 | 0.176 |
| uses | 1 | 0.477 | 0.477 |
| mining | 1 | 0.176 | 0.176 |
| techniques | 1 | 0.477 | 0.477 |

**Most important term in D2:** *"uses"* or *"techniques"* (both 0.477). Semantically, *"techniques"* dominates.

**Document 3:** *"science and data analytics"*

| Term | TF | IDF | TF-IDF |
|------|----|----|--------|
| science | 1 | 0.176 | 0.176 |
| and | 1 | 0.477 | 0.477 |
| data | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| analytics | 1 | 0.477 | 0.477 |
